Method: Formulate descriptor fusion as a generalized eigenvalue problem that maximizes between-place separability while suppressing within-place variation across references. Identify linear combinations of descriptor dimensions that preserve place identity (robust to conditions/viewpoints) versus those that capture condition- or viewpoint-specific variation. Produce a single compact fused descriptor from leading eigenvectors and evaluate across datasets (Oxford RobotCar, Nordland, Pittsburgh30k, Google Landmarks v2) and six VPR descriptors.

Result: Empirically, DisPlace outperforms seven multi-reference baselines in 49 of 54 appearance-varying conditions; shows consistent gains for descriptor-level fusion under viewpoint and unstructured settings; achieves lower storage requirements during inference than all compared fusion methods.

Conclusion: Leveraging cross-reference variation via a generalized eigenvalue fusion yields discriminative, compact place representations that improve VPR robustness to appearance and viewpoint changes while reducing storage compared with existing fusion approaches.

Abstract: A key challenge in Visual Place Recognition (VPR) is matching query images against reference maps captured under diverse environmental conditions and viewpoints. While multiple reference traversals improve robustness, existing fusion strategies either aggregate references uniformly or rely on heuristic selection, without distinguishing descriptor variations that preserve stable place identity from those caused by changing conditions or viewpoints. In this paper, we propose DisPlace, a multi-reference VPR framework that fuses multiple reference descriptors into a single compact and discriminative place representation. DisPlace formulates descriptor fusion as a generalized eigenvalue problem that maximizes between-place separability while suppressing within-place variation across references, rather than preserving overall descriptor variance. Unlike existing multi-reference fusion methods, DisPlace exploits variation across reference traversals to identify which linear combinations of descriptor dimensions preserve place identity and which capture condition- or viewpoint-specific variation. We evaluate DisPlace on Oxford RobotCar, Nordland, Pittsburgh30k, and Google Landmarks v2 across six state-of-the-art VPR descriptors. DisPlace outperforms seven multi-reference baselines in 49 out of 54 appearance-varying conditions, consistently improves descriptor-level fusion performance under viewpoint and unstructured settings, and requires less storage during inference than all compared fusion methods.

</details>


### [27] [CameraNoise: Enabling Faithful Camera Control in Video Diffusion through Geometry-Flow-Guided Noise Warping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30774)
*Haoyu Zhao,Jiaxi Gu,Haoran Chen,Qingping Zheng,Yeying Jin,Hongyi Yang,Junqi Cheng,Yuang Zhang,Zenghui Lu,Huan Yu,Jie Jiang,Peng Shu,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based video method (CameraNoise) encodes camera motion into a temporally coherent noise representation to preserve geometric consistency and trajectory fidelity, outperforming prior conditioning approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between abstract camera coordinates and visual content in diffusion models by decoupling motion from appearance and ensuring temporally coherent camera trajectories.

Method: Introduce Geometry-guided Reprojection Flow and a noise warping algorithm that embed camera poses into the diffusion noise space, preserving the Gaussian prior and ensuring consistent noise propagation under camera transformations within the diffusion process.

Result: Experiments show substantial improvements in both visual quality and trajectory faithfulness relative to prior methods, yielding stable, high-fidelity videos.

Conclusion: CameraNoise achieves stable, geometry-consistent video diffusion by embedding motion directly in noise space and decoupling pose from appearance; code and project page are provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Precise camera pose control is critical for video diffusion, yet maintaining geometric consistency remains a challenge. Existing methods that directly inject numerical camera parameters into the diffusion backbone often fail to bridge the gap between abstract coordinates and visual content, leading to structural distortions. To address this issue, we propose CameraNoise, a flow-to-noise warping method that encodes camera motion into a temporally coherent stochastic representation. Unlike conventional conditioning, CameraNoise embeds camera poses directly into the noise space. This decouples motion from scene appearance while faithfully preserving trajectory dynamics. Specifically, we introduce a novel Geometry-guided Reprojection Flow and a noise warping algorithm, which jointly preserve the Gaussian prior of diffusion and ensure consistent noise propagation under camera transformations. By integrating CameraNoise into the diffusion process, our framework delivers stable, high-fidelity videos. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms prior methods in both visual quality and trajectory faithfulness. The project page and code are available at: https://gulucaptain.github.io/CameraNoise/.

</details>


### [28] [Text-guided Feature Disentanglement for Cross-modal Gait Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30784)
*Zhiyang Lu,Ming Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes TCFDNet, a text-guided cross-modal feature disentanglement network for LiDAR-Camera gait recognition, using a GMTD built from LLMs, a CLIP-based encoder, and modules for feature disentanglement (TFD), stability (FSE), and cross-modal patch exchange to achieve state-of-the-art results on SUSTech1K and FreeGait.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the cross-modal gait recognition problem between 2D video and 3D point clouds (LiDAR) by bridging modality gaps with semantic priors and disentangled representations, enabling robust, cross-modal gait features.

Method: 1) Build Gait Modality Text Dictionary (GMTD) from large language models to provide semantic descriptions across modalities. 2) Use a CLIP-based multi-grained feature encoder to align visual and textual features in a unified vision-language space. 3) Apply Text-guided Feature Disentanglement (TFD) to select top-k texts to reconstruct modality-specific representations and derive modality-shared features via residual decomposition and orthogonality constraints. 4) Introduce Feature Stability Enhancement (FSE) to model spatial and channel correlations for robustness. 5) Employ cross-modal patch exchange to improve generalization.

Result: Extensive experiments on SUSTech1K and FreeGait show the method achieves new state-of-the-art results and validate the effectiveness of the proposed modules.

Conclusion: Text-guided cross-modal disentanglement with semantic priors and stability mechanisms effectively bridges LiDAR-RGB gait recognition, establishing a strong performance baseline and offering insights into cross-modal representation learning.

Abstract: Gait recognition is a biometric technique that identifies individuals based on their walking patterns, offering advantages in long-range, non-intrusive scenarios. However, real-world scenarios often involve heterogeneous sensing modalities such as LiDAR and RGB cameras, making LiDAR-Camera Cross-modal Gait recognition (LCCGR) a critical yet challenging task due to the substantial modality gap between 2D videos and 3D point cloud sequences. To address this challenge, we propose TCFDNet, a Text-guided Cross-modal Feature Disentanglement Network, which leverages modality-aware textual priors as semantic anchors to guide the learning of disentangled modality-shared representations. Specifically, we construct a Gait Modality Text Dictionary (GMTD) using large language models to generate rich semantic descriptions of gait across modalities and viewpoints. A CLIP-based Multi-grained Feature Encoder then aligns visual and textual features within a unified vision-language space. Furthermore, the Text-guided Feature Disentanglement (TFD) module selects the topk matched textual descriptions to reconstruct modality-specific representations and derive modality-shared features via residual decomposition and orthogonality constraints. To mitigate the fragility of the disentangled shared features, we propose a Feature Stability Enhancement (FSE) module, which models spatial and channel-wise correlations to improve feature robustness. In addition, a cross-modal patch exchange strategy is introduced to further improve generalization. Extensive experiments on SUSTech1K and FreeGait datasets demonstrate that TCFDNet achieves new state-of-the-art results and validate the effectiveness of the proposed modules.

</details>


### [29] [MechVQA: Benchmarking and Enhancing Multimodal LLMs on Comprehensive Mechanical Drawing Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30794)
*Qian Kou,Xiaofeng Shi,Yulin Li,Xiaosong Qiu,Xinyang Wang,Hua Zhou,Cao Dongxing*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces MechVQA, a large, high-density mechanical drawing QA dataset (3.3k images, 21k QA across 10 tasks; three capability levels: Recognition, Reasoning, Judging) and MechVL, a domain-specialized MLLM trained via multi-stage training to improve understanding of mechanical drawings, achieving a 7.57 point gain over the strongest closed-source baseline on MechVQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle with mechanical drawings due to high annotation density, weak domain knowledge, unreliable spatial relation reasoning under projection rules, and strict geometric constraints, leading to brittle performance; a dedicated dataset and domain-adapted model are needed to bridge the gap between general VQA capabilities and mechanical drawing understanding.

Method: Construct MechVQA through a semi-automated pipeline delivering 3.3k images and 21k QA across 10 fine-grained tasks (Recognition, Reasoning, Judging) to form a testbed for real-world mechanical drawings. Develop MechVL via a multi-stage training paradigm to create a strong domain-specialized baseline for mechanical drawing understanding.

Result: MechVL outperforms the strongest closed-source baseline by 7.57 percentage points on the MechVQA total score, demonstrating substantially improved mechanical drawing understanding and offering a reusable foundation for deployment in mechanical design and inspection contexts.

Conclusion: MechVQA and MechVL provide a new benchmark and baseline that address the brittleness of MLLMs in high-density engineering diagrams, enabling more reliable mechanical design and inspection applications and guiding future work in domain-aware multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated significant achievements in general visual question answering (VQA) tasks. However, they remain brittle on mechanical engineering drawings, where high annotation density and weak domain knowledge, compounded by unreliable spatial relation reasoning under strict projection rules and geometric constraints, make decisive cues easy to miss and frequently lead to wrong answers. To bridge this gap, we introduce the first comprehensive mechanical drawing understanding dataset, MechVQA, created through a semi-automated construction and quality-control pipeline. MechVQA contains 3.3k high-density pictures with 21K question-answer pairs, spanning 10 different fine-grained tasks across three capability levels: Recognition, Reasoning, and Judging, providing a testbed to evaluate and improve MLLM understanding on real-world mechanical drawings. On top of MechVQA, we then develop the MechVL model through a multi-stage training paradigm, building a strong domain-specialized baseline. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that MechVL outperforms the strongest closed-source baseline by 7.57 percentage points on the MechVQA total score, significantly enhancing mechanical drawing understanding ability and providing a reusable foundation for deploying MLLMs in mechanical design and inspection scenarios.

</details>


### [30] [Function2Scene: 3D Indoor Scene Layout from Functional Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30819)
*Ruiqi Wang,Qimin Chen,Daniel Ritchie,Angel X. Chang,Manolis Savva,Kai Wang,Hao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Function2Scene, a constraint-driven, tool-augmented pipeline that generates 3D indoor layouts from functional briefs, outperforming LLM baselines in 30 cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-driven indoor scene synthesis is object-centric and focuses on what furniture to place rather than how a space supports occupants. There is a need to encode occupant needs, activities, and ergonomic/environmental constraints to produce functionally usable layouts.

Method: Parse occupant personas and activities from natural-language briefs, derive a taxonomy of 17 functional design criteria across spatial, ergonomic, activity, and environmental domains, and use these constraints to guide layout generation. Employ an iterative check-and-repair loop that combines geometric measurements, LLM-based contextual reasoning, and VLM-based visual assessment rather than letting an LLM directly generate the final scene.

Result: On 30 professionally written interior-design cases, Function2Scene produced layouts that better satisfy functional requirements than recent LLM-based baselines, with user preferences favoring Function2Scene in 94.3% of pairwise comparisons.

Conclusion: Reframes text-driven indoor scene synthesis from plausible object placement to design of spaces that support human use, enabling function-driven interior layouts.

Abstract: Most text-driven 3D indoor scene synthesis methods generate rooms from object-centric prompts, asking what furniture should be placed rather than how the space is used. Yet in real interior design, a layout is judged by how well it supports its occupants, e.g., their activities and physical needs. We introduce Function2Scene, a framework for generating 3D indoor layouts from functional specifications, i.e., natural-language design briefs describing who will use a room and what they need to do there. Given such a specification, our system parses occupant personas and activities, derives a customized set of functional design constraints from a taxonomy of 17 criteria spanning spatial, ergonomic, activity, and environmental considerations, and uses these constraints to guide layout generation. Rather than relying on an LLM to directly produce a final scene, Function2Scene performs iterative evaluation and refinement through a tool-augmented check-and-repair loop, combining geometric measurements, LLM-based contextual reasoning, and VLM-based visual assessment. Experiments on 30 professionally written interior-design cases show that Function2Scene produces layouts that better satisfy functional requirements than recent LLM-based scene synthesis baselines, with our results preferred in 94.3% of pairwise comparisons. Our work reframes text-driven indoor scene synthesis from placing plausible objects to designing spaces that support human use.

</details>


### [31] [LegSegNet: A Public Deep Learning System for Lower Extremity CT Tissue Segmentation and Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30829)
*Yuwen Chen,Yaqian Chen,Roy Colglazier,Haoyu Dong,Hanxue Gu,Maciej A. Mazurowski,Kevin W. Southerland*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LegSegNet is an end-to-end deep learning system for automatic segmentation and quantification of lower extremity CT tissues, achieving state-of-the-art performance (Dice 89.31) and enabling scalable body composition analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public lower-extremity CT analysis tools are limited to segmentation masks and do not provide end-to-end tissue quantification, especially for inter/intramuscular adipose tissue, hindering scalable clinical and research workflows.

Method: A deep learning model, LegSegNet, segments bone, skeletal muscle, subcutaneous adipose tissue, and inter/intramuscular adipose tissue from CT scans and computes quantitative tissue measurements. Trained on 1,302 manually annotated slices, evaluated on 900 held-out slices with radiologist review, and benchmarked against diverse 2D segmentation methods; also tested for generalization on an external public CT dataset. Code and weights released.

Result: LegSegNet achieves the best overall segmentation performance with an average Dice score of 89.31 on the held-out test set; identified as the first publicly available end-to-end system for lower-extremity CT tissue segmentation and quantification.

Conclusion: LegSegNet enables comprehensive, scalable, end-to-end analysis of lower-extremity CT tissues and provides a practical evaluation platform for future medical image analysis research; the accompanying codebase facilitates reproducibility and external validation.

Abstract: Lower extremity computed tomography (CT) contains clinically relevant information for body composition analysis, sarcopenia assessment, and musculoskeletal disease monitoring, but extracting these measurements at scale requires accurate tissue segmentation and an automated quantification workflow. Existing public segmentation tools are not designed for comprehensive lower extremity CT analysis, particularly for clinically important inter/intramuscular adipose tissue, and most public methods only provide mask prediction rather than an end-to-end quantification system. To address this problem, we present LegSegNet, a deep learning system for lower extremity CT tissue segmentation and body composition quantification. Given an input CT scan, LegSegNet segments bone, skeletal muscle, subcutaneous adipose tissue, and inter/intramuscular adipose tissue. It then computes quantitative tissue measurements for downstream analysis. We developed the segmentation model using 1,302 manually annotated CT slices and evaluated it on 900 held-out test slices, with all annotations reviewed by radiologists. We benchmark LegSegNet against a broad set of 2D segmentation methods, including CNN-based models, transformer-based models, and finetuned foundation models, and further evaluate its generalization on an external public CT dataset. LegSegNet achieves the best overall segmentation performance, with an average Dice score of 89.31 on the held-out test set. To our knowledge, LegSegNet is the first publicly available end-to-end system for lower extremity CT tissue segmentation and quantification, providing a practical evaluation tool for future computer vision research in medical image analysis. The code and model weights are available at: https://github.com/mazurowski-lab/LegSegNet

</details>


### [32] [Count Anything](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30846)
*Mengqi Lei,Shuokun Cheng,Wei Bao,Shaoyi Du,Jun-Hai Yong,Siqi Li,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A generalist, text-guided object counting framework (Count Anything) built on the CLOC dataset that uses dual-granularity instance counting (region-level and pixel-level) with point-centric supervision to achieve cross-domain counting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counting models are highly domain-specific and struggle to generalize across categories, domains, and density. A unified, text-guided counting framework with interpretable localization is needed.

Method: Introduce CLOC, a Cross-domain Large-scale Object Counting dataset spanning six domains (~220k images, 619 categories, 15M instances). Propose Count Anything with two branches: Region-level Sparse Counter (anchors large, sparse targets) and Pixel-level Dense Counter (dense, small targets). Use point-centric supervision to learn from heterogeneous annotations and a parameter-free Complementary Count Fusion to combine counts from both counters.

Result: Extensive experiments show strong accuracy and robust multi-domain generalization, surpassing existing open-world counting methods.

Conclusion: The work delivers a unified, text-guided counting framework that couples interpretable localization with cross-domain generalization, facilitated by a new dataset and a dual-branch counting mechanism.

Abstract: Object counting remains fragmented across domain-specific datasets and task formulations, despite rapid progress in generalist vision models. Existing counting models are often tailored to scenarios such as crowds, vehicles, cells, crops, or remote-sensing objects, and thus struggle to generalize across categories, visual domains, object scales, and density distributions. In this paper, we study text-guided object counting across domains, where a model takes an image and a natural-language query as input and returns an instance-grounded set of target points whose cardinality gives the count. This formulation unifies category-conditioned counting with interpretable spatial localization. To support this setting, we construct CLOC, a Cross-domain Large-scale Object Counting dataset that reorganizes diverse public data sources into a unified benchmark. CLOC covers six visual domains: General Scene, Remote Sensing, Histopathology, Cellular Microscopy, Agriculture, and Microbiology, with about 220K images, 619 categories, and 15M object instances. Based on CLOC, we propose Count Anything, a generalist model for text-guided object counting. Unlike density-map-based methods, which dominate counting models, Count Anything adopts discrete instance points and performs dual-granularity instance enumeration. A Region-level Sparse Counter provides object-level anchors for large and sparse targets, while a Pixel-level Dense Counter handles small, crowded, and weakly bounded targets via dense point prediction. A point-centric supervision strategy enables learning from heterogeneous annotations, and Complementary Count Fusion combines both counters in a parameter-free manner. Extensive experiments show that Count Anything achieves strong accuracy and multi-domain generalization, outperforming existing open-world counting methods. Code is available at: https://github.com/Mengqi-Lei/count-anything.

</details>


### [33] [Robust Dreamer: Deviation-Aware Latent Gaussian Memory for Action-Controlled AR Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30855)
*Hanlin Chen,Jiaxin Wei,Xibin Song,Yifu Wang,Steve Wang,Hongdong Li,Pan Ji,Gim Hee Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Memory-augmented 3D-aware image-to-video framework (Robust Dreamer) that stabilizes long-horizon generation via Latent Gaussian Memory and Dynamic Deviation Archive, addressing latent-RGB cycling and training-inference gaps to achieve state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frame-wise, action-conditioned video generation suffers drift and degraded 3D consistency over long rollouts due to information loss from repeated latent decoding/encoding (latent–RGB cycling) and a gap between clean training memory and corrupted inference memory (error-free hypothesis).

Method: Introduce Latent Gaussian Memory that anchors diffusion latents to Gaussian primitives and recalls them via latent-space Gaussian splatting for geometry-aware conditioning. Propose Deviation Learning with Dynamic Deviation Archive to synthesize rollout-induced latent deviations, store them by autoregressive stage and denoising timestamp, and inject them into historical memory during training to expose the generator to realistic corrupted memory.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art long-horizon performance on ScanNet, DL3DV, and OmniWorldGame datasets, indicating improved long-term fidelity and 3D consistency.

Conclusion: Robust Dreamer provides a memory design and robust usage strategy for 3D memory in action-conditioned video generation, mitigating drift and training–inference gaps to enable stable, interactive long-horizon generation.

Abstract: Frame-wise action-controlled image-to-video generation is a promising paradigm for interactive world simulation, where each control signal should elicit an immediate visual response. However, maintaining visual fidelity and 3D consistency over long autoregressive rollouts remains challenging. Existing 3D-aware methods often suffer from catastrophic drift due to two impediments: information loss from \textit{Latent--RGB Cycling}, where generated latents are repeatedly decoded to RGB and re-encoded for future conditioning, and the training--inference gap induced by the \textit{error-free hypothesis}, where clean training memory fails to match prediction-corrupted inference memory. To address these challenges, we present \textbf{Robust Dreamer}, a memory-augmented framework built around how to design 3D memory and how to use it robustly. First, we introduce \textbf{Latent Gaussian Memory}, which anchors diffusion latents inherited from the generation process to Gaussian primitives and recalls them via latent-space Gaussian splatting. This provides dense, geometry-aware, view-aligned conditioning while avoiding accumulated degradation from repeated VAE conversion. Second, we propose \textbf{Deviation Learning with Dynamic Deviation Archive}, which synthesizes rollout-induced latent deviations through a one-step approximation, stores them by autoregressive stage and denoising timestamp, and injects them into historical memory during training. This exposes the generator to realistic corrupted memory states and teaches internal correction before inference. Experiments on ScanNet, DL3DV, and OmniWorldGame demonstrate state-of-the-art long-horizon performance.

</details>


### [34] [DSD-GS: Dynamic-Static Decomposition of Gaussian Splatting for Efficient and High-Fidelity Dynamic Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30863)
*Youngtae Han,Sung-hwan Han,Youngmin Yi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dynamic reconstruction framework using static-dynamic decomposition with a Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting encoder and an optical flow model, achieving state-of-the-art quality, speed, and storage efficiency, with fast training and rendering and no COLMAP preprocessing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic scene reconstruction and novel view synthesis are hampered by high computational cost when treating all scene components as dynamic. While decomposition-based approaches reduce computation, they often trade off reconstruction quality or training time. There is a need for a framework that combines efficient decomposition with high fidelity and fast training/rendering, including practical preprocessing-free initialization.

Method: Proposes a static-dynamic decomposition strategy coupled with a Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting encoder and an optical flow model. Static regions receive no dynamic processing to avoid redundant computations. The approach eliminates COLMAP preprocessing and uses deterministic initialization to improve reproducibility. Training and rendering efficiency are demonstrated on a Neural 3D dataset, achieving high FPS on a high-end GPU.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance in rendering quality, training speed, rendering speed, and storage efficiency. Specifically, it reports 10 minutes of training and rendering speeds exceeding 700 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU at 1352x1014 resolution.

Conclusion: The proposed static-dynamic decomposition framework provides a highly efficient and reproducible solution for dynamic scene reconstruction, removing the need for COLMAP preprocessing, reducing redundant static-region computation, and delivering superior performance suitable for real-time or large-scale deployment in VR, robotics, and digital twins.

Abstract: Dynamic scene reconstruction and novel view synthesis are fundamental to next-generation visual intelligence applications such as virtual reality, robotics, and digital twins. However, high-fidelity reconstruction of complex, time-varying scenes from arbitrary viewpoints remains a significant challenge. Existing dynamic 3DGS methods suffer from computational inefficiency, since they model all Gaussians as dynamic components. While recent decomposition-based approaches address this issue, they still struggle with degraded reconstruction quality and prolonged training time. To mitigate these limitations, we propose a novel dynamic reconstruction framework built upon an efficient static-dynamic decomposition strategy using a Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting encoder and an optical flow model. By eliminating redundant computations on static regions, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing baselines across rendering quality, training and rendering speed, and storage efficiency. Notably, on the Neural 3D dataset, our framework requires only 10 minutes for training and achieves a rendering speed of over 700 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU at resolution of 1352x1014. Furthermore, our decomposition strategy eliminates the need for COLMAP preprocessing and enables deterministic initialization, thereby enhancing both efficiency and reproducibility.

</details>


### [35] [GUI-C$^2$: Coarse-to-Fine GUI Grounding via Difficulty-Aware Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30884)
*Junlong Li,Chao Hao,Lap-Pui Chau,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GUI-D and GUI-C^2 tackle data-level training value and strategy-level context trade-offs in GUI grounding; GUI-D introduces data mining and difficulty scoring for weighted training; GUI-C^2 uses area-gated coarse-to-fine refinement with uncertainty-based adaptation and improvement-aware rewards, plus simpler decision-making; experiments show state-of-the-art results and code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic RL methods for GUI grounding suffer from (1) treating all training samples equally despite varying difficulty, reducing training efficiency or causing collapse; (2) difficulty balancing between cropping large regions for context and small ones for precision; and (3) overly complex decision-making increasing inference time, especially for small models.

Method: GUI-D: a data mining and difficulty scoring pipeline that identifies training-worthy samples and assigns difficulty scores to guide training weights. GUI-C^2: an area-gated coarse-to-fine refinement mechanism that progressively narrows the visual field via model-internal uncertainty signals, adaptively reserving context for large targets while amplifying precision for small ones; improvement-aware stage rewards; and simplified decision-making to reduce inference time.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: The proposed data-level and strategy-level pipelines improve training efficiency and grounding accuracy while reducing inference overhead; code and data will be publicly available.

Abstract: Existing agentic reinforcement learning methods for GUI grounding have limitations at two levels. At the data level, current approaches typically treat all training samples equally, although their training value to the baseline model varies with difficulty. Overlooking this can greatly reduce training efficiency or even cause collapse. At the strategy level, existing frameworks struggle to balance the trade-off between cropping larger regions for sufficient context and smaller ones for reduced redundancy, a tension inherent to tool-augmented grounding agents. In addition, overly complex decision-making is difficult for small-parameter models and significantly increases inference time. To address these issues, at the data level, we propose GUI-D, a data mining and difficulty scoring pipeline that identifies the training-worthy samples by proper testing and assigns difficulty scores to guide subsequent training weights. At the strategy level, we propose GUI-C$^2$, which employs an area-gated coarse-to-fine refinement mechanism that progressively narrows the visual field via model-internal uncertainty signals, adaptively reserving context for large targets while amplifying precision for small ones, reinforced by improvement-aware stage rewards that ensure each refinement genuinely advances grounding. Meanwhile, we simplify the decision-making process to greatly reduce additional inference time. Finally, extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code and data will be publicly available.

</details>


### [36] [Foundation VAEs for 3D CT Reconstruction, Augmentation, and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30893)
*Qi Chen,Shuhan Ding,Yu Gu,Nan Liu,Jiang Bian,Alan Yuille,Zongwei Zhou,Jingjing Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A pretrained Foundation VAE on natural images serves as a universal interface for CT reconstruction, augmentation, and generation with frozen encoders/decoders, enabling training-free CT analysis and generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CT VAEs require costly training and often degrade across different scanners, protocols, and diseases. A training-free, foundation-model–based approach could provide a unified, scalable interface for CT reconstruction, augmentation, and generation.

Method: Freeze both encoder and decoder of a Foundation VAE pretrained on natural images. Use it to reconstruct CT volumes (suppressing noise while preserving anatomy). Train segmentation models on these reconstructions. Within the Foundation VAE latent space, pair a conditional latent diffusion model for CT generation. Evaluate improvements in segmentation quality (NSD), diffusion metrics (FVD), and generation fidelity (CT CLIP, AUC) across multiple diseases.

Result: Segmentation surface accuracy improved by 3.9% NSD on average for pancreatic and lung tumors. The conditional latent diffusion model achieves 3.9% lower average FVD and 36.2% higher CT CLIP score within the same latent space, and improves multi-disease generation faithfulness across 18 disease types by 2.76% AUC.

Conclusion: Foundation VAEs enable practical CT representation reuse and faithful CT generation with minimal or no CT-specific training, demonstrating a training-free medical VAE paradigm; code and demo are released.

Abstract: Variational autoencoders (VAEs) compress high resolution CT volumes into compact latents while preserving clinically relevant structure. However, training CT-specific VAEs from scratch or heavily fine-tuning them incurs substantial computational and engineering cost, and often degrades under heterogeneous scanners, protocols, and diseases. This paper makes a progressive stride toward training-free medical VAEs by leveraging a critical observation: a single Foundation VAE, pretrained at scale on natural images and videos, can serve as a unified interface for CT Reconstruction, Augmentation, and Generation. With both encoder and decoder frozen, the Foundation VAE reconstructs CT volumes with preserved anatomy while suppressing acquisition noise; training segmentation models on these reconstructions improves surface accuracy by 3.9% NSD on average for pancreatic tumor and lung tumor. Within the same Foundation VAE latent space, a conditional latent diffusion model achieves 3.9% lower average FVD with 36.2% higher CT CLIP score, and improves multi-disease generation faithfulness across 18 types by 2.76% AUC. These results demonstrate Foundation VAEs as a practical interface for scalable CT representation reuse and faithful CT generation. Our code and demo are available at https://github.com/qic999/Foundation-VAE.

</details>


### [37] [SteerFace: Debiasing Synthetic Face Generation via Adaptive Residue Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30894)
*Yuxi Mi,Qiuyang Yuan,Jianqing Xu,Yichun Zhou,Xuan Zhao,Jun Wang,Rizen Guo,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SteerFace steers identity embeddings on the embedding hypersphere via random orthogonal perturbations during training to reduce visual tendency in synthetic face data, with an adaptive perturbation-strength strategy; it improves downstream face recognition and generalizes across datasets and pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic face data often exhibits a visual tendency where unintended residual cues co-vary with identity due to conditioning on identity embeddings, causing a gap to real data. A regularizer that decouples identity from non-identity visual cues could improve recognition performance when using synthetic data for training.

Method: During training, perturb identity embeddings by steering them toward random orthogonal directions on the embedding hypersphere. This identity-preserving perturbation acts as a regularizer that discourages the generator from leveraging non-identity cues. An adaptive strategy learns perturbation strengths using sample-wise preferences and favorable overall statistics, providing a data-driven balance between identity preservation and regularization.

Result: Empirical results show that SteerFace mitigates visual tendency more effectively than prior methods, improves downstream face recognition accuracy, and generalizes well across different training datasets and generation pipelines.

Conclusion: SteerFace is a simple yet effective training framework for reducing visual tendency in diffusion-based synthetic face data, enhancing downstream recognition performance and robustness across datasets and pipelines, with potential applicability to other generative-encoder conditioning problems.

Abstract: The shortage of legally compliant data for face recognition training has sparked growing interest in using synthetic data as an alternative. While recent diffusion-based methods enable the generation of photorealistic face images with strong identity adherence and data diversity, their downstream recognition performance still exhibits a significant synthetic-real gap. This paper identifies visual tendency as a previously underexplored limitation, whereby synthetic data exhibit an unrealistic prevalence of visual attributes and thus deviate from the real-data distribution. Visual tendency can be attributed to the generator's conditioning on identity embeddings, through which co-occurring residual visual cues are unintentionally absorbed into learned identity semantics. To discourage the generator from exploiting such visual cues, this paper proposes SteerFace, a simple and efficient training framework that perturbs identity embeddings by steering them toward random orthogonal directions on the embedding hypersphere. The perturbation serves as an identity-preserving regularizer that penalizes the generator's reliance on non-identity components, as supported by theoretical analysis. This paper further introduces an adaptive strategy that learns perturbation strengths with both sample-wise preference and favorable overall statistics. Extensive experiments show that SteerFace effectively mitigates visual tendency, outperforms prior methods in downstream face recognition, and generalizes well across different training datasets and generation pipelines.

</details>


### [38] [MergeTok: Unified Continuous and Discrete Visual Tokenization via Token Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30904)
*Luyuan Zhang,Siyuan Li,Zedong Wang,Qingsong Xie,Cheng Tan,Anna Wang,Yanhao Zhang,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Haoqian Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unified tokenizer MergeTok jointly optimizes continuous VAE and discrete VQ tokens with token merging to create semantically organized, generator-friendly representations; achieves competitive reconstruction/generation with lower rFID under token budgets on ImageNet-256.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current visual tokenizers fall into two camps: continuous VAEs offer high-fidelity reconstructions but yield dense, entangled latents with weak semantic control; discrete VQ models enable autoregressive generation but suffer from gradient sparsity, unstable training, and codebook collapse. A single architecture that combines both advantages and provides semantic control is desired.

Method: Encoder-decoder architecture with both continuous (VAE) and discrete (VQ) tokenizers. During encoding, similar tokens are clustered/merged to form a structural prior. This yields dual supervision: (i) merged-token semantic alignment in the VAE branch, regularizing the latent space toward disentangled, semantic-aware representations; (ii) group-wise constraints that promote intra-group diversity and inter-group exclusivity to stabilize VQ training. The scheme yields token representations compatible with autoregressive and diffusion generators.

Result: On ImageNet-256, MergeTok achieves competitive reconstruction and generation with substantially lower rFID than strong VAE and VQ baselines when token budgets are matched, and produces semantically organized token representations suitable for both autoregressive and diffusion-based generation.

Conclusion: MergeTok demonstrates that a single architecture can endow visual tokenizers with robust semantic organization and generator-friendly discreteness. Token merging serves as a semantic bridge, enabling dual supervision that improves latent disentanglement and stabilizes discrete token training.

Abstract: Most visual tokenizers for image generation are bifurcated into two families with complementary limitations: continuous VAEs offer high-fidelity reconstruction but suffer from dense, entangled latents that are poorly suited for semantic control, whereas discrete VQ-based models enable autoregressive generation yet struggle with gradient sparsity, unstable training, and codebook collapse. In this work, we introduce MergeTok, a unified tokenizer that jointly optimizes continuous (VAE) and discrete (VQ) tokenizers within a encoder-decoder architecture, leveraging token merging techniques as a semantic bridge. By clustering similar tokens during encoding, MergeTok establishes a structural prior that provides dual supervision signals: (i) it imposes merged-token semantic alignment in the VAE branch, regularizing its latent space toward disentangled, semantic-aware representations; (ii) it derives group-wise constraints, promoting intra-group diversity and inter-group exclusivity that stabilize VQ training. MergeTok shows competitive reconstruction and generation performance on ImageNet-256, with substantially lower rFID than strong VAE and VQ models under matched token budgets, while producing semantically-organized token representations compatible with both autoregressive and diffusion generators. This shows that a single architecture can endow visual tokenizers with robust semantic organization and generator-friendly discreteness.

</details>


### [39] [What Makes LVLMs Hallucinate Less? Unveiling the Architectural Factors Behind Hallucination Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30911)
*Yusheng He,Jizhe Zhou,Xia Du,Zheng Lin,Jun Luo,Jiancheng Lv*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An architecture-centric analysis of LVLM hallucinations introducing a three-dimensional design taxonomy (Linguistic Foundation, Visual Representation, Semantic Alignment) and a perturbation-driven benchmark CoSimUE to map design choices to three hallucination types (Co-occurrence, Similarity, Uncertainty); finds model scale alone is limited, with improvements achievable by stronger LF, VR, SA, especially when combined.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination in LVLMs remains a major reliability challenge. Prior work focuses on internal model components; there is a need to understand how architecture design choices drive hallucination behavior.

Method: Define architecture design along three dimensions (Linguistic Foundation, Visual Representation, Semantic Alignment); categorize hallucinations into Co-occurrence, Similarity, and Uncertainty. Develop CoSimUE, a benchmark that creates fine-grained hallucination scenarios via controlled textual perturbations and random perturbations. Evaluate across seven design aspects to map design choices to hallucination types.

Result: Key findings: (1) Scaling/model size has limited impact on reducing all three hallucination types. (2) Larger and better-trained language foundations reduce co-occurrence hallucinations. (3) Stronger visual encoders and higher input resolutions mitigate similarity errors. (4) Effective alignment strategies alleviate uncertainty hallucinations. (5) Cross-dimensional analysis shows jointly improving visual fidelity and alignment quality yields the most comprehensive improvements.

Conclusion: First systematic exploration linking architecture-level design to hallucination robustness in LVLMs, offering practical guidance for developing reliable and efficient LVLMs.

Abstract: Hallucination remains one of the key challenges undermining the reliability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). But what makes an LVLM hallucinate less? Many existing efforts focus on improving internal components of the model. We argue that hallucination fundamentally stems from how the model architecture is designed. To investigate this, we factor the architecture design into three dimensions: Linguistic Foundation (LF), Visual Representation (VR), and Semantic Alignment (SA), and categorize hallucinations into Co-occurrence, Similarity, and previously overlooked Uncertainty types. Building on this formulation, we propose CoSimUE, a benchmark that creates fine-grained hallucination scenarios through controlled textual perturbations and random perturbations, enabling mapping between design choices and hallucination behaviors. Experiments across 7 design aspects show that: 1) the widely emphasized scaling of model parameters has only limited impact on reducing all three types of hallucinations; 2) larger and better-trained language foundations can reduce co-occurrence hallucinations; 3) stronger visual encoders and higher resolutions mitigate similarity errors; 4) effective alignment strategies alleviate uncertainty hallucinations. 5) Furthermore, cross-dimensional analysis reveals that jointly enhancing visual fidelity and alignment quality yields the most comprehensive improvements. This study provides the first systematic exploration linking architecture-level design to hallucination robustness, offering practical guidance for developing reliable and efficient LVLMs.

</details>


### [40] [Attend to Evidence: Evidence-Anchored Spatial Attention Supervision for Multimodal RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30912)
*Ruina Hu,Chen Wang,Lai Wei,Jionghao Bai,Bin Yu,Weiran Huang,Kai Wang,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EASE adds evidence-anchored supervision to RL-based VLM training by turning annotated evidence into smoothed visual-token targets to guide attention on high-reward trajectories, improving visual grounding and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-only rewards in RLVR fail to indicate which image regions justify answers, leading to reliance on language priors or guesses.

Method: Use annotated evidence regions converted to smoothed visual-token targets as privileged labels to supervise response-to-image attention during RL training, applied only on high-reward trajectories; training-time evidence labels; inference uses only image and question.

Result: On Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-4B, Qwen3-VL-8B, EASE improves average DAPO scores by 2.5–3.1 points across perception, hallucination, visual math, and multimodal reasoning; diagnostics show stronger alignment of attention with evidence.

Conclusion: EASE enhances visual grounding in multimodal RL by grounding attention to annotated evidence, improving reasoning and reducing reliance on shortcuts.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves vision-language models (VLMs) by optimizing outcome rewards derived from final answers. However, such outcome-only rewards do not tell the model which image regions justify an answer. For questions that require visual grounding, these rewards cannot distinguish responses supported by relevant visual evidence from those produced by language-prior shortcuts or lucky guesses. We introduce EASE (Evidence-Anchored Spatial Attention), which augments multimodal RLVR with visual-evidence process supervision. EASE converts annotated evidence regions into a smoothed visual-token target and uses it to guide response-to-image attention during RL training, but only on high-reward trajectories. The annotations are used solely as privileged training labels, while inference requires only the original image and question. Across Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-4B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, EASE raises average scores over DAPO by 2.5 to 3.1 points on perception, hallucination, visual math, and multimodal reasoning benchmarks. Diagnostics and ablations show that EASE better aligns visual attention with annotated evidence regions.

</details>


### [41] [DiTTo: Scalable Order-aware All-in-One Image Restoration Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30915)
*Seungho Choi,Jihyong Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiTTo introduces a plug-and-play, order-aware image restoration agent that uses a dedicated simulator (DiTTo Simulator) and an alignment stage (ORA) to dramatically improve efficiency and extensibility in multi-degradation restoration, reducing dataset construction from quadratic to linear in the number of degradations and achieving state-of-the-art results among agent-based IR methods on MiO-100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world images typically suffer multiple, ordered degradations; existing agent-based IR pipelines incur O((N^D)^2) calls to restoration experts and are hard to extend with new experts, hindering performance and scalability.

Method: DiTTo combines a Simulator with S-IR for single-step action simulation and AiO-IQA for per-action quality prediction to construct an ORTD with O(N^D) simulator calls. The DiTTo Agent is trained with supervised fine-tuning on simulator-generated ORTD, followed by Order-aware Restoration Alignment (ORA) to align degradation identification, action ordering, and output formatting along separate axes, enabling plug-and-play extensibility by updating only the ORA stage when new restoration experts are added.

Result: On MiO-100 with up to five concurrent degradations, the DiTTo Agent achieves state-of-the-art restoration quality among agent-based IR methods for multi-degradation scenarios.

Conclusion: DiTTo delivers an efficient, extensible framework for order-aware multi-degradation restoration, reducing ORTD construction complexity and accommodating new restoration experts with minimal retraining.

Abstract: Real-world images rarely suffer from a single degradation, and the order in which degradations are removed substantially affects the final restoration quality, motivating agent-based image restoration (IR), where a vision-language model schedules a pool of pre-built restoration-experts. However, existing training-based agents require $\mathcal{O}((N^{\mathbf{D}})^{2})$ restoration-expert calls per image to construct the Optimal Restoration-action Trajectory Dataset (ORTD), where $N^{\mathbf{D}}$ denotes the number of degradation types in the universe $\mathbf{D}$, and couple agent training to a fixed restoration-expert pool, preventing extension to newly introduced restoration-experts without full retraining. To overcome these efficiency and extensibility bottlenecks, we propose \textbf{DiTTo}, a novel order-aware image restoration agent framework consisting of the DiTTo Simulator and the DiTTo Agent. The DiTTo Simulator combines $\cup$S-IR for single-step restoration-action simulation and AiO-IQA for per-action quality prediction, reducing ORTD construction to $\mathcal{O}(N^{\mathbf{D}})$ simulator calls per image; the DiTTo Agent is trained by SFT on the simulator-generated ORTD, followed by \textbf{Order-aware Restoration Alignment (ORA)} that aligns degradation identification, restoration-action-ordering, and output format along independent axes. This enables \textbf{plug-and-play scalable extensibility}: adding a new restoration-expert requires updating only the lightweight ORA stage. On the MiO-100 evaluation set with up to five concurrent degradations, our DiTTo Agent achieves state-of-the-art multi-degradation restoration quality among previous agent-based IR methods.

</details>


### [42] [MultiAct: Text-to-Motion Generation from Composite Text via Tailored Attention Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30925)
*Nathan Sala,Ofir Abramovich,Ariel Shamir,Daniel Cohen-Or,Andreas Aristidou,Sigal Raab*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MultiAct presents an inference-time, unpaired method to achieve compositional text-to-motion by adaptively boosting cross-attention for underrepresented prompt components, without retraining, improving semantic coverage while preserving realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-motion models often collapse a composite prompt into a single dominant action, causing incomplete or ambiguous motions. A lightweight, training-free solution is needed to faithfully realize multiple actions in parallel.

Method: At inference time, MultiAct adaptively amplifies cross-attention scores linked to underrepresented prompt components in pretrained motion generators. It includes a lightweight auxiliary decision scheme to select which tokens and which layers to target, optimizing the attention-strengthening parametrization without architectural changes.

Result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show that MultiAct consistently outperforms baselines on composite prompts, achieving better semantic coverage without sacrificing motion realism.

Conclusion: An effective, non-invasive, inference-time technique for compositional text-to-motion synthesis that mitigates semantic collapse and leverages existing pretrained generators.

Abstract: Text-to-motion generation has progressed rapidly in recent years, offering an expressive interface for animation and human-computer interaction. However, current models remain brittle when handling prompts that describe multiple actions occurring at the same time. Rather than realizing all components of a composite description, models frequently prioritize a single dominant action and neglect the rest, leading to incomplete or ambiguous motion. We present MultiAct, an unpaired, inference-time framework for compositional text-to-motion synthesis that operates directly on pretrained motion generators without retraining or architectural modification. Our method counteracts semantic collapse by adaptively amplifying cross-attention scores associated with underrepresented prompt components. We note that effective modulation depends on prompt-specific choices, such as which tokens and layers to target, and introduce a lightweight auxiliary decision scheme that determines the most effective attention-strengthening parametrization. Extensive quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that MultiAct consistently outperforms existing baselines on composite prompts, achieving improved semantic coverage while preserving motion realism. Project page: https://natsala13.github.io/multiact.github.io.

</details>


### [43] [IAF-Net: Illumination-Adaptive Fusion for Low-Light Urban Road Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30939)
*Bingtao Wang,Daojie Peng,Fulong Ma,Jun Ma,Liang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IAF-Net provides illumination-aware adaptive fusion for robust semantic road segmentation across lighting conditions, using an Illumination-Adaptive Fusion (IAF) module to dynamically weight RGB and geometric features and a brightness-modulated attention decoder. It introduces two datasets (nuScenes-NRS, CARLA-MWRS) and achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes-NRS and robustness under adverse weather on CARLA-MWRS. Ablation on 40% training data shows IAF yields the largest individual gain (0.70% MaxF).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-modal fusion methods often employ static fusion strategies and assume fixed modality reliability, causing RGB degradation at night to propagate into the fused representation. There is a need for illumination-aware fusion to maintain cross-modal reliability under low-light conditions.

Method: An end-to-end framework featuring an Illumination-Adaptive Fusion (IAF) module that dynamically adjusts the fusion weights of RGB and geometric features according to illumination, and a brightness-modulated attention decoder to enhance low-light feature selection. Also introduces two datasets: nuScenes-NRS and CARLA-MWRS. Extensive experiments and ablations, including a 40% training subset.

Result: On nuScenes-NRS, achieves state-of-the-art overall performance among compared methods. CARLA-MWRS demonstrates robustness across adverse weather. Ablation on 40% training subset shows IAF module yields the largest individual gain of 0.70% in MaxF.

Conclusion: Illumination-adaptive fusion improves the robustness of semantic road segmentation across varying lighting. The proposed datasets enable comprehensive evaluation under night and adverse weather. The IAF module is effective, with potential for future work on efficiency, generalization, and integration with additional modalities.

Abstract: Semantic road segmentation is important for autonomous driving, but existing methods suffer severe performance degradation under low-light conditions. Many existing multi-modal fusion methods do not explicitly adapt to illumination-dependent changes in modality reliability, which can propagate degraded RGB features into the fused representation at night. We propose IAF-Net (Illumination-Adaptive Fusion Network), an end-to-end framework with illumination-adaptive fusion for robust road segmentation across different lighting conditions. It dynamically adjusts fusion weights of RGB and geometric features via the core Illumination-Adaptive Fusion (IAF) module, and enhances low-light feature selection with a brightness-modulated attention decoder. We also construct two dedicated datasets: nuScenes Nighttime Road Segmentation (nuScenes-NRS) and CARLA Multi-Weather Road Segmentation (CARLA-MWRS). Experiments on nuScenes-NRS show state-of-the-art overall performance among the compared methods, while CARLA-MWRS further validates robustness across adverse weather conditions. Ablation studies on a 40% training subset further highlight the importance of the IAF module, which provides the largest individual gain of 0.70% in MaxF.

</details>


### [44] [Variational Adapter for Cross-modal Similarity Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30968)
*WenZhang Wei,Zhipeng Gui,Dehua Peng,Tiandi Ye,Huayi Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VACSR reframes image-text matching as variational inference over a latent cross-modal similarity space to address coarse binary annotations and improve generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Binary annotation boundaries in cross-modal similarity compress a continuous space into binaries, causing false negatives and hurting generalization; prior intra-modal ambiguity modeling overlooks annotation flaws.

Method: Variational Adapter for Cross-modal Similarity Representation (VACSR) constructs a latent cross-modal similarity space and uses regularization within a variational inference framework to mitigate overfitting to binary annotations.

Result: Empirical validation on image-text retrieval, domain generalization, and base-to-novel generalization demonstrating improved performance and robust generalization.

Conclusion: A principled probabilistic framework that accounts for annotation scarcity to improve cross-modal representations and generalization.

Abstract: The core of vision-language models lies in measuring cross-modal similarity within a unified representation space. However, most image-text matching or multi-class image classification datasets lack fine-grained cross-modal matching annotations, forcing the continuous similarity space into binary classification boundaries. This compression induces false negative samples and significantly impairs the generalization performance of cross-modal tasks. While prior research has attempted to mitigate this by modeling intra-modal ambiguity, it often overlooks inherent annotation flaws, leading to suboptimal uncertainty allocation. To address these challenges, we propose a Variational Adapter for Cross-modal Similarity Representation (VACSR). This approach reformulates image-text matching with fine-grained semantic scarcity as a variational inference problem. It constructs a latent space for cross-modal similarity and uses regularization techniques to mitigate overfitting to binary annotations. Experiments on image-text retrieval, domain generalization, and base-to-novel generalization demonstrate the proposed method's effectiveness and robust generalization ability.

</details>


### [45] [Omni-Supervised Motion Editing: Balancing Change and Invariance through Positive-Negative Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30969)
*Zhenwu Shi,Jingyu Gong,Peiwei Wang,Xingzan Wang,Tianwen Qian,Wenxi Li,Yuan Fang,Jiao Xie,Lizhuang Ma,Shaohui Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniME introduces omni-supervised learning for text-driven motion editing, achieving state-of-the-art alignment on MotionFix and STANCE datasets by integrating retrospective feature supervision, motion preservation, and triplet-based semantic alignment; code released at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based text-to-motion editors often rely on coarse global conditioning or heuristic similarity cues, causing motion distortion and weak semantic alignment. The key problem is balancing targeted change with preserving unedited motion parts (change vs. invariance).

Method: OmniME comprises three components: (1) retrospective feature supervision that enforces coarse-to-fine consistency across transformer layers; (2) motion preservation mechanism that focuses on subtle variations based on source-target similarity; (3) triplet-based semantic alignment that strengthens text-motion correspondence. These components form a unified omni-supervised learning paradigm to balance change and invariance.

Result: Extensive experiments on MotionFix and STANCE Adjustment datasets show that OmniME achieves state-of-the-art performance in editing alignment, validating the effectiveness of the unified learning framework.

Conclusion: The proposed OmniME framework effectively balances change and invariance in text-based motion editing, delivering superior editing alignment and demonstrating robust generalization; source code and models are released.

Abstract: Text-based human motion editing aims to modify existing motion sequences according to natural language instructions while maintaining the consistency of the original motion. Existing diffusion-based approaches often rely on heuristic similarity cues or coarse global conditioning, leading to motion distortion and suboptimal semantic alignment. The key challenge lies in balancing change (i.e. precisely editing target regions) and invariance (i.e. preserving unedited parts). To handle such challenge, we propose an Omni-Supervised Positive-Negative Learning framework, named OmniME. Our method integrates three complementary components: (1) retrospective feature supervision that enforces coarse-to-fine consistency across transformer layers,(2) motion preservation mechanism that focuses on subtle variations according to the source-target similarity, and (3) triplet-based semantic alignment that strengthens text-motion correspondence. Together, these components form a unified supervision paradigm that balances change and invariance. Extensive experiments on the MotionFix and STANCE Adjustment datasets demonstrate that OmniME achieves state-of-the-art performance in editing alignment, validating the effectiveness of our unified learning framework. Our source codes and models have been released at: https://github.com/rocket-ycyer/OmniME.git

</details>


### [46] [Can BEV Perception Gracefully Degrade under Sensor Failures?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30983)
*Haifa Zhang,Yijing Wang,Haoyu Wang,Zheng Li,Zhiqiang Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Grace-BEV introduces active reliability-aware multi-modal BEV fusion to improve robustness against sensor corruptions. It uses a TrustGate Router and FailSafe Fusion Block, plus Three-Phase Training with Modality Dropout. Demonstrates strong robustness on nuScenes-R/C (e.g., LiDAR failure raises mAP to 34.7% from 0.0%) with up to 1.4% clean accuracy gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current static fusion in multi-modal BEV systems makes them brittle under missing or corrupted modalities. There is a need for graceful degradation by explicitly assessing modality trustworthiness and dynamically adjusting fusion.

Method: Propose Grace-BEV, a lightweight plug-and-play framework that operates in the BEV space. It uses a TrustGate Router to assess modality reliability and a FailSafe Fusion Block to recalibrate feature integration. Training uses a Three-Phase Strategy with Modality Dropout to prevent modality dominance.

Result: Extensive experiments on nuScenes-R and nuScenes-C show robustness across diverse corruptions. Under catastrophic LiDAR failure, standard baselines collapse to 0.0% mAP, while Grace-BEV reaches up to 34.7% mAP and improves clean accuracy by up to 1.4%.

Conclusion: Grace-BEV delivers graceful degradation and robust performance with an efficient, plug-and-play design for multi-modal BEV fusion, enabling a favorable robustness–efficiency trade-off.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable success of multi-modal bird's-eye view (BEV) perception in autonomous driving, current systems exhibit a critical vulnerability: existing fusion mechanisms are highly brittle to sensor corruptions, often causing catastrophic performance degradation. This vulnerability largely stems from the fact that standard fusion frameworks typically integrate multi-modal representations in a static manner, leading to a precipitous performance collapse under missing or corrupted modalities. In contrast, we show that graceful degradation is achievable through active modality reliability assessment. To this end, we present Grace-BEV, a lightweight and plug-and-play framework that enforces active reliability awareness during multi-modal fusion. Instead of relying on computationally expensive cross-modal interactions, Grace-BEV leverages the aligned BEV space to explicitly assess modality trustworthiness via a TrustGate Router and dynamically recalibrate feature integration using the FailSafe Fusion Block. Furthermore, we devise a Three-Phase Training strategy with Modality Dropout to prevent modality dominance and encourage balanced cross-modal learning under unreliable inputs. Extensive experiments on nuScenes-R and nuScenes-C show that Grace-BEV maintains robust performance across diverse corruption settings. Notably, under catastrophic LiDAR failures where standard baselines collapse to 0.0% mean Average Precision (mAP), Grace-BEV restores performance to as high as 34.7% mAP. Moreover, it improves clean accuracy by up to 1.4%, achieving a strong trade-off between robustness and efficiency.

</details>


### [47] [Generating Reports or Repeating Templates? Measuring and Mitigating Template Collapse in 3D CT Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30984)
*Tom Maye-Lasserre,Yitong Li,Bailiang Jian,Morteza Ghahremani,Benedikt Wiestler,Christian Wachinger*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CLarGen to combat Template Collapse in 3D CT report generation by explicitly grounding reports through a decoupled detection-and-synthesis pipeline, improving clinical accuracy while preserving fluency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D medical VLMs struggle to ground reports due to limited data, severe label imbalance, and weak signals from volumetric encoders, leading to fluent but non-specific or rare-finding-omitting outputs (Template Collapse).

Method: (CLARGen) Decoupled pipeline: (i) Latent Query Transformer for multi-label pathology detection; (ii) pathology-guided retrieval of clinically matched exemplars; (iii) a medical language model to synthesize the final report from detected findings and retrieved context.

Result: Across state-of-the-art 3D CT report baselines, CLarGen mitigates Template Collapse and substantially improves clinical accuracy (macro-F1 0.487 vs. 0.189; CRG 0.472 vs. 0.368) while maintaining fluent reporting.

Conclusion: Explicit, measurable clinical grounding is essential to resist template-collapse in 3D CT report generation; a decoupled detection-and-synthesis framework with retrieval can substantially improve clinical accuracy without sacrificing fluency.

Abstract: Modern 3D medical vision-language models (VLMs) can generate fluent radiology-style text while exhibit critically low pathology detection and output diversity, collapsing to generic templates that under-report rare yet critical findings. We identify this failure mode as Template Collapse. This failure stems from the unique constraints of 3D medical imaging, e.g., limited data, severe label imbalance, and weak signals from volumetric encoders. Under these constraints, text-generation objectives encourage shortcut learning and fluent but weakly grounded reports. We systematically diagnose the Template Collapse through clinical fidelity, output diversity, normal-template bias, and rare-finding survival. To mitigate it, we propose CLarGen, a decoupled framework that separates what to say (clinical detection) from how to say it (language synthesis). CLarGen uses (i) a Latent Query Transformer for multi-label pathology detection, (ii) pathology-guided retrieval for clinically matched exemplars, and (iii) a medical language model to synthesize the final report from detected findings and retrieved context. Across state-of-the-art 3D CT report generation baselines, CLarGen mitigates Template Collapse and substantially improves clinical accuracy (macro-F1 0.487 vs. 0.189; CRG 0.472 vs. 0.368) while maintaining fluent reporting. Our results suggest that explicit, measurable clinical grounding is essential for template-collapse-resistant 3D CT report generation. Code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [48] [NTR: Neural Token Reconstruction for Scene Token Bottleneck in End-to-End Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31116)
*Jiahui Li,Jiawei Sun,Zixiang Ren,Ming Liu,Jiamin Shi,Ruiteng Zhao,Zhiyang Liu,Liying Liu,Zuoguan Wang,Kaidi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NTR introduces a self-distillation masked latent reconstruction objective to constrain the scene-token bottleneck in perception-free E2E driving, using semantic priors to bias reconstruction targets; achieves state-of-the-art on Waymo E2E and NavSim benchmarks; improves token efficiency (lower redundancy, higher rank) with no inference-time overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Perception-free E2E driving compresses dense image patches into a compact scene-token bottleneck, but supervision comes only from planning objectives, giving limited constraints on the encoded visual information. A representation-learning objective is needed to enrich the bottleneck with task-relevant visual structure.

Method: Neural Token Reconstruction (NTR) uses a self-distillation masked latent reconstruction objective that reconstructs masked patch-level latent features from the compact scene tokens, forcing gradients to pass through the scene-token bottleneck. Semantic priors from foundation-model annotations provide weak semantic targets for driving-related structures without explicit perception heads. All auxiliary reconstruction components are discarded at inference, leaving the planner unchanged.

Result: State-of-the-art on three public autonomous driving benchmarks: 8.0461 in RFS on Waymo E2E and 94.1 PDMS / 90.9 EPDMS on NavSim1&2. The learned scene tokens show lower pairwise redundancy and higher effective rank, indicating improved bottleneck efficiency for planning.

Conclusion: Bottleneck supervision via NTR yields richer, less redundant scene-token representations, enhancing planning performance in perception-free driving. The approach combines representation learning with planning while keeping deployment unchanged; semantic priors provide a soft semantic interface without additional perception heads.

Abstract: Recent perception-free end-to-end (E2E) autonomous driving methods bypass explicit perception outputs by compressing dense image patch tokens into compact scene tokens for downstream trajectory generation and scoring. While these scene tokens form a compact visual bottleneck for the planner, they receive supervision solely from the planning objective, providing limited constraints on the encoded visual information. To address this limitation, we introduce Neural Token Reconstruction (NTR), a representation learning framework to directly constrain the compact scene-token bottleneck in perception-free driving. NTR introduces a self-distillation masked latent reconstruction objective that reconstructs masked patch-level latent features using only compact scene tokens as reconstruction memory. This forces reconstruction gradients to pass exclusively through the scene-token bottleneck, encouraging scene tokens to preserve richer and less redundant visual representations for planning. We further introduce semantic priors derived from foundation-model annotations as a weak semantic interface biasing reconstruction targets toward driving-related structures without introducing explicit perception heads. All auxiliary reconstruction components are removed at inference time, leaving the deployed planner unchanged. NTR achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public autonomous driving benchmarks, including 8.0461 RFS on Waymo E2E and 94.1 PDMS / 90.9 EPDMS on NavSim1&2. The learned scene tokens exhibit lower pairwise redundancy and higher effective rank, indicating that effective bottleneck supervision improves both compact visual representation learning and planning performance.

</details>


### [49] [Benchmarking Single-Step Inpainting Methods for Multi-Object 3D Gaussian Splatting Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30987)
*Finn Dröge,Cecilia Curreli,Abhishek Saroha,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 2D reconstruction-based inpainting outperforms diffusion models for 3D consistency in object removal/inpainting of 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes. Initializing a 3DGS scene from scratch beats fine-tuning an existing one;  a strong baseline underscores the need for pre-removal of objects before inpainting. A new multi-object, ground-truth dataset with many occluded views is introduced to address lack of real-world 360° benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust, 3D-consistent object removal and inpainting in 3DGS scenes. 360° datasets lack ground-truth and challenging occlusion scenarios, hindering fair evaluation of 3D inpainting methods. Identifying which 2D inpaintings translate best to 3D informs method design and data requirements.

Method: Systematic comparison of 2D inpainting approaches (reconstruction-based vs diffusion models) within single-step 3DGS scene creation/finetuning pipelines. Evaluation of two initialization strategies (from scratch vs finetuning) for 3DGS scenes. Baseline established using a state-of-the-art 2D inpaintor to emphasize the sequence of object removal then inpainting. Introduction of a novel multi-object scene with ground-truth data and extensive multi-view occlusion coverage for 360° benchmarking.

Result: Reconstruction-based 2D inpainters yield superior 3D consistency over diffusion models. Initializing 3DGS scenes from scratch produces higher-quality results than fine-tuning existing ones within the presented pipelines. Object removal prior to inpainting is validated as a critical step when transferring 2D inpainting to 3D. The proposed multi-object, ground-truth dataset with numerous occluded views provides a valuable benchmark for future 3D inpainting work.

Conclusion: For 3DGS scene editing, 2D reconstruction-based inpainting is more effective than diffusion-based approaches in maintaining 3D consistency. Starting from scratch is generally preferable to finetuning for higher quality. The order of operations—removing objects before inpainting—is crucial in 3D. The introduced dataset fills a benchmark gap, enabling rigorous evaluation of occlusion-rich, ground-truth scenarios in 360° contexts.

Abstract: The tasks of object removal and inpainting 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes face challenges such as 3D consistency across camera views. In comparing 2D inpainters and their suitability for the 3D domain, we find that reconstruction-based inpainters outperform generative diffusion models in 3D consistency. Integrating these 2D inpainters into different single-step methods for creating and finetuning 3DGS scenes, our results indicate that initializing the scene from scratch produces higher quality results than finetuning the existing scene. Using a state-of-the-art generative 2D inpainter, we create a straightforward baseline to underline the importance of object removal before inpainting in the 3D setting. Since 360° datasets rarely include real-world ground truths, and challenging occlusion scenarios are equally sparse, we introduce a novel multi-object scene with recorded ground truth data and many views with object occlusions.

</details>


### [50] [Probing Collision Grounding in Vision-Language Models for Safe Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31196)
*Jun Wang,Xiaohao Xu,Xiaonan Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TouchSafeBench introduces a physics-grounded benchmark for collision grounding in vision–language models (VLMs). It reveals that current VLMs struggle with safety-state classification and imminent-collision warning: Macro-F1 scores stay below 50%, explicit depth is not effectively leveraged as collision evidence, and robot–scene contact is harder than human-contact risk. The benchmark, built in Habitat 3.0, comprises 2,940 simulated indoor co-presence episodes with synchronized RGB-D, top-down maps, camera metadata, and simulator-derived contact labels. The study argues that visual fluency does not imply physical accountability and highlights the need for representations that explicitly bind viewpoint, robot morphology, metric geometry, and future collision. A public release is planned upon acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe human–robot collaboration requires grounding visual observations to robot geometry, camera viewpoint, scene layout, human proximity, and temporal motion to infer current and imminent contact. Existing vision–language models lack this causal, physically grounded understanding, risking unsafe deployments.

Method: Construct TouchSafeBench in Habitat 3.0 with 2,940 simulated episodes covering social navigation and social rearrangement. Provide synchronized multi-view RGB-D data, top-down trajectory maps, calibrated camera metadata, and simulator-derived contact labels. Evaluate two deployment-facing tasks: (i) classify the current safety state; (ii) warn about imminent collision before contact. Compare three frontier/robotics-oriented VLMs across nine visual representations.
