However, state-of-the-art architectures for point cloud semantic segmentation remain dominated by U-Nets architectures where convolutions are interleaved with local or windowed attentions. In this work, we show how to effectively leverage vanilla, non-hierarchical ViTs for segmentation of large-scale automotive lidar scenes. We bridge the performance gap thanks to a carefully designed tokenizer, a lightweight decoder segmentation head, and tailored data augmentations. Our approach, VaViT for Vanilla ViT, matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art methods while maintaining the simplicity of ViT architecture. We provide extensive evaluations on nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, and Waymo Open Dataset to validate the efficiency of our method. Code and models are available at https://github.com/valeoai/VaViT.

</details>


### [77] [From Local Geometry to Global Pseudo Labeling for Robust Positive Unlabeled Learning under Covariate Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31187)
*Firas Gabetni,Alexandre Rocchi Henry,Nacim Belkhir,Ziyi Liu,Gianni Franchi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Spectral PU Neighborhood Annotation (SPUNA) presents a geometry-aware PU learning framework to detect covariate shift, achieving SOTA in PU settings and closing the gap with fully supervised methods, with strong cross-shift generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting covariate shift is essential for reliable vision systems but is often impractical due to the need for labeled data from both original and shifted distributions; PU learning offers weaker supervision but struggles under covariate overlap. A robust method is needed.

Method: Introduce SPUNA, a Spectral PU Neighborhood Annotation framework that leverages local manifold structure of visual features to progressively discover shifted data under PU supervision, making use of geometry-aware, iterative annotation.

Result: Empirical results show SPUNA achieves state-of-the-art performance among PU-based methods and matches fully supervised baselines; demonstrates strong transferability across different shift types.

Conclusion: SPUNA offers an effective, robust solution for covariate shift detection under weak supervision, with strong generalization across shifts, narrowing the gap to fully supervised methods.

Abstract: Detecting covariate shift is critical for building reliable vision systems. While most prior work focuses on improving robustness to shift, explicitly detecting covariate shift remains underexplored. Existing approaches typically rely on fully supervised training, requiring labeled examples from both original and shifted distributions, which is often impractical. In this paper, we show that covariate shift detection can be effectively addressed with weaker supervision using Positive Unlabeled (PU) learning. However, under covariate shift, in distribution and shifted data overlap significantly, making classical PU methods unstable and sensitive to noise. To overcome this challenge, we introduce Spectral PU Neighborhood Annotation (SPUNA), a geometry aware framework that progressively discovers shifted data by leveraging the local manifold structure of visual features. Extensive experiments show that SPUNA achieves state of the art performance in PU settings and remarkably matches the performances of fully supervised methods. Moreover, our approach transfers robustly across different types of shifts, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [78] [The Regularizing Power of Language-Training Deepfake Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31192)
*Benedikt Hopf,Zongwei Wu,Radu Timofte*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-encoder deepfake detector combining a frozen specialist detector with a LoRA-tuned MLLM encoder, trained in two stages (alignment and reinforcement learning) to produce descriptive reasoning and improve generalization and interpretability, achieving strong cross-dataset performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deepfake detectors often overfit to low-level, dataset-specific artifacts, hindering generalization. Leveraging language-trained models and describable artifacts can regularize learning and enhance interpretability.

Method: A dual-encoder architecture with a frozen specialist detector and a LoRA-tuned MLLM encoder. Two-stage training: (1) binary alignment to fuse features and reduce overfitting, (2) reinforcement learning that rewards explainable reasoning (explain-then-classify) using only binary labels, encouraging high-level feature use while enabling low-level cues when necessary.

Result: The approach yields interpretable artifact descriptions and improves cross-dataset performance, with state-of-the-art or near-state-of-the-art results on benchmark datasets; performance gains persist even if reasoning chains are omitted at inference.

Conclusion: Language-based regularization via explain-then-classify with LLMs can boost both interpretability and generalization in deepfake detection, by promoting high-level, describable features without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Recently, thanks to the advent of Multimodal-LLMs, deepfake detectors are striving not only to be generalizable but also interpretable. We propose that these two challenges can effectively be tackled jointly, since describable artifacts typically generalize better, opening the possibility to use language as a regularization mechanism. Since deepfake detection generally suffers from overfitting to low-level domain-specific artifacts, our intuition is that an LLM that has been pretrained on language would prefer high-level artifacts that can be described better. This way, we can use high-level features where possible, while training the model to use low-level features where necessary. We utilize a dual-encoder architecture, pairing a frozen specialist detector with a LoRA-tuned MLLM encoder, and a two-stage training curriculum: first, a binary alignment phase demonstrates that the intrinsic capability of MLLMs can effectively combine features to mitigate overfitting to dataset-specific artifacts. To further bolster generalization and achieve interpretability, we employ a reinforcement learning stage that encourages the model to generate descriptive reasoning before classifying, using only binary labels. By rewarding this "explain-then-classify" behavior, we explicitly incentivize the model to prioritize high-level, robust features. Crucially, this process yields both interpretable descriptions and a further boost in cross-dataset performance, even when reasoning chains are omitted at inference. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets validate our approach, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by a large margin.

</details>


### [79] [Probabilistic Precipitation Nowcasting with Rectified Flow Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31204)
*Johannes Schusterbauer,Jannik Wiese,Nick Stracke,Timy Phan,Björn Ommer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FREUD introduces a frame-wise encoder and unified decoder built on rectified flow transformers for efficient, uncertainty-preserving compression of spatio-temporal weather data, enabling high-resolution nowcasting with temporal consistency and improved performance via ensembling and test-time scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing weather nowcasting methods rely on deterministic compression, which undercuts the ability to model uncertainty in decoding, especially for extreme weather. Diffusion-based approaches offer probabilistic modeling but face high complexity; there is a need for efficient yet uncertainty-aware compression that supports continuous updates.

Method: A frame-wise encoding scheme paired with a unified video decoder (FREUD) using rectified flow transformers. The first stage preserves aleatoric uncertainty enabling ensembling to capture variability in extreme events. Temporal consistency is ensured by a unified decoder. The model supports model-scale and test-time scaling to boost performance; code is released.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art precipitation nowcasting on the SEVIR benchmark with a compact latent-space rectified flow transformer. Further gains are observed with model scaling and test-time scaling.

Conclusion: FREUD provides an efficient, uncertainty-preserving compression framework for spatio-temporal weather data, enabling continuous forecast updates with temporal consistency and improved nowcasting performance, particularly for extreme weather scenarios.

Abstract: Accurate weather forecasts are essential across various domains and are safety-critical in extreme weather conditions. Compared to simulation-based forecasting, data-driven approaches show greater efficiency, enabling short-term, high-resolution nowcasting. In particular, diffusion models proved effective in weather nowcasting due to their strong probabilistic foundation. However, existing methods rely on deterministic compression to reduce the complexity of high-dimensional weather data, limiting their ability to capture uncertainty in the decoding process. In this work, we introduce $\textbf{FREUD}$, a $\textbf{Fr}$ame-wise $\textbf{E}$ncoder and $\textbf{U}$nited $\textbf{D}$ecoder model based on rectified flow transformers for efficient compression of spatio-temporal weather data. Frame-wise encoding enables continuous forecast updates, while the unified video decoder ensures temporal consistency. Our uncertainty-preserving first stage allows us to capture aleatoric uncertainty via ensembling, which is particularly beneficial for extreme weather events with high decoding variability. We achieve state-of-the-art performance in precipitation nowcasting with a compact latent-space rectified flow transformer on the SEVIR benchmark and show further performance gains by model and test-time scaling. Code available here: https://github.com/CompVis/weather-rf

</details>


### [80] [Benchmarking and Enhancing Text-to-Image Models for Generating Visual Representations in Early Arithmetic Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31212)
*Junling Wang,Boqi Chen,Heejin Do,Mubashara Akhtar,April Yi Wang,Mrinmaya Sachan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Equation-to-visual generation benchmark (E2V-Bench) for pedagogy-preserving visuals; T2I models struggle with counts and relational structure; benchmark-guided strategies yield partial gains but gaps remain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between pedagogical intent and AI-generated visuals. Visuals must faithfully encode arithmetic equations, preserving numerical values and relational structure for effective education.

Method: Construct E2V-Bench with four pedagogically grounded visual types; develop automatic metrics for visual correctness; inform benchmark design via teacher interviews and educational-material analysis; evaluate current T2I models and apply benchmark-guided enhancement strategies.

Result: Current T2I models frequently err, dominated by incorrect object counts and broken relational structure; benchmark-guided strategies improve representative models but significant gap remains in numerical and relational grounding.

Conclusion: E2V-Bench serves as a diagnostic tool highlighting the need for stronger numerical/relational grounding in T2I models; improvements are partial, and future work should focus on grounding visuals in arithmetic semantics and relational reasoning.

Abstract: AI systems are increasingly used to support educational content creation, yet it remains unclear whether they can generate outputs that faithfully represent the pedagogical concepts they are intended to teach. Thus, we introduce equation-to-visual generation, a task that, in contrast to conventional image generation, requires producing pedagogically meaningful visuals from arithmetic equations while precisely preserving their numerical and relational structure. Informed by interviews with teachers and an analysis of educational materials, we construct E2V-Bench, a benchmark spanning four pedagogically grounded visual types, along with automatic metrics for evaluating visual correctness. Our evaluation reveals that recent text-to-image (T2I) models frequently fail on this task, with errors dominated by incorrect object counts and broken relational structure. Building on this, we explore benchmark-guided enhancement strategies. These strategies improve representative models, while the remaining gap calls for stronger numerical and relational grounding in future T2I models.

</details>


### [81] [TALON: Token-Aligned Lightweight Adapters for 6-DoF Spacecraft Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31217)
*Abid Ali,Arunkumar Rathinam,Djamila Aouada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight, pre-attention spatiotemporal adapter approach (TALON) for frozen ViTs enables temporal integration for monocular 6-DoF spacecraft pose estimation with minimal parameter overhead, achieving significant improvements on SPADES and SwissCube and strong zero-shot cross-domain transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit temporal information in image sequences during spacecraft maneuvers to improve pose estimation while avoiding full backbone fine-tuning or heavy optical-flow networks, mitigating catastrophic forgetting and computational cost.

Method: Introduce 3D spatiotemporal adapters inserted before the self-attention layers of a frozen ViT. Employ a patch-token alignment loss with a prototype-conditioned KL-divergence objective to ground tokens to keypoints. Pre-attention placement allows temporally enriched tokens to be reasoned over by frozen attention. Use one adapter per block with <5% parameter overhead. Evaluate on SPADES, SwissCube, and SPARK zero-shot transfer; include ablations on adapter depth.

Result: On SPADES: pose error reduced by 50% versus prior state-of-the-art. On SwissCube: ADD-0.1d improved by 21.8% over previous best. Zero-shot SPARK real data: pose error reduced by 4.7x. Ablations highlight the role of adapter depth in both in-domain and cross-domain settings.

Conclusion: Temporal integration via lightweight, pre-attention adapters in a frozen ViT, guided by an alignment loss, yields strong monocular 6-DoF pose estimation with minimal parameter overhead and robust cross-domain performance.

Abstract: Monocular 6-DoF spacecraft pose estimation methods predominantly process individual frames, discarding the temporal information present in an image sequence acquired during spacecraft manoeuvres. Few temporal approaches require full backbone fine-tuning or auxiliary optical flow networks, risking catastrophic forgetting or increasing computational cost, respectively. We propose TALON (Token-Aligned Lightweight adapters for Orbital Navigation): spatiotemporal 3D adapters injected before the self-attention layers of a frozen ViT vision transformer, combined with a patch-token alignment loss that geometrically grounds the adapted features to keypoint structure through a prototype-conditioned KL-divergence objective. Pre-attention placement allows the frozen attention to reason over temporally enriched tokens, achieving stronger performance with a single adapter per block than post-attention alternatives. The alignment loss shapes the intermediate representations so that each keypoint induces a spatially precise activation in the token field, while the framework adds less than 5% parameters to the frozen backbone. On SPADES dataset, TALON reduces the pose error by 50% over the prior state-of-the-art, and on SwissCube dataset it surpasses the prior best by 21.8% in ADD-0.1d accuracy. Zero-shot cross-domain evaluation from sim-to-real on SPARK real data reduces pose error by 4.7x, and ablations characterise the role of adapter depth across in-domain and cross-domain settings.

</details>


### [82] [Latent Geometric Chords for Query-Efficient Decision-Based Adversarial Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31219)
*Ei Hmue Khine,Yao Li,Jiebao Sun,Shengzhu Shi,Zhichang Guo,Boying Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Latent Geometric Chords (LGC) with Residual-based Adversarial Generation (RAG) for query-efficient decision-based black-box attacks; achieves high perceptual fidelity and cross-dataset transferability, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines under strict perceptual constraints with ~5000 queries; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-wise adversarial attacks introduce unnatural high-frequency artifacts; latent-space methods are constrained by small search spaces and reconstruction flaws. A method that operates on a semantic latent manifold with curvature-aware search and an effective residual perturbation mechanism can reconcile fidelity, efficiency, and transferability.

Method: Propose Latent Geometric Chords (LGC) with a variant LGC-H. LGC performs curvature-aware geometric search within a compressed semantic manifold to navigate decision boundaries. To ensure high visual fidelity and overcome dimensionality bottlenecks, introduce Residual-based Adversarial Generation (RAG), which isolates semantic perturbations as geometric chords and superimposes them directly onto the source image, thereby addressing reconstruction flaws and expanding the usable search space.

Result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art baselines with robust cross-dataset transferability. LGC achieves high perceptual fidelity (SSIM > 0.99, LPIPS < 0.01) at 5000 queries while maintaining strong attack success rates against adversarially trained robust models.

Conclusion: LGC (with RAG) provides a scalable, high-fidelity, query-efficient framework for decision-based black-box attacks by leveraging latent geometry and residual perturbations. Demonstrates strong transferability and robustness, but its performance hinges on latent generator quality and requires further evaluation against adaptive defenses.

Abstract: While decision-based black-box adversarial attacks present a severe security threat, current methodologies suffer from fundamental limitations. Pixel-wise attacks frequently introduce unnatural, high-frequency visual artifacts, while latent-space frameworks are confined by the limited search space of low-dimensional manifolds and inherent reconstruction flaws. To resolve these limitations, we propose Latent Geometric Chords (LGC) for Query-Efficient Decision-Based Adversarial Attacks alongside a variant, LGC-H. At its core, LGC navigates decision boundaries by executing a curvature-aware geometric search within a compressed semantic manifold. To guarantee high visual fidelity and circumvent dimensionality bottlenecks, we introduce a Residual-based Adversarial Generation (RAG) mechanism. RAG isolates semantic perturbations as geometric chords and superimposes them directly onto the original source image. RAG substantially resolves baseline reconstruction flaws and effectively doubles the permissible search space dimensions. Experimental results demonstrate that LGC achieves robust cross-dataset transferability and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Notably, our method, LGC, minimizes perturbation magnitudes while achieving state-of-the-art visual fidelity--with a Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) exceeding 0.99 and a Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) below 0.01 at 5000 queries--and sustaining high attack success rates under stringent perceptual constraints, successfully compromising adversarially trained robust models. The source code is available at: https://github.com/eihmuekhine/Latent-Geometric-Chords.

</details>


### [83] [HiERO-StepG @ Ego4D Step Grounding Challenge: hierarchical activity understanding enables zero-shot step grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31227)
*Andrea Zenotto,Simone Alberto Peirone,Francesca Pistilli,Giuseppe Averta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A weakly-supervised, zero-shot step grounding method (HiERO-StepG) uses HiERO to discover hierarchical procedural steps from uncurated videos, achieving strong zero-shot performance on Ego4D with no task-specific annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Procedural activities naturally form hierarchies of steps, but traditional grounding needs extensive annotations and scales poorly. The work aims to induce hierarchical structure from uncurated videos via recurring co-occurring actions, reducing annotation effort.

Method: Extends HiERO, a weakly-supervised representation learning approach that clusters actions in feature space based on functional relatedness using only fine-grained narrations. Step detection is performed by clustering in this space; Ego4D Step Grounding constraints enforce fine/coarse level agreement, strict temporal monotonicity, and post-processing to reduce noise.

Result: On Ego4D Step Grounding, HiERO-StepG achieves 56.27% R@1 (IoU=0.3) on the global leaderboard (submission time), ranking second, with zero-shot performance and no procedure-specific annotations.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that hierarchical procedure structure can emerge from weakly-supervised signals and uncurated videos, enabling competitive, zero-shot step grounding while reducing the need for procedure-specific annotations; promising for scalable procedural understanding.

Abstract: Procedural activities follow well-defined structures: whether we consider a cooking recipe or a mechanic repairing a car, these activities naturally decompose in a hierarchy of steps and sub-steps. Traditional approaches for step grounding require extensive annotations and scale poorly. Instead, we argue that such hierarchical structure can emerge naturally from uncurated videos of human activities through recurring patterns of co-occurring actions and activities. Our approach builds on HiERO, a weakly-supervised representation learning approach that maps close in the feature space actions that are functionally related to each other, leveraging only fine-grained action-level narrations. In this feature space, procedure steps can be detected by a simple clustering, with no additional task-specific fine-tuning. For the Ego4D Step Grounding challenge, we augment this approach by ensuring fine and coarse level agreement in step assignments, enforcing strict temporal monotonicity of the grounded steps and post-processing the detected steps to reduce the impact of noisy predictions. We call this approach HiERO-StepG and it achieves 56.27 % on the R@1 (IoU = 0.3) metric on the global leaderboard at submission time, ranking second while being completely zero-shot and not requiring procedure-specific annotations. Project page: https://github.com/andreazenotto/HiERO-StepG.

</details>


### [84] [Beyond Classification: Dynamic Adapter Routing for Continual Multimodal Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31229)
*Alicja Dobrzeniecka,Filip Szatkowski,Sebastian Cygert,Szymon Lukasik,Bartlomiej Twardowski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a principled continual multimodal retrieval (CMR) evaluation framework, finds standard class-incremental learning (CIL) methods inadequate for CMR, and proposes Dynamic Adapter Routing (DAR) using prototype-based routing and model merging, achieving superior performance and robust OOD generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current continual retrieval research largely follows CIL paradigms, which do not capture retrieval-specific dynamics; a dedicated CMR framework is needed to evaluate and drive progress in continual multimodal retrieval across diverse visual domains.

Method: 1) Propose and formalize a principled evaluation framework for CMR across diverse visual domains. 2) Systematically evaluate existing CIL methods and retrieval-oriented adaptations within this framework. 3) Introduce Dynamic Adapter Routing (DAR): adapters are selected via prototype-based routing and then merged to form the final model.

Result: Standard CIL methods fail to yield meaningful gains in the challenging CMR setting. DAR achieves superior performance over baselines and shows strong generalization under out-of-distribution evaluation.

Conclusion: CMR presents unique challenges distinct from traditional CIL, warranting further research. DAR provides a promising approach for continual multimodal retrieval and encourages exploration of retrieval-focused continual learning.

Abstract: While retrieval is a core function of vision-language models, continually updating these models for retrieval tasks remains critically underexplored. Existing work often approaches continual retrieval through the lens of class-incremental learning (CIL), evaluating both standard CIL methods and retrieval-oriented adaptations in settings that may not fully capture the retrieval-specific dynamics. To address this, we introduce a new, principled evaluation framework for continual multimodal retrieval (CMR) spanning diverse visual domains, and systematically evaluate common approaches within this setting. Our empirical analysis shows that standard CIL methods fail to yield meaningful gains in our more challenging scenario. Therefore, we propose Dynamic Adapter Routing (DAR), a novel approach based on adapters selected through prototype-based routing and combined via model merging.DAR achieves superior performance over the previous baselines and demonstrates strong generalization under out-of-distribution evaluation. Our results highlights the unique challenges of CMR and encourages further research in this direction.

</details>


### [85] [ERGeoBench:A Comprehensive Benchmark for Embodied Reasoning and Geo-localization in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31251)
*Kaiwen Xue,Tao Wei,Guoxin Zhang,Zhonghong Ou,Kaoyan Lu,Yu Feng,Yifan Zhu,Haoran Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ERGeoBench introduces a diagnostic benchmark for vision-driven embodied geo-localization with three progressive settings (single-view, panorama-view, embodied-view) and 2,207 street-view panoramas; it evaluates perception, spatial awareness, commonsense reasoning, and geo-localization reasoning; results show current MLLMs capture high-level geographic semantics but struggle with fine-grained perception, metric localization, and cross-view spatial consistency; geo-localization is tightly linked to integrated perceptual and reasoning abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of fine-grained evaluation in embodied geo-localization for multimodal LLMs and provide a unified diagnostic framework to diagnose and advance embodied geo-localization capabilities.

Method: Proposes ERGeoBench with three progressive observation settings that allow active observations via yaw, pitch, and zoom changes; uses 2,207 globally distributed street-view panoramas; defines four capability dimensions (foundational perception, spatial awareness, common sense reasoning, geo-localization reasoning); evaluates leading proprietary and open-source MLLMs.

Result: Existing models can infer some high-level geographic semantics but struggle with fine-grained perceptual operations, metric localization, and maintaining spatial consistency across views; geo-localization correlates with the other capabilities indicating integrated perception and reasoning are essential.

Conclusion: ERGeoBench provides a unified framework to diagnose and push forward human-like embodied geo-localization; a project page is provided for further details.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential as embodied agents, yet embodied geo-localization remains underexplored due to the lack of fine-grained evaluation. We introduce ERGeoBench, a diagnostic benchmark for vision-driven embodied geo-localization. ERGeoBench evaluates models under three progressive settings -- single-view, panorama-view, and embodied-view -- where agents may actively acquire observations through sequential changes in yaw, pitch, and zoom. The benchmark contains 2,207 globally distributed street-view panoramas and measures four complementary capabilities: foundational perception, spatial awareness, common sense reasoning, and geo-localization reasoning. Evaluations of leading proprietary and open-source MLLMs show that current models can infer high-level geographic semantics, but still struggle with fine-grained perceptual operations, metric localization, and spatial consistency across views. We further observe that geo-localization is strongly correlated with the other capability dimensions, suggesting that accurate localization depends on integrated perception, spatial reasoning, and commonsense inference rather than isolated visual recognition. Overall, ERGeoBench provides a unified framework for diagnosing and advancing human-like embodied geo-localization. Project Page: https://kaixuewen.github.io/ERGeoBench/

</details>


### [86] [Envisioning Beyond the Few: Disentangled Semantics and Primitives for Few-Shot Atypical Layout-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31266)
*Nan Bao,Yifan Zhao,Wenzhuang Wang,Jia Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a representation-driven L2I framework that disentangles semantics from primitives to improve few-shot layout-to-image generation, using Semantic Anchoring, Primitive Imbuing, and Conceptual Steering; shows consistent 5-shot gains over state-of-the-art across atypical domains, with public code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Layout-to-image methods struggle with representation fragmentation in few-shot atypical settings due to a granularity mismatch between semantic identity and visual details, causing fragmented/distorted generations. A robust approach is needed to preserve identity while modeling fine-grained details under data scarcity.

Method: Introduce a representation-driven framework with three components: Semantic Anchoring (aggregate category semantics into stable anchors for identity); Primitive Imbuing (recombine primitives to model local details robustly); Conceptual Steering (saliency-aware optimization to preserve foreground semantic consistency). The framework targets robust 5-shot adaptation and is evaluated against state-of-the-art L2I methods on diverse atypical domains.

Result: Extensive experiments show consistent improvements in 5-shot L2I performance, with better visual fidelity and alignment to target layouts across varied atypical domains, outperforming SOTA methods.

Conclusion: Disentangling semantics from primitives via anchored semantics, recomposable primitives, and saliency-aware optimization yields robust few-shot L2I with improved identity preservation and local detail modeling; code is publicly available.

Abstract: The layout-to-image (L2I) task enables fine-grained control over image generation via object categories and spatial layouts. However, existing L2I methods yield fragmented and distorted generations under few-shot atypical settings. We term this failure as representation fragmentation, arising from a granularity mismatch that entangles semantic identity with visual details. To address this issue, we propose a representation-driven framework that disentangles semantics from primitives for robust few-shot adaptation. Specifically, Semantic Anchoring aggregates categorical semantics into anchors for stable identity, while Primitive Imbuing models recomposable primitives for robust local detail modeling. Conceptual Steering further regulates optimization with a saliency-aware objective to preserve foreground semantic consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in the 5-shot regime over state-of-the-art L2I methods in both visual fidelity and alignment across diverse atypical domains. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/iCVTEAM/DSP.

</details>


### [87] [DriveMA: Driving Vision-Language-Action Models with verifiable Meta-Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31271)
*Weicheng Zheng,Yixin Huang,Qiao Sun,Derun Li,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveMA introduces verifiable meta-actions for Driving Vision-Language-Action models, enabling language-domain intentions derived from ego motion. It uses a trajectory-grounded annotation pipeline and rule-based verification to align high-level language decisions with low-level planning, combined with action-centric supervised learning and turn-level credit assignment reinforcement learning. It achieves state-of-the-art results on Waymo Vision-based E2E Driving and competitive NAVSIM performance, with plans to release code and data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the language-action gap in driving VLAs by enabling verifiable, compact meta-actions that summarize future ego motion in language-like intent, ensuring alignment between high-level decisions and low-level trajectories.

Method: Construct verifiable meta-actions that encapsulate future ego motion as language-domain intentions. Build meta-action annotations from expert trajectories via a trajectory-grounded annotation pipeline. Verify generated trajectories against these meta-actions using rule-based projection. Train with action-centric supervised learning and a turn-level credit assignment RL framework using dense rewards to align high-level decisions with low-level trajectory planning.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art on the Waymo Open Dataset Vision-based End-to-End Driving (Rater Feedback Score: 8.060 for 2B, 8.079 for 4B). Also competitive in closed-loop planning on NAVSIM.

Conclusion: A simple meta-action interface, when made verifiable and optimized for language-action alignment, can achieve state-of-the-art driving planning performance. The authors pose that verifiability is key to effective language-action integration and plan to release code, data, and models.

Abstract: Driving Vision-Language-Action Models (Driving VLAs) aim to use language to improve end-to-end planning, but the language-action gap limits this promise. We propose DriveMA, a Driving VLA framework built on verifiable meta-actions, which summarize future ego motion into compact language-domain intentions and can be constructed from expert trajectories with a trajectory-grounded annotation pipeline and can be verified against generated trajectories through rule-based projection. DriveMA exploits this verifiability with action-centric supervised training and a data-efficient turn-level credit assignment reinforcement learning framework, explicitly aligning high-level decisions with low-level trajectory planning through dense rewards and precise credit assignment. DriveMA sets a new state of the art on the Waymo Open Dataset Vision-based E2E Driving, achieving a Rater Feedback Score of 8.060 with a 2B model and further improving it to 8.079 with a 4B model; it also obtains competitive closed-loop planning performance on NAVSIM. These results show that even a simple meta-action interface can achieve state-of-the-art planning when made verifiable and optimized for language-action alignment. Code, data, and models will be released to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [88] [Topologically Consistent Multi-view 3D Head Reconstruction via Coarse-Guided Layered Surface Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31283)
*Timo Bolkart,Daoye Wang,Prashanth Chandran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SHELLS introduces a hierarchical, layer-based sampling framework for dense 3D head reconstruction from multi-view images that decouples feature extraction from mesh resolution, dramatically reducing memory and inference time while maintaining surface consistency and achieving strong synthetic-to-real generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods refine vertices independently using localized feature volumes, which is memory-intensive and scales poorly to dense meshes, introducing surface noise and requiring costly pre-registered multi-view data. A scalable, efficient, and generalizable approach is needed.

Method: Extract multi-view features with a DINOv2 backbone (LoRA-adapted), projectively sample a sparse global feature cloud, and predict an intermediate coarse mesh. Build layered, surface-aware sampling shells around the coarse mesh to form a discrete search space for final reconstruction, decoupling feature extraction from mesh resolution and enabling scalable, dense topology. Trained entirely on synthetic data.

Result: Memory usage reduced by 88% (2.4GB vs. 20GB). Inference speed increased ~3.5x (0.08s vs. 0.29s) for 18k-vertex meshes. Median registration error decreased by 21–29%. Generalizes effectively to real-world captures despite synthetic-only training; eliminates need for costly pre-registered multi-view datasets.

Conclusion: SHELLS offers scalable, surface-consistent dense head reconstruction with substantial memory and speed benefits and strong generalization from synthetic data, addressing core limitations of prior volumetric approaches.

Abstract: We present SHELLS (Semantic Head Estimation via Layered Local Sampling), an efficient feed-forward framework for 3D head reconstruction in dense semantic correspondence from multi-view images. Existing methods typically refine vertices independently via localized feature volumes. This approach couples memory-intensive feature sampling to mesh resolution, which limits scalability for dense topologies (> 10k vertices) and introduces surface noise. In contrast, SHELLS decouples feature extraction from mesh resolution via a hierarchical sampling strategy. We extract multi-view features using a DINOv2 backbone with LoRA adaptation, projectively sample a sparse global feature cloud, and predict an intermediate coarse mesh. This coarse prior guides the construction of layered, surface-aware sampling shells that serve as a discrete search space for the final reconstruction. SHELLS maintains surface consistency while using 88% less inference GPU memory (2.4GB vs. 20GB) than volumetric baselines. It reduces median registration error by 21% to 29% with a 3.5x inference speedup (0.08s vs. 0.29s) for 18k-vertex meshes. Notably, our model is trained exclusively on synthetic data yet generalizes effectively to real-world captures, eliminating the need for the costly, pre-registered multi-view datasets common in prior work.

</details>


### [89] [SAM for Robust Mitochondria Instance Segmentation in Fluorescence Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31284)
*Suyog Jadhav,Dilip K. Prasad,Krishna Agarwal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fine-tuning a foundation model (SAM) on synthetically generated fluorescence microscopy data to perform mitochondria instance segmentation; achieves improved precision and Dice on real FM data, bridging the domain gap due to limited annotated datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome domain shift from natural images to FM, and address severe lack of high-quality, manually annotated mitochondria segmentation datasets by leveraging scalable synthetic data for training.

Method: Generate large-scale realistic synthetic FM mitochondrial images incorporating optical properties (PSF, noise, intensity distribution, overlap) and obtain pixel-accurate instance annotations. Finetune SAM on this synthetic FM dataset and evaluate on a curated real FM mitochondria dataset with manual annotations.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative analyses show improved precision and average Dice score over strong baselines, supporting the viability of simulation-assisted training for FM instance segmentation.

Conclusion: Simulation-driven fine-tuning of foundation models can mitigate data scarcity in fluorescence microscopy interdisciplinary tasks, enabling robust mitochondria instance segmentation and potentially generalizing to other FM segmentation challenges.

Abstract: The morphological analysis of mitochondria in fluorescence microscopy (FM) is crucial for understanding cellular health, energy production, and metabolic regulation. While foundation models like the Segment Anything Model (SAM) have revolutionized natural image segmentation, their direct application to FM is hindered by a significant domain shift characterized by diffraction-limited resolution, low contrast, and complex overlapping organelle networks. Furthermore, the development of robust models is bottlenecked by a severe lack of high-quality, manually annotated instance segmentation datasets for mitochondria. In this paper, we propose a scalable solution to this data scarcity by finetuning SAM exclusively on synthetically generated FM data. We simulate realistic mitochondria data and emulate the optical properties of fluorescence microscopes to create a large-scale annotated dataset. We evaluate our fine-tuned model on a curated dataset of real, manually annotated FM images. Qualitative and quantitative analyses demonstrate that our synthetically fine-tuned model improves precision and average dice score over strong baselines. This work establishes the potential of simulation-assisted training for FM instance segmentation.

</details>


### [90] [Authentication of Copy Detection Patterns via Cross-Camera Dual-Synthetic Referencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31292)
*Ivan Oleksiyuk,Roman Chaban,Slava Voloshynovskiy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Enrolment-based cross-camera dual-synthetic referencing for Copy Detection Patterns (CDPs) improves robustness and security by generating dual references from the digital template and an enrolled capture, with information-theoretic justification and mobile-camera validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Printer stochasticity and camera distortions degrade CDP verification. Prior work synthesized references in the verification camera domain but ignored printing variability, reducing robustness against counterfeits.

Method: During enrolment, capture the printed CDP with a controlled camera. A deep-learning translator uses both the digital template and the enrolled capture to generate a high-quality verification reference. At verification, the system uses the dual reference (together with the captured image) to authenticate. An information-theoretic justification argues the dual reference is more informative than template-based references.

Result: Experiments on heterogeneous mobile cameras show improved authentication performance, greater robustness against ML-based copy attacks, and reliable verification from small CDP regions and on low-end devices.

Conclusion: The dual-reference approach yields more informative verification signals than template-only references, enhancing cross-camera CDP authentication robustness and enabling practical deployment on diverse devices and small regions.

Abstract: Copy Detection Patterns (CDPs) are structures printed on physical objects to enable cost-effective authentication. Verification is achieved by comparing a captured image with the digital template from which the CDP was printed. In practice, printer stochasticity and camera distortions hinder this comparison, limiting robustness against counterfeiting. Prior work addressed camera effects by synthesising reference images in the verification camera domain, but it ignored printing variability. We introduce an enrolment-based cross-camera dual-synthetic referencing framework. Each printed CDP is first captured by a controlled enrolment camera, and a deep-learning-based translator jointly exploits the digital template and the enrolled capture to generate a high-quality reference for the verification image. We provide an information-theoretic justification showing that the dual reference is more informative than template-based references. Experiments on heterogeneous mobile cameras demonstrate improved authentication performance, robustness to machine-learning-based copy attacks, and reliable verification from small CDP regions and on low-end devices.

</details>


### [91] [TokTalk: Expressive Real-time Facial Animation from Audio-LLM Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31294)
*Qingcheng Zhao,Yifang Pan,Karan Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TokTalk directly outputs expressive 3D facial animation from streaming audio-tokens, bypassing multi-stage pipelines. It uses a novel audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset and a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model, with lightweight adaptation to connect to token-based Audio-LLMs. It achieves real-time latency with improved perceptual quality and flexibility across avatar and animation scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Audio-LLMs enable conversational agents but facial animation remains robotic due to sequential processing across ASR, text generation, TTS, and animation. If audio-tokens contain sufficient information to reconstruct plausible facial performances, direct mapping can reduce latency and improve expressivity.

Method: Create a dedicated audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset. Train a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model to map audio-tokens to 3D facial motion. Implement a lightweight adaptation layer to connect TokTalk with any token-based Audio-LLM. Employ chunk-based processing to trade latency and facial quality. Validate with ablations, latency comparisons, and perceptual studies, plus demonstrations across multiple avatar applications.

Result: TokTalk achieves real-time facial animation from audio-tokens with latency comparable to prior methods, while delivering significantly higher quality and expressivity per perceptual studies. The approach provides flexible integration with various avatars and interfaces, and allows user control of latency-quality trade-offs.

Conclusion: Directly generating expressive facial animation from audio-tokens is viable and beneficial, enabling more natural, responsive avatars with adjustable latency-quality trade-offs and broad applicability across chatbot avatars, voice-driven avatars, and animation workflows.

Abstract: Recent advances in Audio-LLMs like GPT-4o have ushered in an era of conversational interaction with language models. Conversational avatars however, still seem robotic in facial expression and conversational flow, in part due to sequential stages of speech recognition, text generation, turn-based text response, speech synthesis, and audio driven facial animation. Based on our insight that audio-tokens produced by current Audio-LLMs carry sufficient information to reconstruct a plausible facial performance, we present TokTalk, a system that directly outputs expressive facial animation in real-time from streaming audio-tokens. We construct a novel audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset, on which TokTalk is trained using a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model. A lightweight adaptation strategy allows our trained model to seamlessly connect to any token-based Audio-LLM at minimal computational overhead. Our chunk-based processing further enables parametric trade-off between latency and facial quality, shown through ablation studies. We further show that the real-time performance of TokTalk is comparable in latency to prior art solutions, and significantly favorable (via a perceptual study) in terms of quality, expressivity and control of the 3D facial performance. We showcase TokTalk's flexibility using a chatbot Avatar, a voice-driven user Avatar, and an animation Director's interface, as diverse audio-visual face applications.

</details>


### [92] [Learning from Fine-Grained Visual Discrepancies: Mitigating Multimodal Hallucinations via In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31312)
*Haolin Deng,Xin Zou,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Xuming Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IC-VCO introduces in-context visual contrastive optimization to align visual and textual guidance, with a reliability-gated distillation (VCDist) and a contrastive sample editing strategy to create hard negatives, achieving state-of-the-art performance on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models suffer from multimodal hallucination due to weak visual supervision. Standard textual DPO lacks explicit visual constraint, and prior visual preference DPO methods use coarse negatives and suffer from theoretical inconsistencies due to partition function mismatches.

Method: In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization (IC-VCO) places multiple contrastive images within a shared multi-image context to enforce a sound objective. Visual Contrast Distillation (VCDist) acts as a reliability-gated regularizer to align multi-image contrastive training with single-image inference. A contrastive sample editing strategy generates hard negatives via precise semantic perturbations.

Result: Experiments on five benchmarks show IC-VCO achieves best overall performance and validate the effectiveness of the sample editing strategy. Code and data are released at the provided repository.

Conclusion: IC-VCO provides a mathematically rigorous objective for visual contrastive optimization in DPO, improving robustness against multimodal hallucination. VCDist ensures consistency between training and inference, and the sample editing strategy yields harder negatives to further improve performance.

Abstract: Multimodal hallucination remains a persistent challenge for Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Standard textual Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) often fails to mitigate it due to a lack of explicit visual supervision. While existing works introduce visual preference DPO by contrasting original images against negative ones, they suffer from a theoretically inconsistent objective caused by partition function mismatches and rely on coarse-grained negatives that could enable shortcut learning. In this work, we propose In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization (IC-VCO). By placing contrastive images within a shared multi-image context, IC-VCO ensures a mathematically rigorous objective. We further introduce Visual Contrast Distillation (VCDist), an auxiliary reliability-gated regularizer that encourages consistency between multi-image contrastive training and single-image inference. Finally, we propose a contrastive sample editing strategy that generates hard negatives via precise semantic perturbations. Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate IC-VCO's best overall performance and the effectiveness of our sample editing strategy. Code and data are available at https://github.com/OPPO-Mente-Lab/IC-VCO.

</details>


### [93] [DecMem: Towards Minute-Long Consistent World Generation with Decoupled Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31336)
*Zhenhao Yang,Xiaoshi Wu,Zhengyao Lv,Xiaoyu Shi,Xintao Wang,Pengfei Wan,Kun Gai,Kwan-Yee K. Wong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces DecMem, a decoupled memory architecture for long-horizon video generation that overcomes inefficiency and attention dispersion in naïve learnable memory, enabling fine-grained, minute-level controllable long videos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address computational inefficiency and attention dispersion in long-horizon, learnable memory architectures during video generation, enabling stable, fine-grained spatiotemporal consistency over extended sequences.

Method: Proposes a decoupled memory design with Sparse Global Memory for efficient access to global history and Anchored Local Memory for stable, high-fidelity extrapolation; includes systematic analysis of attention dispersion to motivate the architectural choices.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows DecMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in long-video generation, delivering precise long-term memory, efficient access, and high fidelity/extrapolation, enabling minute-level controllable generation.

Conclusion: DecMem provides a scalable, precise memory mechanism that balances global coherence and local detail, enabling reliable long-horizon video generation with improved efficiency and extrapolation quality.

Abstract: Recent advances in video generative models have promoted rapid progress in controllable world models. However, maintaining fine-grained spatio-temporal consistency under long-horizon reasoning remains a key challenge. In this work, we move beyond explicit 3D memory and coarse frame-level implicit modeling, and propose a fine-grained, learnable, and scalable memory for consistent world generation. We first identify two fundamental limitations of naïve learnable memory architectures in long-horizon extrapolation, namely computational inefficiency and attention dispersion. Through a systematic analysis of attention dispersion, we propose DecMem, a decoupled memory architecture that employs Sparse Global Memory for efficient fine-grained access to global history and Anchored Local Memory for stable and high-quality extrapolation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DecMem significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods. By ensuring precise and efficient long-term memory and achieving superior extrapolation capabilities, DecMem enables minute-level controllable long video generation with high fidelity and consistency.

</details>


### [94] [YARD: Y-Architecture Register Decoding for Efficient Hallucination Mitigation in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31429)
*Ting Chen,Geng Li,Guohao Chen,Yu Hu,Guan Huang,Mai Chen,Langsheng Lei,Jun Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: YARD is a training-free contrastive decoding method that builds an internal degraded branch at the middle of the decoder by sharing shallow computations and using register tokens in place of patch tokens, enabling effective hallucination mitigation with lower latency than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training-free contrastive decoding (CD) approaches struggle: dropping visual tokens is too aggressive and causes language hallucinations, while image corruption leads to coarse control and requires two full forward passes (high latency). A degraded branch that preserves global semantics while remaining under-grounded and latency-efficient is needed.

Method: YARD introduces a Y-architecture register decoding framework. It shares early-layer computations and branches at the middle decoder layer to form the degraded branch. In this branch, patch-level visual tokens are replaced with register tokens, preserving global image semantics but reducing fine-grained local evidence. This yields an image-aware yet locally under-grounded design and avoids an extra forward pass, enabling a training-free contrastive signal.

Result: Extensive experiments on generative and discriminative hallucination benchmarks show that YARD achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation across multiple LVLMs, with a significant reduction in inference latency.

Conclusion: YARD provides a faithful, robust contrastive signal by balancing image awareness and local grounding, achieving strong hallucination mitigation without training and without a second forward pass.

Abstract: Contrastive decoding (CD) seeks to mitigate hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) by contrasting the output distributions of a standard model and a visually degraded model. However, existing training-free CD methods suffer from sub-optimal degraded branches: completely dropping visual tokens is too extreme and induces language hallucinations, while corrupting input images offers coarse control over visual evidence and suffers from high inference latency due to requiring two full forward passes. To address these dilemmas, we propose YARD, a training-free Y-Architecture Register Decoding framework. Motivated by the observation that reliable text-to-vision grounding predominantly emerges in the middle decoder layers, YARD constructs the degraded branch internally by sharing shallow-layer computations and branching exactly at this critical stage. For the degraded branch, YARD replaces patch-level visual tokens with register tokens, which preserve global image semantics but lack fine-grained local evidence. This image-aware yet locally under-grounded design provides a faithful contrastive signal without extreme modality mismatch, while the Y-architecture strictly avoids a costly second forward pass. Extensive experiments on generative and discriminative hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that YARD consistently achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation across multiple LVLMs, alongside a significant reduction in inference latency.

</details>


### [95] [Astra: a generalizable report generation foundation model for 3D computed tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31437)
*Zhuhao Wang,Fang Chen,Chaohui Yu,Zihan Li,Yuchao Zheng,Jing Wang,Xuan Yang,Jia Guo,Zhenlu Yang,Xingju Zheng,Yihua Sun,Haojie Han,Xiaoxiao Qin,Zhan Feng,Wenbo Xiao,Chao Zhu,Yuehua Li,Shipeng Zhang,Hao Luo,Yunsong Peng,Fan Wang,Hongen Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Astra is a generalizable foundation model for CT report generation trained on a large multi-region dataset, achieving strong performance and workflow efficiency gains across cohorts, with potential as infrastructure for CT AI development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a generalizable CT report generation foundation model capable of multi-region reporting and robust external cohort performance, and reduce reliance on inconsistent textual supervision.

Method: Train on 90,678 thoracoabdominal CT–report pairs (CTRgDB) covering 353,671 abnormalities across eight organ systems; harmonize report styles; refine diagnostic consistency via reinforcement learning; evaluate on CTRgDB and six external cohorts.

Result: Achieves 44.1% average improvement in fine-grained diagnostic metrics (P<0.001); real-world workflow gains: chest report drafting +29.6%, abdominal report completeness +11.3% (P<0.001); demonstrates utility as a foundation for CT AI development and downstream improvements.

Conclusion: Astra provides a broadly accessible clinical assistant and a scalable infrastructure for next-generation AI-powered healthcare in CT report generation, enabling better generalization across regions and organs.

Abstract: CT interpretation requires radiologists to review hundreds of volumetric slices per examination, making reporting time-consuming and highly expertise-dependent. Automated CT report generation offers a promising route to improving clinical efficiency, yet the field still lacks a generalizable CT report generation foundation model that supports multi-region reporting and remains robust across external real-world cohorts. Intrinsic inconsistencies in reporting style and diagnostic terminology across cohorts make naive joint training prone to noisy textual supervision, thereby limiting model generalizability. Here we present Astra, a generalizable CT report generation foundation model trained on 90,678 thoracoabdominal CT-report pairs (CTRgDB) with 353,671 abnormalities spanning eight organ systems. By harmonizing report style and further refining diagnostic consistency via reinforcement learning, Astra achieves style-consistent and diagnostically accurate report generation across diverse anatomical regions and institutions. Evaluating on CTRgDB and six external cohorts, Astra achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 44.1% average improvement in fine-grained diagnostic metrics (P<0.001). In real-world clinical workflows, Astra assistance accelerates chest report drafting by 29.6% and improves abdominal report completeness by 11.3% (P<0.001). Furthermore, Astra also demonstrates broad utility as a foundation for CT AI development, improving downstream diagnostic performance and scaling vision-language pretrain through high-quality report synthesis. Overall, Astra serves as a broadly accessible clinical assistant and a pivotal infrastructure for the next generation of AI-powered healthcare.

</details>


### [96] [VisionPulse: Dynamic Visual Sparsity for Efficient Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31457)
*Hengbo Xu,Shengjie Jin,Yanbiao Ma,Zhiwu Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VisionPulse introduces step-wise visual token pruning for large multimodal models, pruning only the most critical tokens at each decoding step to reduce inference cost and shorten reasoning traces with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time overhead in large multimodal models; prefill pruning assumes static visual evidence, but evidence is step-dependent and a redundant context can mislead reasoning (query-irrelevant regions).

Method: Compute a lightweight visual attention mass to estimate a per-step retention budget that correlates with effective visual token usage; retain only the top tokens under this budget; enforce sparsity during reasoning to filter redundant context while preserving relevant evidence.

Result: Retains about 5% of visual tokens per step; reasoning traces shortened by 11.2%; accuracy remains nearly unchanged.

Conclusion: Step-wise visual token pruning via VisionPulse effectively reduces inference cost and trace length while preserving accuracy, addressing both the evolution of relevant visual evidence and the coupled bottleneck from redundant context.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large multimodal models (LMMs), inference-time overhead has become a key bottleneck for real-world deployment. Existing methods typically prune visual tokens at prefill, assuming the required visual evidence remains static during reasoning. However, we empirically show that visual evidence is strongly step-dependent: only a sparse subset of visual tokens is critical at each decoding step, and the critical set evolves across reasoning. Furthermore, we identify a coupled bottleneck where redundant visual context can steer the model toward query-irrelevant regions, lengthening the reasoning trace. Guided by these insights, we propose VisionPulse, a step-wise visual token pruning framework during reasoning. VisionPulse computes a lightweight visual attention mass to estimate the step-wise retention budget by exploiting its strong positive correlation with LMMs' effective visual token usage and retain only the most critical tokens under this budget. By enforcing visual sparsity during reasoning, VisionPulse filters redundant visual context while preserving relevant visual evidence, shortening reasoning traces naturally. Extensive experiments show that VisionPulse only retains 5% of visual tokens per step with reasoning traces shortened by 11.2%, while keeping accuracy almost unchanged.

</details>


### [97] [VolFill: Single-View Amodal 3D Scene Reconstruction with Volumetric Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31466)
*Tuan Duc Ngo,Chuang Gan,Evangelos Kalogerakis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VolFill reconstructs complete 3D scene geometry from a single RGB image using a hybrid 3D VAE and latent diffusion transformer, guided by geometry priors, enabling direct surface extraction and occupancy queries with improved baselines on SCRREAM and NRGB-D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-view 3D reconstruction struggles with hidden structures and incomplete evidence. Traditional pixel-aligned regression or per-ray methods fail to reconstruct holistic geometry; a generative, prior-informed approach is needed to infer complete scenes from a single image.

Method: VolFill uses a hybrid 3D VAE to compress sparse truncated unsigned distance function grids into a compact latent space, paired with a latent Diffusion Transformer that denoises this representation to recover the complete scene. Generation is conditioned on geometry foundation models to leverage rich spatial priors, producing a structured representation suitable for direct surface extraction and occupancy queries at scale.

Result: Extensive experiments on SCRREAM and NRGB-D demonstrate that VolFill significantly outperforms current baselines, establishing a robust foundation for holistic spatial understanding.

Conclusion: VolFill offers a robust, priors-informed generative framework for single-image 3D reconstruction that produces complete scene geometry in a structured form, enabling scalable surface extraction and occupancy querying.

Abstract: Reconstructing the complete geometry of a scene from a single RGB image remains challenging - especially when inferring hidden structures where visual evidence is incomplete. We introduce VolFill, a generative framework that predicts the 3D structure of the complete scene rather than relying on traditional pixel-aligned regression. Our method utilizes a hybrid 3D VAE to compress sparse truncated unsigned distance function grids into a compact latent space, paired with a latent Diffusion Transformer that denoises this representation to recover the complete scene. We condition the generation on geometry foundation models, leveraging rich spatial priors for robust reasoning. Unlike existing methods limited by per-ray constraints or unstructured point-cloud queries, VolFill provides a structured representation that supports direct surface extraction and occupancy queries at scale. Extensive experiments on the SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms current baselines, providing a robust foundation for holistic spatial understanding.

</details>


### [98] [Enhancing Computer Vision Model Generalization in Warehouse Facilities: A Case Study on Anomaly Detection in Vertical Material Handling Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31487)
*Ruiliang Liu,Tina Dongxu Li,Joshua Migdal,Ken Meszaros,Trevor Dardik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lab-only development framework for warehouse CV to generalize to real facilities via optimized camera placement, image triggering, and ensemble models; experimental validation suggests reduced deployment effort and annotation/retraining needs, not production-ready.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce resource-intensive deployment in new warehouse environments by addressing camera mounting constraints and environmental variability, aiming to cut annotation and retraining requirements.

Method: Replicate the standard deployment procedure in a laboratory setting focusing on vertical material handling systems and fork anomaly detection; optimize camera placement, image triggering, model selection, and model ensembles; perform extensive experiments to test generalization from lab to diverse facilities.

Result: Laboratory-optimized setups with proper camera configuration, triggering, and ensemble modeling generalize to varied warehouse environments, potentially simplifying deployment to camera mounting, image collection, and model deployment while saving annotation/retraining time; results are within an experimental study, not production.

Conclusion: The approach shows promise for lab-to-field transfer in warehouse CV but remains experimental; if validated further, it could streamline deployment and reduce resources, albeit not a production deployment yet.

Abstract: Deploying computer vision models in Warehouse Facilities traditionally requires extensive resources for camera mounting, image collection, annotation, training, and deployment - a process often needing repetition in each new environment due to camera mounting constraints and environmental variability. This paper explores an innovative approach to streamline this process by conducting the standard procedure solely in a laboratory setting, focusing on vertical material handling systems and anomaly detection in forks of the systems. Through extensive experimentation, we have found that combining optimal camera placement, strategic image triggering, careful model selection and model ensemble enables effective generalization from laboratory conditions to diverse warehouse facilities environments, potentially transforming warehouse automation implementation by simplifying warehouse facilities deployment to just camera mounting, image collection, and model deployment, thereby saving significant resources and time typically spent on image annotation and model retraining. This is an experimental research study and not a production deployment.

</details>


### [99] [How can embedding models bind concepts?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31503)
*Arnas Uselis,Darina Koishigarina,Seong Joon Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLIP-like models bind concepts poorly; scene embeddings decompose additively into object representations, enabling object info recovery via uni-modal probes; CLIP's binding is high-complexity and hinders generalization to unseen concept combinations; in controlled transformers, binding generalizes with sufficient data, via low-complexity multiplicative interactions; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how binding of concepts to scene representations works in vision-language models, whether the difficulty is intrinsic or solvable with data, and what binding function complexity implies for generalization.

Method: Empirically analyze CLIP-like bindings: treat scene embeddings as additive decompositions of object representations; test recovery of object information with uni-modal probes; assess the complexity of the binding function; train controlled transformer models from scratch with varying data coverage to observe binding generalization and identify whether low-complexity multiplicative interactions support systematic generalization.

Result: CLIP’s binding function exhibits high complexity, limiting generalization to novel concept combinations; scene embeddings decompose additively into object representations, explaining recoverability by uni-modal probes; in controlled transformers, binding generalizes when data coverage is sufficient, and relies on low-complexity multiplicative interactions between concepts.

Conclusion: The observed binding limitation in CLIP is not fundamental; with adequate data, models can learn low-complexity, multiplicative-binding functions enabling systematic generalization. Public code provided to reproduce.

Abstract: Humans easily determine which color belongs to which shape in multi-object scenes, an ability known as concept binding. Vision-language embedding models such as CLIP struggle with binding: they recognize individual concepts but fail to represent which concepts form which objects. Although CLIP behaves like a bag-of-concepts model in cross-modal retrieval, object information is recoverable from its image and text embeddings separately. We study this tension through the binding function, which maps concepts to scene embeddings. We find that scene embeddings decompose additively into object representations, explaining why uni-modal probes can recover object information. However, CLIP's binding function is high-complexity, which likely prevents the image and text encoders from learning a shared binding mechanism that generalizes to unseen concept combinations. We then ask whether this limitation is fundamental. We show that it is not. In controlled transformer models trained from scratch, binding generalization emerges with sufficient data coverage. These models learn low-complexity binding functions characterized by multiplicative interactions between concepts, enabling systematic generalization. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/oshapio/binding-concepts-complexity.

</details>


### [100] [Internalizing Temporal Consistency in Video Object-Centric Learning without Explicit Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31508)
*Rongzhen Zhao,Zhiyuan Li,Juho Kannala,Joni Pajarinen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes xSSC: enforce temporal consistency in video object-centric learning via two near-zero-cost mechanisms—Chrono-Channel Decomposition (static/dynamic sub-spaces) and Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (reconstructs features using a standard OCL decoder)—eliminating explicit SSC losses while achieving SOTA results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SSC-based methods rely on explicit one-to-one object correspondence across frames and add extra loss terms, which can hinder efficiency and generalization. The authors argue for enforcing temporal consistency through model design rather than losses, aiming for a simpler, faster, and more robust paradigm.

Method: Introduce Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) to split slot representations along the channel dimension into static (time-invariant) and dynamic (time-variant) sub-spaces. Propose Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) that uses current slots' static channels and target slots' dynamic channels to reconstruct target features, within a single standard OCL decoder and with minimal training tweaks. This setup eliminates the need for the SSC loss (xSSC).

Result: Empirically, xSSC-based methods achieve higher training efficiency and set new state-of-the-art results on video object discovery and recognition. PCA and gradient analyses support that static sub-space encodes time-invariant semantics while dynamic sub-space encodes time-variant kinematics.

Conclusion: Temporal consistency in video OCL can be effectively enforced via model design (CCD + CTR) rather than explicit SSC losses, enabling efficient training and strong performance. The authors provide code, checkpoints, and training logs for reproducibility.

Abstract: Video Object-Centric Learning (OCL) aims to represent objects as \textit{slot} vectors and maintain their consistency across frames. Slot-Slot Contrastive (SSC) loss has become the cornerstone for state-of-the-art (SOTA) video OCL methods. While highly effective, SSC relies on one-to-one object correspondence across frames and introduces an extra loss. Following Occam's Razor, we propose a paradigm shift: temporal consistency is better enforced as an implicit model design rather than an explicit loss. To elegantly exclude SSC (\textbf{xSSC}), we introduce two quasi-zero-overhead synergistic mechanisms: (\textit{i}) Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) structurally disentangles slot representations along the channel dimension into \textit{static} and \textit{dynamic} sub-spaces, serving as an empirically unified information bottleneck; (\textit{ii}) Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) stochastically reconstructs target features of either the current or previous time step by fusing current slots' static channels and target slots' dynamic channels, using a single standard OCL decoder with minor training adaptation. Thereby, the slot sets inherently learn temporal consistency by minimizing the standard reconstruction error alone. Extensive experiments show that integrating xSSC into leading baselines not only improves training efficiency but also establishes new SOTAs on video object discovery and recognition tasks. Furthermore, our PCA and gradient analyses confirm that objects' time-invariant semantics and time-variant kinematics are encoded into the proposed sub-spaces. Our source code, model checkpoints and training logs are provided on https://github.com/Genera1Z/xSSC.

</details>


### [101] [Personalize Your Large Vision-language Models With In-context Prompt Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31513)
*Yanshu Li,Jiaqian Li,Kuai Yu,Xi Xiao,Dongfang Liu,Tianyang Wang,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ICPT is a method for LVLM personalization that uses a lightweight projection module to extract fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images and convert them, along with identity-label mappings, into continuous prompts. It adaptively adjusts prompt length based on concept visual complexity and introduces two geometric regularizations to decouple identities from environmental biases and separate concepts, addressing cross-concept interference. It reportedly achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones while improving efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable fast, effective LVLM personalization that generalizes to out-of-distribution concepts, without inference-time training, and robustly handles complex multi-image, multi-concept scenarios and environmental bias.

Method: Introduce In-Context Prompt Tuning (ICPT) with a lightweight projection module that extracts fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images and maps them, along with identity-labels, into continuous prompts. The module adapts prompt length based on intrinsic visual complexity. Propose two geometric regularizations to decouple identities from environmental states and to separate concepts to prevent semantic confusion.

Result: Extensive experiments show ICPT achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones.

Conclusion: ICPT provides an efficient and robust approach to LVLM personalization, enabling broad deployment by reducing inference-time training needs and mitigating cross-concept interference and environmental biases.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated strong general multimodal capability and are increasingly deployed in downstream systems. This trend has driven growing interest in LVLM personalization, which aims to enable models to quickly and effectively learn out-of-distribution multimodal concepts to meet user-specific needs. However, many existing methods rely on inference-time training, which reduces efficiency. They also struggle to maintain accuracy in complex multi-image, multi-concept settings. These limitations restrict the broader deployment of LVLM-based systems. Therefore, this paper proposes in-context prompt tuning (ICPT). Specifically, ICPT employs a lightweight projection module capable of operating in complex scenarios to extract fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images, seamlessly transforming these features alongside identity-label mappings into continuous prompts. To maximize computational efficiency, this module adaptively determines the prompt length based on the intrinsic visual complexity of each concept. Crucially, to overcome the environmental biases and cross-concept interference prevalent in real-world applications, we introduce two novel geometric regularizations. These constraints refine prompt representations by decoupling key identities from transient environmental states and separating concepts to avoid semantic confusion. Extensive experiments show that ICPT achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones.

</details>


### [102] [SVI-Bench: A Dynamic Microworld for Strategic Video Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31529)
*Yulu Pan,Han Yi,Seongsu Ha,Md Mohaiminul Islam,Benjamin Zhang,Lorenzo Torresani,Gedas Bertasius*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SVI-Bench introduces a large-scale, verifiable, multi-agent video intelligence benchmark using professional team sports to probe perception, causal reasoning, simulation, and agentic synthesis; results show strong perceptual performance but a steep decline across higher cognitive levels, with agentic tasks being the hardest.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in benchmarks that assess causal reasoning and strategic planning in video understanding. In-the-wild data lacks verifiable ground truth for causal/strategic questions, while synthetic environments lack realistic multi-agent dynamics. Team sports provide realistic, rule-governed, adversarial multi-agent microworlds with verifiable outcomes.

Method: A data engine converts raw broadcast game data into a dense, cross-referenced corpus: ~35k hours of video, 15M annotated actions, 15k hours of expert commentary, 23k game reports, and 103k structured statistics across basketball, soccer, and hockey. Nine tasks span four pillars: Dynamic Scene Understanding, Causal Reasoning, Strategic Simulation, and Agentic Synthesis, evaluated with strong multimodal/agentic baselines.
