Result: On perceptual tasks, models reach ~73% on fine-grained action QA. Performance declines at each higher cognitive level. The strongest model achieves only 5% accuracy on agentic tasks, which require autonomous evidence gathering/integration over ~1.8M clips.

Conclusion: SVI-Bench reveals a pronounced capability cliff between perception and higher-level reasoning, with agentic synthesis remaining highly challenging. The benchmark provides scalable, verifiable evaluation to drive future models toward integrated, multi-step reasoning and evidence synthesis in complex multi-agent video domains.

Abstract: True video intelligence demands more than recognizing what is visible: it requires reasoning about why events unfold, predicting what would change under different conditions, and deciding what to do next. We refer to this progression, from perception through causal reasoning and simulation to strategic planning, as Strategic Video Intelligence (SVI). No existing benchmark evaluates this capability stack: in-the-wild videos lack verifiable ground truth for causal and strategic questions, while synthetic environments sacrifice the complexity of real multi-agent systems. To bridge this gap, we introduce SVI-Bench, a large-scale benchmark that leverages team sports as a dynamic microworld, combining the complexity of real-world multi-agent interaction (10-22 agents making coordinated decisions under adversarial pressure) with the verifiability of explicit rules and definitive outcomes. SVI-Bench comprises approximately 35K hours of broadcast video, 15M annotated actions, 15K hours of expert commentary, 23K game reports, and 103K structured statistical records across basketball, soccer, and hockey, all constructed via a data engine that transforms raw game data into a dense, cross-referenced corpus. We organize evaluation into 9 tasks spanning a progressive four-pillar hierarchy: Dynamic Scene Understanding, Causal Reasoning, Strategic Simulation, and Agentic Synthesis. Evaluating strong multimodal and agentic baselines, we find a capability cliff: models perform competently on perceptual tasks, achieving approximately 73% on fine-grained action QA, but degrade sharply at each successive cognitive level. Agentic tasks prove hardest: the strongest model achieves only 5% accuracy when required to autonomously gather and integrate evidence across a corpus of 1.8M clips.

</details>


### [103] [Feature-Optimized Vision for Adaptive 3D Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31534)
*Eric Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adaptive feature selection front-end for 3D reconstruction that allocates per-view budgets using multi-criterion scoring to improve reconstruction quality and coverage; outperforms baselines on synthetic scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed feature thresholds and uniform budgets can waste compute on non-informative regions; an adaptive, quality-aware feature budget can focus computation on discriminative evidence to improve 3D reconstruction efficiency and accuracy.

Method: Score candidate features by texture, repeatability, distinctiveness, expected triangulation angle, and spatial coverage; allocate a per-view feature budget to maximize useful tracks within a fixed reconstruction pipeline; evaluate four selection policies on synthetic multi-view prototypes across corridor, facade, object-table, and cluttered scenes.

Result: Adaptive policy achieves the best quality-aware completeness and the lowest aggregate reconstruction RMSE compared to random, texture-only, and uniform-grid baselines; preserves broad image coverage.

Conclusion: The approach is a modular front-end policy that can augment both classical and learned 3D pipelines; not a replacement for modern learned matching or neural reconstruction systems, but directs computational resource toward the most informative visual evidence.

Abstract: Three-dimensional scene reconstruction depends on local image evidence that is both visually discriminative and geometrically useful. Fixed feature thresholds and uniform feature budgets are easy to deploy, but they can waste computation on repeated texture, low-parallax regions, or unstable points. This paper proposes an adaptive feature-optimized vision front end for 3D reconstruction. The method scores candidate features by texture, repeatability, distinctiveness, expected triangulation angle, and spatial coverage, then allocates a per-view feature budget to maximize useful tracks under a fixed reconstruction pipeline. A small synthetic multi-view prototype evaluates four selection policies across corridor, facade, object-table, and cluttered scenes. Compared with random, texture-only, and uniform-grid baselines, the adaptive policy obtains the best quality-aware completeness and the lowest aggregate reconstruction RMSE while preserving broad image coverage. The result is not a replacement for modern learned matching or neural reconstruction systems; it is a modular front-end policy that can make classical and learned 3D pipelines more deliberate about which visual evidence they spend compute on.

</details>


### [104] [RayDer: Scalable Self-Supervised Novel View Synthesis from Real-World Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31535)
*Ulrich Prestel,Stefan Andreas Baumann,Nick Stracke,Björn Ommer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RayDer is a single-backbone transformer that unifies camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering for self-supervised NVS. It uses a minimal dynamic state as a nuisance to absorb time-varying content, enabling stable training on real videos, and exhibits power-law scaling with data and compute while achieving competitive zero-shot open-set performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling self-supervised novel view synthesis (NVS) is hindered by brittle training on realistic videos and the complexity of multi-network designs. A unified, scalable model that preserves static-scene NVS while leveraging dynamic content as supervision could improve data efficiency and generalization.

Method: A unified feed-forward transformer backbone performs camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering. A minimal dynamic state acts as a nuisance factor to absorb time-varying content, enabling stable training on unconstrained real-world video. Static-scene NVS remains the target; dynamic content provides scalable supervision rather than being reconstructed in a dynamic (4D) NVS setting. The approach emphasizes model-centric, single-model scaling and demonstrates power-law data/compute scaling across model sizes and data regimes, with evaluation on many benchmarks.

Result: RayDer shows clean power-law scaling with data and compute across model sizes and data regimes, outperforming mixtures of static-scene data, and achieving strong zero-shot open-set performance competitive with state-of-the-art supervised methods across a wide benchmark suite.

Conclusion: RayDer provides a scalable, single-model solution for self-supervised NVS by unifying vision tasks within a single backbone and leveraging a dynamic state as nuisance for stable training. It maintains static-scene NVS as the target and delivers competitive open-set generalization without reconstructing dynamic scenes.

Abstract: Self-supervised novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging to scale, despite the abundance of video data, largely due to the brittleness of training on realistic videos and the hard-to-predict scaling behavior of multi-network system designs. We introduce RayDer, a unified, feed-forward transformer that consolidates camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering into a single backbone, turning self-supervised NVS into a well-posed single-model scaling problem. A minimal dynamic state, treated as a nuisance factor, absorbs time-varying content and enables stable training on unconstrained real-world video. Importantly, RayDer keeps static-scene NVS as its target task: dynamic content is leveraged purely as scalable supervision, not reconstructed as in dynamic-scene (4D) NVS. Across multiple model sizes and orders of magnitude in data, RayDer exhibits clean power-law scaling with data and compute, and outperforms static-scene data mixtures. On a large number of benchmarks, RayDer achieves strong zero-shot open-set performance competitive with state-of-the-art supervised approaches. Project Page: https://compvis.github.io/rayder

</details>


### [105] [Automated Prediction of Postoperative Pancreatic Fistula Using Preoperative Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31539)
*Ashok Choudhary,Chris Varghese,Leo Y. Li-Han,Frank G. Lee,Ellen L. Larson,Elizabeth B. Habermann,Cornelius A. Thiels,Hojjat Salehinejad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end deep learning pipeline for preoperative POPF risk estimation from CT pancreas segmentation to classification; evaluates multiple 3D CNN architectures (CNN3D, R(2+1)D ResNet-18, ResNet-MC3-18); shows promising predictive performance and potential to guide preoperative planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Postoperative pancreatic fistula (POPF) significantly affects morbidity and costs after pancreatic surgery; reliable preoperative risk stratification from imaging could enhance decision-making and outcomes.

Method: End-to-end pipeline using preoperative CT with auto-segmented pancreas volumes; comparative evaluation of several 3D architectures: a lightweight CNN3D baseline, R(2+1)D ResNet-18, and ResNet-MC3-18; performance is assessed for POPF risk classification.

Result: Across multiple 3D architectures, the approach demonstrated promising predictive performance for POPF risk estimation, validating feasibility of an automatic pancreas-to-classification pipeline.

Conclusion: An automated, clinically useful tool and methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification that can support improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

Abstract: Postoperative pancreatic fistula (POPF) is a serious complication after pancreatic resection, increasing morbidity, hospital stay, and healthcare costs. We present an automatic, end-to-end deep learning pipeline-from pancreatic segmentation to classification-for preoperative POPF risk estimation and stratification using preoperative CT scans. A data set with auto-segmented pancreas volumes and surgical outcomes was used to evaluate multiple architectures, including a custom lightweight 3D CNN baseline (CNN3D), R(2+1)D ResNet-18, and ResNet-MC3-18 models. Evaluation across multiple 3D architectures demonstrated promising predictive performance. This approach offers a clinically valuable tool and a methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification, supporting improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

</details>


### [106] [Vision-Language Models Suppress Female Representations Under Ambiguous Input](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31556)
*Arnau Marin-Llobet,Simon Henniger,Mahzarin R. Banaji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LALS: a zero-shot metric to quantify how visual tokens in VLMs align with gender concepts in ambiguous images; reveals internal female associations that are suppressed in outputs, with a layer-wise asymmetric propagation and color cues modulating bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether model alignment for avoiding demographic bias in the face of ambiguous inputs matches internal representations, and to diagnose where biases originate within the network.

Method: Introduce Latent Association Leaning Score (LALS), projecting visual-token activations into the model's text-embedding space to measure concept associations per token and per layer in a zero-shot framework. Evaluate across 15 occupations, ~800 gender-ambiguous images, and 4 VLMs. Analyze outputs vs internal activations, perform layer-wise analysis, and color ablation.

Result: Internal representations encode female associations for ambiguous inputs, but outputs often default to male. End-to-end amplification of male signals with mid-network peaks for female signals, which are suppressed before generation. Color cues (e.g., clothing color) modulate these internal associations. Across occupations and models, outputs and internals are systematically decoupled.

Conclusion: There is a decoupling between internal concept encoding and output generation in VLMs under ambiguity. LALS provides a zero-shot diagnostic to diagnose latent biases and layer-wise propagation, highlighting the need to address mid-network suppression of certain associations and the impact of culturally loaded cues on bias in VLM alignment.

Abstract: Alignment teaches vision-language models (VLMs) to avoid expressing demographic biases, and when gender is clearly visible they largely succeed. Far less is known about ambiguous inputs (a worker in full gear, a figure seen from behind) cases common in practice yet rarely studied. We find that minimal prompting pressure exposes occupation-gender defaults when prompting ambiguous input images, with models collapsing to male even for strongly female-stereotyped occupations. But do these outputs reflect what models actually encode internally? We introduce LALS (Latent Association Leaning Score), a zero-shot metric that projects visual-token activations into the model's text-embedding space to measure concept associations per token and layer. Across 15 occupations, over 800 gender-ambiguous images, and four VLMs, internal representations and outputs are systematically decoupled: models often encode a female association internally yet output male. Layer-wise analysis reveals an asymmetric filter -- male signal amplifies end-to-end while female signal peaks mid-network and is suppressed before generation -- and a color ablation shows that culturally loaded visual cues such as clothing color further modulate these internal associations.

</details>


### [107] [EGOSTREAM: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Streaming Episodic Memory in Egocentric Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31557)
*Rosario Forte,Giuseppe Lando,Antonino Furnari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: egostream is a diagnostic benchmark for streaming episodic memory in egocentric vision, enabling controlled, recall-conditioned evaluation across multiple cognitive dimensions and time horizons; baseline experiments reveal distinct memory-management profiles and important gaps under real-time constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current streaming video benchmarks inadequately diagnose what models remember or forget over time in dynamic, real-world scenes; a diagnostic testbed is needed to measure memory capabilities and guide architecture improvements.

Method: Create 2,250 curated questions across seven cognitive dimensions (detail, spatial, temporal, event, social, causal, prospective). Introduce Answer Validity Window (AVW) to define how long an answer remains valid as the scene evolves, expanding to 8,528 recall-conditioned evaluations. Evaluate a unified streaming MLLM framework with Qwen3-VL backbone across multiple memory-management techniques (sliding windows, attention sinks, KV-cache pruning, merging, offloading).

Result: Memories exhibit distinct profiles across mechanisms; token pruning preserves fine-grained detail and temporal structure better than token merging; quantized offloading supports ultra-long-term recall; all methods under real-time constraints (>1 s per frame) with top accuracy around 45%.

Conclusion: egostream provides a diagnostic platform to reveal memory-management gaps in current architectures and to guide the development of more effective streaming episodic memory for egocentric vision.

Abstract: Continuous episodic memory is a core capability for autonomous agents operating in dynamic, real-world environments, yet current streaming video benchmarks provide limited tools for diagnosing what models remember and for how long. We introduce \egostream, a diagnostic benchmark for streaming episodic memory evaluation in egocentric vision. \egostream organizes 2,250 curated questions along seven cognitive dimensions: detail, spatial, temporal, event, social, causal, and prospective memory. We introduce the Answer Validity Window (AVW), which specifies the temporal span an answer remains valid as the observed scene evolves. This allows us to expand the questions into 8,528 recall-conditioned evaluations, enabling controlled testing from instant to ultra-long-term recall while separating genuine model forgetting from natural world-state changes. We rigorously establish baseline performance through a unified streaming MLLM framework that compares several state-of-the-art memory-management mechanisms, covering sliding windows, attention sinks, KV-cache pruning, merging, and offloading. Experiments within a unified Qwen3-VL backbone reveal that comparable aggregate accuracies mask starkly different memory profiles. For instance, token pruning preserves fine-grained details and temporal structure significantly better than token merging, while quantized offloading rescues ultra-long-term recall. Ultimately, all mechanisms operate well below real-time (>1s per frame), and top performing methods ceil at about 45\% accuracy, exposing critical gaps in current architectures. \egostream provides the diagnostic testbed needed to close these gaps.

</details>


### [108] [nuReasoning: A Reasoning-Centric Dataset and Benchmark for Long-Tail Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31572)
*Zhiyu Huang,Johnson Liu,Rui Song,Zewei Zhou,Ruining Yang,Yun Zhang,Tianhui Cai,Hanyin Zhang,Mingxuan Gao,Valeria Xu,Jiali Chen,Yishan Shen,Yiluan Guo,Tony,Qi,Jiaqi Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces nuReasoning, a large-scale real-world autonomous driving dataset and benchmark focused on reasoning in long-tail scenarios; includes 20k clips with multi-modal data and human-verified reasoning annotations, enabling both reasoning and planning evaluation; experiments show benefits of reasoning-supervised training for QA and planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AD datasets emphasize perception/prediction/planning with limited supervision for reasoning over realistic long-tail driving scenes; there is a need for datasets that ground reasoning in real-world data to improve robustness and interpretability.

Method: Create nuReasoning with 20,000 clips (20 seconds each) collected across multiple cities, featuring synchronized multi-camera images, LiDAR, HD maps, object annotations, and human-verified reasoning annotations across Spatial, Decision, and Counterfactual Reasoning. Provide support for both reasoning evaluation and planning evaluation. Conduct experiments where finetuning vision-language models (VLMs) on nuReasoning improves driving-specific question answering, and where incorporating reasoning supervision into VLA training improves planning even if textual reasoning outputs are disabled at inference.

Result: Shows substantial improvements in driving-specific QA when VLMs are finetuned on nuReasoning; incorporating reasoning supervision into VLA training boosts planning performance without relying on textual reasoning at inference. Validates nuReasoning as a foundation for evaluating and improving robust, interpretable, reasoning-driven AD in realistic long-tail scenarios.

Conclusion: nuReasoning provides a foundation for evaluating and enhancing reasoning-driven autonomous driving systems in realistic long-tail environments, enabling both reasoning-enabled QA and planning evaluation and promoting interpretable AD through explicit reasoning supervision.

Abstract: Reasoning is essential for autonomous driving (AD) in long-tail scenarios, where vehicles must apply commonsense knowledge, understand spatial relations, infer agent interactions, and make safe decisions. However, existing AD datasets and benchmarks mainly target perception, prediction, or planning, and provide limited supervision for reasoning over realistic long-tail driving scenes. We introduce nuReasoning, a large-scale real-world dataset and benchmark for reasoning-centric AD. Following the lineage of nuScenes and nuPlan, nuReasoning advances real-world AD datasets and benchmarks toward reasoning in long-tail driving scenarios. The dataset contains 20,000 clips, each 20 seconds long, collected across multiple cities, with synchronized multi-camera images, LiDAR data, HD maps, object annotations, and human-verified reasoning annotations spanning Spatial Reasoning, Decision Reasoning, and Counterfactual Reasoning. Unlike prior datasets that focus primarily on visual question answering, nuReasoning supports both reasoning evaluation and planning evaluation, enabling a direct study of how reasoning supervision affects driving performance. Experiments show that fine-tuning VLMs on nuReasoning substantially improves driving-specific question answering, while incorporating reasoning supervision into VLA training improves planning performance even when textual reasoning outputs are disabled at inference time. These results establish nuReasoning as a foundation for evaluating and improving robust, interpretable, reasoning-driven AD systems in realistic long-tail settings.

</details>


### [109] [Joint Multi-Camera LiDAR Extrinsic Calibration via Learned Pairwise Initialization and Geometric Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31576)
*Aziz Al-Najjar,Marzieh Amini,James R. Green,Felix Kwamena*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage multi-camera LiDAR extrinsic calibration: per-camera initial estimates via CMRNext, then joint multi-frame bundle adjustment with reprojection and priors to enforce global consistency; improves per-camera accuracy and inter-camera consistency, especially under domain shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-camera or pairwise calibration can yield locally accurate but globally inconsistent extrinsics in multi-camera rigs. Explicitly coupling cameras via a global optimization over multiple frames addresses rigid geometric constraints.

Method: Stage 1: Apply CMRNext independently to each camera to obtain initial extrinsics and dense 2D-3D correspondences. Stage 2: Joint refinement by multi-frame bundle adjustment using a reprojection term, per-camera priors, and relative-pose priors to enforce cross-camera consistency, converting pairwise predictions into a globally coherent calibration.

Result: On KITTI (in-domain for CMRNext): translation error 0.89 cm; rotation error 0.038. On Walkley (out-of-domain): translation error reduced from 108.6 cm to 3.1 cm, showing gains from explicit multi-camera coupling when single-camera predictions are unreliable.

Conclusion: A principled two-stage pipeline that merges learned pairwise correspondences with geometric refinement yields globally consistent extrinsics and improved per-camera accuracy, with notable robustness to domain shift.

Abstract: Most learning-based camera-LiDAR calibration methods treat each camera-LiDAR pair independently, ignoring the rigid geometric coupling in multi-camera platforms. As a result, per-camera estimates may be individually accurate yet inconsistent at the system level. We present a two-stage framework for joint multi-camera LiDAR extrinsic calibration that combines learned pairwise matching with geometric refinement. First, CMRNext is applied independently to each camera to produce initial extrinsic estimates and dense 2D-3D correspondences. These predictions are then jointly refined through a multi-frame bundle adjustment with reprojection, per-camera prior, and relative-pose prior terms. This approach converts pairwise predictions into a globally consistent multi-camera calibration. Experiments on KITTI (in-domain for CMRNext) and Walkley (out-of-domain) datasets show improved per-camera accuracy and inter-camera consistency. On KITTI, the method achieves 0.89 cm translation error and 0.038 rotation error. On Walkley, it reduces translation error from 108.6 cm to 3.1 cm, highlighting the benefit of explicit multi-camera coupling when single-camera predictions are less reliable.

</details>


### [110] [SurGe: Improved Surface Geometry in Point Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31577)
*Karim Knaebel,Gonzalo Martin Garcia,Christian Schmidt,Ilya Fradlin,Lucas Nunes,Daan de Geus,Bastian Leibe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SurGe improves local surface geometry in monocular 3D reconstruction by a point map normal metric, a depth-normalized gradient loss, and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder, achieving top global AbsRel and better local metrics across eight zero-shot benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feedforward 3D reconstruction methods predict point maps well globally but fail to capture accurate local surface geometry. Local errors are visually evident yet poorly captured by standard metrics, necessitating explicit evaluation and targeted improvements.

Method: Introduce two components: (1) point gradient matching loss that supervises depth-normalized 3D finite differences to better align local geometry, and (2) a Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and uses Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing.

Result: Across eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, SurGe achieves the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal evaluations.

Conclusion: The combination of a point map normal metric with gradient-based supervision and the NAD architecture yields improved local surface orientation and robust zero-shot generalization, indicating effective enhancement of both global and local 3D predictions.

Abstract: Recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods predict point maps and estimate global 3D geometry remarkably well. However, their predictions still exhibit inaccurate local surface geometry, which is clearly visible qualitatively but only weakly reflected in common metrics. To make these errors more explicit in evaluation, we introduce a point map normal metric that evaluates the local surface orientation induced by neighboring 3D predictions. To reduce these errors, we propose two complementary components: a point gradient matching loss that supervises depth-normalized 3D finite differences, and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and uses Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing. Across eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, our model, SurGe, achieves the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal evaluations.

</details>


### [111] [Recognizing Co-Speech Gestures in-the-Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31589)
*Sindhu B Hegde,K R Prajwal,Andrew Zisserman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GRW: a large-scale, frame-accurate dataset mapping unconstrained co-speech gestures to words across 150 action/descriptive categories; enables semantic gesture classification, word recognition, and temporal localization; provides benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the scarcity and imprecision of annotated co-speech gesture data, which limits multimodal models’ ability to link gestures to specific spoken words with temporal boundaries.

Method: Introduce Gesture Recognition in the Wild (GRW), a large, manually annotated video dataset (156,688 clips) spanning a 150-word taxonomy. It enables three tasks: (a) semantic-vs-nonsemantic gesture classification, (b) gesture-to-word recognition, and (c) temporal localization of gestures; and establishes benchmarks for these tasks.

Result: GRW provides the first large-scale, frame-accurate benchmark for mapping gestures to words and offers annotated data to train and evaluate models on three related tasks (semantic classification, word recognition, and temporal localization).

Conclusion: GRW advances co-speech gesture understanding by delivering a comprehensive, richly annotated dataset and benchmarks that enable training and evaluating models on semantic gesture mapping and precise temporal localization.

Abstract: While humans naturally gesture during speech, only a sparse subset of these movements are visually depictive and semantically linked to specific spoken words. Current multimodal models struggle to capture these semantic co-speech gestures, heavily bottlenecked by a lack of precisely annotated training data. To address this, we introduce the Gesture Recognition in the Wild (GRW) dataset, the first large-scale benchmark designed to map unconstrained human gestures to specific words with frame-accurate temporal boundaries. Comprising 156,688 manually annotated video clips, GRW spans a highly diverse 150-word taxonomy of physical actions, spatial descriptors, and abstract concepts. We leverage GRW to train video models to (a) classify gestures as semantic or not, (b) recognize the word corresponding to a co-speech gesture, and (c) temporally localize the gesture. We also use GRW to establish benchmarks for these three tasks.

</details>


### [112] [TunerDiT: Training-free Progressive Steering of Diffusion Transformer for Multi-Event Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31590)
*Ruotong Liao,Guowen Huang,Qing Cheng,Guangyao Zhai,Lei Zhang,Xun Xiao,Thomas Seidl,Daniel Cremers,Volker Tresp*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TunerDiT is a training-free method for long-horizon text-to-video generation using diffusion transformers, introducing two progressive steering handles—Event-Partitioned Masking and Cross-Event Prompt Fusion—and a Meve benchmark. It achieves state-of-the-art across 8 metrics, with a tunable trade-off between video consistency and event separation; text alignment improves with more events, suggesting good scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon T2V generation with multiple events struggles to maintain global layout while refining fine details. Existing methods often require training or struggle with event boundaries. A training-free, progressive steering approach could enable multi-event generation without retraining.

Method: Two steering handles integrated into a DiT-based diffusion pipeline: (1) Event-Partitioned Masking to enforce explicit event boundaries while allowing controlled cross-event transitions; (2) Cross-Event Prompt Fusion to inject neighboring event semantics during late stages for refinement. The method leverages intrinsic turning points in the DiT denoising trajectory where conditioning shifts from global to local features. A self-curated benchmark suite, Meve, is introduced for multi-event generation evaluation.

Result: TunerDiT achieves state-of-the-art performance across 8 metrics for multi-event T2V, offers a tunable trade-off between video consistency and event separation, and demonstrates improved text alignment as the number of events increases. It is training-free and scalable with event count.

Conclusion: Progressive, training-free steering can effectively guide long-horizon T2V generation by controlling event boundaries and semantics, with performance improving as more events are considered. The approach enables scalable multi-event generation and provides a benchmark suite (Meve) for evaluation.

Abstract: Text-to-video (T2V) generation faces challenging questions when generating videos with long horizons containing multiple events. Inspired by the intrinsics of the diffusion process, we probe video diffusion transformers (DiTs) and uncover intrinsic turning points in the DiT denoising trajectory where conditioning text affects generation from global layout to fine-grained details. Building on this finding, we present TunerDiT, a simple yet effective progressive steering method that requires no additional training for multi-event generation. TunerDiT comprises two steering handles: (1) Event-Partitioned Masking that enforces event boundaries while allowing cross-event transition bands; (2) Cross-Event Prompt Fusion that injects neighboring event semantics for late-stage refinement. We contribute a self-curated prompt suite for benchmarking multi-event generation, i.e., Meve. TunerDiT achieves state-of-the-art performance across 8 metrics and offers a tunable trade-off between video consistency and event separation, compared with other training-free methods. The improvement in text alignment increases with the event count, indicating a scaling possibility with increasing event count.

</details>


### [113] [CoFiDA-M: Concept-Aware Feature Modulation for Cross-Domain Adaptation with Image-Only Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31591)
*Nurjahan Sultana,Moi Hoon Yap,Xinqi Fan,Wenqi Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Privileged-information distillation (CoFiDA-M) uses MONET-derived concept probabilities to bias a FiLM-modulated teacher; an image-only student learns to mimic the edited feature space, yielding strong cross-dataset melanoma recall improvements over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shift between expert dermoscopic and consumer-grade images; need to exploit semantic concepts and probabilistic metadata which are unavailable at test time; address deployment paradox for image-only screening.

Method: Train a teacher network that uses MONET concept probabilities to guide a FiLM modulator to transform visual features into a semantically edited space. Train a lightweight image-only student to reproduce the edited representation, i.e., reflect clinical reasoning without access to privileged metadata at test. Distill through editing features rather than final predictions.

Result: On a challenging multi-dataset benchmark, the image-only student significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with notable gains in melanoma recall and cross-dataset robustness.

Conclusion: CoFiDA-M provides a practical, generalizable framework for leveraging probabilistic metadata as privileged information to improve deployment of image-only screening models beyond dermatology.

Abstract: Models for AI-based skin cancer screening suffer a severe performance drop when shifting from expert dermoscopic (source) images to consumer-grade clinical (target) images, hindering real-world deployment. Existing domain adaptation methods often ignore crucial semantic invariants, such as clinical concepts. While new foundation models like MONET can provide this semantic information as dense, probabilistic scores, this metadata is unavailable at test time, creating a deployment paradox for practical image-only screening tools. We address this gap by proposing CoFiDA-M, a privileged information framework that learns from concepts at training time but deploys as an image-only model. Our method trains a teacher network that uses MONET concept probabilities to guide a FiLM modulator, transforming visual features into a semantically ``edited" feature space. A lightweight, image-only student is then trained to reproduce this edited representation, not just the teacher's final predictions. This distillation ``bakes" the clinical reasoning into the student's weights. On a challenging multi-dataset benchmark, our image-only student significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, especially in melanoma recall. Our work provides a practical and generalizable framework for leveraging noisy, probabilistic metadata as privileged information, demonstrating strong cross-dataset robustness and potential for real-world deployment beyond dermatology. Implementation code is available at: https://github.com/mmu-dermatology-research/CoFiDA.git

</details>


### [114] [Lumos-Nexus: Efficient Frequency Bridging with Homogeneous Latent Space for Video Unified Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31603)
*Jiazheng Xing,Hangjie Yuan,Lingling Cai,Xinyu Liu,Yujie Wei,Fei Du,Hai Ci,Tao Feng,Jiasheng Tang,Weihua Chen,Fan Wang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lumos-Nexus presents a training-efficient unified video generation framework using a two-stage design and Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB) to couple a lightweight training generator with a high-capacity pretrained generator, achieving high fidelity and strong reasoning-driven generation; VR-Bench is introduced to assess reasoning-to-video translation. A VR-focused benchmark suite and public code/models are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational burden of training unified video generation with large high-fidelity generators while preserving reasoning-driven control and output quality.

Method: Two-stage training: (1) train a lightweight generator aligned with an understanding block to learn reasoning-driven semantic control; (2) during inference, apply Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging to transfer generation to a high-capacity pretrained generator in a shared latent space for coarse-to-fine refinement. VR-Bench is proposed to evaluate reasoning-based video generation. Extensive experiments compare visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench and reasoning performance on VR-Bench.

Result: Lumos-Nexus yields substantial improvements in visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench while maintaining strong reasoning-based generation on VR-Bench; performance gains are reported with code and models released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that efficient, two-stage training combined with latent-space bridging enables high-fidelity, reasoning-driven video generation without prohibitive compute, and the proposed VR-Bench addresses the evaluation gap for such models.

Abstract: Connector-based video unified models have demonstrated strong capability in instruction-grounded video synthesis, but integrating a large high-fidelity generator into the unified training loop is computationally prohibitive, limiting achievable visual quality. We therefore propose Lumos-Nexus, a training-efficient unified video generation framework that facilitates the development of strong reasoning-driven generation capabilities while significantly enhancing visual fidelity. Lumos-Nexus adopts a two-stage design: 1) During training, only a lightweight generator is aligned with the understanding block to learn to take in reasoning-driven semantic control. 2) During inference, we introduce Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB) to progressively hand off generation to a high-capacity pretrained generator in the shared latent space, enabling coarse-to-fine refinement and producing high-fidelity videos without compromising reasoning quality. To fill the gap in reasoning-driven video generation benchmarks, we introduce VR-Bench, which assesses a model's capability to translate inferred intent into coherent and semantically aligned video content. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Lumos-Nexus achieves substantial gains in visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench, while exhibiting strong reasoning-based generative performance on VR-Bench. Code and models are available at https://jiazheng-xing.github.io/nexus-lumos-home/.

</details>


### [115] [Learning Global Motion with Compact Gaussians for Feed-Forward 4D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31595)
*Mungyeom Kim,Minkyeong Jeon,Honggyu An,Jaewoo Jung,Hyuna Ko,Jisang Han,Hyeonseo Yu,Donghwan Shin,Sunghwan Hong,Takuya Narihira,Kazumi Fukuda,Yuki Mitsufuji,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A feed-forward 4D reconstruction framework (C4G) for monocular dynamic scenes using timestamp-conditioned Gaussian query tokens, with diffusion-based rendering and 4D feature lifting, achieving strong novel-view synthesis without camera poses and with fewer Gaussians.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feed-forward dynamic scene methods suffer from per-frame 3D Gaussian predictions that cause duplicate Gaussians and view-dependent biases, hindering coherent motion learning; need compact, pose-free 4D representation with robust motion modeling.

Method: C4G introduces timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian query tokens; each token aggregates features over the full temporal context and decodes a 3D Gaussian whose position is modulated by the target timestamp. A video diffusion-based rendering enhancement module improves details. Feature aggregation into Gaussians is extended to 4D feature lifting for point tracking and dynamic scene understanding, all without per-scene optimization or camera poses.

Result: Demonstrates strong novel-view synthesis with significantly fewer Gaussians, robustness to large temporal gaps, and improved motion modeling; no camera poses required; diffusion-based rendering enhances detail.

Conclusion: C4G provides a compact, pose-free 4D reconstruction framework for dynamic scenes with strong motion modeling and efficient representation, aided by diffusion-based rendering and 4D feature lifting.

Abstract: Dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. Existing feed-forward methods predict 3D Gaussians pixel-wise for each frame, suffering from duplicated Gaussians and view-dependent biases that hinder effective learning of scene motion. We present C4G, a feed-forward 4D reconstruction framework built upon a compact set of timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian query tokens. Each token aggregates corresponding features across the full temporal context and decodes a 3D Gaussian whose position is modulated by the target timestamp, enabling globally coherent motion modeling without per-scene optimization. To capture fine-grained details, we further introduce a video diffusion model-based rendering enhancement module. Since our framework effectively aggregates features into Gaussians, we extend this capability to feature lifting, producing a 4D feature field that supports point tracking and dynamic scene understanding. C4G achieves strong novel-view synthesis performance using significantly fewer Gaussians and without requiring camera poses, while exhibiting stronger motion modeling and robustness to large temporal gaps.

</details>


### [116] [KLIP: localized distribution shift detection via KL-divergence with diffusion priors in Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31596)
*Alireza Kheirandish,Jihoon Hong,Sara Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KLIP: a calibration-free OOD metric for diffusion-based inverse problems using KL divergence between the diffusion prior and posterior; detects global and local distribution shifts without calibration data, demonstrated on liver CT healthy vs tumor, generalizes across models, datasets, and inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models provide data-driven priors for computational imaging and show some OOD capabilities, but existing methods rely on shifted-distribution knowledge, miss subtle/local shifts, and operate on full images rather than indirect measurements. A calibration-free, localized OOD detector for inverse problems is needed.

Method: Introduce an OOD score defined as the KL divergence between the diffusion prior and the posterior given measurements. Compute this score globally and per patch to localize OOD regions, enabling detection on whole images and patch-level localization without calibration data.

Result: Experiments show detection of subtle, semantically meaningful shifts (e.g., healthy vs tumor liver CT), and generalization across diffusion models, datasets, and inverse problems. Code is available at the referenced GitHub repository.

Conclusion: KLIP provides a calibration-free, robust OOD detector for diffusion-prior-based inverse problems, capable of whole-image and localized OOD detection with cross-domain generalization.

Abstract: Diffusion models have shown promising performance as data-driven priors for computational imaging, as well as some capacity to detect out-of-distribution (OOD) images. However, existing approaches to OOD detection often require some knowledge of the shifted distribution, fail to detect subtle or localized distribution shifts, and operate on full images, rather than the indirect measurements available in inverse problems. We propose an OOD detection metric based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the diffusion prior and the posterior distribution, that (i) does not require any calibration data or knowledge of the shifted distribution, and (ii) can detect whole images as OOD as well as localize OOD patches within an image. Experimentally, we show that this metric can detect subtle yet semantically meaningful distribution shifts, such as the shift from healthy liver CT scans to those with tumors, and generalizes across different types of diffusion models, datasets, and inverse problems. Our code can be found at https://github.com/voilalab/KLIP.

</details>


### [117] [SOCO: Benchmarking Semantic Object Correspondence in Vision Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31597)
*Olaf Dünkel,Basavaraj Sunagad,Haoran Wang,David T. Hoffmann,Christian Theobalt,Adam Kortylewski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SOCO is a new semantic object correspondence benchmark spanning 100 categories with 1M correspondence pairs, featuring a taxonomy of correspondence types, consistent keypoint annotations, and keypoint language descriptions to evaluate vision and multimodal models. It enables LVLM evaluation and correlates with dense downstream tasks, revealing gaps in existing backbones and language-guided localization capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a unified, scalable, part-level evaluation for semantic object correspondence in vision foundation models, enabling systematic assessment and cross-model comparison.

Method: Introduce SOCO: a taxonomy of correspondence types; 100 object categories; over 1M correspondence pairs; consistent, functional keypoint annotations; natural language descriptions for keypoints; standardized evaluation protocols for visual and multimodal models; public benchmark for structured object understanding.

Result: Comprehensive experiments show (i) vision backbones encode semantic structure but transfer of correspondences across related categories and precise part-position encoding are limited; (ii) LVLMs excel at text-prompted part localization but underperform in cross-image visual-reference matching; (iii) correspondence quality correlates more strongly with dense downstream tasks (segmentation, tracking, 3D pose/detection) than with ImageNet classification.

Conclusion: SOCO provides a principled benchmark for evaluating structured, part-level representations in vision and multimodal models, guiding development toward models with stronger object-part understanding and transferability.

Abstract: Measuring structured object understanding in vision foundation models remains challenging due to inconsistent evaluation protocols and limited part-level supervision. Semantic correspondence (SC) evaluates this capability by testing whether object parts can be matched across instances and categories under large variations in appearance, viewpoint, and geometry. To enable a systematic SC evaluation, we introduce SOCO, a new benchmark for Semantic Object Correspondence that introduces a taxonomy of correspondence types and provides consistent, functionally meaningful keypoint annotations across 100 categories and over 1M correspondence pairs. In addition, SOCO includes keypoint language descriptions, enabling the evaluation of large vision-language models (LVLMs) and their fine-grained part-level understanding. Comprehensive experiments reveal that (i) vision foundation backbones encode strong semantic structure but transfer correspondences poorly across related categories and only partially capture object-part position, (ii) LVLMs are stronger at text-prompted part localization than at visual-reference cross-image matching, exposing a gap between language-grounded localization and fine-grained visual correspondence, and (iii) correspondence performance predicts performance on dense downstream tasks, including segmentation, tracking, 3D pose estimation, and 3D detection, more strongly than ImageNet classification. Together, these findings position SOCO as a benchmark for structured, part-level representation quality in vision and multimodal foundation models.

</details>


### [118] [Linear Scaling Video VLMs for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31598)
*Cristobal Eyzaguirre,Jiajun Wu,Juan Carlos Niebles*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StateKV enables linear-time video prefill for long-video VLMs by maintaining a fixed-capacity, importance-based recurrent state for cross-frame context and a separate per-frame cache for decoding, avoiding full retraining or architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video encoders rely on quadratic spatiotemporal self-attention, causing high compute/latency for long videos; existing efficiency methods often sacrifice accuracy through frame/token dropping or coarse attention approximations.

Method: Inference-time adaptation of pretrained long-video VLMs using a fixed-capacity recurrent state to carry cross-frame context, plus a full per-frame cache used during decoding; no fine-tuning or architecture changes required.

Result: Across three long-video benchmarks and seven models across three families, StateKV stays close to full self-attention, outperforms sliding-window/recency-based streaming methods, and reduces video-prefill FLOPs, enabling larger models within a fixed compute budget.

Conclusion: A practical, scalable approach toward long-video understanding that preserves accuracy while achieving linear-time prefill and enabling use of larger models without retraining.

Abstract: Video vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used in long-horizon and streaming settings, yet most video encoders still rely on spatiotemporal self-attention, causing compute and latency to grow quadratically with the number of frames. Existing efficiency methods improve scalability but often lose accuracy relative to full self-attention, for example through aggressive frame/token dropping or coarse attention approximations. We introduce StateKV, an inference-time method that adapts pretrained long-video VLMs to linear-time video prefill by carrying cross-frame context in a fixed-capacity, importance-based recurrent state, paired with a second full per-frame cache used for decoding. Across three long-video benchmarks and seven models spanning three families and multiple scales, StateKV remains close to full self-attention and consistently outperforms dominant sliding-window / recency-based streaming approximations, without fine-tuning or architectural changes. StateKV also reduces video-prefill cost measured FLOPs, enabling stronger accuracy at a fixed compute budget by running larger models. These results suggest a practical step toward scalable long-video understanding.

</details>


### [119] [Representation Forcing for Bottleneck-Free Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31604)
*Yuqing Wang,Zhijie Lin,Ceyuan Yang,Yang Zhao,Fei Xiao,Hao He,Qi Zhao,Zihan Ding,Fuyun Wang,Shuai Wang,Youliang Zhang,Haoqi Fan,Xihui Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Represents a bottleneck-free, end-to-end approach for unified multimodal models by conditioning generation on native visual representations predicted as intermediate tokens, removing the need for external generative latent spaces; achieves competitive image generation and superior understanding vs VAE-based variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottleneck and misalignment introduced by relying on a frozen pretrained VAE for image generation in unified multimodal models, and push toward end-to-end training that jointly handles perception and generation.

Method: Introduce Representation Forcing (RF): the decoder autoregressively predicts visual representations as intermediate tokens before pixels; these tokens stay in context to guide pixel diffusion within the same backbone; uses perception outputs as generation targets to eliminate external latent spaces.

Result: For image generation, the pixel-space model with RF matches state-of-the-art VAE-based unified models; for image understanding, RF generally outperforms its VAE-based variant.

Conclusion: RF enables end-to-end, bottleneck-free unified multimodal models, delivering competitive generation and improved understanding by internalizing the latent representation prediction within the model.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) aim to handle perception and generation in a single model. Yet existing UMMs still rely on a frozen, separately pretrained VAE for image generation, imposing a structural bottleneck. Naively removing it introduces a quality gap, as the model must learn both high-level structure and low-level details from raw pixels. In this paper, we propose Representation Forcing (RF), a technique that closes this gap by making representation prediction a native capability of the model. Concretely, RF forces the decoder to autoregressively predict visual representations as intermediate tokens before pixels; these tokens then stay in context to guide pixel diffusion within the same backbone. By turning representations from perception outputs into generation targets, RF eliminates the need for any external generative latent space. We find that RF benefits both understanding and generation. On image generation, our pixel-space model with RF matches state-of-the-art VAE-based unified models. On image understanding, pixel-space RF generally outperforms its VAE-based variant. Together, these results offer an effective step toward end-to-end, bottleneck-free UMMs.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [120] [PhyDrawGen: Physically Grounded Diagram Generation from Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30512)
*Nafiul Haque,Syed Nazmus Sakib,Shifat E Arman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic pipeline, PhyDrawGen, generates physics-consistent diagrams by separating semantic scene understanding from physical constraint satisfaction and using a PSLG solver plus a visually grounded verifier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current image-based physics diagram generation yields hallucinated forces, violates conservation and geometric constraints; need enforceable physics.

Method: LLM extracts typed scene graph from text; deterministic PSLG solver encodes force balance, optical paths, and field topologies as exact geometric primitives; fine-tuned Qwen-VL implements a propose-verify loop to correct violations.

Result: On 1,449 problems across mechanics, optics, EM, PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 3 Pro; robust accuracy even for unusual objects.

Conclusion: A decoupled semantic-constraint approach with a neuro-symbolic pipeline improves physical correctness in diagrams and generalizes across problem domains.

Abstract: Generating physics diagrams from text requires strict adherence to physical laws. While current generative models produce visually plausible outputs, they systematically hallucinate force vectors, ignore conservation laws, and violate geometric constraints. We present PhyDrawGen, a neuro-symbolic pipeline that decouples semantic scene understanding from physical constraint satisfaction. First, a large language model extracts a typed scene graph from the problem text. A deterministic solver then converts this graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG), encoding force balance, optical paths, and field topologies as exact geometric primitives. Finally, a fine-tuned Qwen-VL model implements a visually grounded propose-verify loop to iteratively correct any constraint violations. Evaluated on a benchmark of 1,449 problems spanning mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism, PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro, demonstrating robust physical accuracy even on unusual-object problems.

</details>


### [121] [Physically Viable World Models: A Case for Query-Conditioned Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30542)
*Adam J. Thorpe,Stepan Tretiakov,Cheng-Hsi Hsiao,Su Ann Low,Xingjian Li,Hassan Iqbal,Neel P. Bhatt,Ufuk Topcu,Krishna Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Argues for physically grounded, modular world models with an orchestrator to preserve interventional semantics; shows limitations of purely observation-predictive models and outlines a design principle (simplest adequate abstraction) and feasibility tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observation-predictive world models can be visually plausible but physically incorrect under interventions; need models that capture causal structure and are verifiable/auditable for safe planning and control.

Method: Introduce controlled benchmarks that fix visible scenes while varying latent physics; propose a modular architecture (environment representation, latent state/parameters, actions, interventional dynamics, query response) and a dynamic orchestrator that composes components per query; allow transition models to be analytic, simulated, learned, or hybrid; provide a feasibility test and design guidance.

Result: Demonstrates that existing systems fail on queries requiring correct interventional reasoning; shows that modular, auditable abstractions enable correct answers and safer planning; outlines how to assemble/adapt physically viable models for planning, control, and verification.

Conclusion: The right abstraction is the simplest one that preserves query-relevant distinctions, not the most detailed world; the paper offers a design principle and a feasibility test, plus a roadmap for building orchestrated, physically viable world models.

Abstract: World models for embodied AI must be physically viable: constructed to answer intervention queries by representing the physical structure governing action outcomes, rather than merely predicting future observations. Existing observation-predictive world models can produce visually plausible but physically wrong rollouts. This failure is structural; distinct physical systems can look identical yet diverge under intervention. We expose this problem with controlled benchmarks that fix the visible scene while varying latent physics. We show that such models may recommend infeasible actions, mispredict interaction outcomes, or certify unsafe behavior. We argue that embodied AI requires world models that identify the simplest physical abstraction sufficient to answer an intervention query. Such a model comprises modular components, including environment representation, latent state and parameter estimation, action specification, interventional dynamics, and query-level response. An autonomous orchestrator should identify the relevant abstraction and compose compatible learned and structured components per query. When closed-form physics is unavailable, uncertain, or costly, the transition model may be analytic, simulated, learned, or hybrid, but it must preserve the structure that determines interventional outcomes. This decomposition makes the model interpretable, its components verifiable, and its outputs auditable against the query. It also provides a design principle for new world models and a feasibility test for existing ones: the right abstraction is not the most detailed model of the world, but the simplest model that preserves the distinctions relevant to the query. We demonstrate this approach on queries that existing systems fail to answer correctly, and outline how an orchestrator can dynamically assemble and adapt physically viable models for planning, control, and verification.

</details>


### [122] [Transforming and Encoding FTS for SAT Solving: What Helps, What Hurts (Extended Version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30563)
*João Filipe,Álvaro Torralba,Gregor Behnke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes SAT-based encodings for factored planning tasks (SAS+-like with disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and angelic nondeterminism), examining multiple encoding strategies, parallelism opportunities, and how common task transformations affect SAT-based planner performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend the reach of SAT-based planning to factored tasks, enabling compact representations and leveraging SAT solvers and parallelism to tackle planning problems that include disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and nondeterminism.

Method: Develop several propositional encodings of the factored transition relation; analyze different translation strategies from factored tasks to SAT formulas; explore parallelism at various levels (e.g., within SAT solving, across encodings, or in problem decomposition); study how typical task transformations influence solver performance.

Result: Introduces multiple SAT encodings for factored tasks, analyzes parallelism opportunities, and investigates how common task transformations impact the performance of SAT-based planners.

Conclusion: SAT-based planning for factored tasks is feasible with carefully chosen encodings; exploiting parallelism and transformation-aware encoding choices can significantly affect planning performance, guiding the design of practical SAT-based planners for advanced task representations.

Abstract: Factored tasks are a classical planning representation that extends SAS+ with limited forms of disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and angelic nondeterminism. This allows for a more compact representation of tasks than traditional formalisms such as STRIPS or SAS+, and supports a wide range of task transformations. However, existing planning approaches for factored tasks have been limited to heuristic search methods.
  In this work, we investigate how to encode factored tasks in SAT. We propose several ways to encode the tasks, focusing on different strategies for translating the factored transition relation into propositional logic. We also analyze how to exploit parallelism at various levels in this setting and study the impact of common task transformations on the performance of SAT-based planners.

</details>


### [123] [Procedural Generation of First Person Shooter Maps using Map-Elites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30570)
*Simone de Donato,Pier Luca Lanzi,Daniele Loiacono*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAP-Elites with new FPS map representations (Point-Line, Spatial-Layout) outperforms traditional All-Black/Grid-Graph in diversity and quality for FPS map design, using metrics for topology and emergent gameplay, via MAP-Elites with Sliding Boundaries (MESB).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aim to apply quality-diversity search to FPS map design and identify map features that best guide illumination; address limited representational expressivity by introducing two novel representations to capture spatial layouts.

Method: Define four map representations (two existing, two novel). Propose a set of topology-only and emergent gameplay metrics. Use MAP-Elites with Sliding Boundaries (MESB) to evolve populations of maps across feature spaces. Compare representations in terms of diversity and quality of evolved maps.

Result: The new representations (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout) produce maps with higher diversity and higher quality than the traditional representations for evolving FPS maps.

Conclusion: Novel FPS map representations improve MAP-Elites illumination and diversity-quality tradeoffs, suggesting more expressive encodings enable better exploration of the design space; future work could explore additional metrics and scalability.

Abstract: We investigate the application of MAP-Elites (a well-known quality diversity algorithm) to design levels for First-Person Shooter (FPS) games. We consider two well-known map representations (All-Black and Grid-Graph) and introduce two novel representations (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout) that improve the characterization of FPS maps. We define a series of metrics to describe maps' topological properties (which solely depend on maps' layout), and emergent properties (which must be evaluated through actual gameplay). We perform an in-depth analysis to identify the most suitable features to guide MAP-Elites illumination process. We apply MAP-Elites with Sliding Boundaries (MESB) to evolve populations of FPS maps. Our results show that the new representations can generate maps with higher diversity and quality than the representations previously used for evolving FPS maps.

</details>


### [124] [Uncertainty-Aware and Temporally Regulated Expert Advice in Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30576)
*Ahmed Abouelazm,Felix Klingebiel,Philip Schörner,J. Marius Zöllner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An uncertainty-aware, expert-guided exploration framework for sensor-based RL in autonomous driving, using adaptive uncertainty thresholds, commitment-cooldown, and stochastic early-stop. It uses a shared replay buffer with IQN backbone and expert trajectories; CARLA results show 5-7% higher success and fewer failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploration in RL for autonomous driving is inherently unsafe; need to learn novel behaviors while avoiding collisions; rely on expert advice to guide exploration adaptively without long-term dependence.

Method: Trigger expert guidance when epistemic or aleatoric uncertainty exceeds adaptive thresholds derived from rolling buffers; use commitment-cooldown with stochastic early-stop to regulate guidance duration/frequency; combine expert and agent data in a shared replay buffer; train with an off-policy implicit quantile network (IQN); evaluate on sensor-based edge cases like unsignalized intersections.

Result: In CARLA, method outperforms IQN baseline, increasing success by 5-7% and reducing failures; demonstrates risk-sensitive uncertainty with regulated expert integration improves safe/exploration efficiency.

Conclusion: Risk-aware, regulatory expert integration can enable safer, more efficient exploration for sensor-based RL in autonomous driving scenarios like unsignalized intersections.

Abstract: Exploration in reinforcement learning for autonomous driving is inherently unsafe: agents must experience novel behaviors to learn, yet exploration can lead to collisions or off-road driving. We propose an uncertainty-aware framework that leverages expert advice to guide exploration while avoiding long-term dependence. Advice is triggered when epistemic or aleatoric uncertainty exceeds adaptive thresholds derived from rolling buffers, ensuring advice evolves with the agent's confidence. A commitment-cooldown strategy with a stochastic early-stop heuristic regulates the duration and frequency of guidance, exposing the agent to coherent maneuvers without exhausting the advice budget. Expert and agent experiences are combined in a shared replay buffer within an off-policy implicit quantile network (IQN) backbone, enabling efficient reuse of expert trajectories. Experiments in CARLA show that our method outperforms the IQN baseline, improving success by 5-7% and reducing failures, demonstrating that risk-sensitive uncertainty coupled with regulated expert integration enables safer and more efficient exploration for sensor-based RL policy learning in unsignalized intersection navigation.

</details>


### [125] [Harness Updating Is Not Harness Benefit: Disentangling Evolution Capabilities in Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30621)
*Minhua Lin,Juncheng Wu,Zijun Wang,Zhan Shi,Yisi Sang,Bing He,Zewen Liu,Tianxin Wei,Zongyu Wu,Zhiwei Zhang,Dakuo Wang,Xiang Zhang,Benoit Dumoulin,Cihang Xie,Yuyin Zhou,Suhang Wang,Hanqing Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Harness self-evolution updates to external harnesses show similar gains across base models (flat updating) and non-monotonic benefit with base capability (mid-tier models benefit most). Weak models struggle to activate/follow harness updates. Implication: focus on improving the task-solving agent and harness invocation rather than evo-reriding base models; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how a model's base task-solving capability relates to the usefulness of harness self-evolution updates, addressing whether more capable models derive greater benefits from updated harnesses.

Method: Evaluate two capabilities: (i) harness-updating — ability to produce useful persistent harness updates from execution evidence; (ii) harness-benefit — ability to benefit from updated harnesses during task solving. Compare across models of varying base capabilities, include experiments showing that updating gains are similar across tiers and that benefit is strongest at mid-tier. Analyze failure modes for weak-tier models (not activating or not following harness artifacts).

Result: Two key findings: (1) Harness-updating is flat in base capability; models from different capability tiers produce harness updates yielding similar gains (e.g., Qwen3.5-9B vs Claude Opus 4.6). (2) Harness-benefit is non-monotonic in base capability: weak-tier gain little, mid-tier gains peak, strong-tier gains are smaller than mid-tier. Identified failure modes: weak-tier models may fail to activate harness artifacts or fail to follow them faithfully.

Conclusion: Invest in improving the task-solving agent and harness invocation/long-horizon instruction following, rather than solely expanding the evolver capability. Target harness invocation mechanics and long-horizon instruction following during agent training. Source code available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as systems built around editable external harnesses, including prompts, skills, memories and tools, that shape task execution without changing model parameters. Harness self-evolution adapts such agents by updating these harnesses from execution evidence. Yet it remains unclear whether a model's base capability in task-solving predicts its capabilities in harness self-evolution: which models produce useful harness updates, and which actually benefit from them? We analyze two harness self-evolution capabilities: (i) harness-updating, the capability to produce useful persistent harness updates from execution evidence; (ii) harness-benefit, the capability to benefit from updated harnesses during task solving. Our analysis reveals two findings. First, harness-updating is flat in base capability: models from different capability tiers produce harness updates that lead to surprisingly similar gains; even Qwen3.5-9B's updates yield gains comparable to those of Claude Opus~4.6. Second, harness-benefit is non-monotonic in base capability: weak-tier models benefit little from updated harnesses, mid-tier models benefit most, and strong-tier models benefit less than mid-tier. We trace low gains at the weak tier to two failure modes: weak-tier models may fail to activate relevant harness artifacts, or activate them but fail to follow them faithfully. These findings suggest investing capability budget in the task-solving agent rather than the evolver, and targeting harness invocation and long-horizon instruction following in agent training. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/A-EVO-Lab/a-evolve/tree/release/harness-evolution.

</details>


### [126] [EHRBench: An Automated and Reliable EHR-based Benchmark for Clinical Decision Making with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30637)
*Yuzhang Xie,Keqi Han,Yunpeng Xiao,Hejie Cui,Guanchen Wu,Ziyang Zhang,Kai Shu,Jiaying Lu,Xiao Hu,Carl Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A scalable, automated EHR-grounded benchmark (EHRBench) for evaluating LLM-driven clinical decision-making, built via an EHR-LLM-KB pipeline that yields ~960k QA items across diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis; includes KB-based verification to reduce hallucinations; benchmarks 30+ LLMs and analyzes performance and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the need for reliable, scalable evaluation of LLM-based clinical decision support using real EHR data, bridging gaps in existing benchmarks by grounding tasks in authentic clinical trajectories and knowledge bases.

Method: Automated pipeline converting encounter-level EHR trajectories into structured QA templates, deterministically instantiating templates into QA items; KB-based verification and enrichment to filter hallucinated/ambiguous relations; evaluation across >30 LLMs; analysis of performance and robustness.

Result: Constructed 960,067 QA items across three inference tasks (diagnosis, treatment, prognosis); benchmarked >30 LLMs; provided detailed analyses of performance and robustness; results show consistent capability trends and highlight actionable gaps.

Conclusion: EHRBench offers a scalable, reliable, EHR-grounded benchmark for evaluating LLM-based clinical decision-making and helps identify gaps toward clinically reliable LLM systems.

Abstract: Clinical decision-making (CDM) is central to real-world clinical workflows, where clinicians infer diagnoses, select treatments, or anticipate future health outcomes under incomplete evidence. LLMs are increasingly used to support these decisions due to strong language capabilities, broad biomedical knowledge, and efficiency, yet the reliability of LLMs on real-world clinical decision tasks remains insufficiently understood. To evaluate CDM models, especially LLM-based models, an ideal and practical medical decision benchmark should be constructed via an automated yet reliable pipeline to ensure both scale and quality. Moreover, the grounding of a CDM benchmark in real patient EHRs can better support evaluation on practical CDM tasks that require substantive biomedical knowledge and clinical inference. To fill the gaps, we introduce EHRBench, an automated and reliable EHR-grounded benchmark for evaluating LLM-based clinical decision-making at scale. To ensure scalability and reliability, EHRBench is constructed through an EHR-LLM-KB(knowledge-base) interaction pipeline. For efficiency, we use a specialized LLM to automatically convert encounter-level EHR trajectories into structured templates and deterministically instantiate the templates into QA items. In parallel, we apply systematic KB-based verification and enrichment to filter hallucinated or ambiguous relations and to improve reliability. Using this pipeline, we construct nearly 1M (960,067) QA items spanning three core inference-required clinical decision tasks: diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis. We benchmark more than 30 representative LLMs on EHRBench and provide detailed analyses of performance and robustness. The results show consistent capability trends across settings, further validating the reliability of EHRBench and highlighting actionable gaps toward clinically reliable LLM systems.

</details>


### [127] [Structure-Induced Information for Rerooting Levin Tree Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30664)
*Jake Tuero,Michael Buro,Laurent Orseau,Levi H. S. Lelis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Learned 'rerooter' via sqrt-LTS to implicitly decompose tasks, enabling scalable subgoal-free search with three designs and improved training efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subgoal-based policy tree search is effective but relies on explicit subgoal generation, which incurs significant overhead and hurts scalability. A learned rerooter can decompose problems into soft subtasks without explicit subgoal reconstruction, reducing overhead.

Method: Introduce three rerooter designs integrated with the sqrt-LTS framework: (i) clustering-based rerooter exploiting global state-space structure, (ii) heuristic-based rerooter using learned cost-to-go estimates, (iii) a hybrid combining both signals. The approach avoids explicit subgoal reconstruction and guides search through soft subtasks.

Result: Empirically, the rerooting-based methods scale to complex environments where subgoal-based policy tree search fails and achieve state-of-the-art online training efficiency on the tested domains.

Conclusion: The rerooter framework offers a scalable, low-overhead alternative to explicit subgoal generation, with flexible designs that balance structure and learned signals, broadening applicability to complex problems.

Abstract: Subgoal-based policy tree search, which uses a policy to guide search, is effective for complex single-agent deterministic problems but often relies on explicit subgoal generation that can incur substantial overhead and hinders scalability. In this paper, we overcome these limitations by using a learned ``rerooter'' through the recently-introduced $\sqrt{\text{LTS}}$ algorithm. A rerooter implicitly decomposes the problem into soft subtasks. While previous work focused on the formal guarantees for given or handcrafted rerooters, in this work we propose three rerooter designs: (i) a clustering-based rerooter that exploits global state-space structure, (ii) a heuristic-based rerooter that leverages learned cost-to-go estimates, and (iii) a hybrid that combines both signals. Our framework avoids having to explicitly reconstruct and reason over generated subgoals, thereby enabling scalable allocation of search effort with significantly lower computational overhead. Empirically, our rerooting-based methods scale to complex environments where subgoal-based policy tree search fails, and achieve state-of-the-art online training efficiency on the domains tested.

</details>


### [128] [Healthcare Mechanisms from Policy-as-Code Search under Strategic Provider Response](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30680)
*Zihan Wang,Xiang Xu,Hongyuan Zha,Wenhao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reframes hospital mechanism design as program synthesis for LLMs; uses Medi-Sim to reveal incentive-induced equilibria; identifies classic distortions and how an LLM-guided mixed-objective program mitigates them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes limitations of fixed-provider responses in healthcare AI benchmarks, enabling evaluation of mechanism-induced equilibria and design of robust hospital incentive structures.

Method: Define typed, inspectable rule programs controlling provider channels (coding, selection, delay, effort, triage); execute in Medi-Sim with five channels; perform incentive sweep; implement audit lever; use LLM-guided evolutionary code search over rule-program space to synthesize a mixed-objective program.

Result: Incentive sweep recovers up-coding, low-complexity selection under profit pressure, and Goodhart drift; an audit lever shows pressure migration (closing coding more than doubles low-complexity selection); synthesized mixed-objective program eliminates up-coding, halves rejection, preserves most of baseline profit.

Conclusion: Program-synthesis-based mechanism design with simulators enables transparent, inspectable governance of provider incentives and can produce reward structures that resist strategic manipulation while maintaining financial viability.

Abstract: Healthcare mechanisms are inseparable from the strategic provider response they induce: existing healthcare AI benchmarks hold this response fixed and so cannot evaluate mechanisms by the equilibrium they produce. We recast hospital mechanism design as program synthesis for language models: typed, inspectable rule programs are executed and scored by Medi-Sim, a multi-agent simulator with five strategic provider channels (coding, selection, delay, effort, triage).
