An incentive sweep recovers classical health-economics findings as adjacent regimes -- up-coding and low-complexity-patient selection under profit pressure, and Goodhart-style drift where measured performance becomes anti-correlated with true outcomes -- and a single audit lever exposes pressure migration: closing the coding channel more than doubles low-complexity selection. LLM-guided evolutionary code search over the same rule-program space then synthesizes an inspectable mixed-objective program that eliminates up-coding, halves rejection, and retains most of the profit-oriented baseline's funds.

</details>


### [129] [MAVEN: Improving Generalization in Agentic Tool Calling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30738)
*Omkar Ghugarkar,Vishvesh Bhat,Muhammad Ahmed Mohsin,Asad Aali*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAVEN introduces a modular symbolic scaffold (Modular Agentic Verification and Execution Network) for structured decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification—achieving strong results on multiple tool-calling benchmarks and enabling scalable, cost-efficient reasoning with open-weight models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization gaps in agentic tool usage: current LLMs struggle with composing reasoning strategies, preserving intermediate states, and coordinating tools across domains. A lightweight, verification-centered scaffold could improve reliability and interpretability of multi-step reasoning.

Method: Propose MAVEN architecture: a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold enabling structured decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification. Evaluate on BFCL v3, TauBench, Tau2Bench, AceBench, and introduce MAVEN-Bench as a stress-test for multi-step mathematical/physical reasoning with explicit verification and adversarial task composition.

Result: MAVEN reveals a sizable gap between partial reasoning quality and end-to-end task success. In MAVEN-Bench, GPT-OSS-120b base model accuracy improves from 48% to 71% without additional training. MAVEN is competitive with frontier proprietary baselines while using an open-weight backbone at an estimated cost ratio of ~1/10.

Conclusion: Verification-centered, modular scaffolds can strengthen compositional reasoning and enable more process-aware evaluation of agents in the wild, suggesting a path toward more efficient, reliable agentic systems.

Abstract: Generalization across agentic tool-calling environments remains a central challenge for reliable agentic reasoning systems. Although large language models achieve strong results on individual benchmarks, their ability to compose reasoning strategies, preserve intermediate states, and coordinate tools across domains remains underexplored. We present MAVEN (Modular Agentic Verification and Execution Network), a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold for structured decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification. We evaluate MAVEN across established tool-calling benchmarks, including BFCL v3, TauBench, Tau2Bench, AceBench, and introduce MAVEN-Bench, a stress-test benchmark for multi-step mathematical and physical reasoning with explicit verification and adversarial task composition. MAVEN-Bench exposes a substantial gap between partial reasoning quality and end-to-end task success; in direct MAVEN-Bench runs, MAVEN improves its GPT-OSS-120b base model from 48% to 71% accuracy without additional training. It also remains competitive with frontier proprietary baselines while using an open-weight backbone with an estimated cost ratio of roughly 1/10, suggesting that lightweight verification-centered scaffolds can strengthen compositional reasoning and motivate more process-aware evaluation of agents in the wild.

</details>


### [130] [Generating Graph-like Rules for Knowledge Graph Reasoning via Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30747)
*Haoxiang Cheng,Yunfei Wang,Chao Chen,Kewei Cheng,Zhipeng Lin,Haoxuan Li,Changjun Fan,Shixuan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRiD reframes graph-like rule mining as a discrete generative process conditioned on the target relation; it uses supervised pre-training on KG meta-graph substructures, followed by reinforcement learning optimized by non-differentiable rule quality metrics; achieves competitive KG completion and shows graph-like rules complement chain-like rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rule mining focuses on chain-like rules and suffers from combinatorial search space and limited exploitation of graph-like structures. Generative models struggle due to objective misalignment and non-differentiable metrics. GRiD targets graph-like rule discovery with a discrete generative process and metric-guided RL.

Method: Two-phase training: (1) supervised pre-training to capture structural priors from subgraphs sampled from the KG meta-graph; (2) reinforcement learning with policy gradient to fine-tune using non-differentiable rule-quality metrics, directly optimizing the target objective.

Result: On six benchmark datasets, GRiD achieves competitive performance on KG completion. Ablation studies show efficiency and robustness; graph-like rules complement chain-like rules. Code and datasets are publicly available.

Conclusion: GRiD effectively enables graph-like rule discovery in knowledge graphs through a discrete generative process with metric-guided RL, enhancing rule coverage and synergy with chain-like rules; it offers a scalable approach with reproducible results.

Abstract: Logical rules constitute a cornerstone of knowledge graph (KG) reasoning, valued for their interpretability and ability to model relational patterns. However, existing rule mining methods predominantly focus on simple chain-like rules and therefore neglect the richer relational information encoded in graph-like structures, such as cycles and branches. This limitation is further exacerbated by computational bottlenecks caused by the combinatorial explosion of the search space, which is especially challenging for graph-like rules. Meanwhile, generative approaches such as diffusion models, despite their success in other domains, can not be directly applied to rule mining because their training objectives are not aligned with the goal of learning high-quality rules, and non-differentiable KG rule quality metrics cannot directly guide model optimization. To address these limitations, we propose GRiD, a framework that reformulates graph-like rule discovery as a discrete generative process conditioned on the target relation. GRiD employs a two-phase training strategy. First, supervised pre-training enables GRiD to capture structural priors from subgraphs sampled from the KG meta-graph. Subsequently, reinforcement learning is applied to fine-tune GRiD through policy gradient optimization guided directly by non-differentiable rule-quality metrics. Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that GRiD achieves competitive performance on KG completion tasks. Ablation studies confirm the efficiency and robustness of GRiD and further show that graph-like rules complement chain-like rules in KG completion. Our codes and datasets are available in https://github.com/Haoxiang-Cheng/GRiD

</details>


### [131] [Learning Agent-Compatible Context Management for Long-Horizon Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30785)
*Lu Yi,Runlin Lei,Liuyi Yao,Yuexiang Xie,Yuyang Li,Wenhao Zhang,Zhewei Wei,Yaliang Li,Jian-Yun Nie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AdaCoM trains an external LLM to manage the context of a frozen agent via flexible modification actions and reinforcement learning, improving long-horizon tasks by preserving task constraints while pruning stale content; reveals a Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off; transfers best among agents with similar capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tasks exhaust context windows, degrading reasoning; existing solutions rely on agent-internal adaptation or fixed summarization, which are unsuitable for closed-source or heterogeneous agents.

Method: Use an external LLM as a context manager that applies configurable context-modification actions to a frozen agent; optimize these actions end-to-end with reinforcement learning to balance fidelity and pruning.

Result: Substantial performance gains on web search and deep research benchmarks; maintains task constraints/progress while pruning stale content; identifies a Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off and shows transferability primarily across agents with similar vanilla ReAct performance.

Conclusion: AdaCoM offers reusable, external context management for heterogeneous agent systems; effectiveness depends on the baseline capability of the agent; provides a practical path for cross-agent context managers.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly face long-horizon tasks such as web search and deep research in real-world applications, where accumulated context can cause long-context degradation and reasoning failures. Prior work mitigates this through context management with agent-side context control or fixed strategies such as summarization, which require training the agent itself for adaptation - making it impractical for closed-source agents and ignoring that different agents may require different strategies. We introduce Adaptive Context Management (AdaCoM), which trains an external LLM to manage the context of a frozen agent through flexible modification actions and end-to-end reinforcement learning. Across diverse agents on web search and deep research benchmarks, AdaCoM substantially improves performance by preserving task constraints and progress while pruning stale content. The learned strategies reveal a Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off: agents with higher vanilla ReAct performance benefit from higher-fidelity context preservation, whereas lower-performing agents require more aggressive compression to stay within a reliable reasoning regime. Transfer experiments show that AdaCoM generalizes most effectively across agents with similar capability (measured by vanilla ReAct performance), suggesting a practical path toward reusable context managers for agent systems.

</details>


### [132] [PReMISE: Policy Rubrics as Measurement Specifications for LLM Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30803)
*Swastik Roy,Rajkumar Pujari,Tharindu Kumarage,Charith Peris,Rahul Gupta,Anna Rumshisky,Pradeep Natarajan,Venkatesh Saligrama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PReMISE provides a framework to discover policy-level rubric sets for LLM judges and audit them across four axes, with two repair operations that improve accuracy and reduce exploitability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rubrics condition LLM-judge scores; vague rubrics enable deception/exploitation; there is a need for auditable, reliable, and robust measurement specifications for open-ended evaluation.

Method: Using pairwise human-preference data, discover a policy-level rubric set; audit rubric sets across four axes (structural adequacy, reliability, preference fit, adversarial robustness); introduce two repair operations: (1) preference-rank selection to improve judge accuracy on paired responses; (2) reliability-constrained refinement to curb exploitation without harming inter-judge agreement.

Result: Across rubric sources, no raw source is simultaneously reliable, preference-predictive, and adversarially robust; high inter-rater agreement does not imply low exploitability. PReMISE uniquely scores non-trivially on applicability, specificity, and effective dimensionality. Preference-rank selection improves judge accuracy on paired responses from 65.0% to 68.6% and is competitive with baselines; reliability-constrained refinement reduces exploitation rates from 46.4% to 36.0% with little change in inter-judge agreement (α from 0.531 to 0.519).

Conclusion: PReMISE enables auditable rubric discovery and targeted repairs to improve LLM judge evaluation, revealing rubric-induced variability and providing practical improvements in accuracy and robustness.

Abstract: LLM judges are increasingly used to evaluate open-ended responses, but their scores depend strongly on the rubrics that condition them. A vague rubric asking for a response to be ``helpful and factual'' can reward polished answers that invent facts or violate user intent. We treat reusable rubrics as measurement specifications: changing the rubric changes the response quality measurement induced by a fixed judge. We introduce PReMISE, a framework that, given pairwise human-preference data, (i) discovers a policy-level rubric set, and (ii) audits any rubric set under LLM-judge use along four axes: structural adequacy, reliability, preference fit, and adversarial robustness. Across rubric sources no raw source is simultaneously reliable, preference-predictive, and adversarially robust; and high inter-rater agreement does not imply low exploitability. PReMISE is the only rubric source to score non-trivially on applicability, specificity, and effective dimensionality simultaneously. We contribute two audit-targeted repair operations: preference-rank selection raises judge accuracy on paired responses from $65.0\%$ to $68.6\%$, competitive with the strongest rubric-discovery baselines and leading on two of three judges in our cross-judge sweep; reliability-constrained refinement reduces the rate at which exploit responses receive high scores from $46.4\%$ to $36.0\%$ with little change in inter-judge agreement ($α{=}.531\to.519$).

</details>


### [133] [Planner-Centric Reinforcement Learning for Deep Research with Structure-Aware Reward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30824)
*Mustafa Anis Hussain,Xinle Wu,Yao Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DecomposeR introduces a planner-centric framework that represents research plans as typed DAGs, enabling explicit, rewardable planning. It trains a model in two RL stages (planner then answerer) to separate planning from execution and improve long-form research tasks, achieving 5.1–8.0 point gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-form research requires explicit planning, evidence retrieval, and synthesis across branches. Existing methods optimize either short-form QA or monolithic long trajectories, leading to poor credit assignment and weak planning signals.

Method: Represent plans as typed directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Train Qwen3-8B in two RL stages: (1) planner RL to learn graph structure and query decomposition; (2) answerer RL to execute branches and synthesize final output conditioned on the learned plan. Rewards are assigned to explicit planner tokens and structured plan components rather than a flat trajectory.

Result: DecomposeR-8B outperforms strong open baselines by 5.1–8.0 points on long-form benchmarks, indicating improved planning and answering capabilities.

Conclusion: A planner-centric decomposition enables finer-grained credit assignment for planning, improves long-form reasoning, and reduces end-to-end training ambiguity, yielding stronger overall performance.

Abstract: Deep research tasks require LLMs to plan what to investigate, retrieve evidence, and synthesize long-form answers across multiple branches of inquiry. Existing training paradigms either rely on short-form verifiable QA as a proxy or optimize monolithic long trajectories, which makes planning and execution difficult to disentangle and yields weak credit assignment for the planning process. We propose DecomposeR, a planner-centric deep research framework that represents research plans as typed directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), allowing planning to be made explicit, structured, and rewardable. We train a Qwen3-8B model in two stages: planner reinforcement learning (RL) first learns graph structure and query decomposition to improve research planning, and answerer reinforcement learning (RL) then learns branch-level execution and final synthesis conditioned on the learned plan. By assigning rewards to explicit planner tokens and structured components rather than to a flat trajectory, DecomposeR enables finer-grained optimization of planning while reducing the ambiguity of end-to-end training. Experiments show that DecomposeR-8B improves over strong comparable open baselines by 5.1-8.0 points on popular long-form benchmarks due to improved planning and answering capabilities.

</details>


### [134] [SLAT: Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming for Efficient CoT Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30832)
*Jian Yao,Xiongcai Luo,Ran Cheng,Kay Chen Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RL-based, segment-aware trimming (SLAT) reduces redundant high-probability reasoning segments to cut 50% of reasoning length while preserving competitive accuracy, yielding a favorable accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier for chain-of-thought in large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-driven CoT often suffers from overthinking; token-uniform penalties fail to selectively shorten only low-utility segments, causing loss of useful reasoning and inefficient computation.

Method: Develop a theoretical characterization of segment suboptimality under a correctness-length trade-off; propose SLAT (Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming), an RL framework that selectively suppresses redundant segments based on this criterion.

Result: On standard benchmarks, SLAT achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, reducing reasoning length by ~50% relative to uncompressed baselines while maintaining competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: Theoretically grounded, segment-aware trimming is a promising direction for efficient CoT reasoning in large language models.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Reasoning Models have significantly improved chain-of-thought (CoT) capabilities via reinforcement learning (RL). However, generated reasoning chains frequently suffer from structural redundancy (i.e., \emph{overthinking}), incurring high computational overhead without improving answer correctness. Existing mitigation strategies typically rely on token-uniform length penalties, which provide coarse, segment-agnostic pressure toward shorter outputs and can inadvertently suppress useful reasoning alongside redundancy. To address this, we demonstrate that inefficiency concentrates in high-probability segments with low marginal utility. We derive a theoretical characterization of segment suboptimality under the correctness-length trade-off objective and propose \textsc{SLAT} (Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming), an RL framework that selectively suppresses redundant segments based on this criterion. Empirical results on standard benchmarks indicate that \textsc{SLAT} establishes a superior accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, reducing reasoning length by $50\%$ relative to uncompressed baselines while maintaining competitive accuracy. Overall, our results suggest that theoretically grounded, segment-aware trimming is a promising direction for efficient CoT reasoning in large language models.

</details>


### [135] [COMPASS: Cognitive MCTS-Guided Process Alignment for Safe Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30838)
*Wenkai Shen,Pengyang Zhou,Jiahe Xu,Jiaming Qian,Haozhe He,Zhihao Huang,Chaochao Chen,Xiaolin Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COMPASS is a framework for robust safety alignment in LLM-powered search agents, combining cognitive MCTS-guided exploration (CTE) and introspective step-wise alignment (ISA) to reveal and supervise stealthy, multi-step safety violations, achieving a favorable safety-utility trade-off with less training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses retrieval-induced safety degradation in multi-step LLM agents where harmful intents decompose into innocuous sub-queries; existing alignment methods fail to capture sparse safety signals and supervise diverse, multi-step violations across workflows.

Method: Introduce COMPASS, integrating cognitive tree exploration (CTE) to synthesize stealthy attack trajectories, and introspective step-wise alignment (ISA) to isolate risky intermediate actions for fine-grained process supervision, preserving general utility.

Result: Empirical results show COMPASS achieves a favorable safety-utility trade-off while requiring substantially less training data.

Conclusion: COMPASS provides robust safety alignment across the agent workflow by combining CTE and ISA, enabling detection and supervision of multi-step, stealthy risks with reduced data requirements.

Abstract: LLM-powered search agents enable multi-step reasoning and tool use. However, these capabilities introduce retrieval-induced safety degradation, as harmful intents may decompose into seemingly innocuous sub-queries that lead to unsafe outcomes. Existing alignment methods struggle to capture sparse safety signals and fail to supervise diverse violations across multi-step interactions. We propose COMPASS, a Cognitive MCTS-Guided Process Alignment framework designed to achieve robust safety alignment throughout the agent workflow while preserving general utility. COMPASS integrates cognitive tree exploration (CTE) to efficiently synthesize stealthy attack trajectories, and introspective step-wise alignment (ISA) to isolate risky intermediate actions for fine-grained process supervision. Empirical results show that COMPASS achieves a favorable safety-utility trade-off while requiring substantially less training data.

</details>


### [136] [Distilling LLM Feedback for Lean Theorem Proving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30861)
*Gaetan Narozniak,Gérard Biau,Rémi Munos,Ahmad Rammal,Pierre Marion*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Feedback Distillation (FD) is a token-level self-distillation method that uses privileged feedback from a language model to guide post-training for reasoning models, improving trajectory diversity and entropy over GRPO; GRPO can be enhanced by initializing from an FD checkpoint, and the two methods are complementary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training for reasoning models often relies on reinforcement learning with sparse rewards (e.g., GRPO). This leads to limited exploration, mode collapse, and inefficiency. There is a need for richer supervision and the ability to inject external knowledge to improve long-horizon reasoning tasks.

Method: Train a model to match its own token-level distribution conditioned on privileged feedback provided by a language model. This token-level self-distillation adds external knowledge and guides the policy without relying solely on sparse rewards. The method is evaluated on Lean4 theorem-proving tasks, comparing against GRPO.

Result: FD maintains greater diversity in generated trajectories (higher policy entropy) and achieves better pass@k scaling than GRPO. The two methods are complementary: initializing GRPO from an FD checkpoint outperforms either method alone.

Conclusion: Feedback Distillation is a promising approach to enhance post-training for complex reasoning tasks by providing token-level supervision from privileged feedback and by increasing exploration. It can also serve as a beneficial prelude to or supplement for traditional policy-gradient methods like GRPO.

Abstract: Post-training for reasoning models typically combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards, most commonly with GRPO. However, this algorithm suffers from sparse rewards, limited exploration, and mode collapse. Building upon recent works on self-distillation, we propose Feedback Distillation, a training method where the model is trained to match, at the token level, its own distribution conditioned on privileged feedback produced by a language model. Feedback Distillation offers token-level supervision and can inject external knowledge. Evaluating our method for Lean4 theorem-proving, we find that Feedback Distillation maintains greater diversity in generated trajectories than GRPO, yielding higher policy entropy and better pass@k scaling. The two methods are complementary: initializing GRPO from a Feedback Distillation checkpoint outperforms either method alone. All in all, our results suggest a promising avenue to improve post-training for complex reasoning.

</details>


### [137] [UniScale: Adaptive Unified Inference Scaling via Online Joint Optimization of Model Routing and Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30898)
*Kaiyu Huang,Xingyu Wang,Mingze Kong,Zhubo Shi,Yuqian Hou,Hong Xu,Zhongxiang Dai,Minchen Yu,Qingjiang Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unified Inference Scaling (UIS) unifies model routing and test-time scaling (TTS) into a single optimization space, enabling fine-grained, dynamic control over quality and cost. UniScale models adaptive UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit and learns policies via LinUCB, with efficiency-aware learning and cost modeling to handle high-dimensional action spaces, yielding improved quality-cost trade-offs in dynamic inference scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches treat model routing and TTS separately, causing coarse performance changes with discrete model scales and capacity ceilings for fixed-model TTS. A unified framework promises finer-grained control and better adaptability in dynamic inference environments.

Method: Formulate UIS as a single optimization space and implement UniScale, an online framework that treats adaptive UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem and learns policies with LinUCB. Incorporates efficiency-aware learning and cost modeling to manage large action spaces and dynamic contexts.

Result: Empirical evaluation demonstrates that UniScale exploits UIS synergy to achieve a fine-grained and consistently better quality-cost trade-off across diverse, dynamic inference scenarios.

Conclusion: UIS enables a synergistic, scalable approach to inference scaling by unifying routing and TTS; UniScale provides stable online optimization in high-dimensional action spaces and shows robust performance gains in dynamic settings.

Abstract: In real-world deployments of large language models (LLMs), balancing inference quality and computational cost has become a central challenge. Existing approaches tackle this trade-off along two largely independent dimensions: model routing, which switches among models of different scales to match request complexity, and test-time scaling (TTS), which adjusts inference-time compute within a fixed model for fine-grained control. However, this decoupled design introduces inherent limitations. Model routing yields coarse-grained, discrete performance changes due to the sparse set of model scales, while single-model TTS often encounters capacity ceilings and exhibits diminishing returns as compute increases. Moreover, treating the two mechanisms separately restricts adaptability in dynamic inference environments. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Unified Inference Scaling (UIS), which unifies model routing and TTS in a single optimization space. Building on this formulation, we propose UniScale, an online framework that models adaptive UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem and learns inference policies via LinUCB. The framework incorporates efficiency-aware learning and cost modeling to ensure stable and scalable optimization over high-dimensional action spaces. Evaluation shows that UniScale effectively exploits the synergy in the UIS space to deliver a fine-grained and consistently better quality-cost trade-off across diverse, dynamic inference scenarios.

</details>


### [138] [BilliardPhys-Bench: Benchmarking Physical Reasoning and Visual Dynamics of Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30900)
*Ben Wang,Xiaogang Li,Ruochen Gao,Peiyao Xiao,Chengliang Xu,Zeyu Wang,Zichao Chen,Bing Zhao,Hu Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A synthetic billiards physics benchmark (BilliardPhys-Bench) tests multimodal models’ physical reasoning across collisions, wall bounces, and final resting positions; it reveals time/geometry-dependent drops and a persistent “stasis bias.”


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal LLMs excel at static recognition but struggle with intuitive physical reasoning from visual input. A dynamic, procedurally generated benchmark isolates and analyzes this weakness.

Method: A procedural engine creates randomized billiard scenarios with friction and elastic collisions. The benchmark evaluates three abilities: (1) predicting ball-to-ball collisions, (2) wall bounces, and (3) final positions after motion stops. It benchmarks recent MLLMs from GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Qwen families.

Result: Model performance declines as simulation time increases and scene geometry becomes more complex. A consistent failure mode, termed 'stasis bias', emerges where models predict no interaction when the correct outcome is harder to infer.

Conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs show clear weaknesses in visual dynamics and physical reasoning; the work motivates incorporating stronger physical inductive biases into multimodal architectures.

Abstract: Current multimodal models handle static image recognition well, but intuitive physical reasoning remains a weakness. Predicting how objects will move and interact from a single image is still difficult for these systems. We present BilliardPhys-Bench, a benchmark for physical reasoning in synthetic billiards environments. Its procedural engine generates randomized scenarios with friction and elastic collisions. The benchmark tests three abilities: (1) predicting ball-to-ball collisions, (2) reasoning about wall bounces, and (3) estimating final ball positions after motion stops. We evaluate recent MLLMs from the GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Qwen families. Performance drops as simulation time increases and scene geometry grows more complex. We also observe a consistent failure mode we call "stasis bias": when the correct physical outcome is harder to infer, models tend to predict no interaction. These findings show where current MLLMs break down on visual dynamics and point toward the need for better physical inductive biases in multimodal architectures.

</details>


### [139] [A Persona-Based Evaluation Framework for Pluralistic Alignment in Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31021)
*Atahan Karagoz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a persona-based, state-space evaluator for AI alignment that reveals pluralistic variability but exhibits drift; advocates dynamic viability regulation to maintain coherent evaluation over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of monolithic benchmarks that obscure cultural, demographic, and contextual variability in human judgment during AI evaluation.

Method: Introduce a state-space constrained emulation framework with a structured manifold of synthetic cognitive profiles (evaluative personas). Instantiate these personas in modern generative models, assess consistency, and analyze stability under sequential inference and prompt perturbations as a dynamical system on latent manifolds.

Result: Evaluators show high persona consistency but exhibit systematic degradation in coherence over time due to state-space drift and semantic instability under sequential prompting and stochastic perturbations.

Conclusion: Static alignment constraints are insufficient; embed dynamic, viability-driven regulatory mechanisms to sustain coherent cognitive emulation, enabling adaptive, context-sensitive, pluralistic evaluation.

Abstract: Current alignment paradigms for generative artificial intelligence rely predominantly on monolithic benchmarking frameworks that reduce the plurality of human judgment to aggregated statistical baselines, thereby obscuring cultural, demographic, and contextual variability in evaluation. We introduce a state-space constrained emulation framework for AI evaluation that replaces singular assessment functions with a structured manifold of synthetic cognitive profiles representing diverse human perspectives. We show that modern generative architectures can instantiate and maintain these evaluative personas with high consistency, enabling a form of pluralistic, perspective-dependent benchmarking that more closely reflects real-world consensus variability. However, we further analyze the stability of these simulated evaluators under sequential inference and stochastic prompt perturbations, revealing systematic degradation in persona coherence that manifests as state-space drift and semantic inconsistency. These findings suggest that static alignment constraints are insufficient for sustaining robust evaluative behavior over time. Instead, we argue for the necessity of embedding dynamic, viability-driven regulatory mechanisms within generative systems to preserve coherent cognitive emulation. By framing persona-based evaluation as a structured dynamical system over latent representation manifolds, this study provides a foundation for more adaptive, human-aligned, and context-sensitive approaches to AI evaluation.

</details>


### [140] [HADT: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Differential Transformer for Autonomous Earth Observation Satellite Cluster](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31023)
*Mohamad A. Hady,Muhammad Anwar Masum,Siyi Hu,Mahardhika Pratama,Jimmy Cao,Ryszard Kowalczyk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a transformer-based, model-free RL approach for autonomous resource management in a heterogeneous satellite cluster for EO missions, using relational observations-actions tokenization and differential attention. Shows improved performance and transferability to varying cluster sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional optimization-based scheduling relies on explicit models that are often unavailable or inaccurate in dynamic space environments. A model-free, adaptive method could better handle uncertainties and enable real-time decision making with minimal ground control.

Method: Introduce a novel transformer-based architecture tailored for heterogeneous satellite clusters. Key features include relational observations-actions tokenization and a differential attention mechanism to handle heterogeneous agents and dynamic interactions. The approach uses model-free reinforcement learning and emphasizes transferability across different cluster sizes.

Result: Experimental results show significant performance improvements over baselines. The architecture demonstrates strong adaptability and transferability when the number of satellite clusters varies.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture advances autonomous EO mission management by enabling adaptive, scalable, and transferable decision-making in heterogeneous satellite clusters, with strong empirical performance against existing baselines.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of autonomous resource management in heterogeneous satellite cluster conducting Earth Observation (EO) missions including optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) satellites. In autonomous operation mode, satellites are equipped with intelligent capabilities enabling real-time decision-making based on the latest conditions, while requiring minimal interaction with ground operators. Traditional scheduling approaches typically rely on mathematical models to represent satellite mission and resource management. Then, this problem is solved by using optimization algorithms. However, such solutions become less effective when the underlying models are not available, over complex, and inaccurate due to dynamic changes and uncertainties inherent in the space mission environment. A promising alternative is to reformulate the problem as a sequential decision-making process and apply model-free reinforcement learning techniques to enable adaptive and real-time resource management. To this end, we propose a novel transformer-based architecture tailored for heterogeneous satellite cluster autonomous EO Mission with relational observations-actions tokenization and differential attention mechanism. Our experimental results demonstrate significant performance improvements compared to the available baselines. Moreover, the proposed architecture exhibits strong adaptability and transferability with respect to varying numbers of satellite clusters.

</details>


### [141] [GraphARC: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Graph-Based Abstract Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31031)
*Saku Peltonen,August Bøgh Rønberg,Andreas Plesner,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new GraphARC benchmark for abstract reasoning on graph-structured data, extending ARC to graphs with few-shot transformation tasks; reveals language models' comprehension-execution gap and scaling limitations, proposing a scalable testbed for graph foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap that existing ARC-like benchmarks are grid/text based and lack scalable, diverse graph structures. Graph reasoning is core to intelligence, and a graph-based benchmark enables evaluation of structural generalization and transformation abilities across graph families and sizes.

Method: Define GraphARC tasks where models infer a transformation rule from a small set of input-output graphs and apply it to a test graph. Tasks cover local, global, and hierarchical graph transformations across diverse graph families and sizes, enabling scalable evaluation. Assess state-of-the-art language models on both graph-property questions and full transformation tasks, revealing limitations.

Result: LMs can answer questions about graph properties but struggle with the full graph transformation task, showing a comprehension-execution gap. Performance worsens on larger instances, indicating scaling barriers. GraphARC integrates node classification, link prediction, and graph generation within a single framework, offering a fertile ground for future graph foundation models.

Conclusion: GraphARC provides a scalable, diverse benchmark for abstract reasoning on graphs, highlighting current models’ limitations and offering a promising platform for developing and evaluating graph foundation models that can perform complex transformations and generalized reasoning.

Abstract: Relational reasoning lies at the heart of intelligence, but existing benchmarks are typically confined to formats such as grids or text. We introduce GraphARC, a benchmark for abstract reasoning on graph-structured data. GraphARC generalizes the few-shot transformation learning paradigm of the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC). Each task requires inferring a transformation rule from a few input-output pairs and applying it to a new test graph, covering local, global, and hierarchical graph transformations. Unlike grid-based ARC, GraphARC instances can be generated at scale across diverse graph families and sizes, enabling systematic evaluation of generalization abilities. We evaluate state-of-the-art language models on GraphARC and observe clear limitations. Models can answer questions about graph properties but often fail to solve the full graph transformation task, revealing a comprehension-execution gap. Performance further degrades on larger instances, exposing scaling barriers. More broadly, by combining aspects of node classification, link prediction, and graph generation within a single framework, GraphARC provides a promising testbed for future graph foundation models.

</details>


### [142] [Vector Linking via Cross-Model Local Isometric Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31100)
*Ziying Chen,Yang Cao,He Sun,Beining Yang,Tianjian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Vector Linking recovers cross-model correspondences between embedding clouds from different black-box encoders using a small seed of paired anchors; it relies on local geometric consistency of contrastive embeddings and an iterative reference-based geometric embedding hashing to link vectors via anchors and hash-space matching, aggregated with a Beta-Bernoulli posterior to bootstrap new anchors; robust across overlap and seed budgets, enabling vector DB integration and cross-model clustering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable integration and comparison of embeddings produced by independently trained encoders when data partially overlaps and access to model internals is limited.

Method: First, establish that short-range geometric structure is preserved up to scale in independently trained contrastive embeddings; identify that long-range distortions occur. Then propose an iterative hashing approach: represent each vector by distances to sampled paired anchors; perform hash-space candidate linking; aggregate evidence across views using a Beta-Bernoulli posterior to bootstrap new anchors; repeat to refine a set of high-confidence links.

Result: Empirical and theoretical results show accurate, robust linking across multiple benchmarks and model pairs, under varying overlap, seed budgets, and out-of-domain anchors; demonstrates applicability to vector database integration and cross-model clustering.

Conclusion: VecLinking provides a practical pipeline for cross-model vector linking from limited seeds, enabling interoperability of embedding spaces without requiring access to model internals.

Abstract: We study Vector Linking: given two embedding clouds produced by different black-box encoders over partially overlapping datasets, recover cross-model object correspondences using only vectors. Empirically and theoretically, we show that independently trained contrastive encoders exhibit local geometric consistency: short-range distances are approximately preserved up to a scale factor, while long-range distances are not due to model-specific distortion. Building on this, we propose an iterative, reference-based geometric embedding hashing that recovers vector links from a tiny seed set of paired anchors. It represents each vector by distances to sampled paired anchors, proposes candidate links via hash-space matching, and aggregates evidence across views in a Beta-Bernoulli posterior to bootstrap high-confidence links as new anchors. Experiments across multiple benchmarks and embedding model pairs demonstrate accurate and robust linking under varying overlap, seed budgets, and out-of-domain anchors, with applications to vector database integration and cross-model clustering. Code is available at https://github.com/DBgroup-Edinburgh/VecLinking.

</details>


### [143] [LLM-FACETS: A Privacy-Preserving Framework for Evaluating LLM Transparency and Accountability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31167)
*Tom Lucas,Alessio Buscemi,Alfredo Capozucca,German Castignani,Barbara Delacroix*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-source, browser-based framework that enables auditable evaluation of LLM outputs across factuality, epistemic calibration, and reproducibility, with self-hosted deterministic metrics, external-API metrics under user control, a plugin system, and cross-validation against 18 metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing LLMs is inaccessible to non-technical practitioners due to setup barriers and data privacy concerns; aligning evaluation with regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act, NIST RMF) and providing transparent data flows.

Method: LLM-FACETS features a browser UI and plugin architecture with three practitioner profiles. Deterministic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) run entirely on a self-hosted server (no outbound data). LLM-judge metrics call external APIs with explicit credential control. Transparency mechanisms include token-level log-probability visualization, multi-judge consensus to reduce bias, and RAG Triad metrics (Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, Context Relevance). The architecture supports easy integration of new metrics or datasets via plugins, ensuring reproducibility by cross-checking multiple metrics for the same property.

Result: Cross-validation of 18 metric implementations against canonical reference libraries demonstrates alignment and reproducibility of the evaluation suite.

Conclusion: The framework enhances transparency, reproducibility, and regulatory-aligned accountability in LLM evaluation; its open-source, plug-in design decouples AI accountability from the model-building teams and enables auditable, compliant oversight.

Abstract: Assessing whether Large Language Models outputs are factually grounded, epistemically calibrated, and methodologically reproducible is a prerequisite for responsible AI deployment. Yet auditing LLMs remains inaccessible to non-technical practitioners: existing tools require programming expertise and non-trivial environment setup, and cloud-hosted platforms transmit evaluation data to external services, creating barriers for domain experts and compliance officers legally responsible for AI oversight. We introduce LLM-FACETS (LLM FActuality Cross-EvaluaTion System): an open-source framework with a browser-accessible interface and a plugin architecture, structured around three practitioner profiles (technical experts, domain experts, compliance officers) that mirror the stakeholder categories identified in the EU AI Act and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. The architecture makes data flows explicit: deterministic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) run entirely within the self-hosted server with no outbound transmission; LLM-judge metrics contact external APIs explicitly, with users retaining full credential control. The framework operationalizes transparency through three mechanisms: token-level log-probability visualization for epistemic uncertainty, multi-judge consensus to mitigate judge bias, and RAG Triad metrics (Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, Context Relevance) to detect and localize hallucinations. A plugin architecture allows any new metric or dataset to be integrated without modifying the evaluation pipeline. The open-source implementation enables cross-checking across multiple metrics targeting the same property, ensuring reproducibility and decoupling AI accountability from the teams building the systems assessed. We verify the framework through cross-validation of 18 metric implementations against canonical reference libraries.

</details>


### [144] [Formalizing and falsifying causal pathways of rare events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31254)
*Anahita Haghighat,Dominik Janzing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes causal pathways for rare-event analysis in structural equation models, derives testable implications, and introduces a pathway-based causal-abstraction that depends only on the rare-event pathway rather than the full causal graph, bridging verbal explanations and detailed modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge intuitive causal narratives about rare events with rigorous causal modeling, by isolating pathway-level information that yields testable predictions independent of the full graph.

Method: Develop a formal definition of a causal pathway within SEMs for rare events, derive testable implications of such pathways, identify conditions where these implications depend solely on the pathway abstraction, and propose an abstraction mapping the full causal structure to pathways of rare events.

Result: Identification of conditions under which testable implications are determined by the rare-event pathway abstraction rather than the full graph, and the proposal of a formal abstraction of causal structure to pathways of rare events.

Conclusion: A pathway-based abstraction provides a principled link between verbal causal explanations and detailed causal modeling for rare events, enabling testable predictions that rely on the pathway rather than the complete causal graph.

Abstract: Building on recent formalizations of root cause analysis for rare events (``outliers'') in structural equation models, we propose a formal definition of a causal pathway and discuss its testable implications. We identify conditions under which these implications depend only on a causal abstraction defined by the pathway of rare events, rather than on the full causal graph of the underlying system. Accordingly, we introduce an abstraction of causal structure to pathways of rare events that bridges simple verbal causal explanations and detailed causal modeling.

</details>


### [145] [COLLEAGUE.SKILL: Automated AI Skill Generation via Expert Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31264)
*Tianyi Zhou,Dongrui Liu,Leitao Yuan,Jing Shao,Xia Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automates distillation of person-grounded AI skills from heterogeneous traces into portable, inspectable skill packages (COLLEAGUE.SKILL) with two tracks for capabilities and bounded behavior; supports versioning, NL feedback, deployment; demonstrated via open-source ecosystem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of an end-to-end workflow for converting scattered evidence of a person’s expertise into usable, correctable AI skills that can be inspected and deployed.

Method: Automated trace-to-skill distillation producing versioned skill packages with two coordinated tracks: a capability track (practices, mental models, decision heuristics) and a bounded behavior track (communication style, interaction rules, correction history); includes an artifact contract, generation workflow, correction lifecycle, deployment surface, and domain presets implemented in an open-source system.

Result: Public artifact with substantial community engagement: ~18.5k GitHub stars, 215 skills from 165 contributors, and >100k cumulative stars across listed skill cards; demonstrates portability, inspectability, and correctness relative to opaque prompts or hidden memories.

Conclusion: Person-grounded skills can be represented as portable, correctable packages rather than opaque prompts or hidden memories; COLLEAGUE.SKILL provides an end-to-end workflow and artifact ecosystem for evaluation and deployment.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly expected not only to complete isolated tasks, but also to carry bounded representations of human expertise, judgment, and interaction style. Building such person-grounded agents remains difficult because actionable knowledge associated with a person or role is usually embedded in heterogeneous traces rather than written as clean instructions. Existing memory and persona systems capture fragments of this evidence, while skill frameworks provide portable packaging formats; however, there is no end-to-end workflow for distilling these traces into inspectable, correctable, and agent-usable skills. We present an automated trace-to-skill distillation system for generating person-grounded AI skills via expert knowledge distillation. Given materials from a target person or role, COLLEAGUE.SKILL produces a versioned skill package with two coordinated tracks: a capability track for practices, mental models, and decision heuristics, and a bounded behavior track for communication style, interaction rules, and correction history. The package can be inspected, invoked, updated through natural-language feedback, rolled back, installed across agent hosts, and optionally prepared for controlled distribution. We describe the artifact contract, generation workflow, correction lifecycle, deployment surface, and domain presets implemented in the open-source system. At the time of writing, the public repository has approximately 18.5k GitHub stars; the gallery lists 215 skills from 165 contributors and more than 100k cumulative stars across listed skill cards. The system illustrates how person-grounded skills can be represented as portable, correctable packages rather than opaque prompts or hidden memories.

</details>


### [146] [Industrializing Prediction-Powered Inference: The GLIDE Library for Reliable GenAI and Agentic Systems Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31278)
*Grégoire Martinon,Ibrahim Merad,Mohammed Raki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GLIDE unifies PPI estimators and samplers for mean estimation under a SciPy-like API; provides validation suite and a decision-tree to reduce annotation effort; demonstrated to achieve similar precision with less labeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable evaluation of agentic systems requires unbiased estimates with valid uncertainty; current approaches trade off annotation cost and bias; an integrated, open-source solution is needed.

Method: Implement GLIDE library that aggregates state-of-the-art PPI methods (PPI++, Stratified PPI, Predict-Then-Debias and stratified variants, Active Statistical Inference) and sampling strategies (uniform, stratified, active, cost-optimal) in a single SciPy-like API; include a Monte Carlo validation suite, a data-driven decision tree for method choice, and a case study; provide reproducible results.

Result: Demonstrates annotation savings at equivalent precision; provides reproducible Monte Carlo validation; presents an empirical decision tree guiding method selection; available at GitHub.

Conclusion: GLIDE offers a cohesive, extensible platform for debiased mean estimation in agentic evaluation, enabling standardized comparisons and practical reductions in labeling costs.

Abstract: Reliable evaluation of agentic systems requires unbiased estimates with valid uncertainty, but standard practice navigates between costly human annotation and biased LLM-as-judge proxies. Prediction-powered inference (PPI) combines both into debiased estimates with valid confidence intervals, yet its various methods remain scattered across papers under partial implementations. We introduce GLIDE, an open-source Python library that unifies state-of-the-art PPI estimators (PPI++, Stratified PPI, Predict-Then-Debias and its stratified variants, Active Statistical Inference) and samplers (uniform, stratified, active, cost-optimal) under a scipy-style API specialized to mean estimation. GLIDE ships with a reproducible Monte Carlo validation suite, an empirically grounded decision tree for method selection, and an agentic evaluation case study showing substantial annotation savings at equivalent precision. The GLIDE package is available at this URL: https://github.com/EmertonData/glide

</details>


### [147] [TraceGraph: Shared Decision Landscapes for Diagnosing and Improving Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31308)
*Junjie Nian,Kang Chen,Ge Zhang,Yixin Cao,Yugang Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceGraph introduces a graph-based framework to analyze multi-model agent trajectories by constructing a shared landscape of observable states, overlaying productive cores and trap regions, and summarizing rollouts with Access, Trap exposure, and Repair events. It reveals navigation differences obscured by aggregate scores and supports trap-aware recovery in SWE-bench, improving resolved rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation of agent benchmarks often reduces rich trajectories to scalar pass/reward metrics, discarding structure and failure regions. A landscape-based representation aims to expose where models diverge, how they fail, and where improvements should focus.

Method: - Build a graph over observable action-observation states from pooled rollouts prior to model identity assignment. - Overlay outcome-informed productive cores and trap regions. - Summarize each rollout with three events: Access, Trap exposure, Repair. - Analyze across five benchmark splits to reveal differences in trap-avoidance vs trap-recovery. - For SWE-bench, introduce a trap-aware recovery pipeline: aruntime detector flags states matching historical trap regions; evaluate continuation policies from the same prefix. - Report improvements in official resolved rate on fired subsets.

Result: TraceGraph profiles reveal navigation differences hidden by aggregate scores; splits differ in whether they reward avoiding traps or recovering from them. In SWE-bench, the trap-aware recovery pipeline raises the official resolved rate from 40.4% to 43.5% on the fired subset and from 41.0% to 44.8% on common-fired instances, with provider-specific active components. It provides a process vocabulary for diagnosing benchmark tests, model divergence on a shared landscape, and guiding improvements based on failure regions.

Conclusion: A landscape-based analysis framework, TraceGraph, enables richer diagnostic insight into agent benchmarks, clarifying where models diverge and how failure regions can drive downstream improvements, including trap-aware recovery strategies.

Abstract: Agent benchmarks increasingly record rich interaction trajectories, yet evaluation often reduces each rollout to a pass rate or reward score. We introduce TraceGraph, a graph-based framework that turns released multi-model agent trajectories into shared decision landscapes. For each task, TraceGraph builds a graph over observable action-observation states from pooled rollouts before model identity is introduced. It then overlays outcome-informed productive cores and trap regions, and summarizes each rollout with three events: Access, Trap exposure, and Repair. Across trajectories spanning five benchmark splits, TraceGraph profiles reveal navigation differences hidden by aggregate scores and show that splits differ in whether they reward avoiding traps or recovering from them. The same TraceGraph landscape also motivates a trap-aware recovery pipeline for SWE-bench: aruntime detector fires on states matching historical trap regions, then lightweight continuation policies are evaluated from the same prefix. On fired states, the best pooled single-factor policy raises official resolved rate from 40.4% to 43.5% on the per-provider fired subset and from 41.0% to 44.8% on common-fired instances, with provider-specific active components. Overall, TraceGraph provides a process vocabulary for asking what agent benchmarks test, where models diverge on a shared landscape, and how failure regions can guide downstream improvement.

</details>


### [148] [Diagnosing Failure Modes of Shared-State Collaboration in Resource-Constrained Visual Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31354)
*Yunpeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Weak-model collaborative visual reasoning with shared memory amplifies hallucinations; CoSee audits read/write/verify loops; higher compute can hurt accuracy without explicit verification; two main failure modes (Noise Reinforcement, Policy Collapse); provides traceable diagnostics and a baseline for modular design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how intermediate state evolution fails in modular visual reasoning with low-capacity models and how shared working memory affects error propagation.

Method: Introduce CoSee auditing framework formalizing the read-write-verify loop; evaluate information flow in document visual question answering across multi-page, chart, and web-based tasks; analyze noise accumulation and failure modes; use cost-accuracy Pareto frontiers.

Result: Shared workspaces can degrade performance by amplifying hallucinations; two dominant failure modes identified (Noise Reinforcement and Policy Collapse); compute vs accuracy trade-offs show more compute can reduce effectiveness without verification; provides trace-level diagnostics and a mechanistic baseline for reliable modular design.

Conclusion: For resource-constrained agents, communication fidelity dominates bottlenecks; emphasize traceable diagnostics and a robust modular design baseline rather than merely increasing reasoning depth.

Abstract: Modular visual reasoning systems increasingly rely on shared working memory for multi-step collaboration, yet the failure dynamics of intermediate state evolution in low-capacity regimes remain underexplored. We study failure modes of collaborative reasoning with weak learners (4B--8B models) through the lens of noise accumulation. We introduce CoSee, an auditing framework that formalizes the read-write-verify loop to trace information flow in document visual question answering. Across multi-page, chart, and web-based benchmarks, we find a counter-intuitive degradation: naive shared workspaces often amplify hallucinations rather than resolve them. We identify two dominant failure modes: Noise Reinforcement, where ungrounded notes are reused as evidence, and Policy Collapse, where added context shifts the model toward under-specified, short-form answers. Using cost-accuracy Pareto frontiers, we show that increased compute can correlate negatively with performance without explicit verification. Our findings suggest that for resource-constrained agents, the bottleneck lies not in reasoning depth but in communication fidelity, providing trace-level diagnostics and a mechanistic baseline for reliable modular design.

</details>


### [149] [Learning to Adapt: Self-Improving Web Agent via Cognitive-Aware Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31365)
*Weile Chen,Bingchen Miao,Qifan Yu,Wendong Bu,Guoming Wang,Wenqiao Zhang,Shengyu Zhang,Juncheng Li,Siliang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SCALE is a self-cognitive, autonomous web-agent framework using Selector, Predictor, and Judger, with SCALE-Hop graph exploration, and SCALE-20k real-web traces to improve multimodal LLMs’ adaptability across dynamic web tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current web agents rely on handcrafted pipelines or costly expert trajectories, hindering adaptability in dynamic, complex environments. A self-guided, cognitive-aware approach can autonomously reveal limitations and extend capabilities.

Method: Introduce three adversarial roles (Selector, Predictor, Judger) to autonomously identify limitations; develop SCALE-Hop for global graph-based exploration and planning; build SCALE-20k by collecting 19 real websites with structured demonstrations from SCALE traces.

Result: Empirical results show improved performance and generalization of multiple MLLMs across varied web environments, demonstrating scalability and adaptability of autonomous web agents.

Conclusion: SCALE offers a scalable, generalizable framework for autonomous, adaptive web agents, enabling broader applicability with reduced reliance on handcrafted pipelines.

Abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have led to promising progress in web agents. However, existing web agents often rely on handcrafted execution pipelines or expensive expert trajectories, limiting their adaptability to complex, dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we propose SCALE (Self-Cognitive-Aware Learning and Exploration), which leverages three adversarial roles, Selector, Predictor, and Judger to autonomously discover the agent's limitations and expand its cognitive boundaries through environmental exploration. Moreover, we propose SCALE-Hop, a graph exploration strategy that facilitates global planning and helps agents avoid local exploration traps. To further support learning, we construct SCALE-20k, a large-scale dataset collected from 19 real-world websites, containing diverse task types and structured demonstrations generated from SCALE's exploration traces. Experimental results show that our approach significantly improves the performance and generalization of multiple MLLMs in various web environments. Our framework offers a scalable and generalizable solution for building truly autonomous and adaptive web agents.

</details>


### [150] [HypoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Interactive Abductive Hypothesis Generation over Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31370)
*Yisen Gao,Yixi Cai,Tianshi Zheng,Jiaxin Bai,Yangqiu Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HypoAgent proposes an agentic framework for interactive abductive hypothesis generation over knowledge graphs, integrating an Intent Recognition Agent, a Hypothesis Generation Agent, and a Root Cause Analysis Agent to ground intents, generate controllable hypotheses, and diagnose failures, achieving state-of-the-art semantic similarity across single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing controllable hypothesis generation methods struggle to ground evolving natural-language intents across multi-turn dialogues and offer limited fine-grained diagnosis when generated hypotheses fail, hindering interactive abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs.

Method: HypoAgent integrates three agents: (1) Intent Recognition Agent to ground user utterances and dialogue history into executable KG conditions, (2) Hypothesis Generation Agent to perform controllable hypothesis generation according to extracted intention, and (3) Root Cause Analysis Agent to diagnose unreliable hypothesis fragments and use KG neighborhood probing to identify supported refinements.

Result: Experiments on commonsense and biomedical domain-specific knowledge graphs demonstrate that HypoAgent achieves state-of-the-art semantic similarity under single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings.

Conclusion: HypoAgent advances interactive abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs by providing grounding, controllable generation, and diagnostic capabilities, with public code available.

Abstract: Abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs aims to generate logical hypotheses that explain observed entities or facts. Existing controllable hypothesis generation methods allow users to guide this process with explicit conditions, but they remain limited in interactive settings: they struggle to ground evolving natural-language intents across multi-turn dialogues and provide little fine-grained diagnosis when generated hypotheses fail. To address these limitations, we propose HypoAgent, an Agentic framework for interactive abductive Hypothesis Generation over knowledge graphs. HypoAgent integrates three agents: an Intent Recognition Agent that grounds user utterances and dialogue history into executable KG conditions, a Hypothesis Generation Agent that performs controllable hypothesis generation according to the extracted user intention, and a Root Cause Analysis Agent that diagnoses unreliable hypothesis fragments and leverages KG neighborhood probing to identify supported refinements. Experiments on commonsense and biomedical domain-specific knowledge graphs demonstrate that HypoAgent achieves state-of-the-art semantic similarity under single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/HypoAgent.

</details>


### [151] [FAM-Bench: A Multimodal Benchmark for Condition-Aware Food-as-Medicine Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31410)
*Mingyang Mao,Bhargav Rishi Medisetti,Utkarsh Grover,Tanvir Ibrahim,Wenyan Li,Tingting Zhang,Xiaomin Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce FAM-Bench, a 2,500-instance multi-modal benchmark for Food-as-Medicine with two tasks (dish-level suitability; comparative dish analysis) across 13 conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges the gap where current food AI benchmarks focus on recognition and QA, neglecting decision-layer about condition-appropriate choices requiring visual, ingredient, and clinical nutrition reasoning.

Method: Constructed dataset with nutrition-expert verification; two tasks: dish-level suitability assessment from image and ingredients; comparative ranking of four dishes; integrates ingredient evidence, visual cues, and clinical constraints.

Result: Provides a standardized testbed enabling grounded health-aware reasoning in language and vision-language models; supports evaluation of condition-specific dish suitability across health conditions.

Conclusion: FAM-Bench offers a platform to evaluate and advance health-aware food AI; future work includes stronger baselines and robust evaluation protocols for real-world decision support.

Abstract: Food-as-Medicine requires models to reason beyond what a dish is or what nutrition it contains: they must decide whether a concrete food choice is appropriate for a specific health condition. Existing food AI benchmarks primarily evaluate dish recognition, recipe understanding, nutrient estimation, or general nutrition question answering, leaving this health-aware decision layer largely untested. We introduce FAM-Bench, a multi-modal Food-as-Medicine benchmark with 2500 nutrition-expert-verified instances across 13 diet-related health conditions. The benchmark contains two complementary tasks: dish-level suitability assessment, where models judge whether a dish is suitable for a condition from its image and ingredient list, and comparative dish analysis, where models rank four candidate dishes by condition-specific suitability. Both tasks require integrating ingredient evidence, visual preparation cues, and clinical nutrition constraints, providing a standardized testbed for grounded health-aware reasoning in language and vision-language models.

</details>


### [152] [Answer-Set-Programming-based Abstractions for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31444)
*Rafael Bankosegger,Thomas Eiter,Johannes Oetsch*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ASP-based CARCASS abstractions enable relational MDP modeling for RL; case studies in Blocks World and Minigrid show promising results when domain knowledge is available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the intractable state spaces in RL by using relational reasoning and abstractions. Extend the CARCASS framework (originally Prolog-based) with Answer-Set Programming to exploit its declarative modeling capabilities.

Method: Implement CARCASS abstractions in Answer-Set Programming; compare with the Prolog implementation; evaluate on Blocks World and Minigrid to assess abstraction quality and RL utility.

Result: The ASP-based CARCASS approach is promising; case studies indicate effective abstraction construction for RL when domain knowledge is available.

Conclusion: ASP can effectively realize CARCASS abstractions for relational RL, offering a declarative modeling alternative; future work should address scalability, integration with learning, and broader domain evaluation.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) enables autonomous agents to learn policies from experience, but realistic problems often involve enormous state spaces, making learning and generalisation challenging. Abstraction and approximation are therefore essential. Relational Reinforcement Learning (RRL) offers a way to reason about objects and their relations, and the CARCASS framework by Martijn van Otterlo demonstrates how logical representations can model Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) in first-order domains. Originally implemented in Prolog, CARCASS leverages domain knowledge to create powerful abstractions. We explore Answer-Set Programming (ASP), which is a rich and, contrary to Prolog, fully declarative modelling language, to realise CARCASS abstractions. We evaluate our ASP-based implementation in case studies of two domains, viz. Blocks World and Minigrid. Our results indicate that CARCASS with ASP provides a promising approach to constructing abstractions for RL, especially when domain knowledge is available.

</details>


### [153] [AutoSci: A Memory-Centric Agentic System for the Full Scientific Research Lifecycle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31468)
*Weitong Qian,Beicheng Xu,Zhongao Xie,Bowen Fan,Guozheng Tang,Jiale Chen,Xinzhe Wu,Mingtian Yang,Chenyang Di,Jiajun Li,Lingching Tung,Peichao Lai,Yifei Xia,Ziyi Guo,Yanwei Xu,Yanzhao Qin,Shaoduo Gan,Xupeng Miao,Bin Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoSci is a memory-centric agentic framework for the full scientific lifecycle, integrating memory, lifecycle execution, DAG-based multi-agent orchestration, and iterative evolution to create a persistent, self-improving research environment across projects (code at GitHub).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the human-intensive, long, multi-project nature of scientific research by providing a unified, memory-rich automation system that persists across projects and improves its procedures over time.

Method: AutoSci comprises four modules: SciMem (schema-governed memory with Long-Term Knowledge Memory and Active Research Memory), SciFlow (a five-stage lifecycle from literature understanding to rebuttal with a state/context/verification/feedback/orchestration harness), SciDAG (DAG-shaped multi-agent operators and reusable stage-specific templates), and SciEvolve (turns feedback from users and environments into versioned updates to SciMem, SciFlow, and SciDAG).

Result: Proposes a unified, persistent research environment capable of executing, remembering, and evolving across projects; implementation is available as an open-source code repository (GitHub link).

Conclusion: AutoSci presents a holistic, memory-centric architecture to automate the scientific research lifecycle, addressing gaps in existing systems and enabling end-to-end, self-improving research workflows.

Abstract: Scientific research has traditionally been human-intensive, requiring researchers to coordinate literature, ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and review responses across long project cycles. The rise of LLM-based scientific agents creates an opportunity to automate this process. Such a system must support the full research lifecycle, maintain structured persistent memory across projects, and improve its own research procedures over time. However, existing systems either partially satisfy or fail to satisfy these requirements, leaving a gap for a unified automated scientific research system. As a result, we present AutoSci, a memory-centric agentic system for the full scientific research lifecycle. AutoSci is organized around four modules. SciMem provides schema-governed research memory, separating Long-Term Knowledge Memory for reusable scientific knowledge from Active Research Memory for project-level artifacts such as ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and reviews. SciFlow executes a five-stage lifecycle from literature understanding to rebuttal through a harness that controls state, context, verification, feedback, and orchestration. SciDAG augments difficult skills with DAG-shaped multi-agent operators and reusable stage-specific templates. SciEvolve converts feedback signals from users, experiments, reviews, and external environments into versioned updates to SciMem organization, SciFlow skills, and SciDAG templates. Together, these modules make AutoSci a persistent research environment that can execute, remember, and evolve across research projects. The code repository is available at https://github.com/skyllwt/AutoSci.

</details>


### [154] [LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31492)
*Liwei Kang,Yee Whye Teh,Wee Sun Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Explicit tree-structured representations of LLM search traces (LinTree) improve reasoning performance and efficiency compared to implicit traces or local-state heuristics; making the search tree explicit mitigates backtracking ambiguity and leverages history more effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs solve reasoning problems by generating traces that resemble search processes. Prior work often uses only the current state or relies on implicit traces. Investigating whether access to the full search trace or an explicit tree structure can enhance decision-making and efficiency in search-like reasoning tasks.

Method: Compare trace-conditioned policies with best-first search guided by an LLM heuristic across three environments (Blocks World, grid Navigation, Sokoban). Analyze whether raw history helps, identify backtracking ambiguities, and propose LinTree by adding parent pointers to explicitly represent the linearized search tree. Evaluate performance and search efficiency against implicit models and LLM-heuristic baselines.

Result: Raw access to search history alone does not reliably outperform heuristic search. A key reason is that implicit traces fail to clearly indicate which earlier state is revisited during backtracking. Introducing LinTree, which adds explicit parent pointers to the tree structure, improves task performance and search efficiency relative to both implicit-reasoning models and LLM-heuristic search.

Conclusion: Explicitly representing the search history as a structured tree (with parent pointers) enhances the effectiveness of LLM-based reasoning. This supports broader adoption of structure-aware representations for improving reasoning with LLMs and suggests directions for designing reasoning systems that cleanly preserve and utilize search history.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often solve reasoning problems by generating intermediate traces that explore and revise partial solutions. From a search perspective, these traces can be viewed as linearized search trees, where the model extends a partial solution, abandons it when it fails, and backtracks to try alternatives. Compared with traditional heuristic-guided search, such a policy has a potential advantage: it conditions on the whole search trace rather than only on the current local state. We first test whether LLMs utilize this advantage by comparing trace-conditioned reasoning policies against best-first search equipped with an LLM heuristic that only observes the current local state. Across three controlled reasoning environments, Blocks World, grid Navigation, and Sokoban, we find that raw access to search history alone is not enough to reliably outperform heuristic search. We then study one possible reason: in LLM reasoning traces, the underlying search tree is only implicitly represented, and when the model backtracks or switches branches, the trace does not explicitly identify which earlier search state is being revisited. We show that adding simple parent pointers to explicitly represent the linearized tree (LinTree) structure improves both task performance and search efficiency relative to implicit reasoning models and LLM-heuristic-guided search. These results suggest that search history becomes most useful when its tree structure is made explicit, motivating more structure-aware representations for LLM reasoning.

</details>


### [155] [Choosing the Lens: Strategic Perspective Activation in Context-Dependent Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31581)
*Albert Sadowski,Jarosław A. Chudziak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Context-dependent defeat in argumentation: CDAFs extend Dung's AFs by making defeat depend on context via a defeat function; agent manipulation via activation; formalizes ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION; provides baseline complexity; tight bounds and multi-agent variants remain open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arguments must be evaluated under varying external regimes, and an agent with influence over the regime has strategic leverage not captured by standard abstract frameworks.

Method: Introduce context-dependent argumentation frameworks (CDAFs) with a defeat function that, per context, determines which attacks succeed. Derive the defeat function via a perspective-labeled specialization from a relevance set ρ (the agent's action space) and a priority π. Present a small worked example showing context-dependent acceptance/rejection of an argument and mirrorability constraints. Define the decision problem ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION and report baseline complexity bounds.

Result: Proposes a formal framework (CDAFs) and a derivation mechanism for defeat; includes a concrete example where an argument is rejected under one regime but accepted under partial activations; defines ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION and provides baseline complexity bounds; notes that tight bounds and multi-agent variants are left open.
