Conclusion: CDAFs provide a mechanism to model context-sensitive evaluation and strategic leverage in argumentation; the framework invites further work on tighter complexity bounds and multi-agent extensions.

Abstract: The same arguments often need to be evaluated under different external regimes. An agent with influence over the regime has a strategic lever that standard formalisms do not directly capture. We introduce context-dependent argumentation frameworks (CDAFs), an extension of Dung's theory in which a defeat function determines, per context, which attacks succeed. A perspective-labeled specialisation derives the defeat function from a relevance set $ρ$ and a priority $π$. The relevance set is the agent's action space. In a small worked example, the agent's target argument is rejected under every full-relevance injective priority, yet accepted under partial activations, one of which no VAF audience can mirror. We define the corresponding decision problem, ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION, and record baseline complexity bounds. Tight bounds and multi-agent variants are left open.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [156] [Gait2Hip-60: A Unified Deep Learning Benchmark for Predicting Hip Muscle Forces and Joint Moments from Multi-Cadence Gait Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30374)
*Jiaqi Zhang,Ji Hou,Qing Sun,Xianzhi Gao,Bo Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer-based deep learning framework predicts hip muscle forces and hip joint moments from gait kinematics; Transformer outperforms LSTM and Mamba in healthy subjects; moderate zero-shot generalization to ONFH; broader validation needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable rapid estimation of hip dynamics without time-consuming musculoskeletal simulations in clinical settings.

Method: Collected gait data from 60 healthy adults under three cadences. Inputs: ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles. Outputs: OpenSim-derived hip muscle forces and hip joint moments. Trained three sequence models (LSTM, Transformer, Mamba) with a unified protocol. Subject-level split, same preprocessing, same metrics. Best model tested on external ONFH cohort (n=9) without retraining.

Result: In healthy benchmark, Transformer: hip muscle forces RMSE 1.33 N/kg, MAE 0.57 N/kg, R2 0.819; hip joint moments RMSE 0.11 Nm/kg, MAE 0.07 Nm/kg, R2 0.862. Cadence-consistent performance. Zero-shot ONFH: hip muscle forces RMSE 1.51 N/kg, MAE 0.70 N/kg, R2 0.537; hip joint moments RMSE 0.17 Nm/kg, MAE 0.12 Nm/kg, R2 0.569.

Conclusion: Transformer is a strong baseline for predicting hip dynamics from gait kinematics; results support feasibility but generalization to pathology requires broader validation and likely model improvements before clinical deployment.

Abstract: Estimating hip muscle forces and joint moments during gait typically relies on musculoskeletal simulation, which is informative but time-consuming and difficult to apply in clinical settings. This study developed a deep learning framework to predict these hip dynamics parameters directly from lower-limb gait kinematics and compared three representative sequence models under a unified protocol. Gait data were collected from 60 healthy adults under three metronome-guided cadence conditions. Ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles were used as inputs, and OpenSim-derived hip muscle forces and hip joint moments were used as reference outputs. Three deep learning models of LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba were trained and evaluated using the same subject-level split, preprocessing pipeline, and metrics. The best model was then directly tested on an external cohort of 9 patients with osteonecrosis of the femoral head (ONFH) without retraining. In the healthy-subject benchmark, Transformer achieved the best subject-level mean performance for both hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.33 N/kg, MAE = 0.57 N/kg, R2 = 0.819) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.11 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.07 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.862), with similar advantages across walking cadences. In zero-shot external validation, Transformer retained moderate predictive ability in ONFH for hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.51 N/kg, MAE = 0.70 N/kg, R2 = 0.537) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.17 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.12 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.569). These findings support the feasibility of estimating hip dynamics from gait kinematics, identify Transformer as a strong baseline, and highlight the need for broader pathological validation and improved generalization before clinical application.

</details>


### [157] [Unicorn: Scaling High-Dimensional Time Series Forecasting via Universal Correlation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30376)
*Haochen Yuan,Yichen Song,Yunbo Wang,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unicorn introduces a universal latent codebook to decouple correlation modeling from channel identities, enabling scalable multi-dataset pretraining for high-dimensional time series and improving cross-domain transfer, especially in few-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series models face a scalability vs. heterogeneity trade-off: channel-independent models scale with data but miss inter-channel dependencies; channel-dependent models capture dependencies but struggle to generalize across datasets with different dimensionalities.

Method: Unicorn (Universal Correlation Network) uses a latent prototype codebook to project heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, learning identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns. This decouples correlation modeling from channel identity and supports cross-domain transfer and few-shot learning via multi-dataset pretraining.

Result: Experiments show Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, notably in few-shot transfer scenarios.

Conclusion: The approach provides a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models by enabling cross-dataset transfer through a reusable, identity-agnostic interaction codebook.

Abstract: Modern time series architectures face a fundamental trade-off: channel-independent models scale well with increasing data volume but ignore critical inter-channel dependencies, while channel-dependent models are expressive but remain ``dimension-bounded'', struggling to generalize across heterogeneous datasets.To bridge this gap, we introduce Unicorn (Universal Correlation Network), a framework for scalable, multi-dataset pretraining on high-dimensional time series. At the core of Unicorn is a latent prototype codebook that decouples correlation modeling from specific channel identities. By projecting heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, UniCorN learns identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns that transfer across domains with diverse dimensionalities and semantics. Extensive experiments show that Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, particularly in few-shot transfer scenarios, offering a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models.

</details>


### [158] [When LLMs Learn to Be Consistently Wrong: A Multi-Model Study of Linear Representations of Synthetic Deception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30381)
*Vahideh Zolfaghari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear probes detect synthetic dishonesty with high accuracy across multiple transformer models, revealing two distinct representational regimes; deception signals become more pronounced in deeper layers; modest fine-tuning suffices to entrench domain-invariant dishonesty representations, with implications for monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the representational basis of deception in language models by using synthetic dishonesty as a controlled, testbed situation, enabling probing of internal representations, transferability, and auditability.

Method: Fine-tune five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) with LoRA on a common question distribution. Train linear probes (logistic regression and MLP) on mean-pooled hidden states to detect deception. Evaluate using AUC; generalize probes to TruthfulQA and MMLU; perform mechanistic analyses (Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, calibration via ECE). Assess robustness to Gaussian noise and analyze how deception direction evolves across layers.

Result: Linear probes achieve near-perfect AUC (≥0.99) in four architectures as early as layers 1–3; Pythia-1.4B peaks at 0.705. Logistic regression matches or outperforms MLP probes, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalize with near-zero loss to held-out MMLU. Late-layer representations show strong resistance to Gaussian noise, with Gemma-2 models particularly stable. Mechanistic analyses reveal two regimes: representational collapse in Pythia/Llama/Qwen versus high-dimensional preservation in Gemma-2. Across models, the dishonesty direction consolidates with depth, and calibration (ECE) improves to <0.01 (except Pythia). These findings indicate that robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, informing activation-based monitoring strategies.

Conclusion: Modest supervised fine-tuning can rapidly entrench robust, domain-invariant representations of synthetic dishonesty in transformers, and linear probes can effectively monitor such representations—highlighting both detectability and potential monitoring strategies for deception in AI systems.

Abstract: Deceptive alignment, in which models maintain accurate internal representations while deliberately producing false outputs, remains a central challenge in AI safety. While strategic deception is the primary long-term concern, synthetic dishonesty - induced via direct optimization on incorrect answers - provides a controlled testbed for studying the representational basis of learned deception. We introduce a multi-model paradigm in which honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) are fine-tuned using LoRA on the same question distribution. Linear probes trained on mean-pooled hidden states detect synthetic dishonesty with near-perfect AUC (greater than or equal to 0.99) as early as layers 1-3 in four architectures, while Pythia-1.4B reaches a peak of 0.705. Logistic regression probes consistently match or outperform MLP probes, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalize with near-zero loss (Delta AUC approx. 0) to held-out MMLU subjects. Late-layer representations show strong robustness to Gaussian noise, with Gemma-2 models exhibiting exceptional stability. Mechanistic analysis of Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, and calibration (ECE) reveals two regimes: representational collapse in Pythia/Llama/Qwen versus high-dimensional preservation in Gemma-2. Across all models, the dishonesty direction consolidates progressively in deeper layers, with optimal calibration (ECE less than 0.01 except Pythia) achievable in layers 1-4. These results demonstrate that robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with implications for activation-based monitoring.

</details>


### [159] [LLMs Without Deep Neural Networks: New Architecture, Benefits and Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30385)
*Vincent Granville*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a DNN alternative based on RBF-like machinery that achieves a closed-form global optimum in one iteration, emphasizing explainability and efficiency; includes case studies and comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address demand for explainable, efficient models and to validate a newly discovered approach that parallels RBF networks while avoiding iterative training.

Method: High-level overview of the technology, alignment with RBF machinery, one-shot closed-form optimization, and comparative case studies.

Result: Claims improved explainability and accuracy, training-free optimization, and favorable comparisons in case studies, though concrete metrics are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Positions the model as an independent DNN alternative with closed-form optimality and practical advantages, awaiting empirical validation against existing RBF and DNN methods.

Abstract: The purpose of this article is to provide validation to my deep neural network alternative in the context of LLMs. Very recently, there has been a significant interest by Chinese researchers in a model called RBF network, as a substitute to standard DNNs, with increased explainability and higher accuracy. It turns out that my new model, discovered independently, is based on the exact same machinery. But with a major twist: it does not need DNN as it finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form, in one iteration, thus eliminating the tedious training step. Here I provide a high-level overview of my technology, with case study and comparison to similar methods.

</details>


### [160] [Functional MRI Time Series Generation via Wavelet-Based Image Transform and Spectral Flow Matching for Brain Disorder Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30387)
*Hwa Hui Tew,Junn Yong Loo,Fang Yu Leong,Julia K. Lau,Ding Fan,Hernando Ombao,Raphaël C. -W. Phan,Chee Pin Tan,Chee-Ming Ting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSFM is a dual-transform fMRI generative framework that uses wavelet (DWT) and cosine (DCT) representations, coupled via spectral flow matching to produce class-conditioned, time-domain BOLD signals; claims improved brain network classification and provides code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome data scarcity and the non-stationary, multi-scale, physiologically varied nature of BOLD signals, which existing generative models struggle to reproduce.

Method: Convert BOLD signals to a wavelet-based DWT map to capture multi-scale dynamics; project into DCT space across regions and time to exploit low-frequency energy; train a spectral flow matching model to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representations; reconstruct to time-domain via inverse DCT and inverse DWT.

Result: Empirical demonstration of improved downstream fMRI-based brain network classification; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The approach imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics, improving generative fidelity and downstream utility.

Abstract: Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) provides non-invasive access to dynamic brain activity by measuring blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signals over time. However, the resource-intensive nature of fMRI acquisition limits the availability of high-fidelity samples required for data-driven brain analysis models. While modern generative models can synthesize fMRI data, they often remain challenging in replicating their inherent non-stationarity, intricate spatiotemporal dynamics, and physiological variations of raw BOLD signals. To address these challenges, we propose Dual-Spectral Flow Matching (DSFM), a novel fMRI generative framework that cascades dual frequency representation of BOLD signals with spectral flow matching. Specifically, our framework first converts BOLD signals into a wavelet decomposition map via a discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to capture globalized transient and multi-scale variations, and projects into the discrete cosine transform (DCT) space across brain regions and time to exploit localized energy compaction of low-frequency dominant BOLD coefficients. Subsequently, a spectral flow matching model is trained to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representation. The generated samples are reconstructed through inverse DCT and inverse DWT operations to recover physiologically plausible time-domain BOLD signals. This dual-transform approach imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics. Ultimately, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through improved downstream fMRI-based brain network classification. The code is available at https://github.com/htew0001/DSFM.git .

</details>


### [161] [A Novel Evaluation Metric for Unsupervised Learning in AIS-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection: MADQI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30388)
*Ismet Gocer,Zakirul Bhuiyan,Raza Hasan,Shakeel Ahmad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes MADQI, an unsupervised, multi-metric quality index to evaluate AIS anomaly detectors without labels; combines ARC, PPS, SDS, ECE; uses Haversine-based spatial-behavior checks; reports 80.37% MADQI with strong ECE/ARC results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised anomaly detection on maritime AIS data lacks reliable evaluation metrics; prevalent methods like Isolation Forest rely on labels for validation, hindering objective comparison.

Method: Develops a four-component MADQI metric with automatic normalization via multi-chunk evaluation and adaptive scaling; uses Haversine distance to assess spatial and behavioral anomalies; integrates with existing unsupervised detectors to produce an overall MADQI score.

Result: On AIS dataset, obtains MADQI 80.37%; ECE 0.907; ARC 1.000; SDS and PPS results not specified but implied favorable; demonstrates effectiveness in detecting abnormal vessel behavior.

Conclusion: MADQI provides a robust, label-free framework for evaluating unsupervised AIS anomaly detectors; encourages reliable comparisons and meaningful interpretation of anomaly detection performance.

Abstract: This paper introduces a new systematic framework for detecting anomalies in maritime Automatic Identification System (AIS) datasets. These anomalies include abnormal vessel behaviours related to speed, position jumps, time gaps, and turn angles. Although unsupervised learning algorithms such as Isolation Forest are widely used for detecting anomalous vessel movements, they often lack systematic and meaningful evaluation measures. To address this limitation, we propose a novel quality metric called Maritime Anomaly Detection Quality Index (MADQI). The prosed MADQI is a composite index designed to evaluate the anomaly detection performance of machine learning models without requiring labelled data. The proposed framework uses Haversine distance calculations to analyse AIS datasets and identify anomalies based on their spatial and behavioural characteristics. The proposed MADQI evaluation framework integrates four interconnected metrics: Anomaly Rate Consistency (ARC), Physical Plausibility Score (PPS), Score Distribution Separation (SDS), and Extreme Case Evidence (ECE). These metrics are combined through automatic normalisation using multi-chunk evaluation and adaptive scaling techniques. Experimental results on the AIS dataset show that the proposed framework achieved a MADQI score of 80.37%, demonstrating its effectiveness for unsupervised anomaly detection. In particular, the algorithm performed strongly in identifying abnormal vessel behaviour. Among the individual MADQI components, ECE and ARC achieved scores of 0.907 and 1.000, respectively, indicating excellent capability in detecting extreme anomalies and maintaining anomaly rate consistency. Overall, these results are encouraging and demonstrate that the proposed framework provides a reliable and meaningful approach for evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data.

</details>


### [162] [NumLeak: Public Numeric Benchmarks as Latent Labels in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30393)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NumLeak offers a paired API-bound and white-box evaluation framework to separate memorized recall from genuine out-of-sample skill in LLMs, revealing strong apparent performance on finance and macro benchmarks that collapses on holdouts, and showing that prompt defenses can substantially reduce memorization risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address concerns that date-conditioned benchmarks and public numeric tasks conflate memorized data with real skill, thereby overestimating LLMs’ generalization, and to develop robust detection and mitigation of memorization channels.

Method: Combine API-bound boundary probes on production models with a white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. Use regression-style and correlation analyses on market factors (e.g., Mkt-RF, Fama-French factors), unemployment, CPI, and NOAA temperature. Assess parse rate vs. r on holdouts; use logprob ranking to detect memorization; reproduce dose-response in a white-box setting; test a one-line system-prompt defense against non-adaptive suffix attacks.

Result: Frontier LLMs recall the Fama-French market excess return at pooled Pearson r=0.97–0.99 and stay within 0.15 (within 25 bps) on five factors; similar fidelity for unemployment, CPI, and NOAA temperature. On recent-release holdouts, parse rate falls to 21–57% while r remains ~0.99 for answered months; memorization is implied by a refuse-or-recall channel. White-box experiments reproduce dose–response; logprob ranking detects memorization that open-ended generation misses, suggesting black-box probes understate the channel. A regression linking date to market sentiment correlates with true Mkt-RF at r=0.74 but collapses to r=0.02 after residualizing the model’s own recall. A one-line system-prompt blocks 99.8% of a non-adaptive suffix-attack with negligible utility cost on factual and historical-narrative prompts.

Conclusion: Evaluations based solely on closed APIs or date-conditioned benchmarks risk conflating memorization with genuine capability. NumLeak’s mixed open/closed framework reveals memorization channels that inflate apparent skill, advocates open-box validation, and demonstrates practical defenses to mitigate prompt-based leakage, highlighting the need for robust, leakage-aware benchmarking in LLM evaluation.

Abstract: Public numeric benchmarks appear in pretraining, so an evaluation that conditions on a date may be measuring memorized recall rather than out-of-sample skill. We introduce NumLeak, a measurement framework that combines API-boundary probes on production models with a white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. Top-tier frontier LLMs recall the Fama-French market excess return at 3-seed pooled Pearson r=0.97-0.99 while staying within 0.15 within-25bps on the five sibling factors; comparable fidelity appears on U.S. unemployment, CPI inflation, and NOAA temperature. On a recent-release holdout, parse rate collapses to 21-57% but r stays at approximately 0.99 on months answered, the refuse-or-recall asymmetry a memorized channel predicts. The white-box experiment reproduces the dose-response, and logprob ranking detects memorization that open-ended generation misses, implying closed-API black-box probes understate the channel. A Sonnet "date to market-sentiment" regression that correlates with true Mkt-RF at r=0.74 collapses to r=0.02 once the model's own recall is residualized out. A one-line system-prompt defense blocks 99.8% of a non-adaptive single-turn suffix attack set at near-zero utility cost on conceptual and historical-narrative queries

</details>


### [163] [LongDS-Bench: On the Failure of Long-Horizon Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30434)
*Kewei Xu,Xiaoben Lu,Shuofei Qiao,Zihan Ding,Haoming Xu,Lei Liang,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongDS is a long-horizon, multi-turn data-analytic benchmark with 68 tasks (from Kaggle notebooks) spanning 2,225 turns across six domains, designed to test agents’ ability to maintain and update evolving analytical state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data analysis is inherently iterative; current benchmarks largely test short-horizon tasks and do not assess long-term state maintenance and reasoning across multiple turns.

Method: Construct LongDS from real-world Kaggle notebooks (68 tasks, 2,225 turns, six domains). Tasks are built around state-evolution patterns (e.g., counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition) with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Evaluate five state-of-the-art models on the benchmark.

Result: Best model achieves 48.45% average accuracy; performance drops about 47 points from early to late turns; long-horizon errors account for 52%–69% of failures. Additional agent steps do not reliably improve performance, indicating state-maintenance is the bottleneck.

Conclusion: LongDS enables research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis; findings highlight state-maintenance as the key challenge over interaction budget; code and data to be released at the project repository.

Abstract: Real-world data analysis is inherently iterative, yet existing benchmarks mostly evaluate isolated or short interactive tasks, leaving agents' ability to track evolving analytical context over long horizons untested. We introduce LongDS, a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis where agents must maintain, update, restore, and compose evolving analytical states. LongDS comprises 68 tasks constructed from real-world Kaggle notebooks, spanning 2,225 turns across six domains including Geoscience, Business, and Education. Tasks are designed around state-evolution patterns (e.g., counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition), with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models, we find that the best model reaches only 48.45% average accuracy, performance drops nearly 47 points from early to late turns, and long-horizon errors account for 52%--69% of failures. Further analysis shows that additional agent steps do not necessarily improve performance, suggesting that the key bottleneck is maintaining a correct analytical state rather than increasing interaction budget. We release LongDS to support research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/DataMind.

</details>


### [164] [Calibrated Preference Learning: The Case of Label Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30447)
*Santo M. A. R. Thies,Viktor Bengs,Timo Kaufmann,Sebastian J. Vollmer,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Formalizes calibration for probabilistic label ranking and presents a hierarchy (full rankings, sub-rankings, top-k). Proves full-ranking calibration implies the others but not vice versa; sub-ranking and top-k calibrations are incomparable. Empirically reveals miscalibration in ranking models and RLHF reward models; calibration correlates with accuracy but is not identical, motivating methods to correct it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration ensures predicted probabilities match true outcome frequencies. While calibration is well-studied for classification/regression, probabilistic label ranking—predicting distributions over label orderings—lacks formal calibration theory. A robust calibration framework is needed to support reliable decisions and interpretability in ranking settings.

Method: Define formal calibration notions for full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k rankings; establish a theoretical hierarchy of these notions; prove implications (full-rank ⇒ sub-rankings/top-k; converse false; sub-ranking and top-k are incomparable). Conduct empirical studies on standard label ranking models to assess calibration, and apply the framework to RLHF reward models to examine the relationship between calibration and benchmark accuracy.

Result: Theoretically, full-rank calibration implies other calibration notions, but sub-ranking and top-k calibration are incomparable. Empirically, popular label ranking models exhibit substantial miscalibration, with notable gaps between sub-ranking and top-k calibration metrics. In RLHF reward-model experiments, calibration correlates strongly but imperfectly with benchmark accuracy, indicating it captures a meaningful quality aspect beyond top-1 accuracy.

Conclusion: Calibration for label ranking is a distinct, multi-faceted property with a clear hierarchy. Miscalibration is prevalent in practice, and calibration relates to—but is not reducible to—standard accuracy metrics. The framework motivates further work to understand downstream effects and to develop methods to diagnose and correct miscalibration.

Abstract: Calibration, the alignment of predicted probabilities with true outcome frequencies, is essential for reliable decision-making. While extensively studied for classification and regression, calibration has not been formally addressed for probabilistic label ranking, where the goal is to predict a distribution over orderings of a label set. Naively treating rankings as classes ignores their structure and fails to capture important modalities such as pairwise and top-k predictions. We formalize calibration for label ranking and develop a hierarchy of notions covering full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k rankings. We prove that full-rank calibration implies the others but not conversely, and sub-ranking and top-k calibration are incomparable. Empirically, we find popular label ranking models are often poorly calibrated, with substantial differences between sub-ranking and top-k metrics. Applying our framework to RLHF reward models, we find that calibration correlates strongly but not perfectly with benchmark accuracy, suggesting it captures a meaningful quality dimension beyond top-1 accuracy. These findings motivate future work on understanding the downstream effects of miscalibration and developing methods to correct it.

</details>


### [165] [Bounded Behavioral Indistinguishability for Black-Box LLM Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30448)
*Munawar Hasan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bounded behavioral indistinguishability formalizes a stricter, adversarial notion of distillation adequacy beyond mere output similarity; LoRA distillation improves semantic similarity but adversarial tests reveal persistent behavioral gaps; broader evaluation (style/robustness/domain prompts, cross-family judges, A/B filtering) shows coverage/diversity matter; semantic fidelity alone is insufficient for black-box LLM distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure distilled black-box LLMs are behaviorally indistinguishable from their teachers under realistic attacker models. Introduce a formal (ε,q,t,A)-indistinguishability framework and demonstrate its necessity by showing residual distinguishability despite improved semantic fidelity.

Method: Define (ε,q,t,A)-behavioral indistinguishability over a fixed prompt distribution. Instantiate on Qwen and Llama teacher–student pairs using a 5,000-prompt behavioral probe suite. Compare base vs LoRA-distilled students against the teacher. Use adversaries (discriminators, cross-family judges, A/B-swap consistency filters) to measure distinguishability. Evaluate semantic similarity (task/response fidelity) and behavioral distinguishability; perform query-budget analyses to compare disagreement-guided acquisition with stratified sampling.

Result: Semantic similarity improves with LoRA (Qwen: 0.788→0.862; Llama: 0.814→0.874). However, adversarial evaluation finds nonzero distinguishing advantage persists; discriminators reveal artifacts concentrated in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts. Cross-family teacher–judge results: Qwen’s distinguishing advantage decreases (0.158→0.081) after LoRA with A/B-swap filtering. Query-budget experiments show disagreement-guided acquisition does not consistently outperform stratified sampling; coverage/diversity remain strong baselines.

Conclusion: Semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient as a sole criterion for black-box distillation. Effective distillation requires bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation; future work should broaden attacker models, prompt distributions, and task categories to ensure true behavioral indistinguishability.

Abstract: Black-box LLM distillation is usually evaluated as an output-matching problem: a student is considered successful when its responses are semantically similar to, or task-consistent with, those of a teacher. However, output similarity does not imply that the student is behaviorally indistinguishable from the model it imitates. We introduce bounded behavioral indistinguishability, formalized as $(ε,q,t,\mathbb{A})$-behavioral indistinguishability over an explicit prompt distribution, where $ε$ bounds distinguishing advantage, $q$ bounds oracle queries, $t$ bounds computation, and $\mathbb{A}$ denotes the adversary class.
  We instantiate this notion on Qwen and Llama teacher-student pairs using a controlled $5,000$-prompt behavioral probe suite. For each family, we compare the teacher with both the base student and the LoRA-distilled student, measuring whether distillation reduces distinguishability rather than merely improving similarity. LoRA raises semantic similarity from $0.788$ to $0.862$ for Qwen and from $0.814$ to $0.874$ for Llama. Yet adversarial evaluation reveals remaining behavioral differences: learned discriminators retain nonzero advantage, and pairwise category analysis shows artifacts concentrated in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts. A pairwise teacher-identification adversary confirms this trend. With a different-family Llama judge and A/B-swap consistency filtering, Qwen distinguishing advantage drops from $0.158$ for the base student to $0.081$ after LoRA distillation. Query-budget experiments show that disagreement-guided acquisition does not consistently outperform stratified random sampling, indicating that coverage and diversity remain strong baselines. Our results show that semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient: black-box LLM distillation requires bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation.

</details>


### [166] [VeriGate: Verifier-Gated Step-Level Supervision for GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30451)
*Aakriti Agrawal,Minghui Liu,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VeriGate extends group-relative policy optimization by gating supervision with a verifier, using future-cumulated PRM rewards and group-normalized token-level advantages to provide continuation-aware credit, achieving substantial accuracy gains and reduced reward-hacking compared to GRPO baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address sparse supervision and lack of step-level credit in GRPO and outcome-only rewards, which cause zero-gradient stalls and poor exploration in scalable reasoning models.

Method: Three design choices: (1) keep verifier in charge when it yields meaningful preferences, otherwise rely on process supervision; (2) convert process reward model (PRM) step scores into future-cumulated rewards for continuation-aware credit; (3) transform rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages to provide informative gradients while reducing reward hacking.

Result: Empirical results on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models show VeriGate improves average accuracy by ~20% (1.5B) and ~12% (7B) across six benchmarks, reduces zero-gradient failures and reward-hacking, and improves reasoning quality relative to outcome-only GRPO and PRM-as-outcome baselines.

Conclusion: VeriGate effectively mitigates the main limitations of GRPO and outcome-only rewards by gating supervision, providing continuation-aware credit, and offering fine-grained, more robust gradient signals, leading to stronger reasoning performance.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is an effective recipe for training reasoning models with verifier-based outcome rewards, but its supervision is sparse: when all sampled trajectories for a prompt receive the same verifier reward, the group-relative advantage collapses to zero and learning stalls. Outcome-only rewards also provide no step-level credit assignment, limiting exploration and making it harder to learn robust reasoning. We present VeriGate (Verifier-Gated Step-Level GRPO), a verifier-gated extension of GRPO that addresses these limitations with three design choices. First, VeriGate keeps the verifier in charge whenever verifier rewards induce a meaningful preference among sampled trajectories, and uses process supervision only when verifier rewards are degenerate. Second, instead of collapsing Process Reward Model (PRM) step scores into a single trajectory reward, VeriGate converts them into future-cumulated rewards to assign continuation-aware credit. Third, VeriGate transforms these rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages, restoring informative gradients and fine-grained credit assignment while remaining less susceptible to reward hacking than methods that optimize aggregated PRM scores. Empirically, training on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models and evaluating on six reasoning benchmarks, VeriGate improves average accuracy by about 20% and 12% for 1.5B and 7B models respectively, substantially reduces zero-gradient failures, decreases reward-hacking behavior, and improves reasoning quality relative to outcome-only GRPO and PRM-as-outcome baselines.

</details>


### [167] [A Unified Framework for Gradient Aggregation in Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30452)
*Zeou Hu,Kelvin Ho,Yaoliang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies gradient aggregation in gradient-based multi-objective optimization, proving optimal convergence to Pareto stationarity via a gradient-alignment condition, and exploring dual-cone projection for feasibility; connects and extends existing methods, introduces capped MGDA, and validates theory experimentally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the fragmented landscape of gradient-aggregation schemes in MOO by providing a single unifying framework with convergence guarantees and to understand how different methods relate and can be improved, including robustness in settings like federated learning.

Method: Develop a unifying framework for gradient aggregation in MOO centered on an alignment condition. Show that selecting non-conflicting directions within the convex hull of gradients yields a sufficient condition for convergence. Use projection onto the dual cone to ensure feasibility. Provide a primal optimization perspective that clarifies relationships among established algorithms and enables new variants. Introduce capped MGDA from a CVaR-based formulation and apply it to adversarial federated learning. Validate with experiments on synthetic problems and practical benchmarks.

Result: The analysis yields optimal rates of convergence to Pareto stationarity under the alignment condition. Feasibility can be achieved via dual-cone projection. The primal viewpoint clarifies relationships among existing gradient-aggregation methods and supports designing novel variants. The capped MGDA variant demonstrates robustness in adversarial federated learning. Empirical results on synthetic tasks and benchmarks corroborate the theory.

Conclusion: The work provides a comprehensive theoretical framework for gradient aggregation in MOO, connects existing algorithms through a common lens, and offers practical variants like capped MGDA with demonstrated robustness, supported by empirical validation.

Abstract: Many machine learning problems involve multiple inherent trade-offs that are best addressed by gradient-based multi-objective optimization (MOO) algorithms. Existing methods are often proposed with various motivations, analyzed case by case, and differ algorithmically in how the component gradients are aggregated at each step. In this work, we develop a unifying framework for gradient aggregation in MOO, establishing (optimal) rates of convergence to Pareto stationarity, the standard measure of performance in MOO. Central to our analysis is a sufficient alignment condition, from which we derive a theorem showing that non-conflicting directions, when chosen within the convex hull of gradients, form a fundamental sufficient condition for convergence. We further show that feasibility can be ensured through projection onto the dual cone, broadening the scope of methods that admit convergence guarantees. In parallel, we present a primal optimization perspective of gradient aggregation that encompasses established algorithms, clarifies their theoretical relationships, and enables the design of new variants. As an illustration, we introduce capped MGDA, derived from a CVaR-based formulation, and demonstrate its robustness in adversarial federated learning. Finally, we validate our theory through experiments on synthetic problems and practical benchmarks.

</details>


### [168] [DisjunctiveNet: Neural Symbolic Learning via Differentiable Convexified Optimization Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30456)
*Shraman Pal,Can Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: End-to-end differentiable framework for hard, input-dependent MIL constraints in neural nets, using disjunctive rules and hierarchical convex relaxations to achieve convex hulls; enables exact rule satisfaction with strong predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage domain knowledge and hard logical/linear constraints to overcome data sparsity, avoiding soft penalties or post-processing; address nonconvexity in incorporating MIL constraints into neural nets.

Method: Represent rules as disjunctive constraints and apply hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations. Embed these as differentiable optimization layers to enforce hard MIL constraints within the network.

Result: Demonstrates perfect rule satisfaction on real-world datasets and achieves strong predictive performance.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical, end-to-end differentiable approach for enforcing hard, input-dependent mixed-integer linear constraints in neural networks, enabling exact feasibility and competitive accuracy.

Abstract: Many learning tasks in science and engineering are characterized by sparse datasets, which limits the effectiveness of purely data-driven approaches. At the same time, these problems are often accompanied by rich domain knowledge derived from physical laws, operational requirements, and expert heuristics. Such knowledge is frequently expressed as rules involving logical propositions and linear inequalities. Existing neuro-symbolic methods typically enforce these rules approximately through soft penalties, assume input-independent rules when designing specialized architectures, or rely on non-differentiable post-processing at inference time to achieve hard constraint satisfaction. While recent advances in differentiable optimization layers enable end-to-end feasibility enforcement within neural networks, extending these approaches to logical or mixed-integer rules remains challenging due to inherent nonconvexity. In this work, we propose a unified end-to-end framework for enforcing hard, input-dependent mixed integer linear constraints within neural networks. Our approach represents rules as disjunctive constraints and applies hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations. These relaxations yield tractable linear constraints that can be embedded as differentiable optimization layers while enabling exact rule satisfaction. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on real-world datasets, achieving perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [169] [Scalable Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning via State Augmentation and Consensus for Separable Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30461)
*Santiago Amaya-Corredor,Miguel Calvo-Fullana,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributed constrained MARL framework using state-augmented policies and neighbor consensus over dual variables; achieves scalable, feasible constraint satisfaction under global resource limits; effective in smart-grid demand response with large agent counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Independent MARL learners struggle to satisfy global constraints due to lack of coordination on contribution toward the constraint. A lightweight, distributed mechanism is needed to enforce global feasibility without centralized training.

Method: Train a single augmented policy per agent offline, conditioned on local state and a dual variable encoding constraint feedback. During execution, agents perform neighbor-to-neighbor consensus to align on the dual variable using local communication. The approach leverages Lagrangian dual decomposition and assumes mild connectivity to bound consensus error.

Result: Consensus error among agents’ multipliers is bounded; constraint violation is also bounded and decreases with better graph connectivity and more consensus rounds. The method scales linearly with the number of agents in both training and execution, unlike CTDE which scales quadratically. In smart-grid experiments, consensus is essential for feasibility; without it, agents defer demand; with consensus, they meet grid constraints and demands even with thousands of agents, outperforming CTDE baselines that handle only dozens.

Conclusion: A lightweight, scalable distributed MARL framework with consensus-based dual variable exchange can achieve feasible, globally coordinated constraint satisfaction without centralized training, making it suitable for large-scale multi-agent systems.

Abstract: We present a distributed approach for constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) that combines state-augmented policy learning with distributed consensus over dual variables. Our method targets systems where agents have separable dynamics but must coordinate to satisfy global resource constraints, a setting in which, as we demonstrate empirically, independent learning fails to produce feasible solutions because agents cannot determine appropriate individual contributions toward collective constraint satisfaction. The key technical contribution is showing that lightweight neighbor-to-neighbor consensus over Lagrange multipliers suffices for globally coordinated constraint enforcement while preserving the scalability of independent training. Each agent learns a single augmented policy offline, conditioned on both its local state and a dual variable encoding constraint feedback. During execution, agents reach agreement on this dual variable through local communication alone. We prove that under mild connectivity assumptions, the consensus error among agents' multipliers is bounded, and show that this translates to a bounded constraint violation that decreases with graph connectivity and the number of consensus rounds. Unlike centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) approaches, whose complexity grows at least quadratically with agent count, our method scales linearly in both training and execution. Experiments on smart grid demand response demonstrate that consensus coordination is \emph{essential for feasibility}: without it, agents satisfy grid capacity constraints only by indefinitely postponing demand, a degenerate non-solution. With consensus, agents converge to a shared dual variable and satisfy both grid constraints and demand fulfillment, scaling to thousands of agents while CTDE baselines are limited to dozens.

</details>


### [170] [idSCD: Identifying Training Datasets through Semantic Correlation Descriptors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30462)
*Andrada Gobeaja,Ionut Hodoroaga,Elena Burceanu,Marius Leordeanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs) enable white-box membership inference by fingerprinting a model’s internal semantic correlations; they outperform baselines across NLP tasks and show dataset membership leaves detectable traces in learned representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To detect dataset-level membership beyond black-box signals by leveraging dataset-specific incidental regularities embedded in a model’s semantic correlation structure.

Method: Define SCDs to summarize semantic correlations learned by a model; use leave-one-dataset-out diagnostics to show SCDs distinguish matching vs non-matching datasets; derive a practical membership score using only the model’s SCDs for training mixtures and a standalone SCD for the target dataset.

Result: In three task domains, the SCD-based score yields the best ROC-AUC with low variance, surpassing RMIA, Attack-P, LiRA, and SIF baselines; gains up to >60% ROC-AUC when semantic separation between datasets is strong.

Conclusion: Dataset membership can be traced through internal semantic correlations; SCDs offer a powerful, white-box diagnostic, with performance contingent on semantic separation and dataset overlap.

Abstract: Can a dataset be recognized from the spurious correlations it induces during training? We argue that datasets leave dataset-specific traces in a model's learned semantic correlation structure: incidental regularities that are predictive within a dataset, but not causal for the underlying task, can be internalized during training. We use this insight to study dataset-level membership inference, moving beyond existing methods that rely on behavioral or distributional evidence such as confidence scores, losses, margins, generated samples, or query responses. We introduce a white-box semantic fingerprinting approach based on semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs), which capture the semantic correlation structure learned by a model and make it comparable across dataset mixtures. In a controlled leave-one-dataset-out diagnostic, SCDs recover dataset-specific changes and perfectly separate matching from non-matching dataset pairs. We then propose a practical SCD-based membership score that tests whether a target dataset is part of a model's training mixture using only the model's SCD and the target dataset's standalone SCD, without requiring leave-one-dataset-out models. Across three diverse experimental settings, with dataset groups for natural language inference, emotion classification, and medical text classification, we test both the advantages and limitations of SCD-based membership inference with different degrees of semantic separation and keyword support between dataset splits. On average, the classifier based on this score achieves the highest performance and the lowest std, outperforming black-box baselines RMIA, Attack-P, and LiRA, as well as the white-box SIF baseline. These results show that dataset membership can be traced through internal semantic correlations, with the largest relative gain exceeding 60% in ROC-AUC when dataset groups expose distinct semantic particularities.

</details>


### [171] [Can Subgraph Explanations Be Weaponized to Steal Graph Neural Networks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30470)
*Ojas Nimase,Jiate Li,Yue Zhao,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A black-box model extraction attack for graph classification leverages explainability outputs and discrete labels to efficiently approximate decision boundaries, using Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation with Hoeffding guarantees and explanation subgraphs to shrink search space; demonstrates vulnerability of explainable GMLaaS and informs defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainable graph ML platforms expose model internals through explanations and discrete outputs. Under strict black-box constraints (no scores or gradients), it is crucial to assess whether such interfaces can be exploited to steal models, and to quantify the risk to inform defenses and policy.

Method: (1) Use model explanations (binary masks) to steer Monte Carlo sampling for edge sensitivity estimation toward decision boundaries with Hoeffding concentration bounds; (2) Use explanation-derived subgraphs to prune the search space for boundary identification; operate under only observed class labels and binary masks, without probabilities, gradients, or confidence values.

Result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark graph datasets across domains shows the proposed attack outperforms baselines under the same black-box constraints, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of exploiting explainability interfaces for model extraction.

Conclusion: Explainability interfaces in GMLaaS define exploitable attack surfaces. The work highlights the need for robust defenses and thoughtful policy frameworks for explainable AI; code for the attack is released to enable further evaluation.

Abstract: Graph Machine Learning as a Service (GMLaaS) platforms increasingly implement explainability interfaces to meet regulatory transparency requirements. However, this transparency creates exploitable vulnerabilities for model extraction attacks. We present the first model extraction attack specifically designed for graph classification under strict black-box constraints where the attacker observes only discrete class labels and binary explanation masks (no probability scores, gradients, or confidence values). Our method (1) uses model explanation outputs to guide Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation toward decision boundaries, with Hoeffding concentration guarantees on estimation accuracy and (2) exploits explanation subgraphs to efficiently narrow the boundary search space. Extensive experiments on benchmark graph datasets across multiple domains demonstrate our method's superiority over comparable baselines. These findings demonstrate that such explainability interfaces create exploitable attack surfaces, informing both defensive mechanisms and policy frameworks for explainable AI mandates. The implementation code is provided in https://github.com/LabRAI/XSTEAL/.

</details>


### [172] [Universal Multiclass Transductive Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30479)
*Steve Hanneke,Hongao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Characterizes learnability in universal transductive online classification with unbounded label space; shows only two optimal mistake-rate regimes (bounded or log-increasing); introduces Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) trees and the indifference property to characterize learnability; extends to agnostic and stochastic-process settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand when online transductive learning is possible under unbounded labels and classify learnable concept classes by mistake-rate behavior.

Method: Define LCLL trees and the indifference property, prove a dichotomy in mistake-rate for learnable classes, and extend results to agnostic and stochastic-sequence models.

Result: Complete characterization: learnable classes exhibit either bounded or increasing logarithmic mistake rates; LCLL trees plus indifference property characterize learnability; extensions to agnostic and stochastic settings.

Conclusion: Provides a dichotomy in optimal rates and introduces a structural tool (LCLL) for learnability, with extensions to agnostic and stochastic-process cases.

Abstract: We consider the problem of universal transductive online classification with a possibly unbounded label space. This setting considers online learning, with the sequence of instances (without labels) known to the learner in advance. We say a concept class $\mathcal{H}$ is learnable if there is a learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$, such that for every realizable sequence, the number of mistakes made by $\mathcal{A}$ grows at most sublinearly with the number of predictions. We characterize the learnability of this setting and show that there are only two possible optimal rates for the learnable classes: either bounded or increasing logarithmically. We introduce a new combinatorial structure, called ``Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree'', which, along with the indifference property, characterizes the learnability. We also extend the learnability result to the agnostic case and the case where only the stochastic process that generates the instance sequence is known.

</details>


### [173] [Discovering a Zeta Map Algorithm on Dyck Paths via Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30482)
*Xiaoyu Huang,Blake Jackson,Kyu-Hwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A small encoder-decoder transformer trained on the zeta map for Dyck paths yields a human-verifiable combinatorial algorithm (scaffolding map) matching the zeta map up to a labeling reversal, illustrating AI-assisted mathematical discovery via mechanistic interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demonstrate that ML can produce explicit, checkable mathematical constructions and use mechanistic interpretability to extract provable algorithms from learned models in a controlled combinatorial setting.

Method: One-layer, one-head encoder-decoder Transformer trained on the zeta map for Dyck paths. Mechanistic interpretability tools used: decoder cross-attention analysis, linear probing, and causal intervention to reveal the computation. From these signals, derive a level-based representation and a structured input-position traversal, leading to the scaffolding map.

Result: Reveals a level-based mechanism: encoder representations render path levels linearly accessible; the decoder selects and traverses input positions in a structured way. Translated to combinatorics, yields the scaffolding map, an explicit peak-centered traversal algorithm for Dyck paths. Proves that this algorithm agrees with the zeta map (up to a reversal convention in labeling).

Conclusion: An explicit, verifiable AI-assisted discovery in mathematics: mechanistic interpretability converts model behavior into a precise combinatorial algorithm, offering a controlled benchmark for AI-assisted proof construction and potential extension to other mathematical maps.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly used in mathematical discovery, but in mathematics the desired output is often not a prediction itself, but an explicit construction that can be checked independently. We study this setting through the zeta map on Dyck paths, a classical bijection in the combinatorics of the q,t-Catalan numbers. We train a deliberately small one-layer, one-head encoder-decoder transformer on this map and analyze its learned computation using mechanistic interpretability tools, including decoder cross-attention analysis, linear probing, and causal intervention. The analysis reveals a level-based mechanism: encoder representations make path levels linearly accessible, while the decoder selects and traverses input positions in a structured way. Translating these signals into combinatorics leads to the scaffolding map, an explicit peak-centered traversal algorithm for Dyck paths. We prove that this algorithm agrees with the zeta map, modulo a reversal convention in the labeling. This gives a controlled example of AI-assisted mathematical discovery in which mechanistic interpretability turns model behavior into a precise, human-verifiable combinatorial algorithm.

</details>


### [174] [Graph-Conditioned Mixture of Graph Neural Network Experts for Traffic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30486)
*Amirhossein Ghaffari,Saeid Sheikhi,Ekaterina Gilman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GC-MoE: graph-conditioned mixture of frozen spatio-temporal GNN experts for node-level forecasting; uses a lightweight router; achieves MAE gains on four benchmarks with minimal trainable parameters (~17k) on top of 1.5M frozen weights; optional output refinement; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different road segments exhibit distinct dynamics; a single backbone may underfit heterogeneous spatio-temporal patterns. Node-wise expert specialization can tailor predictions to local behavior.

Method: Freeze multiple spatio-temporal GNN forecasting experts. Train a graph-conditioned router to assign per-node mixing weights based on graph topology and recent input. Train only the routing module. Optional bounded graph-conditioned output refinement layer. Include node-adaptive ST-LoRA adapters as an ablation. Evaluate on PEMS04, PEMS07, METR-LA, PEMS-BAY.

Result: GC-MoE yields MAE improvements over a zero-parameter ensemble baseline across all four benchmarks; competitive RMSE and MAPE. Parameter count: ~17k trainable parameters on top of 1.5M frozen expert weights.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective node-wise specialization via graph-conditioned MoE with minimal trainable parameters, offering scalable personalized spatio-temporal forecasting. Code released at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal forecasting on sensor graphs is commonly tackled with a single backbone architecture applied uniformly across all nodes, although graph regions can exhibit different dynamics. Road segments differ in functional class, structure, and traffic behavior, suggesting that node-wise expert specialization can be useful. We propose GC-MoE, a graph-conditioned mixture of experts framework that assigns each node a personalized combination of frozen forecasting experts based on graph topology and the recent traffic input window. GC-MoE combines frozen pretrained spatio-temporal GNN experts with an input-aware, spatially contextualized router while training only a lightweight routing module. We also study a bounded graph-conditioned output refinement layer as an optional extension and include node-adaptive ST-LoRA adapters only as an ablation diagnostic. Across four standard benchmarks (PEMS04, PEMS07, METR-LA, and PEMS-BAY), GC-MoE improves MAE over a zero-parameter ensemble baseline, with competitive RMSE and MAPE, while training only ~17K parameters on top of 1.5M frozen expert weights. The implementation is available at https://github.com/Ahghaffari/gc_moe.

</details>


### [175] [MAAT: Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30514)
*Suryash Yagnik,Shubham Gaur,Saksham Thakur,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha,Amitava Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Balanced benchmarking (5WBENCH) and MAAT for targeted unlearning


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unlearning evaluations are skewed toward non-causal knowledge; need balanced measurement of why-type, causal questions

Method: Create 5WBENCH with 5W categories (Who/What/When/Where/Why), 1,000 examples each; analyze Why difficulty (multi-hop, 40.1-token spans); propose MAAT with three phases on LoRA adapters: gradient-projected ascent, SVD pruning, task-vector negation, and hybrid KL-hidden-state retain repair

Result: 5WBENCH exposes baseline failures; MAAT achieves high forgetting and high retention on Why-type, improving the forget-retain Pareto frontier; code released

Conclusion: First quantitative treatment of Why-type causal unlearning; opens Pareto-efficient design space; code public

Abstract: Machine unlearning evaluation is structurally skewed: Why-type questions, which probe causal and relational knowledge, comprise less than 0.06% of CounterFact, 0.6% of ZSRE, and less than 1.3% of TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP-Cyber. This near-zero representation means that methods that fail on causal knowledge can score highly in aggregate, and this failure is undetectable without balanced evaluation. We present 5WBENCH, a balanced 5,000-sample benchmark with 1,000 examples per 5W category (Who, What, When, Where, Why), making causal unlearning failures quantifiable for the first time. Using 5WBENCH, we show that no existing baseline simultaneously achieves high forgetting and high retention on Why-type questions: aggressive forgetting degrades retained knowledge, while conservative methods fail to forget causal facts. Why-type difficulty stems from multi-hop reasoning chains (44% of Why entries vs. less than or equal to 2% for others) and gradient dilution over 40.1-token answer spans. We present MAAT (Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning), a three-phase framework operating on LoRA adapter weights, combining gradient-projected ascent, SVD rank-dimension pruning, task vector negation, and hybrid KL-hidden-state retain repair. MAAT is the first method to simultaneously achieve high forgetting and high retention on Why-type causal knowledge, reaching a new operating point on the forget-retain Pareto frontier. We make our code publicly available.

</details>


### [176] [Revisiting Padded Transformer Expressivity: Which Architectural Choices Matter and Which Don't](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30523)
*Anej Svete,William Merrill,Ryan Cotterell,Ashish Sabharwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Padded transformers simulate circuit classes via padding and precision/depth choices; constant-precision pad -> L-uniform AC^0; growing-precision pad -> L-uniform TC^0; log-depth looping yields FO-uniform AC^d/TC^d; width beyond log and beyond precision does not increase expressivity; results hold for softmax and average hard attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide exact, robust characterizations of transformer expressivity in terms of circuit complexity, addressing sensitivity to modeling choices and clarifying the role of padding, depth, precision, and attention type.

Method: Analyze padded transformer architectures under practical assumptions, relating their computational power to L-uniform AC^0/TC^0 and FO-uniform circuit classes; distinguish constant vs growing precision; use looping to simulate sequential processing; treat both softmax and average hard attention.

Result: Establish formal equivalences: polynomially padded L-uniform constant-precision transformers ≡ L-uniform AC^0; growing-precision → L-uniform TC^0; log^d N loops with constant precision → FO-uniform AC^d; growing-precision loops → FO-uniform TC^d; width or precision beyond logarithmic does not increase expressivity; results hold for both attention types.

Conclusion: Padded transformers provide robust, depth- and precision-driven characterizations of transformer expressivity; the main determinants are numeric precision and depth, with padding enabling circuit-like computation; the findings bridge transformer models and classical circuit complexity and are robust to attention type and width constraints.

Abstract: Recent work describes what transformers can and cannot compute through connections to boolean circuits, but existing results lack exact characterizations and are sensitive to modeling choices. Padded transformers -- to whose input filler symbols such as ``...'' are appended -- emerge as a useful gadget for establishing equivalences to circuit classes by providing polynomial space for adaptive parallel computation. However, only a limited set of padded transformer idealizations has been studied, leaving open how robustly these equivalences hold under changes to attention type, model width, and uniformity. We find that, under practical assumptions, padded transformers are surprisingly robust to all of these, and identify numeric precision and model depth as the main factors affecting expressivity. Concretely, we prove that polynomially padded $\text{L-uniform}$ constant-precision transformers are equivalent to $\text{L-uniform AC}^0$, while growing-precision ones achieve $\text{L-uniform TC}^0$ regardless of width. Furthermore, looping enables sequential processing analogous to circuits: $\log^d N$-looped constant-precision transformers reach $\text{FO-uniform AC}^d$, and growing-precision ones reach $\text{FO-uniform TC}^d$. Interestingly, growing width or precision beyond logarithmic does not increase expressivity, and all our results hold for both softmax and average hard attention transformers.

</details>


### [177] [Representation Collapse in Sequential Post-Training of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30524)
*Yichen Liu,Mingyu Chen,Hao Wang,Xiaoran Xu,Chenxi Lin,Rui Zhang,Yutong Zhou,Yuxin Yang,Jiarui Wu,Wei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential post-training stages compress representations into low-rank, anisotropic, homogeneous spaces, predicting lower plasticity and weaker generalization; lightweight interventions can mitigate this while preserving gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how multi-stage post-training reshapes internal representations and how this affects future adaptability, generalization, and calibration, with a view to designing remedies.

Method: Define a measurement suite for hidden states, logits, token trajectories, and LoRA updates; analyze supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, safety/refusal tuning, math/code specialization, and long chain-of-thought tuning under controlled stage orderings; evaluate interventions (mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, LoRA decorrelation).

Result: Concentrated representations correlate with reduced plasticity, weaker out-of-domain generalization, and poorer calibration; interventions can preserve future learnability without sacrificing behavioral gains.

Conclusion: Careful management of representation geometry during staged post-training is essential; propose practical strategies to maintain adaptability and calibration while retaining improvements from post-training.

Abstract: Large language models are now adapted through chains of post-training stages rather than through a single instruction-tuning pass. This paper studies whether such sequential post-training gradually compresses internal representations into low-rank, anisotropic, and homogeneous feature spaces. We define a measurement suite for hidden states, logits, token trajectories, and LoRA updates, and we use it to analyze supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, safety/refusal tuning, math and code specialization, and long chain-of-thought tuning under controlled stage orderings. The central hypothesis is that excessive representation concentration is not merely a geometric curiosity: it predicts reduced plasticity during later adaptation, weaker out-of-domain generalization, and poorer calibration. We further evaluate lightweight interventions, including mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, and LoRA update decorrelation, as ways to preserve future learnability without giving up the behavioral gains of post-training.

</details>


### [178] [Measuring, Localizing, and Ablating Alignment Signatures in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30526)
*Aniket Anand,Janvijay Singh,Zhewei Sun,Dilek Hakkani-Tür,Nick Feamster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training alignment shifts model style toward AI-like patterns detectable by detectors; the authors localize this signature and causally test it with a training-free ablation method (PASTA) that reduces detectability while preserving coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether post-training alignment produces AI-like stylistic regularities, whether these are localized in internal representations, and whether they can be causally tested.

Method: Compare human text, base-model generations, and aligned-model generations under matched prefixes; introduce PASTA, a training-free method estimating the alignment signature from aligned-base residuals and ablating the corresponding direction during decoding; evaluate across 11 aligned models and 6 AI detectors.

Result: Aligned generations show lower affinity to human corpus and higher AI-detection rates; PASTA lowers detection rates for most aligned models, with effects transferring across detectors and not replicable by random directions; qualitative analysis indicates maintained relevance and coherence with greater stylistic variation.

Conclusion: AI-like stylistic effects of post-training are measurable, localized, and causally testable via activation ablation; PASTA provides a training-free means to probe and modulate post-training alignment signatures.

Abstract: Aligned language models often exhibit a recognizable AI-like style, yet its connection to post-training and internal representations remains poorly understood. In this work, we study whether post-training introduces or amplifies AI-like stylistic regularities and whether these regularities have a localized internal signature. To this end, we compare human text, base-model generations, and aligned-model generations under matched human-source prefixes. Aligned generations show lower human-corpus affinity and higher AI-detection rates than base generations, suggesting that post-training shifts generated text away from human-corpus style and toward detector-visible AI-like text. We then introduce PASTA (Post-training Alignment Signature Targeted Ablation), a training-free method that estimates a post-training alignment signature from aligned-base residual contrasts and ablates the corresponding direction during decoding. Across 11 aligned models and 6 AI detectors, PASTA lowers the detection rate for most aligned models; this effect transfers well across detectors and is not reproduced by random directions. Qualitative analysis suggests that PASTA generations remain relevant and coherent while exhibiting greater stylistic variation. Together, these results show that AI-like stylistic effects of post-training can be measured, localized, and causally tested through activation ablation.

</details>


### [179] [The Long-Term Effects of Data Selection in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30537)
*Yuxin Yang,Aoxiong Zeng,Xiangquan Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Long-horizon data selection for multi-stage fine-tuning can yield myopic strategies that boost short-term performance but hurt future adaptation; a Long-Horizon Aware Selection (LHAS) objective is proposed to balance immediate utility with coverage, transfer potential, and anti-concentration to shape learning trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data selection methods optimize short-term utility and sample quality, but little work evaluates how selections affect long-run learning dynamics, forgetting, robustness, and adaptation speed across multiple fine-tuning stages.

Method: Introduce a unified multi-stage evaluation protocol and compare representative data-selection families (random, loss-based, gradient-based, diversity-based, quality-based, and utility-diversity). Conduct controlled experiments to observe rank-reversal effects, formalize as myopic selection, provide local analysis, and propose LHAS—augmenting immediate utility with coverage, future-proxy transfer, and anti-concentration terms.

Result: Demonstrate rank reversal: selectors that improve current-stage performance can slow subsequent learning and increase forgetting. The paper formalizes this as myopic selection, provides a local explanatory analysis, and shows that LHAS can mitigate these issues by accounting for long-horizon considerations.

Conclusion: Data selection should be treated as a training intervention that shapes the model’s learning trajectory rather than merely a local data-efficiency tool. Long-horizon-aware objectives, like LHAS, are needed to ensure sustained adaptability, reduced forgetting, and improved out-of-distribution robustness across stages.

Abstract: Data selection is increasingly used to reduce the cost of large language model (LLM) fine-tuning, with recent methods prioritizing samples by current utility, diversity, quality, or influence. This paper studies a different question: when fine-tuning occurs over multiple stages, can selection strategies that look optimal now make the model less adaptable later? We introduce a long-horizon view of LLM data selection in which a selector is evaluated not only by immediate task performance, but also by future adaptation speed, forgetting, capability imbalance, and out-of-distribution robustness. We compare representative random, loss-based, gradient-based, diversity-based, quality-based, and utility-diversity selection families under a unified multi-stage protocol. Through controlled experiments designed to instantiate this protocol, we show how short-term selectors can exhibit rank reversal: they improve the current stage while slowing subsequent learning and increasing forgetting. We formalize this behavior as \emph{myopic selection}, provide a simple local analysis of why it can occur, and propose a diagnostic Long-Horizon Aware Selection (LHAS) objective that augments immediate utility with coverage, future-proxy transfer, and anti-concentration terms. The study argues that data selection should be evaluated as a training intervention that shapes the model's learning trajectory, rather than only as a local data-efficiency mechanism.

</details>


### [180] [DisasterLex: An Expert Concept-to-Schema Knowledge Graph for Geospatial Reasoning in Disaster Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30538)
*Yiming Xiao,Ankit Basu,Kai Yin,Sahil Vartak,Christian Swords,Ali Mostafavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DisasterLex introduces an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) to mediate natural language-to-SQL queries over disaster-domain tabular data. It uses a four-stage orchestration to ground SQL from a domain-specific knowledge graph, achieving 1.4x–2.75x improvements over four baselines across seven base models on a 75-query test. Code, data, and the EKG are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disaster-management requires querying heterogeneous geospatial tables and reasoning about causal relationships. General text-to-SQL models struggle with domain-specific schemas and cross-table reasoning; a domain-aligned knowledge graph can facilitate routing, planning, and grounding of SQL queries.

Method: DisasterLex builds an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) containing 107 concepts, 117 causal edges, and 52 concept-to-schema links. It orchestrates four stages: identify query entities, route to the operational domain, plan over causal edges, and ground the SQL, restricting the schema input to the model at each step. The system is instantiated on a disaster-analytics database (36 geospatial tables, 150 columns).

Result: On a 75-query test set, DisasterLex beats four state-of-the-art baselines (LightRAG, HippoRAG 2, ReFoRCE, CHESS) by 1.4x to 2.75x, with absolute scores ranging from 1.65 to 3.56 out of 5.0. Error analysis indicates that baseline failures primarily occur in routing and multi-table SQL composition, which the orchestration targets.

Conclusion: The KG-mediated orchestration effectively improves NL-to-SQL in disaster analytics by providing structured domain knowledge and explicit planning for cross-table reasoning; the approach, data, and EKG are publicly available.

Abstract: Disasters are inevitable and increasingly costly, and effective response depends on querying structured tabular data: precise, information-dense records of hazard, exposure, vulnerability, and lifeline infrastructure that underpin disaster management. Current text-to-SQL methods enable natural-language access to such tables but transfer poorly to the disaster domain, where queries span heterogeneous geospatial schemas and require reasoning over causal relations. We introduce DisasterLex, a knowledge-graph-mediated framework that inserts an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) of curated concepts and typed causal edges between the user query and the database, bridged to schema by concept-to-table links. The orchestration runs four stages (identifying query entities, routing to the operational domain, planning over causal edges, and grounding the SQL), restricting the schema passed to the model at each step.
