We instantiate it on a disaster-analytics database (36 geospatial tables, 150 columns) with an EKG of 107 concepts, 117 causal edges, and 52 concept-to-schema links, evaluated on a 75-query test set. On all seven base models spanning proprietary and open-weight families, DisasterLex beats four state-of-the-art baselines (LightRAG, HippoRAG 2, ReFoRCE, CHESS) by 1.4x to 2.75x, with absolute scores of 1.65 to 3.56 (of 5.0). Error analysis shows baseline failures cluster in routing and multi-table SQL composition, the operations our orchestration explicitly addresses. Code, data, and the EKG artifact are available at https://github.com/YimingXiao98/DisasterLex and on Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20388029.

</details>


### [181] [SubsurfaceGen: Procedural Generation of Field-Scale Earth Models and Seismic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30541)
*Joseph Stitt,Pratik Rathore,Madeleine Udell,Ching-Yao Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SubsurfaceGen, a GPU-accelerated generator for 3D velocity models and seismic data, and a large, field-scale ML-ready dataset for FWI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML-based full waveform inversion (FWI) requires field-scale, geologically diverse, and physically realistic training data; existing datasets (e.g., Marmousi, SEAM, OpenFWI) are insufficient in spatial/temporal extent, geological diversity, and realism.

Method: Develop SubsurfaceGen to generate 3D velocity models and seismic data. Release a paired dataset of 4,276 2D velocity slices, 5 s wavefields, and 8 s shot gathers drawn from 42 realistic, field-scale 3D velocity models (10 km x 10 km x 6.19 km at 10 m resolution), spanning six geological settings (four SubsurfaceGen-built, two from prior sources). Evaluate neural operators on wavefield prediction and encoder–decoders on end-to-end velocity inversion, with one geological setting held out for out-of-distribution (OOD) testing.

Result: The work surfaces failure modes of ML-based FWI at field scale and demonstrates that SubsurfaceGen and the accompanying dataset can meaningfully impact ML-based FWI by enabling realistic training, benchmarking, and OOD evaluation.

Conclusion: SubsurfaceGen and the dataset advance ML-based FWI by providing scalable, diverse, and physically realistic data, enabling robust evaluation (including OOD) and potential impact on carbon sequestration and hydrocarbon exploration.

Abstract: Full waveform inversion (FWI) is the gold standard for subsurface imaging, with applications from carbon sequestration to energy and mineral exploration to earthquake hazard assessment. Machine learning approaches to FWI need field-scale, geologically diverse, and physically realistic training data, but existing resources such as Marmousi, SEAM, and OpenFWI fall short on spatial extent, temporal extent, geological diversity, and physical realism. We address these limitations with SubsurfaceGen, a GPU-accelerated generator for 3D velocity models and seismic data. Along with SubsurfaceGen, we release a paired dataset of 4,276 2D velocity slices, 5 s wavefields, and 8 s shot gathers drawn from 42 realistic, field-scale 3D velocity models, each spanning 10 km x 10 km laterally and 6.19 km deep at 10 m resolution. The dataset spans six geological settings -- four built with SubsurfaceGen and two drawn from prior sources -- relevant for carbon sequestration and hydrocarbon exploration. We use this dataset to evaluate neural operators on wavefield prediction and encoder-decoders on end-to-end velocity inversion, holding out one geological setting for out-of-distribution testing. These experiments surface failure modes at field-scale and demonstrate how SubsurfaceGen and the associated dataset can impact ML-based FWI.

</details>


### [182] [Early Prediction of Future Behavioral Strategy from Process Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30550)
*Robert Kasumba,Dennis Barbour,Chien-Ju Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM) fuses partial process traces from source tasks to infer a shared person-level latent structure and predict target-task strategy; demonstrated on PowerWash Simulator and synthetic simulations, enabling early prediction when target-task data is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Infer stable person-level tendencies from limited evidence across tasks; address limitations of aggregate summaries and single-task process models; enable early cross-task predictions.

Method: Introduce PLVM that encodes task-specific process traces and merges them into a shared latent representation; use partial traces from source tasks to predict strategy in held-out target task; validate with naturalistic telemetry data and controlled simulations.

Result: Cross-task fusion improves prediction of target-task strategy; in PowerWash Simulator, partial traces from two cleaning tasks predict Zone Planner vs Zone Hopper behavior in a held-out Fire Station level; simulations show benefit when source tasks reveal complementary latent dimensions.

Conclusion: Process-level cross-task modeling can enable early prediction of target-task strategy with limited target-task data, by leveraging transferable person-level structure learned across tasks.

Abstract: Adaptive systems often need to make task-specific decisions about people from limited evidence: a tutor may need to anticipate how a learner will approach a new problem, a game may need to adapt when a player enters a new level, and a human-AI system may need to infer whether a partner will persist with a plan or switch goals. These decisions depend on person-level tendencies that shape how people solve related tasks, but such tendencies are difficult to infer from standard behavioral evidence. One approach is to use aggregate outcome summaries, such as scores, completion rates, or productivity; these summaries are compact and available across tasks, but can collapse distinct behavioral processes into similar outcomes. Another approach is to use process-level traces, which record how behavior unfolds; however, process modeling within one task can entangle stable person-level tendencies with task-specific layout and affordances. In this work, we study early cross-task behavioral inference: whether partial source-task process traces can reveal transferable person-level structure that predicts strategy in a held-out target task. We introduce a Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM), which encodes task-specific traces and fuses them into a shared person-level latent representation for cross-task prediction. In PowerWash Simulator, a naturalistic telemetry dataset of human gameplay, PLVM uses partial traces from two cleaning tasks to predict locally persistent Zone Planner behavior versus frequent Zone Hopper behavior in the held-out Fire Station level. Controlled simulations with known latent types show that cross-task fusion helps when source tasks reveal complementary dimensions of a shared latent process. These results suggest that process-level cross-task modeling can support early prediction of target-task strategy when observing sufficient target-task behavior is impractical.

</details>


### [183] [Destruction is a General Strategy to Learn Generation; Diffusion's Strength is to Take it Seriously; Exploration is the Future](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30553)
*Pierre-André Noël*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion models are framed as a destroy-then-generate family that withholds information from inputs, offering a flexible alternative to handcrafted information withholding, especially in data-scarce settings. The work discusses challenges in porting reinforcement learning techniques to diffusion contexts, explores diffusion-native exploration ideas, and provides a tutorial that introduces a novel probabilistic graphical model to aid exposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivate diffusion models as a flexible mechanism for information withholding beyond hand-crafted techniques, with potential benefits in data-scarce regimes. Address the translation of reinforcement learning concepts to the diffusion setting and stimulate exploration of diffusion-native solutions.

Method: Conceptual analysis and synthesis: frame diffusion models as destroy-then-generate information withholding; critique RL-adaptation to diffusion; present a tutorial expanding on this perspective; introduce a novel probabilistic graphical model to facilitate exposition.

Result: No definitive empirical results are claimed; the work presents directions, a tutorial, and a theoretical/diagrammatic framework (the new probabilistic graphical model) to guide future research.

Conclusion: The abstract suggests a promising, flexible paradigm (destroy-then-generate diffusion) for information withholding and exploration in diffusion contexts, with guidelines and tools (tutorial and graphical model) to pursue future work, particularly in data-scarce settings.

Abstract: I present diffusion models as part of a family of machine learning techniques that withhold information from a model's input and train it to guess the withheld information. I argue that diffusion's destroying approach to withholding is more flexible than typical hand-crafted information withholding techniques, providing a rich training playground that could be advantageous in some settings, notably data-scarce ones. I then address subtle issues that may arise when porting reinforcement learning techniques to the diffusion context, and wonder how such exploration problems could be addressed in more diffusion-native ways. I do not have definitive answers, but I do point my fingers in directions I deem interesting. A tutorial follows this thesis, expanding on the destroy-then-generate perspective. A novel kind of probabilistic graphical models is introduced to facilitate the tutorial's exposition.

</details>


### [184] [Supervised Training Rapidly Degrades Early Visual Cortex Alignment Across Biologically Plausible Learning Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30556)
*Nils Leutenegger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Untrained networks can match early visual cortex in RSA; training reduces V1 alignment, especially with backpropagation; predictive coding and STDP preserve brain-like structure; LOC alignment shows minor BP-related gains; suggests inductive biases drive low-level statistics and that global error signals reshape early representations more than local rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether learning improves or degrades brain-model alignment and whether different learning rules differentially shape representational similarity to human cortex.

Method: Compare four learning rules (BP, FA, PC, STDP) using 720 THINGS images and fMRI data from 3 subjects across 6 visual ROIs. Compute representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) for model activations and brain data, then Spearman-correlate them at eight training checkpoints (epochs 0–40). Focus on V1 and LOC alignment changes across training.

Result: Training reduces V1 RSA alignment across rules (BP largest drop: delta r ≈ -0.080; PC and STDP smaller drops: delta r ≈ -0.04). Untrained networks capture low-level statistics via inductive biases. BP causes the most restructuring of early representations due to global error signals, while PC/STDP preserve brain-like structure more. In LOC, BP shows a small but opposite trend with slight alignment increase during training.

Conclusion: Untrained architectures can already capture essential low-level visual statistics; learning via backpropagation reshapes early representations more aggressively than local learning rules, which better preserve brain-like representational structure. These results challenge the notion that learning uniformly improves brain alignment and highlight rule-dependent effects on RSA across visual hierarchies.

Abstract: Random, untrained neural networks consistently match or exceed trained networks in representational similarity to early visual cortex. This puzzling finding challenges the assumption that learning improves brain alignment. We investigate it by tracking representational similarity analysis (RSA) alignment to human fMRI data across training for four learning rules: backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP). Using 720 object images from the THINGS database and fMRI data from three subjects across six visual ROIs, we measure Spearman correlations between model and brain representational dissimilarity matrices at eight training checkpoints (epochs 0-40). We find that (1) a single epoch of training reduces V1 alignment by 25-90%, depending on the learning rule; (2) backpropagation reduces V1 alignment most severely (delta r = -0.080), while predictive coding and STDP preserve substantially more (delta r ~ -0.04); and (3) a weaker, opposite tendency appears in object-selective cortex (LOC), where BP shows the largest increase in alignment during training, although the absolute change is small. These results suggest that untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics through inductive biases alone, and that global error signals (BP) reshape early representations more aggressively than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better preserve brain-like structure.

</details>


### [185] [Zeroth-Order Non-Log-Concave Sampling with Variance Reduction and Applications to Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30573)
*M. Berk Sahin,Behzad Sharif,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin sampler for high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions, with non-asymptotic guarantees in epsilon-relative Fisher information (and TV distance under Poincaré). Also proposes ZO-APMC for black-box inverse problems using score-based priors, with non-asymptotic guarantees and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-box, high-dimensional sampling where gradients are unavailable or expensive. Zeroth-order methods suffer from high variance and lack non-asymptotic guarantees, especially for non-log-concave targets.

Method: Develop a variance-reduced gradient estimator for zeroth-order Langevin dynamics, reducing estimator variance and removing adverse dimension dependence of batch size. Prove non-asymptotic convergence bounds in epsilon-relative Fisher information, and TV under Poincaré. Introduce ZO-APMC for posterior sampling with pretrained score-based priors, with non-asymptotic guarantees. Validate via synthetic and practical experiments on linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

Result: First non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling in the epsilon-rel. Fisher information; TV bounds under Poincaré; ZO-APMC with theoretical guarantees; empirical results showing strong performance.

Conclusion: The work advances black-box sampling by delivering variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin methods with non-asymptotic guarantees and practical posterior sampling algorithms leveraging score-based priors, supported by synthetic and real-world inverse problem experiments.

Abstract: Sampling from high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions with unnormalized densities remains a fundamental challenge in machine learning, particularly in black-box settings where gradient information is inaccessible or computationally prohibitive. While Langevin dynamics provides a principled framework for sampling when gradients are accessible, its extension to the black-box settings suffers from high variance and lacks non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for non-log-concave sampling. To address these limitations, we propose a variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin sampling method. Our method employs a gradient estimator that substantially reduces the variance of the classical batched zeroth-order estimator and eliminates the unfavorable dimensional dependence of the batch size required for accurate estimation, enabling practical and stable sampling. We establish the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling in terms of $\varepsilon$-relative Fisher information, and, under a Poincaré inequality assumption, squared total variation distance. We further propose ZO-APMC, a posterior sampling algorithm for black-box inverse problems with pre-trained score-based generative priors, establishing the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for such methods. We validate our theory through synthetic experiments and demonstrate strong empirical performance on practical linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

</details>


### [186] [Benchmarking Machine Learning Uncertainty Quantification Methodologies for Predicting Turbine Gas Temperature Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30585)
*Jostein Barry-Straume,Changmin Son,Adrian Sandu,Gavan Burke,Rekha Sundararajan,Andrew Rimell,James G. Steinrock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compare five neural network uncertainty quantification methods (Delta, Bayesian Monte Carlo Dropout, Bootstrap, Lower-Upper Bound Estimation, Mean-Variance Estimation) for turbine gas temperature; unified cross-validated framework; evaluate coverage and interval sharpness; results show trade-offs and guidance for engine health management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable predictive intervals are critical for prognosis and safety in modern engines.

Method: Implement five UQ methods within a unified experimental framework using cross-validation for hyperparameters, repeated train-test splits for robustness, and evaluations with Coverage Probability, Normalized Mean Prediction Interval Width, and Coverage Width-based Criterion; applied to turbine gas temperature dataset.

Result: Methods exhibit distinct trade-offs in coverage, width, and stability; overall guidance provided for selecting and tuning interval methods in engine health management.

Conclusion: The study offers practical guidance on uncertainty quantification choices for neural models in turbine temperature prediction, balancing interpretability and precision.

Abstract: Effective prognostics and health management of modern engines relies on accurate turbine gas temperature predictions and robust uncertainty quantification to ensure reliability and safety. This paper investigates five major approaches for constructing prediction intervals -- namely the Delta method, Bayesian Monte Carlo Dropout, Bootstrap method, Lower-Upper Bound Estimation, and Mean-Variance Estimation -- as a means of capturing the uncertainty in neural network predictions of turbine gas temperature. Each approach is implemented within a unified experimental framework that employs cross-validation for hyperparameter selection, repeated train-test splits for performance robustness, and multiple metrics to evaluate both the accuracy and tightness of the intervals. In particular, Coverage Probability, Normalized Mean Prediction Interval Width, and the Coverage Width-based Criterion are measured to comprehensively assess each method's reliability and sharpness. Experiments conducted on a representative turbine gas temperature dataset reveal distinct trade-offs among the five methods in terms of interval coverage, width, and stability. These findings provide a practical guide for selecting and tuning prediction interval methods in engine health management and prognostics, ensuring both interpretability and precision in real-world applications.

</details>


### [187] [Counterfactual Evaluation Reveals Hidden Capability Profiles in Clinical LLMs and Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30590)
*Matt Turk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSS is a new interventional metric for clinical AI evaluating causal sensitivity; it reveals responsiveness differences beyond CMS, surface safety blind spots, and translates to tool-using agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inadequacies of coverage-based metrics in capturing how models adapt to clinically meaningful input changes; ensure safety and reliability in decision-support.

Method: Pre-registered interventional study mutating oncology tumor-board cases along five clinically meaningful dimensions (biomarker flips, prior-treatment failures, biomarker removals, surgery-status changes, stage perturbations) and scoring whether models update in the correct direction on a {0,0.5,1.0} scale; comparison with CMS across six frontier models from three labs in single-shot inference on 224 cases; also includes a ReAct-style tool-use phase, cross-judge replication, and three-rater medical-professional validation.

Result: CSS re-orders model rankings opposite to CMS for all six frontier models; CMS-worst becomes CSS-best; a universal safety blind spot is revealed in surgery-status interventions (e.g., ≤17.2% CSS on Family D); tool-use improves CSS for five of six models but the lowest-CSS model still fails to update; cross-judge and three-rater validation support findings.

Conclusion: Interventional, pre-registered metrics like CSS complement coverage metrics by capturing model responsiveness to clinically meaningful perturbations, providing a dense reward signal for agentic RL systems and aiding safety-focused evaluation.

Abstract: Two clinical AI systems can score nearly identically on coverage-based rubrics yet behave radically differently when their patient inputs change: one updates its recommendations to match the new clinical signal, while the other produces the same output regardless. We introduce the Causal Sensitivity Score (CSS), a pre-registered interventional metric that mutates oncology tumor-board cases along five clinically meaningful dimensions - biomarker flips, prior-treatment failures, biomarker removals, surgery-status changes, and stage perturbations - and scores whether each model updates its recommendations in the pre-registered correct direction using a {0, 0.5, 1.0} scale. Benchmarked against the Consensus Match Score (CMS), a coverage-based weighted recall metric, six frontier models from three labs evaluated in single-shot inference across 224 cases rank in nearly opposite orders: all six models change rank, the CMS-worst model becomes CSS-best, and one upper-mid CMS model ranks last on CSS. We further surface a universal safety blind spot: every frontier model fails on surgery-status interventions (at most 17.2% CSS on Family D), a finding CMS does not expose. The metric also transfers to tool-using agents: in a ReAct-style experiment, tool use improves CSS for five of six models (+2.5 to +20.3 percentage points), yet the lowest-CSS model retrieves the same chart sections and still fails to update its recommendations - revealing a structural responsiveness deficit visible only under counterfactual evaluation. Cross-judge replication and three-rater medical-professional validation confirm the aggregate findings. Interventional pre-registered metrics like CSS complement coverage-based evaluation for clinical AI agents: they capture responsiveness that coverage metrics miss and offer a candidate dense reward signal for future agentic RL systems.

</details>


### [188] [Learning Transferable Predictability Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30592)
*Diyali Goswami,Auroop R. Ganguly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Gauge-Fixed Ordinal Network (GON) assigns interpretable five-level predictability scores to short trajectory windows, fixing gauge ambiguity with an anchor-and-variance objective so scores transfer across systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for cross-system, numerically consistent predictability scores that go beyond simple deterministic-versus-stochastic discrimination and enable transfer, model selection, and early-warning diagnostics.

Method: Propose GON, a temporal convolutional model on 2-jet trajectory features, trained with an anchor-and-variance objective to fix level-wise means to shared coordinates (gauge fixing) across five predictability levels. Evaluation on five held-out dynamical systems, with pretrained initialization vs scratch, across various window budgets; zero-shot and adaptation-depth analyses.

Result: Pretrained GON consistently outperforms scratch training across all window budgets on five held-out systems; zero-shot scores retain ordinal structure near stochastic boundaries; adaptation depth correlates with geometric proximity to the training family; pairwise discrimination and globally coherent ordinal scoring are distinct properties requiring a stable score coordinate; across transfer, the anchored, gauge-fixed coordinates enable meaningful cross-system interpretation.

Conclusion: GON yields stable, cross-system ordinal scores for predictability, enabling consistent model selection, early-warning diagnostics, and cross-domain transfer in dynamical systems.

Abstract: We study the problem of assigning a scalar score to a short trajectory window that reflects its position on an ordered continuum of predictability regimes, spanning structured deterministic dynamics to unstructured stochastic noise. Existing methods address deterministic-versus-stochastic discrimination within a single system and do not produce scores with a consistent numerical interpretation across systems. We formalize this as ordinal estimation over a five-level predictability ladder and identify a structural source of cross-system ambiguity: ranking supervision alone leaves the score coordinate unfixed up to a monotone reparameterization, which we term the gauge freedom of ordinal scoring. We propose the Gauge-Fixed Ordinal Network (GON), a temporal convolutional model trained with an anchor-and-variance objective that pins level-wise score means to shared target coordinates. GON operates on 2-jet features that expose local trajectory geometry, preserved by smooth flows and disrupted by stochastic surrogate procedures. On five held-out dynamical systems, initializing from a pretrained GON checkpoint consistently outperforms training from scratch across all window budgets, with adaptation depth reflecting geometric proximity to the training family. Zero-shot scores retain ordinal structure at the stochastic boundary, where surrogate procedures most strongly disrupt nonlinear geometry, and pretrained initialization consistently beats scratch across all window budgets. Pairwise discrimination and globally coherent ordinal scoring are distinct properties requiring a stable score coordinate for cross-system transfer, with direct implications for predictability assessment, model selection, and early-warning diagnostics across natural and engineered dynamical systems.

</details>


### [189] [Scientific Machine Learning for Engine Health Management and Remaining Useful Life Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30593)
*Jostein Barry-Straume,Changmin Son,Adrian Sandu,Gavan Burke,Rekha Sundararajan,Andrew Rimell,James G. Steinrock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-task deep learning framework for turbine prognostics that jointly forecasts TGTU, DTGT, and RUL with uncertainty via prediction intervals, using a shared encoder and task-specific heads; tunable to align with maintenance policies; evaluated with both point and interval metrics and stratified by operational context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecast remaining useful life and thermal indicators in heterogeneous, non-stationary fleet data while providing calibrated uncertainty to support risk-aware maintenance decisions.

Method: A multi-task architecture with a shared sequence encoder (convolutional front-end, residual bidirectional LSTM, attention pooling) feeding task-specific heads. Includes mean-variance estimation for probabilistic regression and an optional survival head. Tunable through practitioner-facing parameters (e.g., DTGT thresholding, RUL target construction). Evaluation with MAE, PICP, MPIW, CWC; results reported in aggregate and stratified by flight phase and maintenance segment.

Result: The paper demonstrates predictive performance using both point estimates and calibrated prediction intervals, with analyses across flight phase and maintenance segments to reveal operational-context effects and support uncertainty-aware monitoring.

Conclusion: A tunable, multi-task, uncertainty-aware prognostic framework for turbine health management that aligns with in-house policies and provides calibrated interval predictions alongside point estimates, enabling risk-aware maintenance decisions.

Abstract: Engine Health Management (EHM) depends on reliable forecasting of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) and on tracking thermal indicators such as turbine gas temperature (TGT). In practice, real-world fleet data are heterogeneous and non-stationary, and point predictions alone are insufficient for risk-aware maintenance decisions. This paper presents a multi-task scientific machine learning framework for turbine prognostics that jointly predicts turbine gas temperature untrimmed (TGTU), Delta Turbine Gas Temperature (DTGT), and RUL, with quantified uncertainty in the form of prediction intervals whose empirical coverage is evaluated. A shared sequence encoder (convolutional front-end with residual bidirectional LSTM layers and attention pooling) feeds task-specific heads, including mean--variance estimation for probabilistic regression and, optionally, a survival head for threshold-based event modeling. The framework is designed to be tunable via a small set of practitioner-facing parameters (e.g., DTGT thresholding rules and RUL target construction) so that deployment can align with in-house policies and proprietary criteria. The predictive performance of the proposed framework is evaluated using both point and interval metrics, including mean absolute error (MAE), prediction interval coverage probability (PICP), mean prediction interval width (MPIW), and the coverage--width criterion (CWC). Results are reported both in aggregate and stratified by flight phase and maintenance segment to highlight operational-context effects and to support uncertainty-aware monitoring.

</details>


### [190] [Improving Relative Representations with Learned Anchors and Whitened Inner Products](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30596)
*Oscar Thorsted Svendsen,Nikolaj Holst Jakobsen,Fabian Mager,Hiba Nassar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a robust cross-model communication framework by learning semantic prototype anchors and using a geometry-aware similarity that preserves magnitude and is affine-invariant, improving cross-model alignment across vision and language and enabling near-lossless transfer and stable zero-shot communication between heterogeneous architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Independent neural models converge to incompatible latent representations, hindering modular AI. RR offers a shared space via anchor similarities but commonly relies on random anchors and cosine similarity, which fail to capture anisotropic geometries of modern models like Transformers.

Method: Learn anchors as robust semantic prototypes; adopt a geometry-aware similarity metric that preserves discriminative magnitude information and is invariant to affine shifts; evaluate across vision and language tasks, including zero-shot communication between heterogeneous architectures (e.g., small LMs).

Result: Significant gains in performance and consistency across tasks; enables nearly lossless information transfer and stable zero-shot communication between heterogeneous architectures; robust to architecture scale differences.

Conclusion: A robust RR framework with learned semantic prototypes and a geometry-aware, affine-invariant similarity enhances cross-model alignment, enabling modular AI with reliable cross-model communication across vision and language domains.

Abstract: Independently trained neural models typically converge to incompatible latent representations, creating a fundamental barrier to highly modular AI systems. While Relative Representations (RR) address this by mapping absolute coordinates to a shared space defined by similarities to common anchor points, traditional implementations rely on randomly sampled anchors and cosine similarity, which frequently fail to capture the anisotropic geometries of modern architectures like Transformers. In this work, we propose a robust framework for cross-model communication based on two improvements. We learn anchors as robust semantic prototypes and utilize a geometry-aware similarity metric which preserves discriminative magnitude information and is invariant to affine shifts. Our approach demonstrates significant gains in performance and consistency across vision and language tasks. Notably, it enables nearly lossless information transfer and stable zero-shot communication even between highly heterogeneous architectures, such as small language models of varying scales.

</details>


### [191] [ScaleMAP: Preserving Local Density and Neighborhood Structure in Low-Dimensional Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30597)
*Rajas Poorna,Marcus T. Cicerone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ScaleMAP introduces a scale-aware embedding that divides pairwise displacements by the geometric mean of the endpoints’ local radii, preserving density without sacrificing neighborhood structure, and extends to PaCMAP, outperforming UMAP/DensMAP on density metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear DR methods that normalize local distances (e.g., UMAP, PaCMAP) can erase density structure, suppressing sparse bridges between cell types and narrow spectral spikes; a method that preserves density while maintaining neighborhood relations is desirable for heterogeneous, high-dynamic-range data.

Method: Replace the standard embedding update with a scale reparameterization: each pairwise displacement is divided by the geometric mean of the two points’ original-space local radii (a change of variables). This injects scale information rather than acting as a separate density penalty. Apply the same principle to PaCMAP and evaluate across datasets from transcriptomics, hyperspectral imaging, and flow cytometry.

Result: ScaleMAP achieves density preservation comparable to DensMAP while retaining UMAP-level neighborhood preservation. It recovers sparse bridges in transcriptomic data that UMAP collapses and preserves extremely skewed density in flow cytometry (across ~17 orders of magnitude). The PaCMAP adaptation also yields improved density preservation, indicating the approach generalizes beyond UMAP.

Conclusion: A scale-aware reparameterization—dividing embeddings by the geometric mean of local radii—preserves both density structure and local neighborhood relations, offering a generalizable enhancement over existing nonlinear DR methods (UBMAP/PaCMAP/DensMAP) for heterogeneous-density data.

Abstract: Nonlinear dimensionality-reduction methods such as UMAP and PaCMAP adaptively normalize local distances during graph construction, erasing neighborhood scale from the data. This distorts more than relative cluster sizes: sparse structures like bridges between transitioning cell types and narrow spectral spikes in hyperspectral images can be suppressed or lost entirely. DensMAP adds a density penalty to correct this, but this penalty competes with UMAP's attraction-repulsion forces, scattering points far from their neighborhoods. ScaleMAP takes a different approach: each pairwise embedding displacement is divided by the geometric mean of the two endpoints' original-space local radii, re-injecting scale information as a change of variables rather than as a competing objective. Across standard benchmarks and scientific datasets from transcriptomics, hyperspectral imaging, and flow cytometry, ScaleMAP matches DensMAP on density preservation while maintaining UMAP-level neighborhood preservation. In transcriptomic data, it recovers sparse bridges between cell populations that UMAP collapses; in flow cytometry, it faithfully represents density structure across 17 orders of magnitude. The same principle applied to PaCMAP yields consistently improved density preservation, suggesting the approach generalizes beyond UMAP.

</details>


### [192] [When LLM Reward Design Fails: Diagnostic-Driven Refinement for Sparse Structured RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28918)
*Youting Wang,Yuan Tang,Bowen Liu,Xuan Liu,Dingyan Shang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframe LLM-based reward shaping as debugging; introduce diagnostic-driven iterative refinement to fix sparse, structured RL with semantic reward interfaces, yielding large gains over one-shot prompts while revealing boundary conditions and limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address failure modes in LLM-guided reward shaping for sparse, structured tasks under PPO, where semantic reward interfaces can be misinterpreted or overwhelmed by reward signals.

Method: Perform an audit to identify dominant one-shot failure modes (reward flooding, semantic/API misunderstanding, rare weak-shaping). Propose a diagnostic-driven iterative refinement loop guided by training diagnostics and a failure-mode taxonomy; implement taxonomy prompts and dynamic labels; run controlled experiments (budget-matched, Best-of-3, component-removal, sensitivity analyses) across MiniGrid (DoorKey-8x8, KeyCorridor) and MuJoCo as stress tests; compare with static-vocabulary controls and avoid confounding training-time effects.

Result: Refinement dramatically improves success rates: DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6%; KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% (high seed variance). Controls show gains mainly from taxonomy-driven prompts rather than repeated retraining; static vocabulary recovers large portions of gains (87.6%; 70.7% in certain metrics). Budget-matched comparisons separate refinement from selection; across four environments, larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates but with wide bootstrap intervals. Boundary conditions identified: success-based diagnostics can misfire in dense rewards; return-trend feedback helps remove some false positives. The approach is bounded to sparse, structured tasks with reliable interfaces under PPO; event_text fields may have mixed impact.

Conclusion: A debugging-oriented, diagnostic-guided refinement loop yields substantial performance gains in sparse, structured RL with semantic reward interfaces, highlighting the importance of explicit failure-mode diagnosis and dynamic labeling. The method’s benefits are bounded by task type and interface reliability; while gains generalize under the tested conditions, calibration and boundary effects warrant careful diagnostics and further validation.

Abstract: For sparse, structured reinforcement-learning tasks with semantic reward-function interfaces, LLM-generated reward shaping is better framed as debugging than one-shot generation. We study PPO-trained agents using MiniGrid as core evaluation and MuJoCo as boundary stress test. Our audit finds two dominant one-shot failure modes -- reward flooding and semantic/API misunderstanding -- plus a rarer weak-shaping case. We propose diagnostic-driven iterative refinement, where training diagnostics and a failure-mode taxonomy guide targeted reward-function revision. Refinement improves DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6% and KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% with high seed-to-seed variance. Controls show these gains are not from retrying or extra training: metrics-only re-prompting yields large drops, while a static-vocabulary control recovers much of the gap (87.6%; 70.7%), showing the taxonomy prompt is a major mechanism and dynamic labels provide only partially isolated incremental evidence. Budget-matched and Best-of-3 comparisons separate refinement from selection and training-time effects. Component-removal tests, sensitivity analyses, and an audit against author labels provide converging evidence for the debugging interpretation while revealing calibration limits. Continuous-control results show the boundary: success-based diagnostics can misfire in dense-reward locomotion, and return-trend feedback removes one false-positive mechanism without robust gains. The low-call protocol is a cost contrast with population-based reward search, not a benchmark comparison. In four crossed-variance-design environments, point estimates suggest larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates but bootstrap intervals are wide. The method is bounded to sparse structured tasks with reliable interfaces under PPO; fields like event_text may help, hurt, or be neutral.

</details>


### [193] [AMNESIA: A Large Scale Medical Unlearning Benchmark Suite with Disease-Informed Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30599)
*Saeedeh Davoudi,Reihaneh Iranmanesh,Ophir Frieder,Nazli Goharian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AMNESIA is a large-scale, open-source benchmark for medical unlearning comprising 70,560 QA pairs from 8,820 patient notes across 11 disease categories, enabling evaluation of unlearning methods and revealing leakage across patients with same conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical LLMs require unlearning capabilities to remove or forget specific training data. Existing benchmarks use synthetic/general data, leaving clinical unlearning underexplored and inadequately evaluated at scale.

Method: Construction of AMNESIA dataset with 70,560 QA pairs derived from 8,820 de-identified patient notes spanning 11 disease categories; includes both factual recall and clinical inference questions; evaluation of four common unlearning methods at random-patient and disease-level; introduction of a leakage-detection metric to assess residual medical terminology leakage.

Result: Findings indicate that unlearning a single patient can erode knowledge about other patients with the same condition (collateral forgetting); a new metric detects leakage of medical terminology; current unlearning methods fail to fully isolate patient-specific knowledge from shared clinical knowledge, signaling the need for methods that better separate patient data from general medical knowledge.

Conclusion: AMNESIA provides a scalable framework for evaluating clinical unlearning, highlights limitations of existing methods in preserving essential clinical knowledge while erasing targeted data, and motivates development of techniques that disentangle patient-specific information from shared medical knowledge.

Abstract: Medical knowledge is continuously evolving. This creates a need to update or selectively forget information encoded in already-trained medical LLMs. Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific training data from a model without full retraining. Yet, existing unlearning benchmarks rely on synthetic or small-scale general data, leaving clinical unlearning understudied. We introduce AMNESIA, the first large-scale, open source benchmark for medical unlearning, with 70,560 question-answer pairs from 8,820 patient notes across 11 disease categories. AMNESIA includes both factual questions testing direct recall and reasoning questions testing clinical inference. We use it to evaluate four widely used unlearning methods at both random patient and disease-level, and introduce a new metric for detecting leakage of medical terminology. We show that unlearning individual patients erodes knowledge of others with the same condition, calling for methods that can better separate patients from shared clinical knowledge.

</details>


### [194] [The Fast Mixing Mechanism for Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30600)
*Omri Lev,Moshe Shenfeld,Vishwak Srinivasan,Katrina Ligett,Ashia C. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A DP sketching framework using fast transforms is proposed, yielding state-of-the-art privacy guarantees and favorable runtimes that match Gaussian sketches up to constants in certain regimes; when integrated with sketch-based DP linear regression, it delivers a fast, high-utility DP-OLS method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve scalability of differential privacy in linear regression by combining privacy-preserving sketches with fast transform-based computation to reduce runtime while preserving utility.

Method: Develop a new DP sketching mechanism based on fast transforms (e.g., Hadamard-based schemes) and provide privacy/utility analyses; quantify runtimes relative to classical fast sketches and Gaussian sketches; combine with existing sketch-based DP-LR techniques to form a fast DP-OLS algorithm.

Result: The mechanism achieves state-of-the-art privacy guarantees and, in favorable regimes, matches Gaussian sketch privacy up to a constant factor; yields a DP-linear-regression algorithm with strong utility and improved runtime; claims to deliver the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares.

Conclusion: Fast transform-based DP sketching is a viable path to efficient, private linear regression, improving runtime without sacrificing privacy or accuracy and enabling fast DP-OLS when integrated with sketch-based DP-LR approaches.

Abstract: Randomized sketching is a central tool for compressing large-scale optimization problems while preserving accuracy. In particular, sketches that are based on structured matrices, such as the Hadamard matrix, can be applied efficiently and often yield solutions that approximate those of the original problem at much lower computational cost. In differential privacy (DP), Gaussian sketching has been used to solve DP linear regression, beginning with \citet{sheffet2017differentially, sheffet2019old} and later refined by \citet{lev2025gaussianmix, lev2026near}. However, although these methods achieve strong utility guarantees, they usually do not improve runtime over classical DP approaches. In this work, we introduce a new DP sketching mechanism based on fast transforms, which, in certain cases, matches the runtime of classical fast sketching methods. We prove state-of-the-art privacy guarantees for this mechanism and show that, in favorable regimes, they match those of the Gaussian sketch up to a constant factor. As an application, we combine this mechanism with recent sketch-based methods for DP linear regression to obtain a new algorithm with strong utility and improved runtime. We establish privacy and accuracy guarantees for this algorithm, yielding, to the best of our knowledge, the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares.

</details>


### [195] [TASER: Task-Aware Stein Regularisation for Geometry-Driven Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30601)
*Michał Kozyra,Gesine Reinert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TASER is a Stein-regularised training framework that yields data-aware, anisotropic smoothing to improve robustness with minimal clean accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep networks are fragile under distribution shift and adversarial perturbations due to excessive input sensitivity; a training-time regulariser grounded in data density can promote more stable, geometry-consistent predictors.

Method: Introduce TASER based on Langevin Stein operators; penalise pointwise Stein residuals under the training distribution; establish theoretical links to reduced first-order shift sensitivity; provide scalable implementations compatible with modern architectures.

Result: Demonstrates improved robustness and stability across regression and vision benchmarks; CIFAR-10 experiments show adversarial robustness gains for established training methods without significant degradation of clean accuracy.

Conclusion: TASER provides a principled, scalable framework that enforces anisotropic, data-aware smoothness by aligning predictors with data density, improving robustness while preserving clean accuracy on vision tasks.

Abstract: Modern deep networks remain fragile under distribution shift and adversarial perturbations, often due to excessive or poorly structured input sensitivity. We introduce TASER (Task-Aware Stein Regularisation), a training-time regularisation framework derived from Langevin Stein operators. By penalising pointwise Stein residuals under the training distribution, TASER encourages geometric compatibility between predictors and data density, inducing anisotropic, data-aware smoothness. We provide theoretical links between Stein regularisation and reduced first-order shift sensitivity, develop scalable implementation variants compatible with modern architectures, and demonstrate improved robustness and stability across regression and vision benchmarks. Across CIFAR-10 experiments, TASER consistently improves the adversarial robustness of established training methods without incurring statistically significant clean-accuracy degradation.

</details>


### [196] [Constrained Flow Optimization via Sequential Fine Tuning for Molecular Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30610)
*Sven Gutjahr,Riccardo De Santi,Luca Schaufelberger,Kjell Jorner,Andreas Krause*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Constrained Generative Optimization, notably Constrained Flow Optimization (CFO), to balance reward maximization with constraint satisfaction in diffusion/flow models for tasks like molecular design; provides convergence guarantees and empirical validation showing improved rewards with high constraint adherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable, scalable adaptation of generative foundation models to optimize task-specific rewards (e.g., binding affinity) while strictly enforcing practical constraints (e.g., synthesizability) for real-world scientific discovery applications.

Method: Proposes a rigorous optimization-based framework for constrained generation and a concrete algorithm, CFO, that reduces constrained optimization to sequential fine-tuning using established scalable methods (e.g., RL/control). Provides convergence guarantees for both constrained optimization and constrained generation under CFO.

Result: CFO achieves consistent increases in reward while maintaining high constraint satisfaction across synthetic benchmarks and a molecular design task, demonstrating practical utility for constrained generative optimization.

Conclusion: CFO offers a principled, scalable, and convergent approach to constrained generative optimization, enabling reliable trade-offs between objective rewards and constraint satisfaction in diffusion/flow-based generative models.

Abstract: Adapting generative foundation models, in particular diffusion and flow models, to optimize given reward functions (e.g., binding affinity) while satisfying constraints (e.g., molecular synthesizability) is fundamental for their adoption in real-world scientific discovery applications such as molecular design or protein engineering. While recent works have introduced scalable methods for reward-guided fine-tuning of such models via reinforcement learning and control schemes, it remains an open problem how to algorithmically trade-off reward maximization and constraint satisfaction in a reliable and predictable manner. Motivated by this challenge, we first present a rigorous framework for Constrained Generative Optimization, which brings an optimization viewpoint to the introduced adaptation problem and retrieves the relevant task of constrained generation as a sub-case. Then, we introduce Constrained Flow Optimization (CFO), an algorithm that automatically and provably balances reward maximization and constraint satisfaction by reducing the original problem to sequential fine-tuning via established, scalable methods. We provide convergence guarantees for constrained generative optimization and constrained generation via CFO. Ultimately, we present an experimental evaluation of CFO on both synthetic, yet illustrative, settings, and a molecular design task. Across these evaluations, CFO achieves consistent increases in reward while ensuring high constraint satisfaction, showcasing its practical utility for constrained generative optimization.

</details>


### [197] [Improving Selective Classification with Pairwise Queries for Binary Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30615)
*Harsh Vardhan,Sunav Choudhary,Natwar Modani,Arya Mazumdar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pairwise queries to the same model improve selective classification in in-context binary tasks by reducing non-rejected error under budget constraints; theory and experiments show better accuracy-cost tradeoffs than relying on the model's raw confidence alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In selective classification, confidence estimates can be misaligned with true predictions, especially in in-context LLM binary classification, leading to high error on non-rejected samples and inefficient use of expensive expert labeling.

Method: Introduce pairwise queries to the same model to detect high-error samples and incorporate them into selective classification. Provide theoretical conditions under which this simple pairwise-query algorithm outperforms inconsistent confidence estimates. Validate with experiments on 1 synthetic and 4 real in-context learning-based binary classification datasets, demonstrating improved accuracy-cost tradeoffs over using only raw confidence (e.g., next-token logits).

Result: The pairwise-query approach yields a better accuracy-cost tradeoff than relying solely on raw confidence estimates across all tested datasets. Theoretical analysis delineates the conditions under which the approach outperforms inconsistent confidence.

Conclusion: Pairwise querying is a practical enhancement for selective classification in LLM-based binary tasks, improving non-rejected accuracy under budget constraints and offering clear criteria for when to apply pairwise queries to achieve better resource efficiency.

Abstract: In selective classification, a model predicts the labels of data samples where it is confident, and abstains from predicting labels for samples on which it is not confident. The rejected samples are often labeled by an expert, which is expensive. The budget for the expert is best utilized when the model has low error on non-rejected samples. However, the estimate of a model's confidence might be inconsistent with the model's predictions, which can lead to high error on non-rejected points. Such situations can readily occur in in-context binary classification by LLMs. To remedy this, we propose making additional pairwise queries to the same model. These pairwise queries can detect high-error samples and be incorporated into selective classification techniques to reduce the error on non-rejected samples. Theoretically, we establish the conditions under which a simple algorithm using pairwise queries outperforms an inconsistent confidence estimate. We support this insight through extensive experiments for $1$ synthetic and $4$ in-context learning-based real binary classification datasets. In all these cases, we show that our algorithms, using pairwise queries, obtain a better accuracy-cost tradeoff than using only the raw confidence estimates, for instance, the LLM's next-token logits.

</details>


### [198] [Active Timepoint Selection for Learning Measure-Valued Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30625)
*Nicolas Huynh,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for active experimentation in Wasserstein space using Linearized Optimal Transport to embed distribution snapshots in a tangent space, enabling Gaussian-process-based uncertainty-driven measurement scheduling; outperforms baselines on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inferring continuous probability paths from sparse, destructive, and costly measurements in domains like single-cell biology; standard Euclidean metrics and interpolation lack epistemic uncertainty, and objects live in infinite-dimensional Wasserstein space.

Method: Extend active experimentation to the space of measures by mapping distributions with Linearized Optimal Transport (LOT) into a tangent space suitable for Gaussian Process modeling; build a probabilistic surrogate for the probability path and use an acquisition policy to select measurement times that minimize uncertainty.

Result: Empirical evaluations show the proposed policy outperforms uncertainty-agnostic baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: LOT-based tangent-space GP surrogate enables tractable, uncertainty-aware active experimentation over measure-valued paths, guiding optimal measurement timing and improving data-efficient inference.

Abstract: Inferring continuous probability paths from sparse snapshots is a fundamental challenge in domains like single-cell biology, where high-fidelity data acquisition is often destructive and constrained by prohibitive sequencing costs. This motivates the need for active learning strategies to strategically select optimal measurement times. However, designing active learning policies for this setting remains an open problem: the target objects reside on the infinite dimensional Wasserstein space where standard Euclidean metrics are ill-defined, and current interpolation methods lack epistemic uncertainty quantification. We introduce a framework which extends active experimentation to the space of measures. By leveraging Linearized Optimal Transport (LOT), we map distributional snapshots into a tangent space amenable to Gaussian Process modeling, allowing us to construct a tractable probabilistic surrogate for the underlying probability path. This yields an acquisition policy that iteratively selects measurement times to minimize uncertainty. Empirical results demonstrate that our strategy outperforms uncertainty-agnostic baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [199] [CellBRIDGE: Learning Cellular Trajectories via Interaction-Aware Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30635)
*Silas Ruhrberg Estévez,Nicolas Huynh,Tennison Liu,Roderik M. Kortlever,Gerard I. Evan,David L. Bentley,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CellBRIDGE augments optimal transport with a ligand-receptor–driven interaction cost to improve cross-snapshot trajectory inference in scRNA-seq and enable mechanistic in silico perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard OT for scRNA-seq treats cells as independent points using gene-expression distances, ignoring intercellular communication; trajectory inference from snapshot data is underdetermined; incorporating biologically plausible cell-cell signaling should yield more meaningful couplings and interpretable perturbations.

Method: Augment feature-based OT with a directed, typed interaction cost derived from ligand-receptor activity to model cell-cell communication; compute couplings under this augmented cost; validate on synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets; perform in silico perturbations by silencing specific ligand-receptor pairs to observe trajectory shifts.

Result: Improved cross-snapshot couplings and trajectory estimates compared with feature-only baselines on both synthetic and real datasets; mechanistically interpretable perturbations: silencing certain ligand-receptor pairs induces trajectory shifts that align with expected effects of targeted pathway inhibition (e.g., in lung cancer data).

Conclusion: Integrating cell-cell signaling into OT yields biologically meaningful trajectory inferences and interpretable perturbations, underscoring the value of mechanistic costs in trajectory analyses.

Abstract: Inferring dynamics from population snapshots is a fundamental challenge in machine learning and biology. In scRNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq), destructive measurements preclude direct tracking of individual cells across time, making trajectory inference underdetermined. Optimal Transport (OT) provides a principled framework for snapshot alignment, but a long-standing modeling question is which cost functions yield biologically meaningful couplings. Standard OT approaches rely on gene-expression distances, implicitly treating cells as independent points and neglecting structured cell-cell communication mediated by ligand-receptor signaling. We introduce CellBRIDGE (Cell-Based Regularized Interaction-Driven Gene Expression), which augments feature-based OT with a directed, typed interaction cost derived from ligand-receptor activity. By explicitly modeling cell-cell communication, CellBRIDGE improves cross-snapshot couplings and downstream trajectory estimates across synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets relative to feature-only baselines. Notably, CellBRIDGE enables mechanistically interpretable in silico perturbations: on lung cancer data, silencing specific ligand-receptor pairs induces trajectory shifts that recapitulate expected effects of targeted pathway inhibition.

</details>


### [200] [Score Broadcast and Decorrelation: A General Framework for Broadcast-Based Credit Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30638)
*Mustafa Uzun,Mete Erdogan,Cengiz Pehlevan,Alper T. Erdogan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SBD generalizes error broadcast to general differentiable losses by introducing an orthogonality principle between the output score and hidden activations, enabling principled broadcast-based credit assignment across loss families; it extends EBD beyond MSE, provides a three-factor learning rule grounded neuromodulation as the broadcast score, introduces score vector expansion, and shows empirical gains on CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation that can handle a broad class of differentiable losses by leveraging a principled orthogonality property of the loss score with hidden activations, thereby enabling effective credit assignment without weight transport.

Method: Generalize the EBD framework via an orthogonality principle: the optimal score (gradient of loss w.r.t. final-layer output) should be orthogonal to hidden-layer activations when conditional mean of the score is zero. Derive formulations for cross-entropy, characterize admissible losses (including Bregman divergences, proper scoring rules, exponential-family NLLs), and propose a score vector expansion technique to enrich the broadcast signal while preserving orthogonality. Connect to a three-factor learning rule with neuromodulation equal to the broadcast score.

Result: The framework unifies broadcast-based credit assignment across common differentiable losses, provides theoretical grounding for a three-factor rule, explicit derivation for cross-entropy, and empirical gains on CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet, with additional improvements from score vector expansion.

Conclusion: Score Broadcast and Decorrelation provides a loss-score-centric, orthogonality-grounded mechanism for broadcast-based credit assignment that generalizes beyond MSE, supports a principled three-factor learning rule, and yields practical performance gains with an enriched broadcast signal.

Abstract: We introduce Score Broadcast and Decorrelation (SBD), a principled framework for broadcast-based credit assignment for general families of differentiable losses. Error broadcast is a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation that sends output information to hidden layers without weight transport. The Error Broadcast and Decorrelation (EBD) framework, recently introduced for the mean-squared-error (MSE) setting, grounded this mechanism in the stochastic orthogonality of optimal estimators, under which the optimal residual is orthogonal to functions of the input. We generalize that foundation by introducing an orthogonality principle between the output score (the gradient of loss with respect to the final-layer output) and hidden-layer activations, which holds whenever the optimal score has conditional mean zero. This single principle unifies broadcast-based credit assignment across the standard differentiable-loss families, including cross-entropy, Bregman divergences, proper scoring rules, and exponential-family negative log-likelihoods. The framework supplies a theoretical grounding for the three-factor learning rule under general losses, with the neuromodulatory factor derived as the broadcast loss score. We derive the cross-entropy case explicitly, characterize the admissible loss class, and introduce a score vector expansion technique that enriches the broadcast signal while preserving the orthogonality framework. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet show that SBD substantially improves over existing broadcast approaches, with score vector expansion delivering further gains. Overall, this work identifies the loss score as the signal to broadcast, supplies the orthogonality theory and theoretical grounding for the three-factor learning rule from neuroscience, and shows how score vector expansion enriches the decorrelation directions of the resulting objective.

</details>


### [201] [CSULoRA: Closest Safe Update Low-Rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30640)
*Oleksandr Marchenko Breneur,Adelaide Danilov,Aria Nourbakhsh,Salima Lamsiyah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSULoRA is a post-hoc safety correction for LoRA adapters that builds a safety-aligned subspace from the displacement between a safety-aligned model and base checkpoint, decomposes LoRA updates into aligned/partially aligned/off-subspace parts, and solves a closed-form penalized minimum-change problem to preserve safe updates while attenuating unsafe directions; it reduces adversarial attack success with minimal utility loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe fine-tuning of large language models using Lowe-rank adapters (LoRA) can be compromised by unsafe or adversarial data. Conventional safety-preserving methods rely on hard interventions that may sacrifice task performance. A post-hoc, non-destructive approach is needed to maintain safety without sacrificing utility.

Method: 1) Learn a safety-aligned subspace from weight displacements between a safety-aligned model and its base checkpoint. 2) Decompose each LoRA update into fully aligned, partially aligned, and off-subspace components with respect to the safety subspace. 3) Solve a closed-form penalized minimum-change problem that preserves the fully aligned component and attenuates unsafe directions proportionally to their energy, without discarding subspace components.

Result: Empirical evaluation in adversarial fine-tuning scenarios shows CSULoRA substantially lowers attack success rates while preserving most of the utility gains from standard LoRA fine-tuning.

Conclusion: CSULoRA offers an effective post-hoc, non-disruptive correction for LoRA adapters that enhances safety by projecting updates into a safety-aligned subspace and penalizing unsafe directions, avoiding hard interventions and maintaining most utility.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation has become a standard method for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models, but even small amounts of unsafe or adversarial fine-tuning data can substantially weaken the safety behavior of aligned models. Existing safety-preserving LoRA methods often rely on hard interventions such as projection, pruning, thresholding, or additional training objectives. While these methods can suppress unsafe update directions, they may also remove task-relevant information or require extra tuning. We introduce CSULoRA, a post-hoc method for correcting trained LoRA adapters through closest safe update estimation. CSULoRA estimates a safety-aligned subspace from the weight displacement between a safety-aligned model and its corresponding base checkpoint. It then decomposes each LoRA update into fully aligned, partially aligned, and off-subspace components. Instead of discarding components outside the estimated safety subspace, CSULoRA solves a closed-form penalized minimum-change problem that preserves the fully aligned component while smoothly attenuating potentially unsafe directions according to their relative energy. In adversarial fine-tuning experiments, CSULoRA substantially reduces attack success rate while preserving most of the utility gains obtained from standard LoRA fine-tuning.

</details>


### [202] [BOKBO (Best of K Bad Options): Calibrated Abstention for VLA Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30660)
*Anya Singh,Cabrel Happi,Jai Relan,Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BOKBO is a conformal abstention layer for K-sample vision-language-action (VLA) inference that provides finite-sample, distribution-free guarantees on the executed-violation rate. It offers global and Mondrian per-task variants that improve task-wise safety, analyzes failures of nonconformity scores under perturbations, uses a learned violation predictor for calibration, and demonstrates robust performance across seeds, benchmarks, and distribution shifts; it also highlights a pitfall with globally-set thresholds inflating violations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure safe execution of K-sample VLA policies at test time by providing finite-sample guarantees and mitigating failure-prone nonconformity scores, especially under perturbations and various sampling strategies.

Method: Introduce BOKBO (and Mondrian-BOKBO) as a conformal abstention layer for K-sample VLA inference. Analyze the relationship between base-policy confidence proxies, K-sample disagreement, and action-noise parameter sigma. Test under token-level temperature sampling and policy-stochasticity-based sampling. Employ a learned violation predictor conditioned on semantic visual features and task identity to tighten calibration. Evaluate with conditional CRC bounds, bootstrap splits, multiple seeds, and distribution shifts; compare global vs per-task Mondrian variants; identify and fix a pitfall related to globally-set force thresholds.

Result: Finite-sample distribution-free guarantees on executed-violation rate. Global and Mondrian variants achieve strong per-task coverage; e.g., Mondrian-BOKBO raises per-task hold fraction from 0.71 to 0.93. On libero_object_temp_x0.1 with OpenVLA-OFT, conditional CRC bound holds on 86% of bootstrap splits with 78% coverage and 70% net task success. Results stable across 5 seeds, replicate within bootstrap noise on π0-FAST, hold on libero_spatial_temp_x0.1, and survive four distribution shifts. A pitfall in thresholding inflates violations by ~5x and is corrected.

Conclusion: BOKBO provides calibrated abstention for K-sample VLA inference with distribution-free guarantees. Mondrian per-task variant enhances hardest-task safety. Learned violation predictors improve calibration. Careful threshold design is essential to avoid inflated violation rates; the framework is robust across seeds, tasks, and distribution shifts.

Abstract: Test-time scaling for vision-language-action (VLA) policies, methods such as RoboMonkey, SEAL, MG-Select, and V-GPS, samples K candidate action chunks at inference and executes the verifier-best. When all K candidates are unsafe, the system executes a violating action with no warning. We propose BOKBO, the first conformal abstention layer for K-sample VLA inference, providing finite-sample distribution-free guarantees on executed-violation rate. We provide both global and per-task (Mondrian) variants, with the per-task variant closing the conditional gap on the hardest tasks.
  Our analysis exposes a structural failure of policy-internal nonconformity scores under perturbation-based K-sampling: the base-policy confidence proxy and K-sample disagreement correlate at 0.98 with the action-noise hyperparameter $σ$, while correlating at the noise floor with actual safety violations. We test the failure's scope by replicating the analysis under token-level temperature sampling and find the failure is mechanism-specific and partially mitigated under policy-stochasticity-based sampling. A learned violation predictor conditioned on semantic visual features and task identity supports tight calibration: at $ε$ = 0.05 on libero_object_temp_x0.1 with OpenVLA-OFT, the conditional CRC bound holds on 86% of bootstrap splits with 78% coverage and 70% net task success. Mondrian-BOKBO raises the minimum per-task conditional hold fraction from 0.71 to 0.93. Results are stable across 5 training seeds, replicate within bootstrap noise on $π_0$-FAST, hold on libero_spatial_temp_x0.1 as a co-equal benchmark, and survive four within-suite distribution shifts. We additionally identify and correct a methodological pitfall: globally-set force thresholds well below expert-typical manipulation forces conflate unsafe behavior with normal manipulation, inflating violation rates by $5\times$.

</details>


### [203] [Diffusion Models Preferentially Memorize Prototypical Examples or: Why Does My Diffusion Model Love Slop?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30642)
*Marta Aparicio Rodriguez,Anastasia Borovykh,Grigorios A. Pavliotis,Daniel J. Korchinski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memorization in diffusion models tracks local data substrings and high-level structure, not simply rarity. Common substrings are memorized first; deduplication at the data-point level does not guarantee privacy; fat-tailed data delays memorization and high-level fat tails amplify this; there exists a partial memorization regime where common substrings are learned first and later overproduced, and early stopping yields bland outputs (slop).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To elucidate the mechanisms and conditions under which training data memorization emerges in generative models, with implications for privacy, copyright, and creative diversity.

Method: Train diffusion models on strings generated by the Random Hierarchy Model (RHM) production rules, analyze memorization order, examine deduplication effects, manipulate data tail properties and higher-level rules, and identify regimes of partial memorization.

Result: Memorization is driven by common substrings rather than rarity; deduplication does not prevent memorization; fat-tailed data cause delayed memorization and high-level fat tails amplify memorization effects; increased diversity at higher abstraction levels reduces memorization; an intermediate partial memorization regime exists where common substrings are learned first and later overproduced, and stopping training in this regime yields bland generation known as slop.

Conclusion: Dataset diversity, especially at higher levels of abstraction, mitigates memorization; privacy guarantees solely based on point-level deduplication are insufficient; early stopping trades memorization for bland outputs, highlighting a fundamental tension between memorization control and generative usefulness.

Abstract: Generative models have a persistent limitation: their tendency to memorize training data can create legal liabilities and erode creative diversity. Understanding which samples are memorized in whole or in part, and under what conditions, therefore remains an important open problem. Here we answer the question "Are atypical or rare samples memorized first?" in the negative. We train diffusion models on strings generated according to the production rules of the Random Hierarchy Model (RHM), and find that samples composed of common substrings are preferentially memorized. This holds true even if the training data consists of entirely unique samples, indicating that deduplication at the data point level does not provide a meaningful privacy guarantee. Correspondingly we predict, then observe, delayed memorization for fat-tailed datasets (i.e., those with more atypical samples). This effect is amplified when fat-tails are introduced into high-level production rules. These together suggest that dataset diversity, particularly at higher levels of abstraction, plays an important role in staving off memorization. Finally, we identify an intermediate regime of partial memorization in which common substrings are learned first and subsequently overproduced during generation. If training is stopped in this regime, models will exhibit the reversion-to-the-mean blandness often derided as "slop".

</details>


### [204] [Convergence of Steepest Descent and Adam under Non-Uniform Smoothness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30648)
*Sharan Vaswani,Yifan Sun,Reza Babanezhad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Under curvature that is affine in the objective value, the paper proves linear convergence for steepest descent and for deterministic diagonal RMSProp and Adam across common ML models; sign GD converges linearly on separable logistic regression and softmax policy gradient and outperforms GD; RMSProp/Adam achieve linear convergence with constant step-size and momentum on certain two-layer nets; a lower bound shows these methods beat AdaGrad, AMSGrad, GD, and heavy-ball under this condition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model realistic loss landscapes with non-uniform smoothness and to provide convergence guarantees for popular first-order methods under a broader curvature regime; to compare adaptive methods (RMSProp/Adam) with standard GD and momentum-based methods across representative ML problems.

Method: Assume objective curvature is affine in the objective value and impose gradient domination (PL-type) conditions. Analyze (i) steepest descent, (ii) deterministic diagonal RMSProp, and (iii) deterministic diagonal Adam. Establish general convergence rates; prove linear convergence for sign GD in logistic regression on separable data and for the softmax policy gradient; show linear convergence with fixed step-size and momentum for a class of two-layer neural networks; construct a lower bound showing RMSProp/Adam outperform AdaGrad, AMSGrad, GD, and heavy-ball under the stated condition.

Result: General convergence rate for steepest descent; linear convergence of sign GD for logistic regression (separable data) and softmax policy gradient; linear convergence of RMSProp and Adam with constant step-size and momentum on certain two-layer nets; RMSProp/Adam provably faster than AdaGrad, AMSGrad, GD, and heavy-ball under affine-curvature in the objective value.

Conclusion: Affine-curvature in the objective value provides a unifying framework yielding fast convergence guarantees for both gradient-based and adaptive first-order methods; RMSProp and Adam can outperform classical methods under this regime, with explicit linear rates in several practical ML settings; the analysis highlights regimes (separable data, specific network classes) where adaptive methods offer provable advantages.

Abstract: Recent work has analyzed the convergence of first-order methods under non-uniform smoothness assumptions that better model the loss landscape in machine learning tasks. We generalize this assumption to objectives whose curvature is an affine function of the objective value. This property is satisfied by a broad class of problems, including logistic regression, generalized linear models with a logistic link function, softmax policy gradient in reinforcement learning, and a class of neural networks. Under this assumption and gradient domination conditions, we establish a general convergence rate for the steepest descent method, and deterministic, diagonal variants of RMSProp and Adam. Our results imply that for logistic regression on separable data and the softmax policy gradient objective, sign GD converges linearly and is provably faster than GD. Furthermore, we show that for a class of two-layer neural networks on separable data, RMSProp and Adam can converge at a linear rate with a constant step-size and momentum parameter.
