Across tabular benchmarks, default FlagGAM remains close to EBM in transparent additive mode, improves substantially over ridge regression on mixed-type regression, and shows smaller AUROC degradation than common baselines under missing and noisy perturbations. Flexible heads further improve accuracy and approach strong tree-based baselines, with the caveat that the resulting model should be interpreted as a rule-basis representation followed by a nonlinear predictor rather than as a fully additive GAM. Overall, FlagGAM provides a practical middle ground for tabular settings that require competitive accuracy, communicable rules, and robustness to imperfect inputs.

</details>


### [284] [Student Capacity Moderates Knowledge Distillation Effectiveness: A Systematic Study Across ResNet Teacher-Student Pairs on CIFAR-10](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31191)
*Umut Onur Yasar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KD gains vary with student capacity; Feature-KD can surpass Logit-KD after bug fixes; input-resolution alignment is crucial; strong reproducibility claimed with 3 seeds and public code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how teacher-student capacity relationships modulate KD effectiveness in CNNs (ResNet) on CIFAR-10 and whether Feature-KD offers benefits over Logit-KD under correct implementation and input resolution.

Method: Evaluate three teacher-student pairs (R50->R18, R34->R18, R50->R34) on CIFAR-10; compare Logit-KD and Feature-KD under controlled settings (3 seeds, mean±std). Investigate a gradient clipping bug in Feature-KD; adjust ResNet stem for 32x32 inputs to ensure appropriate input resolution.

Result: Findings: (1) Student capacity moderates KD gains; larger students gain more from KD. +0.30pp for R50->R34 Feature-KD; +0.18pp for R34->R18 Feature-KD; +0.00pp for R34->R18 Logit-KD. (2) Implementation bugs affected Feature-KD; after correction, Feature-KD matches or outperforms Logit-KD in two of three pairs, achieving 95.55% on R50->R34 vs 95.25% baseline. (3) Input-resolution-aware architecture is essential; correcting ResNet stem for 32x32 raises teacher accuracy by >5pp, far exceeding any KD gain.

Conclusion: KD effectiveness is highly sensitive to student capacity, implementation correctness, and input resolution. With proper fixes, Feature-KD is competitive with Logit-KD. The authors provide reproducibility with multiple seeds and public code.

Abstract: We investigate how teacher-student capacity relationships modulate knowledge distillation (KD) effectiveness in ResNet-based image classification on CIFAR-10. Across three teacher-student pairs -- R50->R18, R34->R18, and R50->R34 -- we compare Logit-KD and Feature-KD under controlled, reproducible conditions (3 seeds, mean+/-std reported throughout). We report three main findings. First, student capacity is a key moderating factor in distillation gain: R34 students benefit substantially more from KD than R18 students even when teacher-student accuracy gaps are comparable, with the strongest gain of +0.30pp observed for R50->R34 Feature-KD versus +0.18pp for R34->R18 Feature-KD and +0.00pp for R34->R18 Logit-KD. Second, implementation correctness critically affects Feature-KD: a gradient clipping bug that excluded projection layers suppressed Feature-KD performance and produced misleading comparisons with Logit-KD. After correction, Feature-KD matches or outperforms Logit-KD in two of three pairs, reaching 95.55% on R50->R34 against a baseline of 95.25%. Third, input-resolution-aware architecture is a prerequisite for effective distillation: correcting the ResNet stem for 32x32 inputs raises teacher accuracy by over 5pp -- an order of magnitude larger than any KD gain. All code and results are available at github.com/umutonuryasar/kd-capacity-gap.

</details>


### [285] [Geometry-based Schrödinger Bridges for Trustworthy Multimodal Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31193)
*Jiayu Xiong,Jing Wang,Qi Zhang,Wanlong Wang,Jun Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Geometry-based Multimodal Fusion (GMF) that uses latent-space transport correction as a reliability signal, independent of model predictions, to improve robustness to sensor noise, missing data, and conflicts in multimodal fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the circular dependency of using model confidence for data quality by introducing a geometry-based reliability metric derived from transport corrections.

Method: Employs Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow in latent space; the squared initial velocity serves as a learned correction score; valid data shows low velocity, while noisy/missing/conflicting inputs require stronger transport corrections.

Result: Experiments show GMF substantially improves robustness against severe sensor noise and semantic conflicts compared to confidence-based baselines.

Conclusion: GMF offers a geometry-based, prediction-independent reliability signal for multimodal fusion, effectively flagging unreliable inputs and breaking the confidence-feedback loop.

Abstract: Real-world multimodal systems must be robust against low-quality data, such as sensor noise, incomplete multimodal data and conflicting inputs. However, existing trustworthy fusion methods rely on the model's own prediction confidence to judge data quality. This creates a circular dependency: when a model is confident but wrong, these methods fail to detect the error. To break this loop, we propose Geometry-based Multimodal Fusion (GMF). Instead of relying on predictions, we evaluate reliability by measuring how much transport correction the input needs in latent space. We implement Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow, where the squared initial velocity gives an efficient learned correction score. Valid data has low squared velocity magnitude, while noisy, incomplete data or conflicting data requires stronger transport correction. This geometry-based reliability signal acts as an independent judge, effectively flagging unreliable inputs even when the classifier is fooled. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GMF significantly improves robustness against severe sensor noise and semantic conflicts compared to confidence-based baselines.

</details>


### [286] [Beyond Additive Decompositions: Interpretability Through Separability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31200)
*Jinyang Liu,Munir Eberhardt Hiabu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSL learns a sum of rank-1 products of univariate feature functions via stagewise greedy with orthogonal refitting, enforcing separability to capture interactions while preserving interpretability; provides reconstruction from first-order partial dependence, theoretical guarantees, and competitive performance on regression tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of additive explainability methods (signal cancellation and extrapolation) in the presence of strong interactions; seek interpretable models that are faithful to data structure.

Method: Stagewise greedy learning of a sum of rank-1 tensors (products of univariate functions per feature). Orthogonal refitting. Enforce separability to avoid higher-order interaction information loss. Reconstruct from first-order PDs; provide visualizations faithful to components.

Result: Approximation-rate guarantees for functions with bounded mixed derivatives; competitive with black-box models on regression benchmarks.

Conclusion: TSL offers interpretable, faithful representations with theoretical guarantees and competitive empirical performance; resolves some drawbacks of additive explainability methods.

Abstract: Interpretable machine learning requires models that are accurate and structurally faithful to the data.Existing explainability methods rely heavily on additive representations (e.g., Generalized Additive Models (GAMs), SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), functional ANOVA), which can suffer from signal cancellation and off-support extrapolation in the presence of strong interactions. We propose Tensor Separation Learning (TSL), a regression model that learns a sum of rank-1 products of univariate per-feature functions via a stagewise greedy procedure with orthogonal refitting. By enforcing separability, TSL avoids the information loss inherent in additive projections caused by marginalizing higher-order interactions. The learned TSL model can be fully reconstructed from first-order partial dependence functions, up to constant factors. This stage-wise correspondence ensures that the resulting visualizations are faithful to the fitted components. We establish approximation-rate guarantees for functions with bounded mixed $p$-th order partial derivatives and demonstrate that TSL competes with black-box models on regression benchmarks.

</details>


### [287] [Fixed-Point Masked Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31215)
*Andrea Miele,Yiming Qin,Alba Carballo-Castro,Justin Deschenaux,Pascal Frossard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoFRe introduces Fixed-Point Masked Generative Models (FP-MGMs) with cross-step consistency and three-state reuse (3SR) to enable adaptive-depth masked generation. It replaces part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver over shared attention, allowing cheaper training while maintaining or improving quality at low budgets. Pre-trained MGMs can be fine-tuned to FP-MGMs. Across modalities, CoFRe achieves substantial parameter, time, and memory savings and better generation metrics at low budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Masked Generative Models enable parallel decoding but rely on full-sequence bidirectional transformers, leading to high training cost and degraded quality under low sampling budgets. There is a need to reduce computational resources while preserving or improving output quality, especially at low budgets.

Method: Introduce FP-MMGs by substituting part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver operating on shared attention layers to achieve adaptive depth with fewer parameters. Develop cross-step consistency loss to align hidden representations across neighboring denoising steps, and three-state reuse (3SR) to warm-start the solver using the token state (unchanged, still-masked, newly revealed). Train-to-inference framework named CoFRe; show that pre-trained MGMs can be converted with short fine-tuning rather than full retraining.

Result: On OpenWebText, CoFRe reduces parameters by 38.8%, training time by 11.5%, and VRAM by 16.9%, while improving perplexity from 830.8 to 101.8 at 96 transformer-block forward passes (vs MDLM). On ImageNette, training time decreases by 48.6% and VRAM by 50.7%, with FID improving across sample budgets. Overall, CoFRe yields a cheaper training regime and stronger performance at low budgets across modalities.

Conclusion: CoFRe provides a practical, cross-modal framework for cheaper training of masked generation with adaptive-depth via a fixed-point solver, enhanced by cross-step consistency and 3SR. The approach enables strong low-budget generation, and can leverage pre-trained MGMs with limited fine-tuning.

Abstract: Masked Generative Models (MGMs) enable parallel decoding and achieve strong performance across modalities, but require full-sequence bidirectional transformers at every step, making training costly and degrading quality under low sampling budgets. Existing work improves efficiency via better samplers or cheaper fixed-depth denoisers, but they still allocate a fixed amount of denoiser computation to each refinement step. We introduce Fixed-Point Masked Generative Models (FP-MGMs), which replace part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver over shared attention layers to enable adaptive depth with fewer parameters. To make it more effective for masked generation, we first introduce a cross-step consistency loss, which aligns hidden representations at neighboring denoising steps and, second, three-state reuse (3SR) which warm-starts the solver using the previous solution by treating differently unchanged, still-masked, and newly revealed tokens respectively. Together, these components define our complete training-to-inference framework for fixed-point masked generation, \emph{CoFRe}. We also show that pre-trained MGMs can be converted into FP-MGMs with short fine-tuning, avoiding full retraining. Across modalities, CoFRe improves the quality and cost trade-off. On OpenWebText, CoFRe reduces parameters by 38.8\%, training time by 11.5\%, and VRAM by 16.9\%, while improving generative perplexity from 830.8 to 101.8 at a budget of $96$ transformer-block forward passes, compared to MDLM. In ImageNette, CoFRe reduces training time by 48.6\% and VRAM by 50.7\%, while improving FID in all sample budgets tested. Overall, CoFRe offers a practical framework for cheaper training and stronger low-budget masked generation.

</details>


### [288] [Multivariate Distributional Reinforcement Learning Using Sliced Divergences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31222)
*Baptiste Debes,Tinne Tuytelaars*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDRL generalizes distributional RL to multivariate returns via projection (slicing), with contraction guarantees under structured discounting, analyzes Wasserstein/Cramér/MMD divergences, and validates on toy, gridworld, and Atari subsets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending distributional RL to multivariate return distributions is difficult due to non-contractive operators and metrics that do not generalize well; a tractable, provably contractive multivariate framework is needed.

Method: Introduce Sliced Distributional RL (SDRL) that lifts 1D divergences to multivariate returns via projections (uniform slicing and maximum slicing). Prove Bellman contraction for uniform slicing with shared scalar discount and contraction under dense discount matrices for maximum slicing. Analyze base divergences (Wasserstein, Cramér, MMD) and determine compatibility with standard single-sample Bellman updates. Empirically evaluate on a toy chain, a gridworld image-based environment, and a subset of Atari games.

Result: Provided theoretical contraction guarantees for SDRL under specified slicing schemes; characterized which divergences are suitable; demonstrated empirical feasibility and performance on simple and moderately complex tasks.

Conclusion: SDRL offers a flexible, scalable framework for multivariate distributional RL with contraction guarantees, accommodating a broad class of divergences and slicing strategies, and showing empirical effectiveness across synthetic and real-world-like environments.

Abstract: Distributional reinforcement learning (DRL) models the full return distribution rather than expectations, but extending it to multivariate settings remains challenging. Many common metrics do not naturally generalize beyond one dimension or lose computational tractability, and the multivariate case introduces additional difficulties such as general matrix discounting, for which no contraction results are available. We introduce Sliced Distributional Reinforcement Learning (SDRL), which lifts tractable one-dimensional divergences to multivariate return distributions via projections. We prove Bellman contraction for uniform slicing under shared scalar discounting, and introduce a maximum-slicing variant with contraction under general dense discount matrices. SDRL supports a broad class of base divergences; we analyze Wasserstein, Cramér, and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), and characterize which SDRL variants suit the standard single-sample Bellman update used in distributional RL. We evaluate SDRL on a toy chain problem and a gridworld image-based environment as well as a subset of Atari games.

</details>


### [289] [Bifurcated Remaining Useful Life Prediction: A Hybrid Approach for Realistic Uncertainty Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31241)
*Xabier Belaunzaran,Antonio Nappa,Arkaitz Artetxe,Basilio Sierra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid prognostic framework for turbofan RUL that uses a state-aware partition between healthy and degraded regimes, leveraging an LSTM autoencoder trained on nominal data for state detection, Conditional Weibull survival for healthy MRL, and MC-dropout probabilistic NN for degraded predictions; outputs calibrated uncertainty bands and a weighted ensemble.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable RUL estimation under uncertainty is critical for maintenance planning in turbofan engines. Regime-dependent behavior and meaningful uncertainty quantification (epistemic and aleatoric) are not adequately addressed by single-model approaches.

Method: 1) Train an LSTM autoencoder on nominal data (RUL > 150 cycles); use reconstruction error as a state classifier. 2) Convert the AE output to continuous state probabilities via a calibrated sigmoid. 3) Healthy regime: apply Conditional Weibull Survival Analysis to estimate Mean Residual Life. 4) Degraded regime: use Probabilistic Neural Network with Monte Carlo Dropout to capture predictive uncertainty. 5) Weight final ensemble predictions by state probabilities to produce uncertainty-aware RUL with physically consistent bands.

Result: Framework yields uncertainty bands that are physically consistent, with high confidence near end-of-life and representation of variance during early operation, enabling risk-informed maintenance decisions.

Conclusion: The proposed regime-aware, uncertainty-quantified prognostic framework effectively combines survivors analysis for healthy operation with probabilistic learning for degraded states, offering robust RUL estimates and actionable uncertainty bounds for turbofan maintenance planning.

Abstract: This study presents a novel hybrid prognostic framework for uncertainty-aware Remaining Useful Life (RUL) estimation in turbofan engines using the NASA C-MAPSS dataset. The framework employs a state-aware strategy that bifurcates the engines operational lifespan into "healthy" and "degraded" regimes. An LSTM-based autoencoder, trained strictly on nominal data (RUL > 150 cycles), monitors reconstruction error to act as a robust state classifier. For the healthy regime, a Conditional Weibull Survival Analysis is used for Mean Residual Life estimation. For the degraded regime, a Probabilistic Neural Network with Monte Carlo Dropout captures both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. Rather than using rigid binary labels, a calibrated sigmoid function converts the autoencoders output into continuous state probabilities, dynamically weighting the final ensemble prediction. The primary strength of this framework is its generation of physically consistent uncertainty bands, yielding high-confidence predictions near end-of-life while accurately reflecting the inherent variance of early operation, providing a robust tool for risk-informed maintenance.

</details>


### [290] [Spectral Reach: Understanding Neural Scaling as Progress into the Spectral Tail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31244)
*Konstantin Nikolaou,Jonas Scheunemann,Sven Krippendorf,Samuel Tovey,Christian Holm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces spectral position, a scalable metric to identify which eNTK eigenmodes drive loss reduction, to explain how training moves from dominant eigenmodes toward the spectral tail and how larger models access weaker signals (spectral reach).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To demystify neural scaling laws by providing a scalable diagnostic of training dynamics at the level of the neural tangent kernel spectrum, and to explain why larger models continue to improve as model size grows.

Method: Define spectral position as a scalable measure of which eigenvalues of the empirical NTK (eNTK) are currently responsible for loss decrease; apply this measure to scaling experiments across model sizes and track its evolution during training; compare spectral reach across sizes; analyze the role of feature learning in sustaining progress.

Result: Spectral position decreases during training, indicating a shift from dominant eigenmodes to the spectral tail. Larger models probe deeper into the tail, revealing a size-dependent capacity (spectral reach). Feature learning emerges as a key enabler by adaptively increasing gradient magnitudes, sustaining learning where frozen representations stall.

Conclusion: Larger models achieve lower losses by maintaining learning on weak spectral signals accessed only by larger architectures. Feature learning enables spectral reach, suggesting concrete architectural and optimizer design directions to enhance training dynamics and scaling behavior.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws describe predictable power-law relationships between model size, dataset size, compute, and performance. While these laws guide the development of modern foundation models, the mechanisms underpinning them remain poorly understood, in part due to the absence of scalable analysis tools. To close this gap, we introduce "spectral position": a scalable measure of which eigenvalues of the empirical neural tangent kernel (eNTK) currently drive loss reduction. Applying this measure to scaling experiments, we find that spectral position decreases throughout training: learning shifts from dominant eigenmodes into the spectral tail. Larger models reach further into the tail than smaller models, revealing a size-dependent capacity we call "spectral reach". This suggests why larger models achieve lower losses: they sustain learning on weak spectral signals inaccessible to smaller models. We further identify feature learning as a key enabler of spectral reach. It adaptively amplifies gradient magnitudes as learning advances, sustaining progress where frozen representations stall. This points to concrete interventions through architecture and optimizer design.

</details>


### [291] [Toward Identifiable Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31245)
*Walter Nelson,Theofanis Karaletsos,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAEs are unstable across training runs; introduce identifiability via two iSAE variants (near TopK SAE) with lower reconstruction error; theoretical link to dictionary learning and approximate RIP leading to near-identifiable sparse codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and mitigate the instability of sparse autoencoders so that learned dictionaries and codes are robust and interpretable across training runs.

Method: Diagnose factors driving instability, implement minimal architectural and training modifications to produce two iSAE variants (based on TopK SAE), and relate their behavior to dictionary learning. Provide theoretical justification by connecting to restricted isometry-like conditions.

Result: Two iSAE variants achieve lower reconstruction error and improved stability compared to standard TopK SAE. The learned dictionaries satisfy an approximate restricted isometry condition, leading to near-identifiable sparse codes. The work provides theoretical explanations and links to dictionary-learning literature.

Conclusion: Instability in SAEs can be mitigated with small architectural/training adjustments, yielding identifiable representations (iSAE) that align with RIP-like conditions and improve stability and interpretability of the learned dictionaries and codes.

Abstract: Recently, sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as an attractive tool for interpreting and interacting with representations in practical neural networks. While it is common empirical folklore, we also show theoretically that SAEs are highly unstable: different training runs are likely to produce different concept dictionaries and sparse codes. We characterize the model properties that hinder the stability of real-world SAEs, and address each of these problems through minimal changes to the architecture and training procedure. Together, these changes yield two versions of an \textbf{i}dentifiable SAE (iSAE), a variant of the standard TopK SAE with lower reconstruction error and improved stability. We explain this improvement theoretically by connecting SAEs with traditional dictionary learning approaches, and show that the dictionaries learned in practice satisfy an approximate restricted isometry condition, rendering the corresponding sparse codes in those models near-identifiable.

</details>


### [292] [Fraud Type Decomposition and the Observation-Mechanism Taxonomy:Class-Specific Detection Limits in Payment Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31257)
*Gaurav Dhama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fraud detection should be decomposed into five observation-driven classes; class-specific estimation is strictly more efficient than pooled estimation due to Jensen penalties from heterogeneous observation rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Treating fraud as a single homogeneous binary variable hides heterogeneity in observation processes, causing efficiency loss. Recognizing distinct observation mechanisms can improve estimation efficiency and detection limits.

Method: Proposes an observation-mechanism taxonomy that partitions fraud into five classes defined by distinct censorship and labeling pipelines; proves that class-wise estimation dominates pooled estimation; derives the Jensen penalty as the efficiency gap; identifies per-class binding constraints including endogenous label corruption, structural non-observability, and feature non-informativeness.

Result: Establishes that the efficiency gap between class-wise and pooled estimation is quantifyable via a Jensen penalty; shows class-wise estimation strictly dominates pooling; provides per-class theoretical limits and detection constraints.

Conclusion: Fraud detection is fundamentally a collection of distinct estimation problems, each governed by its own observation structure and detection limit.

Abstract: Fraud detection in payment networks relies on labels generated through heterogeneous and imperfect observation processes, yet existing approaches treat fraud as a homogeneous binary variable. We show that this assumption is structurally incorrect and leads to provable inefficiency. We introduce an observation-mechanism taxonomy that partitions fraud into five classes, each defined by a distinct censorship and labeling pipeline. We prove that estimating fraud rates separately by class and aggregating strictly dominates pooled estimation, with the efficiency gap characterized as a Jensen penalty arising from heterogeneous observation rates. For each class, we derive the binding theoretical constraint on detection, including endogenous label corruption, structural non-observability, and feature non-informativeness. These results establish that fraud detection is fundamentally a collection of distinct estimation problems, each governed by its own observation structure and detection limit.

</details>


### [293] [Lightweight CNN-Based Anomaly Detection for High Voltage Converter Modulators in the Spallation Neutron Source](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31259)
*Alberto D. Cencillo,Leonardo Concepción,Julián Luengo,Isaac Triguero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Investigates how architectural inductive bias, via ordering of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing and per-pulse channel reweighting, affects fault detection in high-power pulsed converters. Achieves strong cross-subsystem performance on the SNS HVCM dataset, with ablations linking performance to dominant channels and fault type manifestations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Downtime from unscheduled trips of high-power pulsed converters (HVCMs) is a major issue at accelerator facilities. Existing multi-channel DL approaches entangle temporal and cross-channel processing, potentially obscuring channel-specific precursors. The study tests whether separating temporal filtering from cross-channel mixing (and adapting channel weights per fault family) improves detection.

Method: Modify neural network architecture to vary the order of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing; implement per-pulse adaptive channel reweighting; evaluate on public HVCM dataset spanning four SNS subsystems (RFQ, DTL, CCL, SCL); perform ablations to identify key channels and how fault families manifest.

Result: Best variant yields pooled AUC-PR of 0.816 and AUC-ROC of 0.934, outperforming state-of-the-art on most subsystems and five of six fault families. Ablations show three dominant input channels and that per-fault-family performance correlates with whether precursors are simple amplitude shifts or require joint channel representations.

Conclusion: Architectural inductive bias—specifically the ordering of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing, together with per-fault-family channel reweighting—enhances fault detection in SNS HVCMs. Different fault families exhibit distinct precursor patterns (per-channel vs joint-channel), justifying tailored representations.

Abstract: Unscheduled trips of high-power pulsed converters are a leading source of downtime at large accelerator facilities. At the Spallation Neutron Source (SNS), the High Voltage Converter Modulators (HVCMs) are consistently the second-largest contributor to lost beam time. Each HVCM pulse is recorded across sensor channels spanning currents, voltages, and magnetic fluxes, whose mutual interactions encode the operating state of the system. Fault precursors do not manifest uniformly across these channels: depending on fault type, they may alter the temporal structure of individual signals, change the statistical dependencies among channels, or both. Existing deep-learning approaches typically process multi-channel signals with standard convolutional pipelines that entangle temporal and cross-channel operations from the first layer, giving the model no explicit mechanism to represent channel independence or structured inter-channel interaction. We hypothesise that architectural inductive bias, specifically the ordering of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing, plays a central role in detection performance on this class of data. To test this, we vary the order in which these two operations are applied, and examine whether per-pulse adaptive channel reweighting further improves sensitivity. Evaluated on the public HVCM dataset across all four SNS subsystems (RFQ, DTL, CCL, SCL), our best variant achieves a pooled AUC-PR of 0.816 and AUC-ROC of 0.934, outperforming the state of the art on most subsystems and five of the six fault families. Ablations identify three dominant input channels and link per-fault-family performance to whether precursors manifest as amplitude shifts in individual channels or as subtler patterns requiring joint channel representations to surface.

</details>


### [294] [Algorithmic Recourse of In-Context Learning for Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31272)
*Wenshuo Dong,Jiaming Zhang,Shaopneg Fu,Hongbin Lin,Di Wang,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First study of algorithmic recourse for tabular data under in-context learning; introduces ASR-ICL, a zero-order, sparse, actionable recourse framework for black-box ICL models with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As predictive models are deployed in high-stakes settings, post-hoc recourse is essential. In-context learning enables tabular prediction without training, but recourse under ICL remains underexplored and requires efficient, actionable solutions.

Method: Theoretically analyze recourse under ICL: recourse is well-defined, bounded, and converges to classical solutions as context size grows. Propose Adaptive Subspace Recourse for In-Context Learning (ASR-ICL), a zeroth-order framework that generates sparse, actionable recourse for black-box ICL models; extends to multi-class tasks.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world datasets shows ASR-ICL achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods with significantly fewer queries; theoretical convergence behavior is validated by experiments.

Conclusion: ASR-ICL provides a practical, efficient recourse method for tabular decision-making under ICL with solid theoretical grounding, and it generalizes to multi-class settings; promising direction for scalable post-hoc recourse in LLM-enabled systems.

Abstract: As predictive models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings such as credit approval, there is a growing need for post-hoc methods that provide recourse to affected individuals. Many such models operate on tabular data, where features correspond to real-world attributes. Recently, in-context learning (ICL) has enabled large language models to perform tabular prediction by conditioning on labeled examples at inference time, without explicit training. However, algorithmic recourse for tabular decision-making under ICL remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first study of algorithmic recourse for tabular data under ICL. We carry out a theoretical analysis, showing that recourse remains well-defined and bounded, and we characterize how recourse converges toward classical solutions as the context size increases. In practice, we propose a novel zeroth-order recourse framework, Adaptive Subspace Recourse for In-Context Learning (ASR-ICL), that efficiently generates actionable and sparse recourse for black-box ICL models. The proposed framework naturally extends to multi-class tabular tasks. Experiments across multiple real-world datasets and models demonstrate that ASR-ICL achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods with fewer queries and empirically confirm the predicted convergence behavior, supporting our theoretical analysis.

</details>


### [295] [Interpretability Without Tradeoffs: Disentangling Polysemanticity At Equal Predictive Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31304)
*Doğukan Bağcı,Bernt Schiele,Simone Schaub-Meyer,Jonas Fischer,Robin Hesse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ELUDe enables explicit, lossless, unsupervised disentanglement of latent representations in pretrained DNNs, improving interpretability without altering outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability is hindered when single neurons encode multiple concepts; existing disentanglement methods (e.g., sparse autoencoders) often require architecture changes that harm performance. A method that preserves functional equivalence is needed.

Method: Reorganizes information flow between layers to re-route concept-specific contributions, preserving original computation by construction. It requires no training, no labels, and can be applied to pretrained models. It yields interpretable sub-units without changing model outputs and is applicable to models like DINOv2 and ViT-B/16.

Result: ELUDe improves interpretability across vision models while keeping downstream accuracy unchanged; it is efficient and enables practical uses such as steering model representations.

Conclusion: ELUDe provides interpretable, scalable insights with negligible or no tradeoff, achieving near-lossless disentanglement in pretrained networks.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are widely used, but interpreting what they actually learn remains difficult. A major obstacle is that individual neurons often encode multiple unrelated concepts, obscuring the decision process of the network. While prior work, such as sparse autoencoders, can separate these mixed signals into more meaningful, "monosemantic" features, this typically requires altering the model in ways that can degrade downstream performance. To overcome this, we introduce ELUDe (explicit, lossless, unsupervised disentanglement), a method for improving the interpretability of DNNs while preserving their functional equivalence. ELUDe breaks latent representations into clear, inspectable sub-units that behave like interpretable features, while guaranteeing that the model's outputs remain exactly the same. It requires no explicit training, no labels, and can be applied to pretrained models. ELUDe works by reorganizing how information flows between layers, re-routing concept-specific contributions while preserving the original computation by construction. Across several vision models, including DINOv2 and supervised ViT-B/16, ELUDe improves interpretability, keeps downstream accuracy unchanged, runs efficiently, and supports practical uses such as steering model representations. In short, ELUDe offers interpretability (almost) without a tradeoff: clearer, scalable, and actionable model insights with no loss in performance.

</details>


### [296] [Survival Reinforcement Learning: Toward Scalable Self-Supervised RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31273)
*Franki Nguimatsia-Tiofack,Fabian Schramm,Théotime Le Hellard,Justin Carpentier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SRL is a classification-based alternative to CRL that maximizes dwell time at goals, addressing the uniformity–tolerance dilemma and improving long-horizon planning, achieving competitive manipulation and superior locomotion performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contrastive RL losses often impose a trade-off between representation uniformity and tolerance to task-relevant variations, hindering long-horizon, goal-conditioned planning; an online classification-based objective could avoid these issues and mitigate undesirable behavior.

Method: Introduce Survival Reinforcement Learning (SRL), an online classification-based objective that extends survival value learning to maximize dwell time at target goals. SRL bypasses CRL's structural constraints and reduces bang-bang control tendencies, within an online RL loop framed as classification.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse robotic benchmarks shows SRL matches state-of-the-art CRL on manipulation tasks and significantly outperforms it (2×–8×) on stable, long-horizon locomotion tasks.

Conclusion: Classification-based objectives like SRL can serve as scalable primitives for RL, offering competitive performance on short-horizon tasks and pronounced gains on long-horizon planning, supporting broader adoption of classification-based RL strategies.

Abstract: While self-supervised Contrastive Reinforcement Learning (CRL) has shown remarkable depth-scaling capabilities, successfully using networks over 64 layers, scaled CRL still struggles with long-horizon goal-conditioned planning due to the uniformity-tolerance dilemma inherent in contrastive losses. We introduce Survival Reinforcement Learning (SRL), an online classification-based alternative that extends the survival value learning framework by maximizing the agent's dwell time at target goals. SRL bypasses the structural constraints of CRL and mitigates the "bang-bang" control solutions inherent to survival frameworks, which often induce undesirable behavior in complex dynamical systems. Evaluated across diverse robotic benchmarks, scaled SRL matches state-of-the-art CRL on manipulation tasks and outperforms it by 2x to 8x on stable, long-horizon locomotion tasks. Our results provide strong additional evidence that classification-based methods may serve as a key primitive in the broader effort to scale reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [297] [A Unifying View of Variational Generative Wasserstein Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31369)
*Paul Caucheteux,Clément Bonet,Anna Korba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified framework for generative modeling via Wasserstein gradient flows (GWF) using JKO discretization; shows many existing methods are parametric JKO updates for f-divergences and extends to Integral Probability Metrics and squared MMD; derives new algorithms, clarifies links to GANs; includes empirical evaluation and analysis of parametric (map-induced) flows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a cohesive theoretical base for generative modeling by unifying divergence-based, metric-based, and GAN-like methods under Wasserstein gradient flows, enabling principled algorithm design via JKO schemes.

Method: Introduce Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF); prove that broad classes of existing generative methods are instances of parametric JKO updates for f-divergences; extend the framework to IPMs and squared MMD; derive new JKO-based generative algorithms; study empirical impact of JKO regularization; analyze parametric (map-induced) distributions.

Result: Demonstrates equivalences among several existing algorithms; derives new JKO-based methods for IPMs and MMD; clarifies connections with GANs; provides empirical insights on JKO regularization; frames parametric flows as a practical extension.

Conclusion: GWF offers a unified, extensible lens for generative modeling, enabling principled construction of JKO-based algorithms and deeper understanding of GAN-related approaches, with broad theoretical and empirical implications for future work.

Abstract: Many modern generative models can be viewed as minimizing divergences between probability distributions, yet they rely on different algorithmic and geometric principles. Wasserstein gradient flows provide a continuous-time formulation for optimizing over distributions, and can be approximated through their implicit discretization via the Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (JKO) scheme. In this work, we present a unified theoretical framework for generative modeling based on Wasserstein gradient flows, which we refer to as Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF). We show that a broad class of existing methods can be derived as instances of parametric JKO schemes for $f$-divergence objectives, and we establish equivalences between several recently proposed algorithms. We extend this framework beyond f-divergence to Integral Probability Metrics and squared Maximum Mean Discrepancy, deriving new JKO-based generative algorithms, and clarifying their connections with GANs. We study empirically the impact of the JKO regularization for a wide set of objectives. Finally, we analyze parametric Wasserstein flows, where the dynamics are restricted to distributions induced by parametrized maps.

</details>


### [298] [Learning Parametric Nitrogen Fertilizer Response Curves Using Neuro Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31276)
*Giorgio Morales,John Sheppard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic regression learns parametric nitrogen-response curves without predefined forms, using transformer-based skeleton discovery across management zones, then fits with a genetic algorithm; yields site-specific, interpretable N-response relationships and outperforms traditional models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need interpretable, data-driven nitrogen-response models that capture spatially varying, site-specific agronomic relationships without assuming a fixed functional form; improves over predefined parametric forms and opaque ML models.

Method: Proposes a neuro-symbolic regression framework with a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction. It builds diverse input subsets across management zones, enforces cross-subset consistency, and recovers robust symbolic skeletons, which are then fitted to data with a genetic algorithm. Validated on synthetic 1D problems under varying epistemic uncertainty and applied to real-world winter wheat data to derive MZ-specific N-response curves.

Result: In synthetic tests, the method robustly recovers correct expressions even in data-scarce regimes. On real-world data, it learns distinct parametric N-response curves for different management zones; the expressions achieve lower fitting errors than quadratic-plateau and exponential models and reveal diverse regional behaviors.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of neuro-symbolic regression to enable site-specific agronomic relationship discovery and support informed decision-making in precision agriculture.

Abstract: Accurately modeling crop response to Nitrogen (N) fertilization is a fundamental challenge in precision agriculture, as it impacts both economic returns and environmental sustainability. Existing approaches either rely on predefined parametric forms or opaque machine learning models, limiting their ability to interpret or discover site-specific functional relationships from data. In this work, we propose a neuro symbolic regression (SR) approach to learn parametric N-response curves without assuming a predefined functional form. Our approach integrates a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction strategy, enabling the discovery of shared functional structures across multiple subdomains or management zones (MZs). By constructing diverse input subsets and enforcing consistency across them, the method recovers robust symbolic skeletons that are subsequently fitted to observed data using a genetic algorithm. This framework was first evaluated on synthetic one-dimensional problems to assess its robustness under varying levels of epistemic uncertainty. The results demonstrate the ability of the proposed SR approach to recover correct expressions even in data-scarce regimes. In this work, we present the results of applying our method to real-world winter wheat data, learning distinct parametric N-response curves for different MZs within a field. The results show that the discovered expressions not only achieve lower fitting errors than traditional models such as quadratic-plateau and exponential functions, but also capture diverse functional behaviors across spatial regions. This demonstrates the potential that neuro SR has to enable the discovery of site-specific agronomic relationships and support informed decision-making in precision agriculture.

</details>


### [299] [Non-Asymptotic Convergence of Stochastic Iterative Algorithms: A Lyapunov Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31309)
*Zaiwei Chen,Siva Theja Maguluri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A survey of Lyapunov-based finite-time analysis for stochastic approximation (SA) with contractive operators and noisy access, introducing generalized Moreau envelopes as universal Lyapunov functions. It derives mean-square convergence guarantees for SGD, linear SA, and value-based RL algorithms (Q-learning, TD learning), and discusses extensions to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive and dissipative operators, plus high-probability bounds. It aims to provide a unified, self-contained roadmap for finite-time SA analysis in RL and related areas, with open problems remaining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a unified, self-contained finite-time analysis framework for stochastic approximation algorithms, enabling quantitative guarantees (rates, bounds) in reinforcement learning settings by leveraging Lyapunov methods and generalized Moreau envelopes that are robust to the choice of norm.

Method: Assume a contractive operator  with respect to some norm and i.i.d. noise; construct generalized Moreau envelopes as Lyapunov functions; develop finite-time drift inequalities yielding mean-square convergence; apply to stochastic gradient descent, linear stochastic approximation, Q-learning, and temporal-difference learning; extend analysis to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive and dissipative operators, and address high-probability bounds.

Result: Derives finite-time, mean-square convergence guarantees for SA-type algorithms under contractive settings with i.i.d. noise; demonstrates applicability to SGD, linear SA, Q-learning, and TD learning; provides a unified Lyapunov-based framework and discusses extensions to broader noise models and operator classes, including high-probability considerations.

Conclusion: Presents a unified roadmap for finite-time analysis of stochastic approximation and its reinforcement learning applications, highlighting open problems in relaxing assumptions, improving rates, and establishing high-probability guarantees across broader operator and noise settings.

Abstract: We survey Lyapunov-based techniques for the finite-time analysis of stochastic iterative algorithms, also known as stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms, for solving fixed-point equations $\bar{F}(x)=x$, where the operator $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ can only be accessed through a noisy oracle. We first focus on the standard setting in which $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ is contractive with respect to some norm and the noise is i.i.d., and explain how generalized Moreau envelopes serve as universal Lyapunov functions, regardless of the underlying norm. We then show how this framework yields mean-square convergence guarantees and applies to stochastic gradient descent, linear SA, and value-based reinforcement learning algorithms such as Q-learning and temporal-difference learning. Finally, we discuss extensions to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive operators, dissipative operators, and high-probability bounds, and conclude with open problems. The goal is to present a unified and self-contained roadmap for the finite-time analysis of SA and its applications, especially in reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [300] [Graph Neural Networks Are Not Continuous Across Graph Resolutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31315)
*Christian Koke,Yuesong Shen,Abhishek Saroha,Marvin Eisenberger,Bastian Rieck,Michael Bronstein,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNNs are not continuous under natural graph-convergence notions and can yield very different embeddings for graphs representing the same object at different resolutions. The paper identifies a structural obstruction in common information-propagation schemes and proposes a principled architectural modification to restore cross-scale continuity, enabling reliable multi-scale integration and generalization, supported by extensive experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile GNN representations across different graph resolutions and ensure stable, consistent embeddings when graphs are refinements or coarsenings of each other.

Method: Analyze the source of discontinuity within standard information-propagation schemes, formulate a structural obstruction, and introduce a principled architectural modification to standard GNNs that enforces continuity across scales (across graph resolutions).

Result: The proposed modification achieves continuity across scales, enabling reliable integration of graphs at different resolutions and improving cross-scale generalization. This claim is validated through a wide range of numerical experiments. 

Conclusion: Continuity across graph scales is achievable in GNNs through the introduced architectural change, which supports consistent multi-scale representations and generalization, addressing a key practical limitation in existing GNN approaches.

Abstract: We show that contrary to conventional wisdom in the community, graph neural networks (GNNs) are not continuous with respect to all natural modes of graph convergence. As a result, GNNs may generate substantially different latent representations for graphs that are very similar. In particular they assign vastly different latent embeddings to graphs that represent the same underlying object at different resolution scales. We trace this failure of continuity back to a structural obstruction arising from commonly used information-propagation schemes. Building on this insight we then derive a principled modification to standard GNN architectures which equips models with continuity across scales. The proposed modification enables consistent integration of distinct resolutions and reliable generalization between them. We systematically validate our theoretical findings in a wide range of numerical experiments.

</details>


### [301] [Forgetting Has Neighbors: Localized Collateral Forgetting in Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31317)
*Polina Dolgova,Sebastian U. Stich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Localized collateral forgetting: unlearned models can diverge non-uniformly from retrained models, especially near the forget set. A Local Teacher Distillation using soft labels from retained neighbors improves local fidelity to retraining without hurting aggregate metrics on CIFAR-100 partial-class deletion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregate unlearning metrics can obscure localized failures. An example-level analysis reveals that surrogate targets during unlearning may be inconsistent with the local prediction structure induced by retraining, causing localized collateral forgetting that propagates through shared representations.

Method: 1) Compare unlearned model predictions to a retrained model after deletions to quantify pointwise discrepancy; 2) show non-uniform, proximity-based growth of this discrepancy for gradient-ascent and random-labeling methods with/without retain-set fine-tuning; 3) identify surrogate-target inconsistency as the mechanism; 4) propose Local Teacher Distillation, replacing random targets with soft labels from a teacher trained only on retained neighbors of the forget set.

Result: The local teacher reduces the pointwise discrepancy near the forget set, making the unlearned model closer to retraining, especially in the neighborhood of the forget set, while keeping aggregate unlearning metrics competitive on CIFAR-100 partial-class deletion.

Conclusion: Localized collateral forgetting is mitigated by aligning surrogate targets with local predictive structure via a local teacher. The Local Teacher Distillation is a simple, effective mitigation that improves local fidelity to retraining without sacrificing overall unlearning performance; further work could test on additional datasets and unlearning regimes.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of selected training examples without full retraining. Standard evaluations often summarize unlearning quality with aggregate metrics, such as accuracy- and forgetting-based scores, which can hide localized failures. We study this failure mode at the example level by comparing the predictions of an unlearned model to those of the model retrained after deletion. We show that this pointwise discrepancy can be highly non-uniform: for gradient-ascent and random-labeling methods, with and without retain-set fine-tuning, it grows with geometric proximity to the forget set. We call this phenomenon localized collateral forgetting. Our analysis identifies a mechanism behind the effect: surrogate targets used during unlearning can be inconsistent with the local prediction structure induced by retraining, and this inconsistency propagates through shared representations to nearby examples. Motivated by this mechanism, we propose Local Teacher Distillation, a simple mitigation strategy that replaces random targets with soft labels from a small teacher trained only on retained neighbors of the forget set. On CIFAR-100 partial-class deletion, this local teacher brings the unlearned model substantially closer to retraining, especially near the forget set, while maintaining competitive aggregate unlearning metrics.

</details>


### [302] [Generalized Intention Modeling in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31318)
*Mateusz Odrowaz-Sypniewski,Jasmine Bayrooti,Ajay Shankar,Amanda Prorok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Task-adaptive opponent modeling via a mixture of intent representations, including an MI-maximizing representation, improving performance across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Common a priori intent embeddings often fail to capture task- and environment-specific opponent behavior; a performance-driven, adaptive representation is needed to align modeling with decision quality.

Method: Learn a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations and introduce a new intention representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego agent's future returns, guiding representation selection by expected performance.

Result: The approach matches or exceeds state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and provides insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

Conclusion: Adaptive, MI-informed opponent modeling improves decision-making and generalizes better across tasks, with interpretability regarding the utility of different representations.

Abstract: Modeling an opponent's intent is critical for effective decision-making in non-cooperative, competitive, and general-sum multi-agent reinforcement learning. Existing opponent modeling methods encode intent using an embedding derived from episode information chosen a priori, such as the opponent's next action or a future environment state, and use this to guide the ego-agent's behavior. These approaches assume that the chosen information is universally representative of intent; however, we show empirically that this is not the case as intentions are often task- and environment-dependent. To address this, we introduce a task-adaptive opponent modeling framework that learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations. We further introduce a new intention representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns, thereby capturing opponent information that is most directly relevant to performance. Our approach consistently matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and yields insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

</details>


### [303] [Trading Complexity for Expressivity Through Structured Generalized Linear Token Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31367)
*Erwan Fagnou,Paul Caillon,Blaise Delattre,Alexandre Allauzen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified framework for token mixing layers that integrates attention and state-space models, introducing structured recurrence patterns to trade runtime for expressivity in causal generation, with theoretical insights and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the efficiency–expressivity trade-off in token mixers for long-range dependencies during causal generation, and provide a unified viewpoint across architectures.

Method: Separate direct input–output influence in a generation step from recursive propagation of past outputs; generalize recurrence to allow multiple past states; design structured recurrence patterns with provable complexity guarantees; analyze expressivity and runtime tradeoffs; validate on synthetic tasks and language modeling.

Result: Establishes a framework applicable to attention and state-space models; derives complexity/expressivity tradeoffs; provides theoretical insights and carries out empirical tests on synthetic and real-language tasks; offers a unified toolkit for efficient and expressive token mixers.

Conclusion: Token mixers across model families can be designed with principled tradeoffs between runtime and expressivity; the framework enables systematic design and analysis of efficient, expressive recurrence patterns in causal generation.

Abstract: Token mixing layers play a key role in how language models can learn and generate long-range dependencies. Their efficiency relies on the necessary trade-off between decoding speed and the memory requirements, along with the cache size. Considering causal generation, this paper explores new trade-offs thanks to a unified framework which separates two crucial features: (i) the direct influence of inputs on outputs in one generation step; (ii) the recurrent propagation of information through past outputs. This framework encompasses major architectures such as attention and state-space models, but also generalizes the recurrence equations by allowing each state to depend on multiple past states rather than only the immediate predecessor. By introducing structure, we design new recurrence patterns that provably achieve the desired complexity, while providing theoretical insights on their expressivity -- trading runtime for expressivity in a principled way. Empirical validation is performed on synthetic tasks, along with language modeling. Together, these results provide a unified toolkit for the understanding and design of efficient and expressive token mixers across model families.

</details>


### [304] [Softsign: Smooth Sign in Your Optimizer For Better Parameter Heterogeneity Handling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31371)
*Dmitrii Feoktistov,Timofey Belinsky,Andrey Veprikov,Amir Zainullin,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Smooth-sign based optimization with temperature-controlled relaxation (SoftSignum) and its matrix variant (SoftMuon) bridge sign-based optimizers and SGD, with adaptive schedules and a geometry-relaxation convergence framework; show improved convergence and performance over hard sign methods and AdamW, demonstrated on diverse DL tasks including LLM pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sign-based/LMO optimizers offer memory efficiency but use fixed-magnitude updates, decoupling updates from gradient magnitudes and ignoring parameter heterogeneity, which can cause oscillations and poor terminal convergence. A tunable, smooth relaxation could recover magnitude sensitivity while retaining benefits.

Method: Introduce SoftSignum as a smooth, temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation; employ an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule; extend to matrix-valued optimizers as SoftMuon; develop a generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates; prove convergence in stochastic non-convex settings.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse deep learning tasks, including large language model pretraining, shows SoftSignum and SoftMuon outperform hard sign-based optimizers and AdamW in terms of convergence stability and final performance.

Conclusion: SoftSignum and SoftMuon provide a tunable bridge between sign-based and magnitude-aware optimization, with convergence guarantees and practical performance gains across tasks; the adaptive temperature strategy is key to balancing update directions and magnitudes.

Abstract: Sign-based and LMO-inspired optimizers have recently attracted substantial attention in deep learning due to their strong performance and low memory footprint. However, their fixed-magnitude updates can hurt terminal convergence: they decouple update mechanisms from gradient magnitudes and fail to account for parameter heterogeneity, often leading to oscillation rather than convergence. We propose SoftSignum, a smooth relaxation of sign-based optimization that replaces the hard sign map with a temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, enabling a parameter-wise transition from sign-like updates to magnitude-sensitive SGD-like steps. We complement it with an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule and extend the same principle to matrix-valued optimizers, obtaining SoftMuon. We also develop a generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates, proving convergence in stochastic non-convex setting. Experiments on diverse deep learning tasks, including LLM pretraining, show that SoftSignum and SoftMuon consistently improve over their hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW.

</details>


### [305] [Constrained Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning with Max-Min Criterion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31388)
*Giseung Park,Hyunyoung Nam,Woohyeon Byeon,Amir Leshem,Youngchul Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A constrained max-min MORL framework that enforces explicit constraint satisfaction alongside fairness objectives, with convergence guarantees in tabular settings and empirical validation across three applied domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend max-min MORL to include feasibility constraints, enabling fair yet feasible multi-objective decision-making in practical systems.

Method: Proposes a MORL framework combining the max-min objective with explicit constraint satisfaction; provides theoretical foundations and convergence analysis; implements a tabular algorithm; validates in simulations of building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion, and greenhouse-gas-emission-aware traffic management.

Result: Convergence is established under stated assumptions; the approach effectively balances fairness and constraint satisfaction; empirical results demonstrate practical effectiveness across the three domains.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled, applicable method for constrained fairness in MORL, suitable for real-world multi-objective control problems where constraints are essential.

Abstract: Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) extends standard RL by optimizing policies with respect to multiple, often conflicting, objectives. While max-min MORL has emerged as an effective approach for promoting fairness, its applicability remains limited, particularly when constraints must be incorporated. In this paper, we propose a MORL framework that integrates the max-min criterion with explicit constraint satisfaction. We establish a theoretical foundation for the proposed framework and validate the resulting algorithm through convergence analysis and experiments in tabular settings. We further demonstrate the practical relevance of our approach in simulated building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion control, and greenhouse-gas-emission-aware traffic management. Across these domains, our method effectively balances fairness and constraint satisfaction in multi-objective decision-making.

</details>


### [306] [Fixed Universal Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31423)
*Jingwen Liu,Alexandr Andoni,Daniel Hsu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Universal transformers are fixed architectures capable of simulating any transformer in a class via appropriate input embeddings. With sufficiently large embedding dimension, explicit sparse constructions achieve universality, and universality is generic: random initializations yield universal models with high probability. Empirical tests on algorithmic tasks support the theory, suggesting input representations largely drive transformer expressivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the source of transformer expressivity by decoupling architecture and learned weights, drawing an analogy to universal Turing machines where a fixed device is programmable via input descriptions.

Method: (1) Provide explicit sparse constructions of universal transformers that can simulate a target model when fed with a suitable embedding describing that model. (2) Prove universality is generic by showing random-initialized transformers are universal almost surely. (3) Empirically test the theory on algorithmic tasks (parenthesis balancing, multi-hop reasoning).

Result: Explicit sparse universal-transformer constructions achieve universality given sufficiently large embedding dimension. Universality holds generically: random initializations yield universal transformers with probability approaching 1. Empirical tasks corroborate the theory, indicating expressivity largely resides in the input embedding rather than in learned weights.

Conclusion: Fixed transformer architectures can be universal across a class of models via input embeddings, challenging the view that learned weights are the primary source of expressivity. This shifts attention to input representations and has implications for model design and training efficiency; results align with recent empirical observations of universality in practice.

Abstract: We introduce \emph{universal transformers}: fixed transformers that can simulate any transformer in a given class via a suitable input embedding. Analogous to a universal Turing machine, the input embedding encodes a description of the target model while all internal parameters remain fixed. We provide explicit sparse constructions achieving universality when the embedding dimension is sufficiently large, and further show that universality is generic: randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely, which aligns with recent empirical results of Zhong and Andreas (2024). We empirically validate our theory on the algorithmic tasks of parenthesis balancing and multi-hop reasoning. Our results suggest that much of a transformer's expressive power may reside in its input representation rather than its learned weights.

</details>


### [307] [DG-CoLearn: An Efficient Collaborative Learning Framework for Dynamic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31427)
*Ashley Hoi-Ting Au,Zikun Zhang,Ligang He,Qiang Ni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DG-CoLearn enables collaborative dynamic graph learning with incremental snapshot processing and server-mediated embeddings to preserve privacy, achieving large training speedups and communication reductions while improving prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic graphs incur high retraining costs and cross-partition data sharing raises privacy concerns; need efficient, privacy-preserving collaboration.

Method: Incremental processing of graph snapshots focusing on regions affected by temporal updates; temporal modeling to preserve history; server-mediated embedding exchange that enables multi-hop message passing without sharing raw cross-client structure.

Result: Up to 33.8x training speedup and 27.4x communication reduction; up to 13.36% F1 improvement (node classification) and 8.27% MAP improvement (link prediction) across tasks.

Conclusion: DG-CoLearn effectively balances efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

Abstract: Dynamic graph learning (DGL) is essential for modelling evolving graph data, but existing methods suffer from significant computational overhead due to repeated full-snapshot retraining and are not well-suited for collaborative settings with partitioned data. In realistic graph systems, cross-partition edges are unavoidable, but direct sharing of graph structure between clients may violate privacy constraints. We propose DG-CoLearn, a client-oblivious collaborative dynamic graph learning framework built on incremental graph snapshot processing, which focuses computation on graph regions affected by temporal updates while preserving historical information through temporal modelling. This incremental design is consistently applied across the entire graph processing pipeline, including a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism to enable accurate multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DG-CoLearn achieves up to 33.8$\times$ speedup in training time and 27.4$\times$ reduction in communication overhead, while consistently improving predictive performance on both node classification (up to 13.36% F1 improvement) and link prediction (up to 8.27% MAP improvement) tasks. These results highlight the effectiveness of DG-CoLearn in bridging efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

</details>


### [308] [Flow map learning in nonlinear vector autoregressive models: influence of the feature-library structure on the training error](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31438)
*Markus Gross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NVAR/NG-RC models' training error scales with time resolution depending on whether the feature library exactly or approximately represents Lie-series coefficients; delays help short-term fit but only improve long-horizon forecasting if the library is sufficiently nonlinear. Numerical experiments with chaotic systems validate the theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address identifiability and learnability in Markovian nonlinear dynamical systems, especially how discretization and feature libraries influence the ability to learn a system and forecast across time horizons, via Koopman operator perspectives and Lie-series coefficients.

Method: Develop pre-asymptotic scaling laws for training error versus time resolution for NVAR/NG-RC models. Analyze polynomial vector fields, with monomial and Fourier feature libraries, and derive how training error depends on temporal resolution, nonlinear degree, and number of delays. Explain mechanism for how delays interact with library richness. Validate theoretically derived scaling with numerical experiments on chaotic systems.

Result: The training error exhibits distinct scaling depending on whether the feature library can represent the Lie-series coefficients exactly or only approximately. Delay terms reduce short-term training error but improve long-horizon forecasts only when the library provides sufficient nonlinearity. Consequently, small training error can coincide with weak generalization when the model class is mismatched to the data-generating process. Numerical experiments on chaotic systems corroborate the predicted scaling laws.

Conclusion: Identifiability and forecasting for nonlinear dynamical systems via NVAR/NG-RC depend on the match between the library and the flow's Lie-series structure; richer nonlinear feature libraries are essential for robust long-horizon predictions, and care must be taken that low training error does not imply good generalization.

Abstract: Time series forecasting often requires learning nonlinear and time-delayed dependencies. A paradigmatic class of forecasting models are nonlinear vector autoregressive processes (NVAR), also known as next-generation reservoir computers (NG-RCs). These models approximate the Koopman operator on the space spanned by their explicit feature library. We consider the identifiability problem for learning Markovian nonlinear dynamical systems and show that the training error as a function of time resolution follows characteristic (pre-)asymptotic scaling laws. These laws depend on whether the feature library can represent the early Lie-series coefficients of the flow map (propagator) exactly or merely approximately. For dynamical systems governed by polynomial vector fields, we demonstrate the mechanism for NVAR/NG-RC models with monomial and Fourier feature libraries. We determine the dependence of the training error on the temporal resolution, the involved nonlinear degree, and the number of delay terms. While delay terms reduce the optimal one-step training error, they improve long-horizon forecasts only when the library provides sufficient nonlinearity. Thus, small training error coexists with weak generalization as the model class is mismatched to the true data-generating process. Numerical experiments on various chaotic dynamical systems confirm the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [309] [DRIFT: Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Efficient Multi-Turn Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31455)
*Jian Mu,Tianyi Lin,Chengwei Qin,Zhongxiang Dai,Yao Shu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRIFT decouples rollouts from optimization by using fixed offline trajectories and return-weighted supervised fine-tuning, achieving reinforcement-learning-like performance with the efficiency of standard supervised fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-turn LLMs require RL for long-horizon interactions, but online RL is costly; offline SFT is efficient but suffers from distribution shift. The paper leverages the KL-regularized RL–importance-weighted SFT equivalence to enable efficient, effective training.

Method: Sample offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, compute return-based importance weights, and optimize the policy via weighted supervised fine-tuning on the resulting dataset, thereby decoupling rollouts from optimization.

Result: Empirically, DRIFT matches or exceeds the performance of multi-turn RL baselines while preserving the training efficiency and simplicity of SFT.

Conclusion: DRIFT offers a practical framework to achieve RL-like performance with the efficiency of supervised fine-tuning for multi-turn interactive LLMs, with available code.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed in multi-turn interactive settings where users or environments can iteratively provide lightweight feedback. Unfortunately, optimizing such behavior presents a sharp dilemma in practice: online reinforcement learning is able to effectively address multi-turn dynamics but is prohibitively expensive due to the cost of generating full correction trajectories at every update, whereas offline supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is efficient but suffers from distribution shift and behavioral collapse. To this end, we novelly propose DRIFT (Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning), a framework that operationalizes the theoretical insight that the KL-regularized RL objective is equivalent to importance-weighted supervised learning. DRIFT decouples rollout from optimization by sampling offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, deriving return-based importance weights, and optimizing the policy via weighted SFT on the resulting dataset. Empirically, we demonstrate that DRIFT matches or exceeds the performance of multi-turn reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining the training efficiency and simplicity of standard supervised fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/2020-qqtcg/DRIFT.

</details>


### [310] [Balanced LoRA: Removing Parameter Invariance to Accelerate Convergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31484)
*Valérie Castin,Kimia Nadjahi,Pierre Ablin,Gabriel Peyré*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BaLoRA improves LoRA by projecting updates onto a balanced low-rank manifold, improving conditioning and speeding convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA is overparameterized: different factor pairs can produce the same adapted weights, and these choices yield different conditioning, affecting convergence.

Method: Introduce Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation (BaLoRA) which projects iterations onto a balanced manifold during optimization; the projection is lightweight and integrates with fine-tuning.

Result: BaLoRA converges faster than standard LoRA and achieves superior performance across various fine-tuning tasks.

Conclusion: Balancing low-rank factors preserves the adapted matrix while improving loss-landscape conditioning, enabling faster convergence and better performance.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the most widely adopted method for fine-tuning large language models. Notably, LoRA is inherently overparameterized: multiple pairs of low-rank factors can yield the same adapted weight matrix. We show--both theoretically and empirically--that these pairs exhibit significantly different condition numbers. As a result, converging to different loss minimizers directly impacts the convergence rate of LoRA. Building on this observation, we introduce Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation (BaLoRA), a variant of LoRA that projects iterates onto a balanced manifold. This manifold improves the conditioning of the loss landscape while preserving the adapted matrix. The projection step is computationally lightweight and integrates seamlessly into existing fine-tuning pipelines. Empirically, BaLoRA converges faster than standard LoRA and achieves superior performance across a range of fine-tuning tasks.

</details>


### [311] [Graphical einops: bridging tensor networks and computation graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31485)
*Vincent Wang-Maścianica,Nikhil Khatri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A formal graphical calculus for the structural fragment of tensor programming underlying einops is introduced, using nested graded tubes to model tensor axes.
