The key rewrite, grade-naturality (sliding spectacles over tubes), enables diagrammatic proofs of tensor-program identities and applications such as converting attention masks into preprocessing to realize efficient sparse attention blocks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To transform representational architecture diagrams into proof-enabled abstractions for tensor programming and to formalize tensor axes so that standard equivariance and other properties can be proven diagrammatically.

Method: Develop a graphical calculus where tensor axes are nested graded tubes around a base type; the tube boundary yields an undirected tensor-network view while the interior preserves directed computation graphs. The central rewrite is grade-naturality: sliding spectacles over tubes, enabling proofs and rewrites.

Result: The calculus enables short, diagrammatic derivations of standard equivariance proofs and demonstrates practical utility by converting attention masks into preprocessing operations, leading to efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.

Conclusion: The framework provides a proof-enabled diagrammatic language for tensor programming grounded in einops, with immediate benefits for proving tensor-program properties and practical optimizations in attention mechanisms.

Abstract: Architecture diagrams are ubiquitous in deep learning, but they are usually only representational: the tensor-program identities they suggest are still proved by prose and tensor-axis manipulation. We introduce a formal graphical calculus for the structural fragment of tensor programming underlying einops, making such diagrams proof-enabling. Our calculus represents tensor axes as nested graded tubes around a base type. The tube boundary recovers the undirected tensor-network view of axes, while the directed interior retains the operational reading of computation graphs. The key rewrite is grade-naturality: sliding spectacles over tubes. Standard equivariance proofs become short diagrammatic derivations. We additionally demonstrate how our rewrite system may be applied to convert attention masks into pre-processing operations, recovering efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.

</details>


### [312] [Assign and Add: A Mechanistic Study of Compositional Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31497)
*Brady Exoo,Alberto Bietti,John Sous*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Small transformers can generalize to unseen variable-number combinations via a shared modular addition mechanism, with training dynamics revealing three learning phases that build compositional structure and a theoretical framework linking it to internal transformer mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how compositional generalization emerges in transformers and identify the internal mechanisms that enable it, using a controlled setting of variable assignment and modular addition.

Method: Experiment with small transformer models trained on partitioned data for variables and numbers; analyze whether the same modular addition MLP module is used across direct inputs and variable-assigned inputs; track training dynamics to identify phases; develop a theoretical framework relating training dynamics to compositionality.

Result: Transformers generalize to unseen variable-number combinations; the same modular addition module is reused for different input routes (direct vs variable-assigned); training dynamics exhibit three phases (learning modular addition, acquiring variable assignment structure, refining to hard sequences); a theoretical framework explains how compositionality emerges from training dynamics.

Conclusion: Compositional generalization in transformers can arise naturally from the inherent modular structure of the models; internal mechanisms support composition of skills without explicit instruction to do so.

Abstract: Large language models are able to compose skills in order to perform complex tasks, many of which might not have been seen during training. The details of how exactly this composition occurs remain elusive. In this paper, we study a mechanism for compositional generalization in transformers by considering a simple controlled setting involving variable assignment and modular addition. By partitioning our training data into disjoint sets, we observe that small transformers are able to generalize to previously unseen combinations of variables and numbers. Our mechanistic analysis shows that the same ``modular addition'' MLP module is used whether the inputs are given directly or indirectly through a separate variable assignment mechanism. We also analyze the training dynamics from an empirical lens, which reveals three phases of learning: first, modular addition is learned, then the structure required for variable assignment, and finally a refinement phase where the model generalizes to some hard sequences not seen in training. Finally, we provide a theoretical framework to explain how compositionality emerges from training dynamics. These results suggest that compositional generalization can be a natural consequence of the compositionality of internal mechanisms in~transformers.

</details>


### [313] [Scalable Inference-Time Annealing with Surrogate Likelihood Estimators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31498)
*Daniel Peñaherrera,Rishal Aggarwal,David Ryan Koes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SITA introduces scalable inference-time annealing for molecular sampling by retraining flow-based models to generate samples at decreasing temperatures, using an energy-based surrogate to avoid score-divergence computations and enabling efficient Boltzmann distribution sampling; achieves state-of-the-art on alanine dipeptide/tripeptide with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational inefficiency of sampling Boltzmann distributions in chemistry/biophysics and the intractability of divergence-based importance weighting in diffusion-model annealing when scaling to larger systems.

Method: Train flow-based models across a temperature ladder (inference-time annealing) and use an energy-based model to provide fast surrogate likelihoods, eliminating the need to compute score-field divergences while progressively lowering temperature.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art sampling performance on Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms; code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: SITA offers a scalable and practical approach to Boltzmann sampling in biomolecular systems by combining flow-based models with energy-based surrogates, bypassing divergence computations and enabling efficient inference-time annealing.

Abstract: A long standing challenge in computational chemistry and biophysics is efficiently sampling the Boltzmann distribution of molecules. Advances in generative modeling have been proposed to address the limitations of conventional sampling techniques by eliminating the computational cost of simulation. A promising direction is iteratively finetuning diffusion models along a temperature ladder whereby training data is generated via importance sampling during inference-time annealing. Unfortunately, these methods require computing a divergence over the score field to estimate importance weights, rendering them intractable for larger systems. Here we present scalable inference-time annealing (SITA), which retrains flow-based models to generate samples at progressively lower temperatures using an energy-based model to facilitate fast surrogate likelihoods. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms. Our code is available at: https://github.com/countrsignal/sita.git

</details>


### [314] [When Are Multimodal Predictions Biologically Supported? A Diagnostic Evaluation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31504)
*Dylan Steiner,Gustavo Arango-Argoty,Gerald Sun,Etai Jacob*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DECAT is a post-hoc, model-agnostic framework for evaluating multimodal oncology representations, classifying them into four diagnostic scenarios of shared biology, modality-specific biology, or confounding, using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure; it requires no confounder labels and returns indeterminate when evidence is insufficient. Validated on synthetic data (>2,500 representations across four model classes) and TCGA data (8,979 patients), showing that entangled models may falsely claim shared biology, and that DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC, confirmed by post-hoc stratification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether multimodal models in oncology learn biology shared across modalities, are limited to modality-specific biology, or rely on spurious correlations due to confounders, beyond standard predictive performance.

Method: Post-hoc evaluation on learned representations using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure to classify representations into four diagnostic scenarios; model-agnostic and requiring no knowledge of specific confounders; outputs include an indeterminate when evidence is insufficient; validated on synthetic data (four model classes, >2,500 representations) and real TCGA data (8,979 patients) across multimodal embeddings and five pretrained pathology foundation models.

Result: DECAT reveals confounding not detectable by AUROC in real foundation model embeddings; entangled models (e.g., CLIP) show near-perfect detection of shared biology but high false-positive rates where shared biology is absent; false-claim rate increases with confound strength; larger cohorts and stronger representations yield higher confidence in incorrect diagnoses; DECAT detects confounding without confounder labels, corroborated by post-hoc stratification.

Conclusion: DECAT provides a robust post-hoc diagnostic tool to assess whether multimodal representations capture genuine cross-modality biology or confounding, reducing misleading claims about shared biology in oncology models and offering a complement to AUROC-based evaluation; applicable to both multimodal embeddings and pathology foundation models.

Abstract: Multimodal models in oncology can produce accurate predictions, but accurate prediction does not reveal whether the model has learned biology that is shared across modalities, biology confined to one modality, or spurious correlations that reflect confounders rather than genuine biology. We introduce DECAT, a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation framework that classifies multimodal representations into four diagnostic scenarios for a given task and modality, using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure. The framework operates on learned representations, requires no knowledge of which specific confounder is present, and returns indeterminate when the evidence is insufficient. We validate DECAT on synthetic data across four multimodal model classes (over 2,500 trained representations) and on real data from 8,979 TCGA patients, evaluating both multimodal embeddings and five pretrained pathology foundation models. Entangled models (e.g., CLIP) achieve near-perfect shared biology detection but falsely claim shared biology in the majority of cases where it is absent on real foundation model embeddings. This false claim rate increases with confound strength so that larger cohorts and stronger representations produce more confident but still incorrect diagnoses. Applied to both multimodal TCGA embeddings and five pathology foundation models without paired RNA, DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC without requiring the confounder labels, as confirmed by post-hoc stratification.

</details>


### [315] [On the Relationship Between Activation Outliers and Feature Death in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31518)
*Elana Simon,Etowah Adams,James Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation outliers drive feature death in sparse autoencoders; mean-centering activations prevents death and recovers dictionary usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why many SAE features never activate and how to mitigate it; provide a simple, principled preprocessing fix applicable across architectures.

Method: Analyze 454 model–layer configurations across language, vision, protein, and genomics; quantify dimension-level activation statistics; define γ = ||μ|| / ||σ||; correlate γ with initial death rates (dead-by-TopK, dead-by-ReLU); test mean-centering.

Result: Death rates scale with γ; high γ yields near-zero to high death; initial death rates correlate Spearman 0.89 (TopK) and 0.82 (ReLU); dead features can revive but require learning the activation mean; mean-centering eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models.

Conclusion: Outlier-driven feature death is mitigated by mean-centering, providing a principled preprocessing step; mechanism validated and generalizable across modalities.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose neural network activations into interpretable features, but many learned features never activate, a problem called feature death that wastes dictionary capacity and can reintroduce superposition. Death rates vary dramatically between models: near-zero on GPT-2, over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations. We find that dimension-level activation outliers (dimensions whose mean magnitude is large relative to per-token variation) cause this by shifting pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean. Features anti-aligned with the mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire. We formalize outlier severity as $γ= \|μ\|/\|σ\|$; it predicts initial death rates (Spearman $ρ= 0.89$ for dead-by-TopK, $0.82$ for dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer combinations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models. Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, a process that is prohibitively slow at high $γ$. Mean-centering (subtracting the activation mean) sidesteps this and eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models, confirming the mechanism and providing a principled basis for when and why this preprocessing step is necessary.

</details>


### [316] [Chem-PerturBridge: a harmonized compendium of small molecule perturbation transcriptomic effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31522)
*Artur Szałata,Olga Novitskaia,Maiia Shulman,Matthew Mella,Altynbek Zhubanchaliyev,Fabian J. Theis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chem-PerturBridge is a harmonized, large-scale perturbation transcriptomics resource enabling cross-dataset agreement analysis and pretrained representation learning for small molecules; it improves downstream embeddings and robustness across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmented transcriptomic perturbation data across technologies and pipelines hinders cross-dataset analyses and model training; a unified resource with standardized identifiers and condition-level effects enables reliable evaluation and transfer learning for chemical perturbations.

Method: Assembles >37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types into Chem-PerturBridge with standardized metadata and replicate-aware condition-level effects. Evaluates cross-dataset agreement of log fold changes (logFC) for matched conditions and conducts replicate agreement analyses. Uses Chem-PerturBridge for pretraining compound representations and evaluates on compound-held-out OP3 splits and a molecule-heldout evaluation across 11 datasets.

Result: Matched same-compound conditions show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings/magnitudes across dataset pairs, often below same-context different-compound baselines. Direction of logFC is more stable and typically exceeds baselines. Pretraining with Chem-PerturBridge improves embeddings over L1000-only, Morgan fingerprints, and the OP3 baseline across metrics; in molecule-heldout evaluations across 11 datasets, models trained with Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match non-Chem-PerturBridge baselines.

Conclusion: Chem-PerturBridge enables diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and supports model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data for improved representation learning in chemical perturbation modeling.

Abstract: Large perturbation models require training data encompassing chemical, cellular, and assay diversity. Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling, however, are fragmented across technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines. We introduce Chem-PerturBridge, a harmonized multi-dataset resource comprising over 37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types, with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. We use the resource to evaluate matched-condition agreement across datasets and replicate agreement within datasets. Matched same-compound conditions generally show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings and magnitudes across most dataset pairs, often falling below same-context different-compound baselines. In contrast, logFC direction agreement is substantially more stable and usually exceeds these baselines. We further evaluate Chem-PerturBridge as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning. Under a compound-held-out OP3 evaluation split, embeddings pretrained on Chem-PerturBridge improve over L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and the descriptor-free OP3 baseline across metrics. An extensive molecule-holdout evaluation across 11 datasets further shows that models trained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match those that are not. Chem-PerturBridge therefore supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data.

</details>


### [317] [Value Functions as Supermartingale Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31524)
*Alessandro Abate,Daniel Contro,Mirco Giacobbe,Agustín Martínez-Suñé,Diptarko Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The work connects RL with formal certificate methods for ω-regular properties by showing that, under a suitable reward, the value function of a policy satisfying the property forms a Streett supermartingale certificate. Validated on finite MDPs and applicable to finite/countable/continuous state spaces, enabling certificate synthesis via RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Certification methods using real-valued supermartingale certificates can prove almost-sure satisfaction of ω-regular properties in general state spaces, but RL methods often lack guarantees for satisfying specifications. Bridging these lines aims to provide formal guarantees for RL policies relative to ω-regular specifications.

Method: Establish a theoretical mapping: for a policy that almost surely satisfies an ω-regular property, there exists a reward structure such that its value function encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for the specification. The authors validate the theory experimentally on finite MDPs and argue applicability to finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces.

Result: A novel theoretical connection is established between RL value functions and Streett supermartingale certificates for ω-regular specifications. Experimental validation on finite MDPs supports the theory, and the approach extends to broader state spaces, suggesting a principled method for certificate synthesis via RL.

Conclusion: The paper provides a principled bridge between formal certification and RL for ω-regular properties, enabling certificate-based guarantees for RL policies across various state-space cardinalities and pointing to a practical route for synthesizing certificates through reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Certification methods for stochastic systems provide sufficient proof rules, based on real-valued supermartingale certificates, to determine the almost-sure satisfaction of $ω$-regular properties (and therefore of linear temporal logic) over general state spaces, encompassing both countably infinite and continuous state spaces. Conversely, reinforcement learning (RL) methods for $ω$-regular tasks have received considerable attention, but they typically lack formal guarantees that the learned policy satisfies the specification, except possibly for finite state and action spaces. We bridge these two lines of research by establishing a novel theoretical connection: under an appropriate reward, the value function associated to a policy that almost surely satisfies an $ω$-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for that specification. Our results, validated experimentally on finite Markov decision processes, hold for finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces, suggesting a principled route to certificate synthesis via RL.

</details>


### [318] [The Dynamic-Probabilistic Consistency Gap in Chaotic Surrogate Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31547)
*Andre Herz,Matthijs Pals,Daniel Durstewitz,Georgia Koppe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DPC: A gap where finite-horizon probabilistic objectives misalign with local dynamics. KAFFEE—a Kalman-filter-based training framework—mitigates this by evaluating likelihood on innovations while propagating covariance via learned Jacobians. Demonstrated on chaotic Lorenz-96 with halting of certain failure modes and preserved predictive performance; enables in-context Bayesian filtering across 13 chaotic systems with minimal zero-shot degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty estimates in dynamical-system surrogates should reflect the true local tangent dynamics. Open-loop probabilistic objectives can cause core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty, weakening physical expansion or decoupling uncertainty from dynamics.

Method: KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation) uses a differentiable extended Kalman filter during training. It computes likelihood on innovations (local predictive residuals) and transports covariance through learned local Jacobians, aligning training with local dynamics while maintaining differentiable optimization.

Result: In stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces the identified DPC failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants relative to open-loop objectives, and keeps predictive scores competitive. Across 13 chaotic systems, DPC gaps persist with standard DSR but KAFFEE enables in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.

Conclusion: KAFFEE effectively mitigates the dynamic-probabilistic consistency gap in dynamical system reconstruction by incorporating local Kalman-filtered likelihood and Jacobian-driven covariance propagation, supporting more faithful uncertainty and invariant reconstruction without sacrificing short-term predictive performance.

Abstract: Dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR) aims to learn surrogate models that capture the dynamics underlying time-series data. Reliably deploying these surrogates requires uncertainty estimates consistent with the learned dynamics. We expose a dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap: the pursuit of finite-horizon probabilistic objectives can degrade dynamics or decouple predictive uncertainty from the local tangent dynamics it ought to reflect. We isolate three mechanisms behind this gap: core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty. Specifically, we show that open-loop Gaussian rollout objectives can penalize Jacobian-generated covariance growth in chaotic systems, encouraging optimization shortcuts that weaken physical expansion or decouple uncertainty from it. To mitigate this gap, we propose KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation), a differentiable extended Kalman filter-based training framework that evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) while transporting covariance through learned local Jacobians. On stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces the identified failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants relative to open-loop objectives, and maintains competitive predictive scores. We further show that the DPC gap appears when probabilistically adapting a DSR foundation model across 13 chaotic systems, where KAFFEE enables in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.

</details>


### [319] [Functional Attention: From Pairwise Affinities to Functional Correspondences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31559)
*Jiefang Xiao,Maolin Gao,Simon Weber,Guandao Yang,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Functional Attention (FUNCATTN) reframes attention as a functional correspondence between adaptive bases, replacing token-wise softmax affinities with structured linear operators to create a resolution-invariant, globally-aware operator learning representation. It matches state-of-the-art across multiple tasks while being robust to discretization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operator learning aims to map between infinite-dimensional function spaces. Traditional transformer-based approaches treat continuous fields as discrete tokens, risking loss of global functional structure and poor generalization across discretizations. A need exists for representations that capture global dependencies and are discretization-robust.

Method: Introduce Functional Attention by reinterpreting attention as a functional map between adaptive bases. Inspired by geometric functional maps, replace softmax-based affinities with structured linear operators to obtain a compact, global representation that encodes dependencies across the entire function space.

Result: Empirical evaluations show FUNCATTN matches state-of-the-art performance on operator learning tasks, including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, and demonstrates robustness to varying discretizations and resolutions.

Conclusion: Functional Attention provides a principled, resolution-invariant framework for operator learning that leverages structured linear operators to capture global functional structure, achieving strong performance across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces, or operator learning, is essential for many machine learning applications. Although transformer-based operators are popular, they often rely on token-wise attention. These methods treat continuous fields as discrete tokens and usually ignore the global functional structure. We introduce \emph{Functional Attention}, which reinterprets attention as a functional correspondence between adaptive bases. Inspired by geometric functional maps, our method replaces softmax affinities with structured linear operators. This yields a compact, generalizable, resolution-invariant representation that explicitly captures global dependencies. Experiments demonstrate that \emph{Functional Attention} can match state-of-the-art performance in many operator learning tasks, including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, while remaining robust to varying discretizations. Project page is available at https://github.com/xjffff/FUNCATTN.

</details>


### [320] [Effective Biological Representation Learning by Masking Gene Expression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31562)
*Kian Kenyon-Dean,Alina Selega,Ihab Bendidi,Jordan M. Sorokin,Luca Bertinetto,David Errington,Hayley Donnella,Oren Kraus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Inductive self-supervised learning with a masked autoencoder (TxFM) yields superior gene representations on RNA-seq when paired with curated DiverseRNA-1.4M data, outperforming larger foundation models; architecture and data curation are crucial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the persistent mismatch where deep representation learning often underperforms linear baselines on transcriptomic data due to technical noise and batch effects, and to evaluate whether inductive SSL can provide a true advantage for RNA-seq representation through careful model design and training data curation.

Method: Develop TxFM, a masked autoencoding model tailored for diverse RNA-seq count data, perform extensive ablations to identify architecture configurations that enable strong transfer; curate DiverseRNA-1.4M as a public training corpus; evaluate gene-level representations on transfer tasks

Result: TxFM trained on DiverseRNA-1.4M yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform FMs trained on atlas-scale corpora >100× larger, demonstrating practical gains for transcriptomic representation via inductive SSL when combined with curated data and architecture choices.

Conclusion: Inductive self-supervised learning is a viable approach for transcriptomics representation provided a careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation; architecture-aware SSL and high-quality curated data can surpass much larger foundation models.

Abstract: RNA sequencing produces rich and diverse datasets of gene expression, offering compelling insights into cellular state and function that have many applications in drug discovery. Modeling such data is challenging due to inherent technical noise and experimental batch effects, as evidenced by many existing transcriptomic foundation models (FMs) underperforming relative to linear baselines. Such results raise the question of whether deep representation learning provides a distinct advantage over the direct use of raw transcript counts. Our work explores this by developing a new self-supervised model, TxFM, with a focus on inductive representation learning evaluations. TxFM employs a masked autoencoding approach tailored to diverse RNA-seq count data, and our ablation study empirically identifies crucial architecture configurations required for strong transfer performance. Additionally, we curate a public training corpus, DiverseRNA-1.4M, and find that TxFM trained on this curated dataset yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform FMs trained on atlas-scale corpora over 100x larger. Overall, our results indicate that inductive self-supervised learning is a viable modeling approach for transcriptomics representation, provided a careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation.

</details>


### [321] [Giving Sensors a Voice: Multimodal JEPA for Semantic Time-Series Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31580)
*Utsav Dutta,Gerardo Pastrana,Sina Khoshfetrat Pakazad,Henrik Ohlsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHARM is a channel-aware Transformer for heterogeneous multivariate time series that uses JEPA with a novel temporal-stability loss and description-conditioned gating to produce robust, interpretable embeddings, achieving strong linear-probe performance across anomaly detection, classification, and forecasting, with cross-dataset generalization aided by channel descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Representing heterogeneous multivariate time series is underexplored by general-purpose methods. Existing approaches struggle to leverage channel-level semantics and maintain robustness to sensor noise while preserving useful inter-channel relationships and interpretability.

Method: Introduce CHARM, a channel-aware Transformer encoder that is equivariant to channel order. Train with Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and a temporal-stability loss. Use latent-space prediction for noise robustness and description-aware gating to learn inter-channel relationships. Channel-level textual descriptions serve as channel identifiers conditioning the model, enabling cross-dataset generalization.

Result: Embeddings learned by CHARM enable strong performance with a linear probe across tasks (anomaly detection, classification, short- and long-term forecasting). The performance is driven mainly by the JEPA objective and the conditioning architecture, while text descriptions provide interpretability via learned inter-channel relationships and aid cross-dataset generalization.

Conclusion: CHARM provides a scalable, interpretable representation learning framework for heterogeneous multivariate time series, delivering robust embeddings through JEPA and channel-aware conditioning. Textual channel descriptions enhance interpretability and cross-dataset generalization, with linear-readout performance indicating high-quality representations.

Abstract: Transformer-based architectures have advanced sequence modeling in language and vision, yet general-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series remains underexplored. We introduce CHARM (Channel-Aware Representation Model), which incorporates channel-level textual descriptions into a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order. CHARM is trained with a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and a novel loss promoting informative, temporally stable embeddings; latent-space prediction encourages robustness to sensor noise while description-aware gating provides interpretability through learned inter-channel relationships. Across anomaly detection, classification, and short- and long-term forecasting, the learned embeddings achieve strong performance using only a linear probe. Performance is driven primarily by the JEPA objective and conditioning architecture, with text descriptions serving as channel identifiers for cross-dataset generalization.

</details>


### [322] [A Tight Theory of Error Feedback Algorithms in Distributed Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31594)
*Daniel Berg Thomsen,Adrien Taylor,Aymeric Dieuleveut*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tight convergence analyses for EF and EF21 gradient compression in distributed optimization, deriving optimal step-sizes and Lyapunov functions, independent of the number of agents, matching single-agent guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication costs dominate distributed learning; gradient compression degrades convergence. Error-feedback methods EF and EF21 require precise performance characterization.

Method: Derive tight convergence proofs for EF and EF21, construct optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each method, and identify optimal step-size choices. Show results hold independently of agent count and recover single-agent guarantees.

Result: Proves convergence with optimal parameter choices for EF and EF21; Lyapunov functions are optimized for each method; guarantees are independent of the number of agents and match the best-known single-agent guarantees.

Conclusion: EF and EF21 can be analyzed with tight, parameter-optimized convergence results in distributed settings; optimal step-sizes and Lyapunov constructions yield agent-count-agnostic guarantees, aligning with single-agent optimality.

Abstract: Communication costs are a major bottleneck in distributed learning and first-order optimization. A common approach to alleviate this issue is to compress the gradient information exchanged between agents. However, such compression typically degrades the convergence guarantees of gradient-based methods. Error feedback mechanisms provide a simple and computationally cheap remedy for this issue, but numerous variants have been proposed, and their relative performance remains poorly understood. This paper provides tight convergence analyses for two of the main error-feedback algorithms from the literature, the classic Error Feedback method (EF) and Error Feedback 21 (EF21), by identifying optimal step-size choices and constructing optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each method. The results hold independently of the number of agents and recover the known best guarantees possible in the single-agent regime.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [323] [Learning-Based Navigation for Indoor Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30468)
*Tri-Tin Nguyen,Tien-Dat Nguyen,Gia-Uy Le,Vinh Nguyen,Vinh-Hao Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots that fuses a supervised neural global planner with a Learning-Based DWA local planner, trained via behavior cloning and PPO with feasibility masking; demonstrated in simulation and real-world; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve robust, obstacle-aware indoor navigation by combining learning-based global path planning with RL-refined local control, enabling feasible routes and reliable motion commands.

Method: Global planner: supervised neural network trained on cost-aware A* trajectories; Local planner: Learning-Based DWA using discrete action lattice; policy trained by behavior cloning then refined with PPO with feasibility-aware masking; evaluation in simulation and real-world; code release.

Result: The framework yields feasible global routes and reliable local commands for safe goal-directed navigation with obstacles; experiments validate effectiveness of the integrated approach.

Conclusion: Integration of learning-based global planning and RL-refined local control enhances indoor mobile robot navigation; the approach is practical with demonstrated performance and publicly available source code.

Abstract: This paper presents a learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots. The proposed method combines a supervised neural global planner, trained from cost-aware A* expert trajectories, with the proposed Learning-Based DWA local planner, which is formulated as discrete candidate selection over the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) action lattice. For local planning, the policy is first trained by behavior cloning and then refined by Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under feasibility-aware masking. The framework is implemented and evaluated in both simulated and real-world indoor environments. Experimental results show that the proposed method generates feasible global routes and reliable local motion commands for safe goal-directed navigation in the presence of obstacles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating learning-based global planning with reinforcement-learning-refined local control for indoor mobile robot navigation. The source code will be released at https://ntdathp.github.io/rl_robot_web/.

</details>


### [324] [ELAN4D: Embodiment-Centric 4D Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Models via Plug-and-Play Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30484)
*Zeyuan He,Bowen Yang,Zhirui Fang,Keru Zhou,Lei Jiang,Jingjing Qian,Fan Mo,Junchi Yan,Philip Torr,Xiu Li,Li Jiang,Jialin Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ELAN4D introduces an embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training framework for Vision-Language-Action policies. It uses forward-kinematics-derived 3D keypoint tracks as predictive spatiotemporal supervision, with a plug-in train-time track decoder and gradient isolation, achieving robust, generalizable manipulation across OOD perturbations while keeping inference unchanged.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA policies are reactive, regressing actions from present observations without explicit future dynamics modeling, limiting generalization under out-of-distribution perturbations.

Method: Compute 3D displacement tracks of robot keypoints (joints, end-effector) from forward kinematics using proprioceptive data; introduce a lightweight track decoder as an auxiliary branch to inject 4D supervision into the action expert with gradient isolation; train with this supervision; discard the decoder at inference; keep the base policy interface intact; evaluate on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0, and real-world tasks.

Result: ELAN4D yields consistent improvements over strong VLA baselines, achieving best overall performance and substantial gains under out-of-distribution perturbations such as camera, background, and layout shifts.

Conclusion: Embodiment-centric 4D supervision effectively enhances robustness and generalization of manipulation policies, enabling VLA models to leverage future spatio-temporal dynamics without altering inference-time interfaces.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise for robotic manipulation, yet most existing policies operate reactively by directly regressing actions from current observations, without explicitly modeling future dynamics. This limits their ability to generalize under out-of-distribution perturbations. To address this issue, we propose ELAN4D, an embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training framework that enhances VLA policies with future robot keypoint tracks as predictive spatio-temporal supervision. Using only forward kinematics from proprioceptive states, we derive 3D displacement tracks of robot keypoints, such as joints and the end-effector, with negligible preprocess cost. These tracks provide metric and compact supervision without requiring external trackers or reconstruction. A plug-and-play auxiliary branch with a lightweight track decoder injects this 4D signal into the action expert while preserving the pretrained vision-language backbone through gradient isolation. The track decoder is discarded during inference, leaving the base policy interface unchanged. Extensive experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0 and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that ELAN4D consistently improves over strong VLA baselines, achieving the best overall performance and substantial gains under out-of-distribution perturbations, including camera, background, and layout shifts. These results highlight the effectiveness of embodiment-centric 4D supervision for building more robust and generalizable manipulation policies.

</details>


### [325] [CoMo3R-SLAM: Collaborative Monocular Dense SLAM with Learned 3D Reconstruction Priors for Outdoor Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30488)
*Zhihao Cao,Qi Shao,Shuhao Zhai,Feng Tian,Anh Nguyen,Hesheng Wang,Baoru Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoMo3R-SLAM is a collaborative monocular dense SLAM system that leverages learned 3D reconstruction priors to enable cross-agent dense mapping without depth sensors, achieving robust, globally consistent metric maps online at 8 FPS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collaborative dense SLAM for outdoor multi-robot teams is hampered by payload and calibration costs of depth sensors and by monocular scale ambiguity and unreliable inter-agent data association, especially in outdoor scenes with low overlap and repetitive structures.

Method: Each agent runs a prior-guided front-end for real-time monocular tracking and local dense fusion. A central coordinator performs dense point-map matching for cross-agent verification, closed-form Sim(3) gauge synchronization, and GPU-accelerated global bundle adjustment with segment-level depth optimization; the system requires neither depth sensors nor parametric intrinsics and relies on learned 3D priors for robust reconstruction.

Result: On Tanks and Temples and Waymo sequences, CoMo3R-SLAM achieves the best ATE on three of four Tanks and Temples scenes and competitive Waymo accuracy, matching or surpassing RGB-D methods while operating online at 8 FPS.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates that monocular collaborative dense SLAM with learned priors can produce robust, globally consistent metric maps in outdoor multi-robot settings without depth sensors, enabling scalable multi-robot perception.

Abstract: Collaborative dense SLAM is essential for multi-robot teams to achieve scalable and consistent 3D perception across large-scale outdoor environments. Existing systems typically depend on depth sensors, incurring significant payload, power, and calibration costs. Monocular RGB cameras are a lightweight alternative, but collaborative monocular dense SLAM remains difficult due to scale ambiguity, unreliable inter-agent data association, especially in outdoor scenes where low overlap and repetitive structures make traditional feature matching unreliable, motivating robust geometric information. We propose CoMo3R-SLAM, the first collaborative monocular dense RGB SLAM system that leverages robust learned feed-forward 3D reconstruction priors for outdoor multi-agent mapping. Each agent runs a prior-guided front-end for real-time tracking and local dense fusion, while a coordinator performs dense pointmap matching for cross-agent verification, closed-form Sim(3) gauge synchronization, and GPU-accelerated global bundle adjustment with segment-level depth optimization. Requiring neither depth sensors nor parametric intrinsics, our system produces robust cross-agent constraints and globally consistent metric maps from monocular RGB alone. On Tanks and Temples and Waymo sequences, CoMo3R-SLAM achieves the best ATE on three of four Tanks and Temples scenes and competitive Waymo accuracy, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art RGB-D methods while running online at 8 FPS.

</details>


### [326] [Physics-informed Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning under Hybrid Contact Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30503)
*Vittorio Giammarino,Anastasios Manganaris,Ahmed H. Qureshi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Naive physics-informed GCRL struggles with contact-rich manipulation due to hybrid dynamics; the paper proposes contact-aware and hierarchical biases to extend Pi-GCRL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable goal-conditioned RL to generalize in sparse-reward settings, addressing challenges from high-dimensional, hybrid, and contact-dependent dynamics in GCRL.

Method: Introduce contact-aware and hierarchical formulations that apply physics-informed inductive biases selectively within the manipulation problem.

Result: Existing Pi-GCRL methods degrade when naively applied to contact-rich tasks; the proposed formulations mitigate this issue and constitute a principled step toward handling contact-rich manipulation.

Conclusion: This work advances Pi-GCRL toward applicability in contact-rich manipulation by selectively leveraging physics-informed priors.

Abstract: Learning to reach arbitrary goals from sparse feedback requires agents to infer a rich notion of reachability across state--goal pairs. Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) tackles this challenge by learning policies that generalize across goals, but this generalization becomes increasingly difficult as the underlying dynamics become high-dimensional, hybrid, or contact-dependent. To address this issue, physics-informed GCRL (Pi-GCRL) introduces optimal-control-inspired inductive biases into goal-conditioned value learning. While Pi-GCRL methods have proven effective in navigation and object-free goal-reaching domains, their reliability in contact-rich tasks remains unclear, where contact interactions induce hybrid dynamics, mode-dependent controllability, and nonsmooth value landscapes. In this work, we show that these structural properties can cause existing Pi-GCRL methods to degrade when applied naively to contact-rich manipulation. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce contact-aware and hierarchical formulations that apply physics-informed inductive biases selectively across the manipulation problem. Our results provide a principled step toward extending Pi-GCRL to contact-rich manipulation.

</details>


### [327] [VLM-GLoc: Vision-Language Model Enhanced Monte Carlo Localization for Robust Semantic Global Localization in Cluttered Quasi-Static Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30506)
*Shivendra Agrawal,Bradley Hayes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A semantic Monte Carlo Localization approach (VLM-GLoc) uses open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models to enhance global localization in geometrically aliased indoor environments, via an inverse semantic proposal mechanism and hierarchical MCL, achieving substantial gains over geometry-only baselines in grocery store and lab settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor environments exhibit geometric and semantic aliasing due to repetitive structure (aisles, furniture). Traditional geometry-only or domain-specific vision pipelines underperform with long-tail semantic distributions and clutter. Open-vocabulary VLMs can provide discriminative semantic cues and filtering to improve localization.

Method: Propose VLM-GLoc: hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization that uses Vision-Language Models as a shared semantic observation front-end. Introduce an inverse semantic proposal mechanism that seeds particles via text-to-map retrieval, enabling global localization. Leverage VLMs for discriminative text features, blur/dynamic object filtering, and permanence-based data augmentation.

Result: Evaluated on two real-world settings: a 3,500 sq. ft grocery store (cellphone platform) and a 3,700 sq. ft lab (quadruped platform). Achieved global localization success of 70% and 74%, respectively, outperforming traditional geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

Conclusion: VLM-GLoc demonstrates that open-vocabulary semantic observations from VLMs can significantly improve global localization in geometrically aliased indoor environments by coupling semantic proposals with hierarchical MCL, outperforming baselines.

Abstract: Global localization in geometrically aliased, quasi-static environments such as grocery stores, offices, schools, and hospitals poses a significant challenge for mobile robots. Grocery stores with parallel aisles and a long tailed distribution of products, as well as offices and labs with repetitive furniture such as chairs, desks, monitors, and doors, exemplify common indoor environments that present geometric and even semantic ambiguity. Traditional approaches rely either on distinct geometric features or on domain-specific vision pipelines that struggle with long-tail semantic distributions and transient visual clutter. We present VLM-GLoc, a method for hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) that leverages open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a unified semantic observation front-end. We hypothesize a three-fold benefit from VLMs: (1) extracting highly discriminative rich text features, (2) implicit quality filtering of blurry or dynamic objects, and (3) permanence reasoning for targeted data augmentation. We introduce an inverse semantic proposal mechanism that seeds particles via text-to-map retrieval. Evaluated across two real-world environments with different characteristics and two different platforms: a 3,500 sq. ft. grocery store with a cellphone and a 3,700 sq. ft. lab space with a quadruped, VLM-GLoc achieves 70% and 74% global localization success respectively, substantially outperforming traditional geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

</details>


### [328] [ARISTO Hand: Sensing-Driven Distal Hyperextension for Fine-Grained Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30508)
*Aaron Kim,Dong Ho Kang,Mark Helwig,Mingyo Seo,Kazuto Yokoyama,Tetsuya Narita,Luis Sentis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ARISTO Hand uses active distal hyperextension and a hybrid fingertip-sensing setup (rigid nail-mounted force-torque sensor plus soft capacitive tactile array) to improve manipulation of thin objects, achieving 2.76x higher pull-out forces for 1–20 mm thicknesses while preserving grasping capability; edge-contact sensing is improved with the rigid sensor; validated via force characterization and an SD card extraction/insertion task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable precise contact geometry and reliable force perception for manipulating thin objects, addressing limitations of current anthropomorphic hands in both mechanical capability and sensing at the fingertip.

Method: A tendon-driven ARISTO Hand implementing active distal hyperextension and a hybrid sensing fingertip: rigid nail-mounted force-torque sensor coupled with a soft capacitive tactile array. Validation includes quantitative force characterization and a multi-stage SD card extraction/insertion task, with supplementary video.

Result: Demonstrates a 2.76× increase in pull-out force for object thicknesses of 1–20 mm without sacrificing nominal grasp, and provides reliable force measurements during edge contacts where proprioceptive estimates degrade near kinematic singularities.

Conclusion: The integrated architecture combining active hyperextension and hybrid fingertip sensing improves thin-object manipulation and edge-contact force perception, validated experimentally and demonstrated via the SD card task; additional materials available online.

Abstract: Manipulating thin objects requires precise contact geometry and reliable force perception, yet many anthropomorphic robotic hands lack the mechanical and sensing capabilities needed for such interactions. We present the ARISTO Hand, a tendon-driven robotic hand that integrates active distal hyperextension with a hybrid fingertip-sensing architecture that combines a rigid, nail-mounted force-torque sensor and a soft capacitive tactile array. Active hyperextension enables controlled fingertip engagement beyond the kinematic limits of standard flexion, increasing pull-out force by 2.76x for object thicknesses of 1-20 mm while preserving the nominal grasp capability. The rigid nail-mounted sensor provides reliable force measurements during edge contacts, where the sensitivity of proprioceptive force estimation degrades as the contact geometry approaches kinematic singularities. We validate the proposed architecture through quantitative force characterization and a multi-stage SD card extraction and insertion task. Video and supplementary materials are available at: https://aristohand.github.io

</details>


### [329] [Any-ttach: Quick End-effector Swapping Enables Manipulation Dexterity with Simplicity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30569)
*Weizhe Ni,Jinzhou Li,Haoyu Li,Cody Andres Alessio-Bunnell,Wenjing Pan,Xianyi Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tool-centric manipulation enables rapid end-effector swapping to achieve dexterity with simpler hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human hand complexity may largely serve tool use; solving dexterity with fixed hardware is hard; proposing rapid tool exchange as a scalable design principle.

Method: Introduce Any-ttach: a low-cost automatic tool-swapping mechanism for an open-close robot interface, a handheld demonstration device, and a planning framework that composes learned, parameterized, and planned tool-use skills; supports diverse tools and end-effector modules via a common interface.

Result: Improved tool-swapping reliability, increased demonstration efficiency, reduced tool-pose variability; demonstrated six tool-use subskills across two long-horizon tasks (sandwich, cucumber) using end-effector switching and execution monitoring.

Conclusion: Robots can expand manipulation capability by enabling rapid tool exchange and modular end-effectors, complementing or reducing the need for increasingly high-DoF hands.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation dexterity is often pursued by building increasingly complex high-DoF multifingered hands. While many robotic hands are designed to replicate human morphology, the functional role of human hands suggests a different perspective: much of their complexity may exist to enable tool use and tool making. This observation motivates Any-ttach, a tool-centric manipulation framework that treats quick end-effector swapping as a mechanism for dexterity with simplicity. Any-ttach combines a low-cost automatic swapping mechanism for an open-close robot interface, a handheld device for collecting human demonstrations, and a task planning framework that composes learned, parameterized, and planned tool-use skills. The system supports diverse tools and end-effector modules, including daily tools, articulated tools such as scissors, Fin Ray fingers, and a low-cost anthropomorphic hand, through the same shared interface. Our experiments show that Any-ttach improves tool-swapping reliability, increases demonstration efficiency, reduces tool-pose variability, and supports diverse tool-use skills. In two long-horizon tasks, making a sandwich and preparing a cucumber, Any-ttach executes six tool-use subskills through end-effector switching and execution monitoring. These results suggest that robots can expand manipulation capability not only through more complex end-effectors, but also through rapidly exchangeable tools and end-effector modules. More details and videos are available at https://any-ttach.github.io/.

</details>


### [330] [Caspar: CUDA Accelerator for Symbolic Programming with Adaptive Reordering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30583)
*Emil Martens,Aaron Miller,Matias Varnum,Annette Stahl*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Caspar is a GPU-accelerated nonlinear solver for robotics that uses symbolic Python-to-CUDA kernel generation (via SymForce) to achieve 5–20x speedups on BAL bundle adjustment with similar accuracy and reduced memory; open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge expressive Python symbolic programming with high-performance GPU runtimes to accelerate nonlinear optimization in robotics; enable automatic generation of optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions (including Lie group operations).

Method: Define residuals symbolically; Caspar uses symbolic differentiation to generate GPU kernels for residuals and optimization interfaces; builds on SymForce; supports Lie groups; performs bundle adjustment on BAL dataset; benchmarks against state-of-the-art.

Result: Caspar achieves 5-20x speedups, lower memory, similar accuracy; demonstrates effectiveness of symbolic GPU programming; released under SymForce; link to GitHub.

Conclusion: Symbolic GPU programming via Caspar is viable and beneficial for robotics optimization; open-source and extensible.

Abstract: We present Caspar, a library that makes the power of modern GPUs more accessible in robotics and provides a state-of-the-art nonlinear GPU solver that can be applied to a wide range of different optimization problems. Caspar bridges the gap between expressive symbolic programming in Python and high-performance GPU runtimes in C++ by automatically generating optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions. Building on the SymForce library, users can easily define and combine symbolic expressions, including Lie group operations, to generate custom CUDA kernels. To use Caspar as a solver, users need only define the symbolic residual functions; Caspar then uses symbolic differentiation to generate the necessary GPU kernels and interfaces to perform nonlinear optimization.
  In this paper, we present the core components of Caspar and showcase its performance by performing bundle adjustment on the Bundle Adjustment in the Large (BAL) dataset. We benchmark Caspar against other state-of-the-art bundle adjusters and show that it is 5 to 20 times faster than the best alternative, requires less memory, and achieves similar accuracy. This illustrates the benefit of our symbolic GPU programming approach. Caspar is released as part of SymForce and is freely available at https://github.com/symforce-org/symforce

</details>


### [331] [ZAPS-DA: Zero-Phase Action Policy Smoothing with Decoupled Actor for Continuous Control in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30612)
*Faiq Shamass*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ZAPS-DA reduces action jitter in off-policy RL deployment without phase lag by using a decoupled, supervised-imitation policy trained on zero-phase filtered targets, enabling a smooth, causal policy with no inference-time filtering and minimal reward loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency action jitter in off-policy RL policies makes deployment on physical actuators impractical; post-hoc filtering introduces phase lag and smoothing that conflicts with reward learning.

Method: Maintain an unmodified main actor trained with the base RL loss; train a separate decoupled actor via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets stored in the replay buffer; deploy the decoupled actor (smooth, causal policy) without filters or history inputs; use a magnitude-matched MSE loss for portability across optimizers.

Result: Applied with Soft Actor-Critic and Savitzky–Golay filtering in two driving simulators. MetaDrive: steering jitter reduced 14–21x, throttle 3–5x (p < 1e-4, Bonferroni-corrected); task completion parity (p = .28) and crash parity (p = .31) with ~6.3% reward cost. Webots adaptive cruise control: same SG filter yields 8–45x steering jitter reduction, reward parity (p = .121), and total task-failure rate drop from 2.0% to 0.7%.

Conclusion: ZAPS-DA enables large reductions in action jitter with negligible performance loss, by decoupling a smooth, causal policy from the main RL actor and training it via imitation of zero-phase-filtered targets; no inference-time filters or action-history inputs are required.

Abstract: Continuous control policies trained with off-policy reinforcement learning frequently exhibit high-frequency action jitter, rendering direct deployment on physical actuators impractical. Post-hoc filtering attenuates jitter but introduces phase lag; embedding smoothness penalties in the actor's loss couples them with the RL gradient and conflates reward regression with over-aggressive smoothing. We present ZAPS-DA, a framework that reduces action jitter at deployment with negligible phase lag and no post-processing. ZAPS-DA pairs an unmodified main actor (trained by the base RL loss) with a separate decoupled actor trained via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets stored in the replay buffer. The deployed policy is the decoupled actor: a feed-forward map from the current observation to a smooth action, with no inference-time filter and no action-history input -- a mechanism we term causal distillation of a non-causal filter. A magnitude-matched MSE loss provides zero-hyperparameter portability across optimizer classes. Validated with Soft Actor-Critic and a Savitzky--Golay filter in two driving simulators using paired n=150 evaluation protocols: on MetaDrive, ZAPS-DA reduces steering jitter by 14--21x and throttle jitter by 3--5x (all $p < 10^{-4}$, Bonferroni-corrected) while matching task-completion (p=0.28 success, p=0.31 crash) at a 6.3% reward cost; on a custom Webots adaptive cruise control environment, the same SG configuration produces a Pareto improvement -- reward parity (p=0.121), 8--45x steering jitter reduction, and total task-failure rate reduced from 2.0% to 0.7%.

</details>


### [332] [Exploiting Chordal Sparsity for Globally Optimal Estimation with Factor Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30617)
*Avinash Subramanian,Connor Holmes,Timothy D. Barfoot,Frank Dellaert,Frederike Dümbgen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Structure-exploiting SDP relaxations for factor-graph state estimation in GTSAM enable faster global solutions by leveraging Bayes-tree sparsity; demonstrated on 3D pose-graph SLAM and 2D localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global optimality in state estimation while avoiding poor local minima; existing convex relaxations are costly to formulate/solve and not easily integrated with standard solvers.

Method: Automatic construction of convex SDP relaxations for common factor graphs inside GTSAM; decomposition of the SDP via Bayes-tree to exploit chordal sparsity, enabling faster solving; validation on two case studies.

Result: Significant speedups over standard local solvers for chordally sparse problems; favorable scaling of the structure-exploiting global estimator; successful demonstrations on ring-structured pose-graph SLAM and chain-structured localization; code available.

Conclusion: Integrates a practical, scalable globally-aware estimation approach within GTSAM, lowering barriers to convex-relaxation methods and broadening their applicability to real-world robotics problems.

Abstract: Robust and efficient state estimation is crucial for perception, navigation, and control in robotics. State estimation problems are conveniently modeled using the factor-graph framework as enabled by modern software packages such as GTSAM or g2o. However, the standard solvers included in such frameworks are local and may converge to poor local minima, posing significant safety concerns. Conversely, techniques based on convex relaxations have been shown to provide a means of globally solving or certifying many state estimation problems. However, these relaxations 1) often require substantial effort to formulate, and 2) may incur significantly higher cost compared to efficient local solvers, as they require solving a large semidefinite program (SDP). In this work, we address both shortcomings by 1) creating a new procedure within the GTSAM framework for automatically constructing convex SDP relaxations for any factor graphs with common factor and variable types, and by 2) exploiting the Bayes tree constructions native to GTSAM to decompose the SDP problem, leading to significant speedup in solver time for chordally sparse problems. We demonstrate the favorable scaling of this structure-exploiting global estimator compared to standard local solvers for two case studies: A 3D pose-graph SLAM problem with a ring factor graph and a 2D localization problem with a chain factor graph. The software framework is available at https://github.com/borglab/gtsam.

</details>


### [333] [Bidirectional Incremental Generalized Hybrid A*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30647)
*Sidharth Talia,Oren Salzman,Siddhartha Srinivasa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bi-IGHA* extends IGHA* by incorporating bidirectional search over a multi-resolution, anytime kinodynamic planning framework, preserving IGHA*'s monotonic improvement and termination guarantees while reducing expansions and matching closed-loop performance with fewer searches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient real-time kinodynamic planning in complex, high-dimensional systems where precomputed motion primitives are infeasible and discretization can hamper search due to coupling with tree growth. Bidirectional search can alleviate excessive expansions and hidden-solution issues caused by freezing vertices.

Method: Introduce a bidirectional extension of IGHA* (Bi-IGHA*) that operates over a hierarchy of resolutions in an anytime fashion, freezing vertices instead of pruning to preserve previously found information. The bidirectional setup mitigates the problem of frozen vertices obscuring solution paths, while maintaining IGHA* guarantees of monotonic cost improvement and termination.

Result: Empirically, Bi-IGHA* reduces expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems compared with IGHA*. It achieves equivalent closed-loop performance for kinodynamic planning in high-speed off-road autonomy, with significantly fewer expansions.

Conclusion: Bi-IGHA* preserves IGHA* guarantees while providing substantial practical gains by mitigating the effects of frozen vertices through bidirectional search, making large-scale, multi-resolution kinodynamic planning more efficient in unstructured environments.

Abstract: We focus on the problem of efficient anytime kinodynamic planning for systems with complex dynamics in unstructured environments that make precomputing motion primitives infeasible. Directly applying A* to such problems is computationally infeasible due to the curse of dimensionality. Methods such as Hybrid A* addressed this burden by discretizing the state space, but in turn creating a coupling between tree discovery and the discretization resolution. The Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* (IGHA*) performs search over a hierarchy of resolutions in an anytime fashion to break this coupling, by freezing vertices to use in later search iterations rather than pruning them. However, the frozen vertices can hide solution-supporting vertices from the search at a particular iteration. While classical bidirectional search is motivated by the reduction of search depth, extending IGHA* into the bidirectional setting (termed Bi-IGHA*) obtains additional benefit by fundamentally mitigating the behaviour induced by frozen vertices hiding solutions. We show that Bi-IGHA* preserves IGHA*'s guarantees on monotonic cost improvement and termination. We empirically show that Bi-IGHA* substantially reduces expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems, and achieves equivalent closed-loop performance with kinodynamic planning for high-speed off-road autonomy while requiring significantly fewer expansions. Website: https://personalrobotics.github.io/IGHAStar/biighastar.html

</details>


### [334] [Primitive Subspaces Mediate Few-Shot Transfer in VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30695)
*Anya Singh,Cabrel Happi,Jai Relan,Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Primitive-aware training yields a reusable sub-skill library for vision-language-action policies, enabling strong few-shot transfer; achieves about 78% of fine-tuned upper bound with just 3 demonstrations, a 3x sample-efficiency gain over flat training across seeds and datasets; ablations show primitive representations are causally necessary; identifies an evaluation pitfall that inflates false failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the data and fine-tuning cost required to deploy new tasks for industrial VLA policies by learning transferable primitive sub-skills that can be composed at inference time from few demonstrations.

Method: Train two VLA architectures (OpenVLA and π0.5) with matched LoRA fine-tuning on the REASSEMBLE dataset using flat trajectories versus primitive-segmented episodes with primitive-specific prompts. Hold out 6 object-task pairs; perform few-shot transfer with m ∈ {0,1,3,5,10} demonstrations and evaluate without weight updates. Replicate across three seeds and test on LIBERO-Long. Conduct causal ablations by removing the primitive-decodable subspace and also ablating a random subspace of equal size. Identify evaluation pitfall in chunked policies causing inflated single-step action-range gate failures compared to ground-truth demonstrations.

Result: Primitive-trained models reach 78% of the fine-tuned upper bound with m=3 demonstrations, while flat-trained models require m=10 to reach the same level—yielding a ~3× sample-efficiency gap consistent across seeds, architectures, and datasets. Causal ablation shows a 32-percentage-point drop when removing the primitive-decodable subspace, whereas ablating a random equal-sized subspace has no effect, indicating primitive representations are causally necessary for transfer. The authors also diagnose and correct a methodological pitfall: family-wise inflation of single-step action-range gates yields artificially high false-failure rates against ground-truth demonstrations.

Conclusion: Primitive-aware sub-skill representations enable more data-efficient, out-of-distribution task transfer for VLA policies in industrial settings, suggesting that composing learned primitives at inference time is a viable path to reduce fine-tuning costs. The work also emphasizes the importance of rigorous evaluation of chunked policies to avoid overstated performance due to evaluation artifacts.

Abstract: Deploying vision-language-action (VLA) policies in industrial environments requires the ability to teach new tasks at low cost, a property current VLAs lack, since each new task requires fine-tuning. We investigate whether primitive-aware training produces a transferable artifact: a learned library of sub-skills that can be composed at inference time, conditioned on a small number of demonstrations, to perform tasks the policy was never trained on. We train two VLA architectures with different inductive biases, OpenVLA and $π_{0.5}$, on the REASSEMBLE contact-rich assembly dataset under matched LoRA fine-tuning recipes and locked hyperparameters, varying training between flat trajectories and primitive-segmented episodes with primitive-specific language prompts. We hold out 6 object-task combinations from training and evaluate few-shot transfer: models receive $m \in \{0, 1, 3, 5, 10\}$ demonstrations of a held-out task and attempt execution without weight updates. We replicate across three training seeds and validate on a second dataset (LIBERO-Long). Primitive-trained models reach 78% of fine-tuned upper-bound performance with only m=3 demonstrations, while flat-trained models require m=10 demonstrations to reach the same level -- a $3\times$ sample efficiency gap that replicates across seeds, architectures, and datasets. To establish causation, we ablate the primitive-decodable subspace of hidden states and show few-shot transfer degrades by 32 percentage points while ablating a random subspace of equal dimensionality has no effect, indicating primitive representations are causally necessary rather than incidentally correlated with transfer. We identify and correct a methodological pitfall in evaluating chunked policies: family-wise inflation of single-step action-range gates produces order-of-magnitude higher false-failure rates against ground-truth human demonstrations.

</details>


### [335] [Geometry-Aware Control Barrier Functions for Collision Avoidance via Bernstein Polynomial Approximations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30696)
*Siwon Jo,Yanze Zhang,Yupeng Yang,Wenhao Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A geometry-aware Bernstein-Polynomial SDF (BP-SDF) based CBF is proposed for unified obstacle-robot representation, enabling differentiable safe control with efficient, closed-loop enforcement in single- and multi-robot scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional shape surrogates (spheres, super-ellipsoids) are either overly conservative in irregular scenes or require many primitives, increasing constraint counts and reducing real-time performance. A unified, differentiable barrier that can handle irregular geometries improves safety and efficiency.

Method: Introduce BP-SDFs to represent obstacles and robots with Bernstein polynomials, yielding a unified minimum-distance barrier. Leverage differentiability of Bernstein polynomials to enforce CBF constraints in closed-loop control, likely via a quadratic program or similar optimization. Validate in simulations for single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance across varied environments.

Result: Demonstrates efficiency and performance in guaranteeing safety through simulations, showing applicability to both single-agent navigation and multi-robot collision avoidance in diverse environments.

Conclusion: BP-SDF-based, geometry-aware CBF provides a unified, differentiable barrier representation that reduces conservatism and constraint burden while enabling real-time safe control for complex, irregularly shaped robots and obstacles.

Abstract: Safe navigation often relies on well-defined conditions based on the shape of robots and obstacles, and can be challenging when they have irregular geometries. While Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) offer an efficient mechanism to enforce safe set forward invariance, common shape surrogates (e.g., spheres or super-ellipsoids) either are overly conservative in unstructured scenes or require many local primitives, which inflates constraint counts and degrades real-time performance. In this paper, we introduce a novel geometry-aware Control Barrier Function (CBF) based on Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs). It provides a unified way to represent the obstacles and robots, so as to represent the barrier function with a unified minimum distance. Benefiting from the differentiability of the Bernstein polynomials, one can easily enforce the control constraints in a closed loop. We validate the method's efficiency and performance to guarantee safety in single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance via simulations under different environments.

</details>


### [336] [GSAM: A Generalizable and Safe Robotic Framework for Articulated Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30740)
*Beichen Shao,Mengying Xie,Heng Su,Wanyi Zhang,Mingyan Li,Yan Ding,Fausto Giunchiglia,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GSAM is a generalizable and safe robotic framework for articulated object manipulation that combines vision-based perception, refined via VLM with chain-of-thought reasoning, an interaction constraint function generator guided by LLMs, and a kinematic-aware planner, achieving better generalization and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Articulated-object manipulation is challenging due to the diversity of objects and the complex interactions between the end-effector and handles. Existing methods often fail to generalize and risk destructive collisions.

Method: A vision-based perceiver predicts kinematic parameters of articulated objects. A fine-tuned VLM-based refiner uses chain-of-thought commonsense reasoning to refine these perceptions. An interaction constraint function generator integrates object, interaction pose, and obstacle-avoidance knowledge into a base model. An LLM functionalizes these constraints to drive trajectory and posture planning. A kinematic-aware manipulation planner verifies reachability for the planned trajectory and posture.

Result: Experiments on 50 hinge tasks across 5 object categories and 50 randomly initialized end-effector–handle configurations show GSAM reduces standard deviation by 3.1% and improves manipulation success rate by 36.0% compared to the best baseline.

Conclusion: GSAM demonstrates superior object generalization and interaction safety in practical articulated-object manipulation scenarios.

Abstract: Articulated object manipulation is a unique challenge for service robots. Existing methods employ end-to-end policy learning, visionmotion planning, and large-language/visual-language model (LLM/VLM), but often overlook the diversity of articulated objects and the complexity of interactions between end-effector and handle, leading to limited generalization and destructive collisions. To address this, we propose GSAM, a generalizable and safe robotic framework for articulated object manipulation. Specifically, a vision-based perceiver generates the kinematic parameters. Considering that pre-trained markers in perceiver yield raw estimations that may deviate from commonsense, we present a f ine-tuned VLM-based refiner, using chain-of-thought (COT) commonsense reasoning to refine perception. To prevent destructive collisions, we design an interaction constraint function generator, integrating articulated object, interaction pose, and obstacle avoidance knowledge into a base. LLM then functionalize these constraints and apply them to trajectory and posture planning. A kinematic-aware manipulation planner verifies reachability for trajectory and posture. Experiments on 50 hinge tasks across 5 object categories and 50 randomly initialized end-effectorhandle configurations show that GSAM reduces standard deviation by 3.1% and improves manipulation success rate by 36.0% compared to the best baseline, respectively demonstrating the superior object generalization and interaction safety of GSAM in practical scenarios.

</details>


### [337] [SSR: Scaling Surefooted and Symmetric Humanoid Traversal to the Open World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30770)
*Ruiqi Yu,Yiwen Wang,Yuan Hao,Jun WU,Qiuguo Zhu*
