Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SSR is an end-to-end egocentric vision-based humanoid traversal framework that combines imagined foothold guidance, equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation, and terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to enable safe, natural, long-horizon locomotion in open-world, heterogeneous terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending humanoid traversal to open-world environments requires robust foot placement on diverse terrains under dynamic motion, while producing coordinated, natural whole-body behaviors. Edge-slip reduction, reliable long-horizon planning, and cross-scene generalization remain challenging.

Method: Three core components: (1) imagined foothold guidance that models forthcoming swing-foot contacts and evaluates their support to guide pre-touchdown swings toward stable regions; (2) equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation to induce bilateral coordination efficiently from high-dimensional visual observations; (3) terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to encourage human-like behavior across scenes. SSR integrates these in an end-to-end learning framework for egocentric vision-based traversal.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world terrains show safe, stable, and high-quality locomotion across diverse scenarios, including stairs with varied structures, wide gaps, high platforms, and reliable long-horizon open-world traversal.

Conclusion: SSR enables practical deployment of humanoid robots in open human environments by addressing perception, coordination, and motion priors; the approach demonstrates robustness across challenging terrains and long-horizon navigation.

Abstract: Extending humanoid traversal to the open world is key to practical deployment in human environments, but remains challenging. The robot must use vision to ensure safe and reliable foot placement on heterogeneous terrain under highly dynamic motion, while producing coordinated, natural whole-body behaviors. We propose SSR, an efficient end-to-end framework for egocentric vision-based humanoid traversal that jointly learns these capabilities. SSR introduces imagined foothold guidance, which learns to model forthcoming swing-foot contacts and evaluates their support to guide pre-touchdown swings toward stable regions, reducing edge slips. It further employs equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation to efficiently induce bilateral coordination under high-dimensional visual observations, and uses terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to encourage human-like behavior across scenes. Extensive experiments show that SSR achieves safe, stable, and high-quality locomotion on diverse real-world terrains, including stairs with varied structures and extreme challenges such as wide gaps and high platforms, while enabling reliable long-horizon traversal in open outdoor environments.

</details>


### [338] [Object-Informed Model Predictive Path Integral Control for Non-Prehensile Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30778)
*Nikola Raicevic,Bharath Raam Radhakrishnan,Chenbin Yu,Ki Myung Brian Lee,Nikolay Atanasov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical MPPI for long-horizon non-prehensile manipulation by decoupling object-level planning from robot-level control; uses object-only plan as reference to guide joint planning, improving efficiency and success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-prehensile manipulation with underactuated, discontinuous interactions makes long-horizon planning difficult; leverage object-level planning to guide robot-level control for efficiency and non-myopic behavior.

Method: First solve a simplified object-only problem assuming direct actuation to obtain an object trajectory; use this trajectory as a reference in the joint robot-object planning problem within a hierarchical MPPI framework; evaluate on a 6-DoF xArm6 for object pushing with obstacles.

Result: In simulation, object-informed MPPI increases task success by 40% and speeds up control frequency by 26% compared to regular MPPI; in real experiments, yields ~20% improvement with similar computation as regular MPPI.

Conclusion: Object-informed hierarchical MPPI improves long-horizon planning efficiency and task performance for non-prehensile manipulation by integrating a separable, object-level plan into the robot-object control loop.

Abstract: Long-horizon planning for non-prehensile robot manipulation is challenging due to underactuated and discontinuous interactions. We propose a hierarchical formulation of model predictive path integral (MPPI) control that guides robot-level planning with a separately computed object-level plan to achieve efficient long-horizon prediction. We first solve a simplified object-only problem, assuming the object can be actuated directly, and use the planned object trajectory as a reference in solving the joint robot-object planning problem. We evaluate our method in both simulation and hardware using a 6-DoF xArm6 manipulator to perform object pushing tasks in which the target object must reach a goal while avoiding static obstacles, necessitating non-myopic reasoning. Our object-informed MPPI increases task success by 40\% with a 26\% faster control frequency in simulation, and by 20\% in real experiments with similar computation as regular MPPI.

</details>


### [339] [Two Degree-of-Freedom Vibratory Transport in a Grasp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30780)
*C. L. Yako,Shenli Yuan,Kenneth Salisbury*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Asymmetric vibratory actuation enables 2-DoF in-hand manipulation of grasped parts through stick-slip dynamics, with analytic links between sticking/slipping accelerations and average velocity under gravity; validated experimentally; a pair of 2-DoF vibratory surfaces in a parallel-jaw gripper achieves bidirectional translation and rotation, with translation trends extending to rotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable dexterous in-hand manipulation of grasped parts without complex multi-DOF grippers by exploiting vibratory stick-slip dynamics; provide analytic insight and experimental validation for 2-DoF translation and rotation under gravity.

Method: Use closed-loop position control to generate a periodic stick-slip waveform on a moving surface; derive analytic relations for how sticking and slipping accelerations influence average part velocity against gravity; validate with an experimental setup where squeeze force is controlled and part motion is measured via a high-resolution encoder; develop and test 2-DoF vibratory surfaces capable of translation and rotation, implemented in a parallel-jaw gripper with two surfaces to achieve bidirectional motion; test multiple grasped parts and examine both translation and in-plane rotation.

Result: Analytical trends show how sticking and slipping accelerations affect average velocity; experimental validation confirms the predicted trends under gravity; the 2-DoF vibratory surfaces enable bidirectional translation and rotation of grasped parts; the same waveform trends apply to in-plane rotation.

Conclusion: Asymmetric vibratory actuation provides a viable mechanism for 2-DoF in-hand manipulation, with measurable control over translation and rotation via waveform parameters; findings generalize from translation to in-plane rotation, suggesting broader applicability for vibrotactile in-hand manipulation using multi-DoF vibratory surfaces in grippers.

Abstract: In this paper, we use asymmetric vibrations to demonstrate two degree-of-freedom (DoF) in-hand manipulation of grasped parts. The asymmetric vibrations are achieved through closed-loop position control of a moving surface, which applies a periodic stick-slip waveform to the part to be manipulated. We show analytically how two vibratory waveform parameters, the sticking acceleration and the slipping acceleration, affect average part velocity when moving against gravity. The theoretical trends are then validated using an experimental setup where the squeeze force is controlled and part motion is recorded by a high-resolution encoder. We also develop a 2-DoF vibratory surface capable of translation in one direction and rotation about the surface normal. Using two of these 2-DoF surfaces in a parallel jaw gripper configuration, we bidirectionally translate and rotate a variety of grasped parts, as well as demonstrate that the same waveform trends for translation also persist for in-plane rotation.

</details>


### [340] [Feat2Go: Visual Feature-Grounded Value Estimation for Embodied Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30795)
*Junyang Shu,Zhiwei Lin,Bingqing Wei,Yongtao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Feat2Go introduces a fine-grained value estimation framework for embodied RL in vision-language-action tasks. It creates a continuous progress signal from a pretrained visual world model by measuring patch-level similarity to subgoals and clustering episodes into semantic stages. An embodied value model predicts this progress from observations and instructions and reshapes terminal rewards during policy optimization, compatible with PPO/GRPO. Empirically, it boosts VLA models on ManiSkill3 and RoboTwin 2.0, with large gains in OOD success and strong domain-randomized performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for vision-language-action (VLA) models is hampered by sparse supervision and the difficulty of crafting informative, long-horizon rewards. A fine-grained progress signal can guide learning more effectively than sparse task rewards or imitation signals.

Method: Derive a continuous progress target from a pretrained visual world model by (1) assessing patch-level similarity to subgoal states, (2) partitioning episodes into semantic stages using trend-based clustering. Train an embodied value model to predict this progress from the current observation and task instruction. Use the predicted value to reshape terminal rewards during policy optimization. The approach is compatible with PPO and GRPO and does not require manual reward engineering.

Result: On ManiSkill3, OpenVLAOFT improves from 17.5% to 82.9% average OOD success while maintaining 96.9% in-distribution performance; on RoboTwin 2.0, achieves 88.8% average success in domain-randomized tasks, outperforming prior RL methods.

Conclusion: Feat2Go provides a practical, reward-shaping-free intermediate signal for embodied RL in VLA tasks, improving robustness and performance across single-arm and bimanual manipulation, and integrating with existing RL pipelines.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is a promising approach for improving the capabilities of vision-language-action (VLA) models while avoiding the heavy data requirements of imitation learning. However, its effectiveness for VLA models is often constrained by sparse supervision and the difficulty of designing informative reward signals for long-horizon manipulation. In this work, we present Feat2Go, a fine-grained value estimation framework for embodied reinforcement learning. Specifically, Feat2Go first derives a continuous progress target from a pretrained visual world model by measuring patch-level similarity to subgoal states and partitioning episodes into semantic stages with trend-based clustering. We then train an embodied value model to predict this structural progress from the current observation and task instruction, and use the predicted value to reshape terminal rewards during policy optimization. The proposed framework is compatible with existing VLA policy reinforcement learning pipelines, including PPO and GRPO, and does not rely on manual reward engineering. Extensive experiments on ManiSkill3 and RoboTwin 2.0 demonstrate that Feat2Go consistently improves the performance of existing VLA models under both single-arm and bimanual manipulation settings. More specifically, on ManiSkill3, Feat2Go improves OpenVLAOFT from 17.5% to 82.9% average out-of-distribution success while retaining 96.9% in-distribution performance. On RoboTwin 2.0, Feat2Go achieves an average success rate of 88.8% in domain-randomized task settings, outperforming prior reinforcement learning methods.

</details>


### [341] [Hide-and-Seek in Trajectories: Discovering Failure Signals for VLA Runtime Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30834)
*Seongheon Park,Wendi Li,Changdae Oh,Samuel Yeh,Zsolt Kira,Michael Hagenow,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hide-and-Seek introduces a coarsely supervised failure detection framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, using inter- and intra-trajectory contrastive learning to localize failure signals from trajectory-level supervision, without step-level annotations, achieving state-of-the-art detection with conformal-prediction trade-offs across LIBERO, VLABench, and a real robot on multiple policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models follow natural language instructions but suffer from execution failures in real-world settings. Existing methods depend on expensive action resampling or external models, or spread trajectory labels uniformly across time, masking localized failure cues. There is a need for localized, scalable failure detection that requires only coarse supervision.

Method: Hide-and-Seek formulates failure detection as a coarse supervision problem. It leverages inter-trajectory and intra-trajectory contrastive objectives to localize failure-indicative actions and generate temporally structured failure signals from trajectory-level supervision alone, without any step-level annotations.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art multi-task failure detection performance with a favorable accuracy–timeliness trade-off under conformal prediction. Demonstrates strong generalization to both seen and unseen tasks across LIBERO, VLABench, and a real-world robotic platform, and across VLA policies OpenVLA, π0, and π0.5.

Conclusion: The approach provides effective, temporally structured failure signals without fine-grained annotations, reducing reliance on expensive methods and improving robustness for real-world VLA deployments. It generalizes across tasks and platforms, though it relies on conformal-pprediction assumptions and requires diverse evaluation to cover broader failure modes.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to follow natural language instructions and generalize across diverse tasks, but they remain vulnerable to execution failures that compromise reliability in real-world deployment. Detecting such failures during execution is therefore critical for the robust deployment of embodied systems. Existing failure detection methods either rely on expensive action resampling or external models, while alternatives propagate trajectory-level labels uniformly across every timestep, obscuring localized failure signals. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Hide-and-Seek}, a framework that formulates VLA failure detection as a coarsely supervised learning problem. By combining inter-trajectory and intra-trajectory contrastive objectives, Hide-and-Seek localizes failure-indicative actions and induces temporally structured failure signals from trajectory-level supervision alone, without any step-level annotation. We evaluate Hide-and-Seek on LIBERO, VLABench, and a real-world robotic platform across three representative VLA policies: OpenVLA, $π_0$, and $π_{0.5}$.Our method achieves state-of-the-art multi-task failure detection performance with a practical accuracy--timeliness trade-off under conformal prediction, and generalizes well to both seen and unseen tasks.

</details>


### [342] [High-Load-Density Electro-Permanent Magnetic Foot with Controllable Adhesion for Quadruped Wall-Climbing Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30849)
*An Li,Bo Tao,I-Ming Chen,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A high-load, controllable electro-permanent magnetic foot (CHN-EPM) for quadruped climbing on ferromagnetic surfaces, featuring a 3D magnetic circuit, flux concentration, and a magnetization control system with two-stage pulse control; integrates flexible force sensor enabling reliable adhesion switching; demonstrated on Unitree GO2 with adhesion >1000 N and >200:1 L/W.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable climbing locomotion on ferromagnetic surfaces by addressing air-gap sensitivity and partial contact issues in magnetic adhesion.

Method: Design of CHN-EPM adhesion units with 3D magnetic circuit and flux concentration, distributed parallel flux path; development of magnetization driver and two-stage pulse current control; incorporation of flexible pressure sensor for contact force feedback.

Result: Max adhesion force >1000 N; load-to-weight ratio >200:1; robust adhesion under partial/uncertain contact; effective adhesion on ceiling, vertical walls, painted/perforated/curved ferromagnetic surfaces; integration with Unitree GO2 demonstrates reliable locomotion.

Conclusion: CHN-EPM with feedback control provides high-load, robust adhesion for climbing on ferromagnetic surfaces, enabling reliable locomotion; potential for broader surface types and control strategies.

Abstract: To enable reliable climbing locomotion of quadruped robots on ferromagnetic surfaces, this paper presents a high-load-density electro-permanent magnetic foot with controllable adhesion, featuring force-feedback circular Halbach-net electro-permanent magnet (CHN-EPM) adhesion units and a magnetization control system. Due to its three-dimensional magnetic circuit structure and flux-concentration effect, the CHN-EPM enables a distributed parallel magnetic flux path with enhanced flux utilization, resulting in reduced sensitivity to air-gap variations and allowing effective adhesion to be maintained even under partial contact conditions. The proposed CHN-EPM generates a maximum adhesion force exceeding 1000 N with a load-to-weight ratio over 200:1. A magnetization driver and a two-stage pulse current control strategy are developed to regulate the excitation current amplitude and duration, enabling accurate and reliable magnetization. By incorporating a flexible pressure sensor for contact force feedback, the system can effectively monitor attachment and detachment states, ensuring robust adhesion switching under uncertain contact conditions. The proposed system is integrated into a commercial quadruped robot (Unitree GO2), demonstrating high-load adhesion on ceiling and vertical-wall surfaces and stable locomotion on painted, perforated, and curved ferromagnetic surfaces.

</details>


### [343] [Wall-OSS-0.5 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30877)
*Ryan Yu,Pushi Zhang,Starrick Liu,Brae Liu,Miracle Kang,Shalfun Li,Lights Shi,Ellie Ma,Ping Yang,Chris Pan,Jerry Chen,Dongxiu Liu,Rain Sun,Miles Guo,Byron Zhang,Hugo Zhou,Zach Xu,Vincent Chen,Harrison Huang,James Wang,Dance Kuzi,Andy Zhai,Hang Su,Roy Gan,Lucy Liang,Hao Wang,Qian Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Large-scale VLA pretraining enables directly usable robot behavior, not just better initialization; Wall-OSS-0.5 demonstrates zero-shot and finetuned real-robot performance across multiple embodiments with a 4B VLA on a 3B backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether VLA pretraining yields executable robotic behavior on hardware, and whether it can be a direct contributor to performance without only serving as an initialization for downstream learning.

Method: Wall-OSS-0.5 uses a 4B VLA pretraining framework built on a 3B VLM backbone with action-generation components, trained across >20 embodiments and >1M robot trajectories per epoch. A gradient-bridged co-training strategy combines three objectives: (1) discrete action prediction to inject VLM-native gradients into the backbone, (2) multimodal prediction to preserve grounded V-L understanding, and (3) continuous flow matching as a deployment-time action interface.

Result: The pretrained checkpoint exhibits non-trivial zero-shot real-robot behavior on a 17-task suite, including a held-out deformable manipulation task, achieving high task progress. After fine-tuning on 15 real-robot tasks, it reaches 60.5% average task progress, outperforming the π_0.5 baseline by 17.5%. Multimodal evaluations show that adding action training does not erode grounded V-L competence and can strengthen embodied grounding.

Conclusion: VLA pretraining should be viewed as a directly testable, already useful source of robot capability, rather than merely an initialization for downstream learning.

Abstract: Large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) pretraining is increasingly adopted as the foundation for robot policies, yet the evidence for pretrained VLAs is almost invariably reported after task-specific fine-tuning.This leaves a foundational question unanswered: does VLA pretraining itself yield executable robot behavior, or does it merely furnish a better initialization for downstream policy learning? We present Wall-OSS-0.5, an open-source 4B VLA built upon a 3B VLM backbone augmented with action-generation components, designed so that pretrained robotic capability is directly measurable on physical hardware.The model is pretrained across more than 20 embodiments, processing over one million robot trajectories per epoch alongside a grounded multimodal corpus. We adopt a gradient-bridged co-training recipe in which three objectives play distinct and complementary roles: discrete action prediction routes strong VLM-native gradients into the backbone, multimodal prediction preserves grounded vision-language understanding, and continuous flow matching serves as the deployment-time action interface. Before task-specific fine-tuning, the pretrained checkpoint achieves non-trivial zero-shot real-robot behavior, completing several tasks, including a held-out deformable manipulation task, at high task progress on a 17-task suite. After fine-tuning, the same checkpoint serves as a stronger adaptation prior, reaching 60.5% average task progress on 15 real-robot tasks and outperforming π_0.5 by 17.5%. Multimodal evaluations further confirm that action training does not erode grounded vision-language competence: the model preserves broad vision-language ability while strengthening embodied grounding. Together, these results reposition VLA pretraining from an initialization strategy to a directly testable, already useful source of robot capability.

</details>


### [344] [Trajectory Planning for Non-Communicating Mobile Robots using Inverse Optimal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30906)
*Nina Majer,Yannick Epple,Xin Ye,Stefan Schwab,Sören Hohmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A combined trajectory planning and prediction framework for non-communicating mobile robots, using inverse optimal control to infer unknown goals and perspective-aware joint prediction to improve planning, achieving faster convergence and robust solvability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient collision avoidance among multiple non-communicating robots by accurately inferring unseen goal states from observed motions and by incorporating other robots' viewpoints into predictions, reducing planning ambiguity and failure modes.

Method: Apply inverse optimal control to estimate each robot's unknown goal states from history; each robot adopts others' perspectives and solves a coupled prediction problem using the estimated goals; use resulting predictions to inform the planner.

Result: In simulations with 2–8 robots, the median time to reach all goals is 9.8% faster than planning with constant-acceleration-based estimated goals; the method avoids failure where the solver cannot find a solution in either planning or prediction.

Conclusion: The integrated approach improves efficiency and robustness in multi-robot collision avoidance by combining inverse-optimal-goal estimation with perspective-aware prediction, outperforming a constant-acceleration baseline in both speed and solvability.

Abstract: To enable an efficient interaction of non-communicating mobile robots in collision avoidance scenarios, we present a novel combined trajectory planning and prediction algorithm. Inverse optimal control is used to estimate unknown goal states of all robots based on observed past trajectories. Each robot also takes the perspective of other robots in considering self-prediction and solves a joint prediction problem using the estimated goal states. The resulting predictions are then considered for planning. Simulation results of scenarios with 2-8 robots show that the median of the durations until all vehicles reach their goals is 9.8 % faster compared to planning with constant acceleration based estimated goal states. Moreover, the proposed approach never leads to the solver being unable to find a solution to the planning or prediction problem.

</details>


### [345] [Enhancing Human-Likeness in Reinforcement Learning Agents via Hierarchical Macro Action Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30928)
*Usman Nizamani,M. Shaheer Luqman,Fawad Javed Fateh,Ali Shah Ali,Murad Popattia,M. Zeeshan Zia,Quoc-Huy Tran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-level hierarchical macro-action quantization (HiMAQ) framework for human-like RL improves alignment with human demonstrations while preserving rewards, beating the MAQ baseline on D4RL and generalizing across IQL, SAC, and RLPD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between human-like intent and reward-driven RL, hindering interpretability and reliability; propose a method to predict action sequences closer to human behavior.

Method: Encode human demonstrations into hierarchical macro actions using two-stage vector quantization: a lower level maps actions to fine-grained subaction clusters; a higher level groups subactions into action clusters. Integrations with RL algorithms (IQL, SAC, RLPD) and evaluation on D4RL.

Result: Hierarchical HiMAQ outperforms non-hierarchical MAQ in human-likeness scores, while maintaining comparable or better success rates; results generalize across the tested RL algorithms.

Conclusion: Hierarchical macro-action quantization produces more human-like behavior without sacrificing reward performance and demonstrates cross-algorithm robustness, enhancing interpretability and reliability in RL.

Abstract: Human-like agents are a long-standing goal of artificial intelligence. Despite strong performance, most reinforcement learning (RL) agents remain reward-driven and often exhibit behaviors that differ from humans, limiting interpretability and reliability. In this work, we introduce a novel human-like RL framework that predicts action sequences closely aligned with human behaviors while maximizing rewards. Specifically, we encode human demonstrations into macro actions using a hierarchical macro action quantization approach (termed HiMAQ) consisting of two successive levels of vector quantization. The lower quantization level maps input actions to fine-grained subaction clusters, while the higher quantization level aggregates these subaction clusters into action clusters. Extensive evaluations on the D4RL benchmarks show that our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline (MAQ), achieving better human-likeness scores while maintaining comparable or better success rates than previous RL agents. The improvements generalize across integrations with various RL algorithms, namely IQL, SAC, and RLPD.

</details>


### [346] [RDGen: Demonstration Generation for High-Quality Robot Learning via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30957)
*Zijian Zhu,Menglin Zou,Zhuang Li,Yaojie Tu,Xinhai Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RDGen is a sim-to-real RL-based framework that generates high-quality robot demonstrations to train Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies. It uses a VLM task parser, Grounding DINO localizer, and a transferred RL policy to produce trajectories, harvesting successful rollouts as demonstrations; simulation data supplements training at low cost. It outperforms human teleoperation by producing smoother trajectories and better downstream VLA performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the dependence on labor-intensive human teleoperation for robot demonstrations and scale high-quality data for VLA training by leveraging sim-to-real RL-generated trajectories.

Method: RDGen integrates: (1) a VLM-based task parser to identify task-relevant objects, (2) Grounding DINO for precise object localization, and (3) an RL policy trained in simulation and transferred to the real robot to generate rollouts. Successful rollouts are captured as demonstrations, while simulation provides additional trajectories at low marginal cost for training the VLA model.

Result: The transferred RL policy achieves a high task success rate on a pick-and-place task. Compared with human teleoperation, RDGen yields smoother trajectories and superior downstream VLA performance.

Conclusion: RL-generated demonstrations can serve as reliable, consistent supervisory signals for robot policy learning and enable scalable data generation for VLA models via sim-to-real RL trajectories.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for general-purpose robot control. However, their performance remains fundamentally constrained by the availability of high-quality robot trajectory data. In current robot learning practice, such data are primarily collected through human teleoperation, which is labor-intensive, costly, and difficult to scale. In this paper, we propose RDGen, a sim-to-real reinforcement learning framework for generating high-quality robot demonstrations. Rather than employing reinforcement learning solely as the final control policy, RDGen leverages trained RL policies as a structured trajectory generator. The system consists of a VLM-based task parser that identifies task-relevant objects, a Grounding DINO-based object localizer, and an RL policy transferred from simulation to the real robot. Successful rollouts are then harvested as clean, high-quality demonstrations for downstream VLA training, while the simulation stage further provides a scalable source of additional trajectories at little marginal cost. Experiments on a pick-and-place task demonstrate that the transferred RL policy achieves a high task success rate. Compared with human teleoperation, RDGen produces significantly smoother trajectories and yields superior downstream VLA performance. These results indicate that RL-generated demonstrations can serve as more reliable and consistent supervisory signals for robot policy learning.

</details>


### [347] [A study on a Real-Time VR-Based Teleoperation Framework for Manipulator in Dynamic Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30989)
*InGyu Choi,GeonYeong Go,SunWoo Ahn,HyoJae Kang,Min-Sung Kang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A VR teleoperation framework enabling real-time, collision-aware control for a 7-DoF robot using GPU-accelerated IK and trajectory optimization to handle static and moving obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VR teleoperation studies often function mainly as data collection for imitation learning and neglect dynamic obstacle handling and robustness; real deployments require low latency and resilience to operator mistakes to ensure safety in hazardous environments.

Method: Integrate GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization within a VR interface to generate feasible joint commands at each control cycle under robot constraints; evaluate on a 7-DoF manipulator across obstacle-free, static-obstacle, and moving-obstacle scenarios.

Result: Demonstrates stable online behavior and collision-aware motion generation; motions remain consistent with operator commands while producing safe detours when obstacles interfere with the commanded path.

Conclusion: The proposed VR teleoperation framework enables real-time, obstacle-aware manipulation in dynamic environments and supports operator safety through low-latency, robust collision handling.

Abstract: Robot teleoperation enables safe, non-contact task execution in hazardous environments where direct human access is difficult, and its application has expanded with recent VR technologies. Many VR teleoperation studies, however, have primarily served as data-collection tools for robot imitation learning, so they often do not explicitly address dynamic obstacles, workspace changes, or collision risks during operation. For real deployment aimed at operator safety, teleoperation must react to dynamic situations with low latency and remain robust to mistakes made by inexperienced operators. This paper presents a VR teleoperation framework that supports real-time manipulation while handling collisions with both static and moving obstacles. The framework integrates GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization within a VR interface to generate feasible joint commands at each control cycle under robot constraints. Experiments with a 7-DoF manipulator demonstrate stable online behavior and collision-aware motion generation across three scenarios: obstacle-free, static-obstacle, and moving-obstacle environments. The results indicate that the proposed approach generates motion consistent with the operator's command while producing safe detours when obstacles interfere with the commanded path.

</details>


### [348] [Can Aerial VLA Models Cooperate? Evaluating Closed-Loop Air-Ground Coordination with CARLA-Air](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31066)
*Tianle Zeng,Yanci Wen,Xueang Yu,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unifies CARLA and AirSim in a single-process environment (CARLA-Air) to study air–ground cooperative tasks; current aerial VLA models show limited ability to translate single-agent competence into stable cooperation; requires explicit partner grounding, low-latency coordination, and team-level objective alignment for zero-shot air–ground VLA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether single-UAV vision-language-action (VLA) capabilities transfer to cooperative air–ground tasks within a shared physics world, and quantify coordination latency and alignment.

Method: Introduce CARLA-Air, a unifying evaluation environment that shares world state, physics tick, and sensing between UAV and UGV in Unreal Engine. Use two diagnostic tasks (moving-platform landing and occlusion-recovery escort) to evaluate representative aerial VLA and planning baselines. Measure tracking, following, coordination latency, and the impact of state prompting and bidirectional interaction.

Result: Aerial VLA models can often track or follow a ground partner but fail to achieve stable cooperative behavior in joint tasks. State prompting provides limited benefits; naive bidirectional interaction can worsen performance for most baselines. Three components are needed for effective zero-shot cooperation: explicit partner-state grounding, low-latency action coordination, and team-level objective alignment.

Conclusion: Zero-shot cooperative air–ground VLA requires explicit partner grounding, fast coordination, and aligned team objectives beyond current text-based cue interfaces. The authors provide open-source code for their CARLA-Air platform.

Abstract: Recent aerial vision-language-action (VLA) models show promising single-UAV capabilities, such as tracking moving objects and navigating to language-specified landmarks. However, it remains unclear whether these capabilities can transfer to air-ground cooperation, where a UAV and a UGV must act jointly in a shared, closed-loop physical world.
  We study this question with CARLA-Air, a single-process air-ground evaluation environment that unifies CARLA and AirSim inside one Unreal Engine runtime. By sharing the same world state, physics tick, and sensing pipeline, CARLA-Air enables physically consistent UAV--UGV interaction and precise measurement of simulation-timestamp alignment and effective coordination latency.
  Using CARLA-Air, we evaluate representative aerial VLA and planning baselines on two complementary diagnostic tasks: moving-platform landing and occlusion-recovery escort. The results show that current aerial VLA models can often track or follow a ground partner, but struggle to convert this single-agent competence into stable cooperative behavior. State prompting provides limited benefit, and naive bidirectional interaction fails to consistently improve performance and can amplify errors for most baselines. These findings suggest that, under the tested text-based cue interfaces, zero-shot cooperative air-ground VLA requires three components beyond the current paradigm: explicit partner-state grounding, low-latency action coordination, and team-level objective alignment. Our code is available at https://github.com/louiszengCN/CarlaAir.

</details>


### [349] [Seeing Fast and Slow: Bimodal 3D Scene Graphs for Open-set Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31067)
*Marcel Bartholomeus Prasetyo,Shrutika Vishal Thengane,A Manicka Praveen,Yi Loo,Malika Meghjani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bimodal 3D scene graph method (BiMoSG) for open-set tasks that switches between a fast coarse representation and a slow open-vocabulary fine representation, yielding substantial speedups over SOTA and enabling real-time task-driven deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-set robotic tasks require adaptable scene representations that balance speed and granularity. Current open-set scene graph methods can be too slow for real-time execution, hindering task-driven robotics.

Method: BiMoSG provides a bimodal pipeline with a default fast mode that generates coarse 3D scene graphs and a switchable slow mode that produces a fine, open-vocabulary scene graph for task-relevant objects. The switching is driven by context and evolving information during exploration, enabling seamless granularity adjustment.

Result: The approach is demonstrated to be significantly faster than open-source state-of-the-art methods, enabling integration of scene graph generation with task execution for real-time deployment.

Conclusion: BiMoSG delivers efficient open-set 3D scene graph generation by enabling fast coarse representations with on-demand slow, fine-grained graphs, facilitating real-time robotic perception and action. Future work may address switching criteria, accuracy-speed trade-offs, and scalability to larger environments.

Abstract: Open-set task execution can significantly benefit from seamlessly switching between coarse and fine scene representations depending on the context and the evolving information as the robot explores the environment. For example, it is often sufficient to start with a coarse scene representation initially and only employ a finer, more granular scene representation when the robot encounters regions which are likely to contain the task relevant objects. Hence, in this work, we propose BiMoSG, a bimodal 3D scene graph generation approach for open-set tasks. BiMoSG employs a "fast" mode by default to efficiently generate a coarse 3D scene graph and can switch to a "slow" mode for generating a finer open vocabulary 3D scene graph of task relevant objects. We demonstrate that our proposed 3D scene graph generation approach is significantly faster than the open-source state-of-the-art approaches. This allows us to integrate the scene graph generation process with task execution for real-time deployment.

</details>


### [350] [Building Generalization Into Behavior Generation Via Adaptive Compositions of Regularities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31110)
*Aravind Battaje,Malte Bernhard,Vito Mengers,Oliver Brock*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive composition of regularities in a differentiable network supports generalization in robotics by composing predictable relations into situation-appropriate structures for behavior generation; tested in AICON with gradient-descent-based behavior, showing context-appropriate results across novel conditions except when regularities are insufficient, with ablations showing informativeness-driven gating of regularities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization in robotics suffers when world structure varies across contexts. The authors hypothesize that generalization can emerge from adaptively composing regularities (predictable relationships) within the robot-environment system, creating situation-appropriate behavior without hard-coding all scenarios.

Method: Introduce AICON (Active InterCONnect), a differentiable network where regularities are represented as interacting processes. Sensory feedback enables composition of these regularities, and gradient descent drives behavior. The authors test in a simple simulated problem with identifiable regularities, expose the model to diverse novel conditions, and perform ablations to assess the role of regularities and their informativeness.

Result: The model generates context-appropriate behavior across most novel conditions; one failure case occurs where the encoded regularities are provably insufficient. Ablation studies show the network automatically modulates which regularities influence behavior based on informativeness, indicating adaptive composition as a key inductive bias for generalization.

Conclusion: Adaptive, informativeness-aware composition of regularities within a differentiable control framework constitutes a strong inductive bias for generalization in behavior generation in robotics.

Abstract: Generalization in robotics requires prior knowledge about how the world is structured, yet this structure changes from one situation to the next. This paper investigates the proposition that generalization arises from adaptively composing regularities -- predictable relationships within the robot-environment system -- into situation-appropriate structures for behavior generation. We examine this proposition by analyzing the mechanism in AICON (Active InterCONnect), a framework representing regularities as interacting processes in a differentiable network, where sensory feedback realizes composition and gradient descent generates behavior. To isolate adaptive composition as the key mechanism, we study a simple simulated problem in which all relevant regularities can be identified. We expose the resulting model to a wide range of novel conditions not considered during design, and we find that it generates context-appropriate behavior in all but one case, where encoded regularities are provably insufficient. Ablations reveal that the network automatically modulates which regularities influence behavior based on their informativeness. These results suggest that adaptive composition of regularities constitutes a powerful inductive bias for building generalization into behavior generation.

</details>


### [351] [Don't Fool Me Twice: Adapting to Adversity in the Wild with Experience-Driven Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31119)
*Navin Sriram Ravie,Andrew Jong,Krrish Jain,John Liu,Omar Alama,Bijo Sebastian,Sebastian Scherer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A continual learning framework, Don't Fool Me Twice, enables mobile embodied agents to learn online from disturbances, attribute anomalies to semantic causes via vision-language priors, and model transient disturbances with kernel regression and semantic voxel-based epistemic uncertainty; validated in simulation and hardware across embodiments and adversity modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unseen unstructured environments make danger prediction difficult and interaction-/embodiment-dependent adversities are not well captured by static priors. There is a need to fuse online learning with semantic reasoning (VLMs) to anticipate, attribute causes, and recover from disturbances.

Method: The framework observes disturbances, describes their effects, enriches descriptions with visual context, and queries a vision-language model to predict possible causes. Local disturbances are modeled with kernel regression for few-shot, transient anomalies. Semantic voxel-centric modeling estimates epistemic uncertainty, treating interaction-driven disturbances as learnable spatial behaviors. The approach tests four hypotheses across simulations and hardware.

Result: Empirical validation in both simulation and hardware across multiple embodiments and adversity modes supports the framework’s ability to predict/understand disturbances and improve planning/recovery via semantics and uncertainty estimation; details of metrics are not specified in the abstract.

Conclusion: The work offers a practical pathway to endow mobile embodied agents with continual, semantics-guided disturbance understanding and uncertainty-aware planning, addressing embodiment- and interaction-dependent adversities in unseen environments via online learning and VLM-based causal reasoning.

Abstract: In robotics, dangers and adversity modes are often embodiment-specific and relative to each agent. A frontier of autonomous mobile robotics is to enable agents to operate effectively in the wild in unseen unstructured environments. A significant challenge in unseen unstructured environments is that it may not be possible to predict all the dangers to the specific robot. Although recent work has used large foundation vision-language models (VLMs) to preemptively predict an exhaustive list of common-sense dangers, it remains difficult to capture possible interaction and embodiment-dependent adversities. We propose a continual learning framework for a mobile embodied agent to learn online from disturbances and attribute anomalous behaviours to causes through semantics, enabling better prediction and planning of the world in the future. Our framework, "Don't Fool Me Twice", first observes disturbances and describes their effects on the robot; this description is augmented with visual context to query a VLM to predict possible causes; the local disturbance is characterized using kernel regression, which allows for efficient, few-shot modeling of transient anomalies. We leverage semantic voxel-centric modeling to estimate epistemic uncertainty, enabling richer downstream recovery by treating interaction-driven disturbances as learnable spatial behaviors. We present four hypotheses and validate them in simulation and on hardware across embodiments and adversity modes.

</details>


### [352] [TARIC: Memory-Augmented Traversability-Aware Outdoor VLN under Interrupted Semantic Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31121)
*Tianle Zeng,Hanjing Ye,Jianwei Peng,Jingwen Yu,Hanxuan Chen,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified outdoor VLN framework addresses cue-free degradation by grounding semantic bearings into executable, traversability-consistent headings and extending cue memory to 3D with uncertainty-aware readout, improving success rates on long-range routes for both quadrupedal and wheeled platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outdoor vision-language navigation suffers from semantic-cue interruptions that render remembered cues infeasible and degrade into backtracking and aimless exploration. The work targets maintaining goal-directed guidance during cue-free phases by elevating traversability from a safety constraint to a stability condition.

Method: Extract semantic bearings from visibility-gated cues and convert them into executable headings guided by a real-time near-field traversability profile. Extend intermittent 2D cues into a world-aligned 3D cue memory with an uncertainty-aware readout to keep guidance reachable and stable as the robot moves, even during detours.

Result: On 600–1000 m routes, simulation success improves by >10 percentage points over the strongest baseline; real-world success reaches 40% versus 17.5% for the strongest baseline, with markedly better robustness during prolonged cue-free intervals for both quadrupedal and wheeled platforms.

Conclusion: Traversability-consistent guidance with 3D cue memory and uncertainty-aware readout enables sustained, goal-directed navigation under semantic interruptions in outdoor VLN, generalizing across platform types and longer horizons.

Abstract: Outdoor vision-language navigation (VLN) in long-range, open-world environments is frequently disrupted by semantic-cue interruptions, where informative goal cues become sparse, occluded, or leave the field of view. Once such cues disappear, agents enter a cue-free phase and often degrade into backtracking, oscillatory headings, or aimless exploration. While memory-based methods attempt to bridge these gaps, they often fail under traversability-driven detours: the remembered cue direction may be infeasible, forcing detours that prolong cue-free phases and gradually render robot-centric cues stale and implicit histories blurred. This makes traversability a stability condition for maintaining goal-directed guidance, rather than merely a local safety concern.
  We propose a unified outdoor VLN framework that survives semantic-cue interruptions by maintaining traversability-consistent executable guidance throughout prolonged cue-free phases. Specifically, our method extracts semantic bearings from visibility-gated goal or exploration cues and grounds them into executable headings using a real-time near-field traversability profile, providing goal-consistent feasible guidance beyond reject-only safety filtering. To prevent guidance degradation during detours, we lift intermittent 2D evidence into a world-aligned 3D cue memory with an uncertainty-aware readout mechanism, ensuring guidance remains continuously reachable and stable as the robot moves.
  We evaluate the framework on quadrupedal and wheeled platforms over 600--1000 m routes. Our method improves simulation success rate by over 10 percentage points over the strongest baseline and achieves a real-world success rate of 40%, compared to 17.5% for the strongest baseline, with substantially higher robustness during prolonged cue-free intervals.

</details>


### [353] [Simulation of collision avoidance behavior in crowd movement by data-driven approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31210)
*Xuanwen Liang,Eric Wai Ming Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Collision-Penalized GAN (CPGAN) reduces pedestrian collisions in bidirectional crowd simulation by integrating a lateral-acceleration-based collision loss and Voronoi-based feature extraction, yielding realistic lane formation and N-t curves.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven crowd models under Euclidean metrics exhibit high collision rates, particularly in bidirectional/multidirectional flows. Incorporating pedestrian dynamics into the learning objective can improve safety realism and flow patterns.

Method: GAN-based architecture (CPGAN) with a novel collision loss based on lateral acceleration and Voronoi-based motion feature extraction to capture local density and neighbor relations in bidirectional crowds.

Result: Significant reduction in opposite-direction collision rates to levels comparable with controlled experiments; successful replication of lane formation and N-t curves in bidirectional flow.

Conclusion: Integrating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms into loss functions is a promising direction for enhancing data-driven crowd simulation realism and safety.

Abstract: Crowd movement simulation is essential for pedestrian safety management and facility layout optimization. Data-driven models enhance trajectory prediction accuracy under Euclidean metrics, yet they suffer from excessively high collision rates, especially in bidirectional and multidirectional flows. In this paper, we establish a novel data-driven crowd simulation model that incorporates the pedestrian collision mechanism into the loss function to reduce collisions. A new lateral-acceleration-based collision loss function and a Voronoi-based motion feature extraction approach are proposed. The model is based on a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) architecture and is termed CPGAN (Collision-Penalized GAN). We evaluate CPGAN in bidirectional flow scenarios, which involve frequent collision avoidance behaviors. Results show that the proposed lateral-acceleration-based collision loss significantly reduces opposite-direction pedestrian collision rates to levels comparable with controlled experiments. CPGAN effectively simulates bidirectional flow, reproducing lane formation and N-t curves. The research outcomes can provide inspiration for integrating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms into loss functions in data-driven crowd simulation.

</details>


### [354] [HARP-VLA: Human-Robot Aligned Representation Learning for Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31234)
*Xiang Zhu,Puzhen Yuan,Yichen Liu,Jianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HARP presents a cross-embodiment aligned representation learning framework for VLA pretraining using limited paired human-robot demonstrations as bridges and abundant unpaired videos as dynamics supervision. It learns a robot-adapted visual encoder and a latent action model with alignment losses to align robot representations with human semantics while maintaining discriminative pair-level cues, enabling unified vision-language-to-latent-action representations for VLA policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-embodiment discrepancies between human and robot data hinder effective VLA pretraining. Leveraging limited paired data with large-scale unpaired videos aims to bridge the gap and scale supervision.

Method: Use limited paired human-robot demonstrations as cross-embodiment bridges; leverage abundant unpaired human and robot videos as dynamics supervision. Train a robot-adapted visual encoder and a latent action model with manipulation-centric auxiliary cues and a source-relative pair-discriminative alignment loss to align robot representations with human semantics while preserving pair-level discrimination. Add a lightweight robot action head to ground latent actions into executable commands.

Result: Demonstrates improved human-robot alignment and downstream policy performance in feature visualization, simulation, and real-world manipulation. Reported metrics include 4.481 average length on CALVIN ABC→D and a 7.1% real-world success rate gain over the strongest baseline.

Conclusion: Aligned vision and latent-action representations enable more effective VLA pretraining from human videos, combining limited cross-embodiment bridges with scalable unpaired data to improve downstream robot control performance.

Abstract: Learning generalizable vision-language-action (VLA) models from large-scale human videos is promising but challenging due to cross-embodiment discrepancies in both visual observations and executable actions. While latent action models reduce the action execution gap by learning action abstractions, they still rely on visual features. Thus, misaligned human and robot visual representations can lead to inconsistencies in policy inputs and induce domain-dependent latent actions, hindering effective co-training with human videos. To address this, we propose HARP, a human-robot aligned representation learning framework for more effective VLA pretraining from human videos. Specifically, HARP uses limited paired human-robot demonstrations as cross-embodiment bridges and abundant unpaired human and robot videos as a scalable dynamics supervision data source. It trains a robot-adapted visual encoder and a latent action model with manipulation-centric auxiliary cues and a source-relative pair-discriminative alignment loss, which adapts robot representations toward human semantics while preserving pair-level discrimination. The learned aligned vision encoder and latent action model provide a unified vision and action representation for VLA-style policy learning, where human and robot videos provide vision-language-to-latent-action supervision and a lightweight robot action head grounds latent actions into executable commands. Experiments on feature visualization, simulation, and realworld manipulation show improved human-robot alignment and downstream policy performance, achieving 4.481 average length on CALVIN ABC$\rightarrow$D and a 7.1\% realworld success rate gain over the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [355] [Before Parc Fermé: RL-Time Pruning for Efficient Embodied LLMs in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31256)
*Luca Benfenati,Ali Azimi,Matteo Risso,Fabio Carapellese,Daniele Jahier Pagliari,Alessio Burrello*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces Before Parc Fermé (BPF), pruning embodied LLM controllers during RL (with two variants: BPF-RL and BPF-SFT/RL) to improve task performance, memory, and throughput trade-offs. BPF outperforms post-training pruning and smaller dense baselines; on RobotxR1 it yields up to 1.69x better size–end-to-end performance trade-off and up to 27% decode throughput gains on Jetson AGX Orin.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied LLMs used in robotic control face real-time constraints due to memory and inference latency. Post-hoc pruning fails to account for task-specific closed-loop supervision; pruning during optimization could yield better trade-offs by exploiting feedback signals during training.

Method: Propose Before Parc Fermé (BPF) as an iterative pruning strategy executed during reinforcement learning (RL). Two variants: (1) BPF-RL – prune during RL at predefined training intervals; (2) BPF-SFT/RL – prune during the Supervised Finetuning (SFT) stage and later during RL until target pruning is reached. Evaluation on RobotxR1 using LLM-Pruner, compared against post-training pruning, post-training pruning with RL recovery, SFT-stage pruning, and smaller dense models; measures include parameters removed per loss of control adaptability and end-to-end performance, plus target-device throughput on Jetson AGX Orin.

Result: BPF achieves the best trade-off among pruning strategies. Specifically, BPF-SFT/RL achieves a 1.69× better size–end-to-end performance trade-off than directly selecting a smaller dense model. On target hardware, compact models deliver up to 27% decode throughput improvement.

Conclusion: Pruning during RL, especially when combined with SFT-stage pruning (BPF-SFT/RL), yields superior efficiency for embodied LLM controllers by accounting for task-specific closed-loop dynamics. This approach enables substantial memory and latency reductions with minimal impact on control adaptability, outperforming traditional post-training pruning and smaller dense baselines.

Abstract: Embodied Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as reasoning modules in robotic control pipelines to improve human-robot interaction, but their memory and generation latency make real-time deployment difficult. Pruning can reduce these costs, but for controllers that undergo multiple pre- and post-training phases, the crucial question is not only how much to prune, but when pruning should occur.
  In this work, we propose Before Parc Fermé (BPF), a pruning strategy performed during RL that compresses embodied LLM controllers while they are still being optimized for closed-loop behavior. This allows pruning decisions to account for the task-specific supervision and closed-loop feedback that shape the final controller. We propose two variants: BPF-RL, which performs iterative pruning during RL by removing part of the model at predefined training intervals, and BPF-SFT/RL, which first prunes part of the model structure during SFT and then further compresses it during RL using the same iterative strategy as BPF-RL until the target pruning ratio is reached. We evaluate BPF on RobotxR1, an LLM-based autonomous-driving control pipeline, using an established LLM pruning framework (LLM-Pruner), and compare it against post-training pruning, post-training pruning with RL recovery, SFT-stage pruning, and smaller dense models from the same family.
  Our results show that BPF provides the best task-performance vs. memory and throughput trade-off among the considered pruning strategies. When compressing the larger RobotxR1 models, BPF-SFT/RL achieves a $1.69\times$ better size-end-to-end performance trade-off than directly selecting a smaller dense model from the same family, measured as removed parameters per lost percentage point of control adaptability. On the Jetson AGX Orin mounted on the target robotic platform, the compact models improve decode throughput by up to $27\%$.

</details>


### [356] [DeMaVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Foundation Model for Generalizable Deformable Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31286)
*Taiyi Su,Jian Zhu,Tianjian Wang,Youzhang He,Zitai Huang,Jianjun Zhang,Chong Ma,Hanyang Wang,Tianjiao Zhang,Munan Yin,Weihao Ding,Yi Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeMaVLA is a generalizable Vision-Language-Action foundation model for deformable-object manipulation, using a pruned action expert atop a VLM backbone with flow-based continuous control. It pretrains on ~5k hours of real-world demonstrations and further fine-tunes via DAgger on mixed folding data, achieving competitive RoboTwin performance and strong real-world folding results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of generalizable, category-agnostic deformable manipulation policies in VLA systems and the degradation from naïve multi-task training due to task interference.

Method: A VLM backbone paired with a specialized action expert; continuous actions generated via flow matching. The action expert is realized by pruning every other transformer layer to reduce compute while preserving layer-wise alignment. Pretraining on ~5k hours of real-world dual-arm demonstrations; post-training with DAgger on mixed folding data including self-collected demonstrations and corrective trajectories.

Result: Competitive performance on RoboTwin and strong real-world results on a household folding benchmark, demonstrating the effectiveness of scalable real-world data, efficient action generation, and corrective learning.

Conclusion: Scalable real-world data collection, efficient, layer-pruned action generation, and corrective learning via DAgger enable general-purpose VLA policies for deformable-object manipulation across diverse objects and settings.

Abstract: Real-world household robots require Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation models that can acquire reusable manipulation skills across diverse objects, task conditions, and household environments. Deformable-object folding is a representative challenge, requiring robots to handle clothing items from random initial states across varying categories, geometries, materials, and scenes. However, existing VLA systems commonly train separate policies for different object categories, while naively mixed multi-task training often suffers from task interference and degraded performance. To move beyond category-specific folding policies, we introduce DeMaVLA, a VLA foundation model for generalizable Deformable Manipulation. DeMaVLA adopts a VLM backbone with an action expert and formulates continuous action generation using flow matching. To improve efficiency, the action expert is constructed by pruning every other transformer layer while preserving layer-wise alignment with the VLM backbone, reducing training and inference cost. DeMaVLA is first pre-trained on approximately 5,000 hours of selected real-world dual-arm demonstrations to acquire general manipulation priors. It is then post-trained on mixed folding data that aggregates self-collected demonstrations and corrective trajectories from real-robot failures across multiple folding tasks through a human-in-the-loop Data Aggregation~(DAgger) pipeline. Experiments show that DeMaVLA achieves competitive performance on RoboTwin and strong real-world results on our household folding benchmark. These results highlight the value of scalable real-world data, efficient action generation, and corrective learning for general-purpose VLA policies in deformable-object manipulation.

</details>


### [357] [AR Forcing: Towards Long-Horizon Robot Navigation World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31314)
*Yifei Yang,Zehua Fan,Huan Li,Aoqi Wang,Lida Huang,Haibao Yu,Haiyan Liu,Xuanyao Mao,Jason Bao,Liang Xu,Bingchuan Sun,Yan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AR Forcing proposes an autoregressive training strategy for diffusion-based robot navigation, integrating the diffusion loss into the autoregressive loop to expose training to the inference state distribution, improving long-horizon consistency and trajectory accuracy without extra discriminators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training/inference distribution shift in diffusion-based navigation causes instability in long-horizon prediction; there is a need to expose the model to inference dynamics during training to stabilize performance.

Method: At each step, the model uses its own predictions to update the context and optimizes the single-step noise prediction objective within the diffusion framework; integrates the standard diffusion loss into the autoregressive training loop; no additional discriminators or distribution-matching losses; retains the original diffusion framework and sampler; easy to integrate.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multi-domain navigation datasets (RECON, SCAND, HuRoN, TartanDrive) shows improved consistency of generated images during long-horizon navigation and higher accuracy of predicted trajectories compared with strong baselines; demonstrates robustness in complex known and unknown environments; code will be released soon.

Conclusion: AR Forcing effectively mitigates train–inference distribution mismatch in diffusion-based navigation, improving long-horizon performance without altering the diffusion framework or adding extra losses; maintains simplicity and ease of integration.

Abstract: The diffusion based robot navigation world models are typically trained using parallel supervision, while autoregressive inference is employed during path planning. This results in a distribution shift between training and inference, which destabilizes the performance over long-horizon prediction. We propose AR Forcing, an autoregressive training strategy, which integrates the standard diffusion loss into the autoregressive training loop. At each step, the model uses its own predictions to update the context and optimize the single step noise prediction objective, thereby explicitly exposing the model to the inference state distribution during training. Our method does not require additional discriminators or distribution-matching losses, retains the original diffusion framework and sampler, and is easy to integrate. Experiments on multi-domain navigation datasets (RECON, SCAND, HuRoN, TartanDrive) show that compared with strong baselines, AR Forcing improved the consistency of generated images during long-horizon navigation and the accuracy of predicted trajectories, enhancing robustness of the model in complex known and unknown environments. We will release the code soon.

</details>


### [358] [Surface Constraint Policy for Learning Surface-Constrained and Dynamically Feasible Robot Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31321)
*Shuai Ke,Jiexin Zhang,Huan Zhao,Zhiao Wei,Yikun Guo,Jie Pan,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a Surface Constraint Policy (SCP) that combines a 2D Gaussian-encoded surface constraint from demonstrations with a diffusion-based policy over multimodal observations, mapping to surface-constrained Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) for smooth, feasible surface manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based imitation learning struggles with complex free-form surface constraints and dynamic feasibility, leading to stochastic actions that poor align with surfaces and unstable contacts. Explicit surface geometry modeling and feasible motion generation are needed.

Method: 1) Encode surface geometry constraints using a 2D weighted Gaussian kernel learned from demonstrations. 2) Use a diffusion-based policy to infer task-level action intents from multimodal inputs (vision and robot state). 3) Transform intents into surface-constrained DMPs via a similarity-based action mapping to ensure smooth, compliant motion.

Result: Demonstrates SCP can generate structured surface geometric intent and dynamically admissible actions, yielding higher task success rates and improved contact stability under surface constraints across multiple surface manipulation tasks, compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: SCP effectively integrates geometric surface constraints with diffusion-based planning and DMP-based execution, enabling reliable surface manipulation under complex constraints. The approach shows improved performance and generalization potential, with avenues for further work in kernel design, perception robustness, and real-time deployment.

Abstract: Diffusion-based imitation learning methods have driven rapid progress in robot dexterous manipulation tasks. However, they have limitations when applied to tasks that involve complex free-form surface constraints because of their lack of explicit surface geometry constraint modeling and the dynamic feasibility issue, resulting in stochastic action generation that fails to achieve reliable surface alignment and maintain stable contact. To address these limitations, we propose a novel surface constraint policy (SCP) for generating robot actions that satisfy free-form surface constraints on the basis of human demonstrations and real-time visual observations. First, the surface geometry constraint is encoded using a two-dimensional weighted Gaussian kernel function that is derived from demonstrations. Building on the encoded surface geometry constraints, the diffusion-based policy is used to infer task-level action intentions from multimodal sensory inputs, including visual observations and robot state feedback. These intentions are further transformed into surface-constrained dynamic movement primitives (DMPs) through a similarity-based action mapping method, thereby enabling smooth and compliant motion execution. The SCP achieves generation of structured surface geometric intent and dynamically admissible actions. The proposed method is validated on multiple surface manipulation tasks and compared with existing techniques. The experimental results demonstrate superior task success rates and contact stability under surface constraints.

</details>


### [359] [Learning Terrain-Aware Whole-Body Control for Perceptive Legged Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31343)
*Sikai Guo,Yudong Zhong,Guoyang Zhao,Botao Dang,Zhihai Bi,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A terrain-aware whole-body controller (TA-WBC) for legged manipulators uses an RL-based unified policy with a hybrid exteroception encoder, end-effector sampling on the foot contact plane, and dual-policy distillation to achieve robust cross-terrain loco-manipulation and an expanded reachable workspace.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing whole-body controllers rely heavily on proprioception and struggle with terrain topology perception. Incorporating exteroceptive terrain information is essential for proactive posture/foothold adaptation and stable loco-manipulation in human-centric, cluttered environments.

Method: Develop TA-WBC with (1) a hybrid exteroception encoder to extract terrain features; (2) an RL-based unified policy for loco-manipulation across terrains; (3) an end-effector sampling method based on the foot contact plane to decouple manipulation goals from base fluctuations; (4) a dual-policy distillation module to fuse expansive gait/motion knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Simulations and real-world experiments show a larger reachable space, reduced tracking error, and fewer unexpected stumbles, indicating robust cross-terrain loco-manipulation and improved terrain adaptability.

Conclusion: TA-WBC demonstrates the feasibility and promise of terrain-aware, exteroception-informed whole-body control for legged manipulators, enabling more reliable operation across diverse terrains; future work may address generalization to unseen terrains, computational efficiency, and sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Legged manipulators integrate exceptional terrain adaptability along with mobile manipulation capabilities, which make them highly promising for deployment in human-centric environments. By coordinating the control of both legs and arms, a whole-body controller can significantly expand the operational workspace of legged manipulators. However, many existing whole-body controllers primarily depend on proprioception and do not incorporate the critical exteroception required for effective terrain topology perception. This limitation can hinder their ability to adapt to varying environmental conditions and navigate complex terrains effectively. In this paper, we introduce TA-WBC, a terrain-aware whole-body control framework for legged manipulators, which features a novel RL-based unified policy tailored to whole-body loco-manipulation tasks in various terrains. Specifically, we employ a hybrid exteroception encoder to extract terrain features, providing an essential basis for the robot to proactively adapt posture and footholds. Furthermore, to facilitate stable cross-terrain loco-manipulation, we propose a novel end-effector sampling method based on the foot contact plane, decoupling manipulation target from base fluctuations. Moreover, a dual-policy distillation module is introduced to integrate expansive whole-body motion with terrain adaptability without catastrophic forgetting. The simulation and real-world experiments validate the robustness of our proposed controller, which leads to a larger reachable space, less tracking error, and reduced unexpected stumbles. This unified policy highlights the promising capabilities of legged manipulators in performing loco-manipulation tasks across complex terrains.

</details>


### [360] [Haptic Sorter: A Unified Planning Framework for Online Shape Estimation and Real-Time Pose Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31352)
*Zhuoyi Lu,Lin Yang,Sri Harsha Turlapati,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified model-based framework for haptic-informed manipulation under object shape/pose uncertainty, combining Bayesian Optimization for haptic shape inference (via superellipses), adaptive manipulation potentials for geometry-aware quasi-static interaction, and an online ODE for real-time pose inference from tactile feedback; validated on a 2D sorting task across varying geometries in both simulation and real-world multi-arm setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve unreliable object geometry and pose due to sensor uncertainties and occlusions by integrating haptic perception with manipulation planning in a single framework.

Method: 1) Bayesian Optimization to guide haptic exploration for shape inference with superellipses as the geometric boundary model. 2) Adaptive manipulation potential encoding that incorporates object geometry for quasi-static robot-object interaction. 3) An online Ordinary Differential Equation that fuses model predictions with tactile feedback for real-time pose inference. The system is evaluated on a 2D robotic sorting task with varying geometries, in both simulation and a real-world multi-arm setup.

Result: The approach is deployed and evaluated across varied object geometries, demonstrating robustness and generalizability of the framework in both simulation and a real-world multi-arm environment for a 2D sorting task.

Conclusion: The work provides a unified, haptic-informed geometric framework that enhances manipulation under geometric uncertainty, showing feasibility and robustness in practical settings; future extensions could explore 3D shapes and more complex manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Robotics manipulation usually assumes that the shape and pose of the object are known to the robot prior to motion planning. However, precise geometric information is not always available in practice, and pose inference suffers from sensor uncertainties and view occlusion. In this work, we propose a unified model-based geometric framework integrating robotic haptic perception, modeling, and manipulation planning. Our novelties involve: \textit{i)} Introducing Bayesian Optimization (BO) to guide the haptic exploration for object shape inference, where superellipses are used to approximate geometric boundary; \textit{ii)} Adaptive formulation of manipulation potential encoding object geometry for quasi-static robot-object interaction; \textit{iii)} Proposing an online Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) for real-time pose inference based on model prediction and tactile feedback. We deploy our system on a 2D robotic sorting task, and vary object geometries to validate the robustness and generalizability of our framework in both simulation and a real-world multi-arm setup.

</details>


### [361] [LiftNav: Path Planning via Semantic Lifting in TSDF-Guided Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31376)
*Hannah Schieber,Dominik Frischmann,Victor Schaack,Angela P. Schoellig,Daniel Roth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiftNav combines a TSDF+GS dual map with real-time YOLO detection, 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization, plus a hinge-loss collision penalty, to enable semantic navigation without dense 3D embeddings. In Replica simulation, it achieves 100% feasibility and shorter trajectories than a radiance field baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between geometry-focused representations (TSDF) that guarantee safe planning and photorealistic, semantically rich representations (GS) that suffer from soft geometry, by fusing them into a hybrid map and a real-time semantic navigation pipeline.

Method: Utilize GSFusion's TSDF+GS dual map; run YOLO-based object detection in real-time; perform TSDF-based 3D lifting for geometry; apply B-spline trajectory optimization; incorporate a hinge-loss-based collision penalty to improve smoothness and safety.

Result: In simulation on the Replica dataset, LiftNav achieves a 100% feasibility rate and produces shorter trajectories compared to a state-of-the-art radiance field baseline.

Conclusion: LiftNav enables flexible semantic navigation without dense 3D embeddings by leveraging a hybrid TSDF+GS map paired with a real-time perception and optimization pipeline, offering improved safety and path efficiency.

Abstract: Autonomous robots in unknown indoor environments require both reliable collision avoidance and object-level understanding. Classical representations such as TSDF support safe planning but lack semantics, while photorealistic methods like Gaussian Splatting (GS) provide rich appearance yet suffer from soft geometry, limiting precise obstacle avoidance. We present LiftNav, a hybrid navigation framework built on GSFusion's TSDF+GS dual map, augmented with a real-time pipeline of YOLO-based detection, TSDF-based 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization. This design enables flexible semantic navigation without dense 3D embeddings. We further introduce a hinge-loss-based collision penalty that improves trajectory smoothness and safety. We evaluate our approach in a simulation using the Replica dataset. Compared against a state-of-the-art radiance field baseline we show a 100% feasibility rate and shorter trajectories.

</details>
